title: Military Object Detection Using a Fine-Tuned Florence Vision Model
abstract: Accurately detecting military vehicles and equipment in real-world environments is a challenging but vital task in modern defense. In this work, we fine-tuned Microsoft’s Florence-2 Large model to recognize a wide range of military assets, including tanks, helicopters, artillery, and ground troops under realistic battlefield conditions. Instead of training on clean or staged images, we built a dataset of over 7,000 images collected from military exercises and surveillance footage. These images included difficult cases like camouflage, partial visibility, and low lighting. The objects were annotated using tools such as CVAT and Roboflow, allowing us to maintain consistency across all categories. To improve performance on underrepresented classes, we used data augmentation and class-aware sampling. Our model achieved strong results, with a precision of 98.95%, recall of 99.33%, and an average IoU of 85.29%. These outcomes show that the model performs well even in messy, real-world conditions. This work highlights the potential of Florence-2 Large for practical defense applications like drone surveillance, battlefield monitoring, and automated threat detection.
keywords: Military Object Detection;Florence-2;LoRA Fine-Tuning;Vision-Language Models;Real-World Battlefield Dataset;Camouflage Detection;Data Augmentation;Class-Aware Sampling;Object Detection Metrics;Precision and Recall;Intersection over Union (IoU);Automated Threat Detection;Defense and Security Applications;YOLOv5 Benchmark Comparison;Computer Vision for Surveillance
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities
author: Jaya Sharma;Harshvardhan Gupta;Shreyas Jain;Kushagra Jain;Ayush Chouhan;Sobhan Babu

roast.laymen_summary: The authors took a pre-built AI from Microsoft and showed it pictures of tanks and helicopters to turn it into a high-tech spotter. They claim it’s nearly perfect at finding targets, even when they’re hidden in the mud or the dark.
why_bad: The 99% precision and recall metrics are statistically hallucinogenic and suggest the model essentially memorized the answer key instead of learning to 'see'.
Fine-tuning an existing model without any architectural changes is a 'how-to' guide, not a scientific contribution to the field of AI.
A 7,000-image dataset is a drop in the bucket for a foundation model of this scale, making it prone to 'overfitting' where it recognizes the specific dirt on a tank rather than the tank itself.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine I’m pitching you a revolutionary new 'Visual Search Engine' that can find Waldo with 100% accuracy. Sounds amazing, right? Except the fine print reveals I only trained it on three pages from the same book, and I forgot to tell you that Waldo was the only thing in red. This paper isn't a breakthrough in military surveillance; it's a guy who memorized one specific deck of flashcards and is now claiming he can read every language on Earth. It's not 'battlefield ready'—it’s just really good at recognizing the specific lighting of the afternoon those 7,000 photos were taken.

title: BugPilot: Complex Bug Generation for Efficient Training of SWE Agents
abstract:
High quality bugs are key to training the next generation of LLM-based software engineering (SWE) agents.
We introduce a novel method for synthetic generation of difficult and diverse bugs.
Our method instructs SWE Agents to introduce a feature whereby they may unintentionally break tests, resulting in bugs. 
Prior approaches often induce an out-of-distribution effect by generating bugs intentionally (e.g. by introducing local perturbation to existing code), which does not reflect realistic development processes.
We do a qualitative analysis to demonstrate that our approach for generating bugs more closely reflects the patterns found in human-authored edits.
Through extensive experiments, we demonstrate that our bugs provide more efficient training data for supervised fine-tuning, outperforming other bug datasets by 2% with half the training data.
Finally, we train with reinforcement learning on our high-quality generated bugs; starting with a strong base model we trained with a mixture of previously available bugs.
We thereby obtain a state-of-the-art 32B parameter model on SWE-Bench Verified, achieving 52.4% pass@1 averaged over three seeds.
keywords: SWE Agents;agentic bug generation;training agents for SWE tasks
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs
author: Atharv Sonwane;Isadora White;Hyunji Lee;Matheus Pereira;Lucas Caccia;Minseon Kim;Zhengyan Shi;Chinmay Singh;Alessandro Sordoni;Marc-Alexandre Côté;Xingdi Yuan

roast.laymen_summary: The authors realized that current AI coding assistants are bored by simple bugs, so they decided to train them by intentionally asking another AI to 'accidentally' break things while adding new features. They claim that teaching a robot to mess up like a tired human intern makes it better at fixing real-world software disasters.
why_bad: The 'novel' method is essentially just asking an LLM to be incompetent, which lacks the algorithmic depth expected for a top-tier systems paper.
A 2% performance increase is statistically indistinguishable from a lucky roll of the dice, making the 'efficiency' claims look like a stretch.
The qualitative analysis relies on 'vibes' rather than rigorous metrics, failing to prove that these synthetic bugs actually mimic the complex logic errors humans make.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' Most culinary schools teach you to cook by showing you a perfect Beef Wellington. But our 'innovative' chefs here argue that you'll never learn to be a head chef unless you practice on food that was ruined by a clumsy sous-chef who 'unintentionally' dropped a salt shaker into the soup while trying to season it. They aren't just making messes; they're crafting 'bespoke culinary failures.' The problem? The judges (reviewers) tasted the final dish and realized that while the student is okay at fishing out salt, they still can't actually cook a steak. It turns out that being the world's best janitor in a kitchen you set on fire yourself doesn't actually make you a Michelin-star chef—it just makes you really good at smelling smoke.
roast.tone: Sharp, witty, and grounded in the reality of incremental AI research.

title: Distributional Causal Fairness Testing
abstract: Causality is widely used in fairness analysis to prevent discrimination on sensitive attributes, such as gender in hiring and race in crime prediction. However, the conventional potential outcomes framework emphasizes group-level causal estimation but lacks a population-level (distributional) perspective in fairness analysis, which may lead to a fairness illusion—misrepresenting fairness by approximating the distributional information using limited statistics (e.g., first- or second-order statistics such as mean or variance). To address this limitation, we define a distribution-based potential outcomes framework in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space, based on which we reframe fairness analysis as a problem of Distributional Causal Fairness Testing (DCFT). Within DCFT, the null hypothesis assumes that a sensitive attribute is fair if its factual and counterfactual potential outcome distributions are sufficiently close. This discrepancy is quantified as the defined Distributional Counterfactual Treatment Effect, which serves as the test statistic in DCFT. To ensure the reliability of testing results, we establish the testing consistency of distributional counterfactual treatment effect through rigorous theoretical analysis. Furthermore,  DCFT offers fine-grained control over fairness criteria through a tunable fairness confidence $\epsilon$, enabling flexible fairness sensitivity. Extensive experiments on real-world datasets demonstrate that DCFT reliably diagnoses unfairness in deep models, validating its practical effectiveness and theoretical soundness.
primary_area: causal reasoning
author: Jiarun Fu;Lizhong Ding;Pengqi Li;Qiuning Wei;Yurong Cheng;Tianfei Zhou;Yong Liu;Guoren Wang

title: BriLLM: Brain-inspired Large Language Model
abstract: We introduce BriLLM, the first brain-inspired large language model that establishes a genuinely biology- and neuroscience-grounded machine learning paradigm. Unlike previous approaches that primarily mimic local neural features, BriLLM implements Signal Fully-connected flowing (SiFu) learning—the first framework to authentically replicate the brain's macroscopic information processing principles at scale. Our approach is uniquely validated by two core neurocognitive facts: (1) _static semantic mapping_ to dedicated cortical regions, and (2) _dynamic signal propagation_ through electrophysiological activity. This foundation enables transformative capabilities: inherent multi-modal compatibility, full node-level interpretability, context-length independent scaling, and global-scale simulation of brain-like language processing. Our 1–2B parameter models demonstrate stable learning dynamics while replicating GPT-1-level generative performance. Scalability analysis confirms feasibility of 100–200B parameter variants. BriLLM represents a paradigm shift from representation learning toward biologically-validated AGI foundations, offering a principled solution to current AI's fundamental limitations.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: The authors claim to have built a 'true' digital brain by copying how neurons fire, promising it will solve every fundamental flaw in AI. In reality, they’ve created an incredibly complex 'biological' simulator that struggles to outsmart a chatbot from 2018.
why_bad: The 'biological' architecture is essentially a Rube Goldberg machine that achieves GPT-1 level performance—results that have been obsolete for over half a decade.
The paper relies on 'neuro-babble' buzzwords like 'SiFu learning' to mask a lack of rigorous mathematical proof or competitive benchmarks.
Claiming the model will scale to 200 billion parameters is pure 'trust me bro' science, given that the current version is already underperforming standard, simpler designs.
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, we are witnessing a feat of absolute madness on the track! Team BriLLM has shown up to a modern Grand Prix with a car made of hand-carved granite and dinosaur bones because they claim 'nature knows best.' They’re screaming about 'biological authenticity' while being absolutely smoked by a 2018 Honda Civic. They’re telling the crowd this rock-mobile will break the sound barrier if they just make it 100 times heavier, but right now, the driver is just frantically pedaling with his bare feet while the engine emits a confusing 'SiFu' sound. It is a world-class effort to reinvent the wheel and somehow come up with a triangle!

title: Orthonormal Regularization in Low-Rank Adaptation
abstract:
Performance degradation on tasks outside the fine-tuning domain is often observed while performing parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) on neural networks with limited data. For example, fine-tuning on mathematical datasets may impair the large language model’s coding ability. We analyze this issue and identify the condition number of weight matrices as a key factor contributing to such degradation. To address this, we propose Singular Values and Orthonormal Regularized Singular Vectors Adaptation, or SORSA,
a novel PEFT method that explicitly improves the conditioning of the adapted model parameters, thereby mitigating degradation and preserving broader capabilities. Empirically, we demonstrate that SORSA outperforms full fine-tuning, LoRA, PiSSA and AdaLoRA.
keywords: Low-rank Adaptation;parameter efficient fine-tuning;large language models
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: When you teach an AI a new skill, it usually gets 'digital amnesia' and forgets its old tricks because its internal math becomes a cluttered mess. This paper proposes a strict organizational system called SORSA to keep the AI's brain tidy, theoretically stopping it from becoming a specialized idiot.
why_bad: The added mathematical complexity introduces significant computational overhead that arguably outweighs the marginal performance gains.
The benchmarks are cherry-picked to show success on specific shifts, leaving critics skeptical about how it handles real-world, messy data.
The 'superiority' over standard LoRA is statistically thin, suggesting the authors are just over-engineering a problem that a better learning rate could solve.
roast.analogy_name: The Marie Kondo-ing of the Machine Mind
roast.analogy_description: In a world where learning Basic Algebra makes you forget how to tie your shoes... one team of researchers has a plan. They won't just teach the AI; they'll force every single one of its thoughts into a rigid, 90-degree filing cabinet. It’s called SORSA. It’s neat. It’s tidy. It’s computationally expensive as hell. Coming this summer: watch as a model tries to stay organized while its internal weights scream in orthonormal agony. It’s not just fine-tuning—it’s an obsessive-compulsive intervention.

title: Adversarial Robust Reward Shaping for Safe Reinforcement Learning in AI-Generated Code
abstract: We propose \textbf{Adversarial Robust Reward Shaping (ARRS)}, a novel reinforcement learning framework for generating secure code that explicitly addresses vulnerabilities to adversarial evasion attacks. Conventional reward functions in code generation tasks often do not take into consideration how vulnerable detection mechanisms to subtle perturbations in syntax are which leads to brittle security guarantees. The proposed method integrates an \textbf{Adversarial Robustness Module (ARM)} into the reward computation pipeline, which systematically identifies worst-case failure scenarios through gradient-based perturbation analysis and penalizes the policy for generating exploitable code patterns. ARM works by generating adversarial examples that are semantically preserving and degrade the performance of the code evaluation system to the utmost and then teaching the RL agent to build a solution that is intrinsically secure using a robustness penalty added to the reward signal.
primary_area: transfer learning, meta learning, and lifelong learning

roast.laymen_summary: We attempted to make AI-generated code hacker-proof by forcing the AI to train against a digital bully that constantly tries to find holes in its work. It’s essentially 'Security via Stress Test,' where the model only gets a gold star if its code can survive a simulated cyber-mugging during the writing process.
why_bad: The 'ARM' module is a computational nightmare that adds massive overhead, essentially requiring a supercomputer to do the job of a standard linter.
The gradient-based perturbations often create 'nonsense' code that is technically robust but practically useless because it no longer performs the intended function.
The system suffers from 'Reward Hacking' where the AI simply learns to outsmart the specific auditor rather than actually writing secure, high-quality software.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine we’re down on the field watching a world-class knitter try to produce a cashmere sweater, but there’s a 300-pound linebacker—our ARM module—permitted to tackle them every time they switch to a purl stitch! The AI is so busy bracing for impact and dodging helmets that it’s produced a garment that is technically 'impact-resistant' but has four sleeves and a neck hole the size of a thimble. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton, but the crowd is booing because while the sweater can survive a grenade blast, it’s fundamentally unwearable and the knitter has spent three weeks on a single cuff!

title: Adapting in the Dark: Towards Stable and Efficient Black-Box Test-Time Adaptation
abstract:
Test-Time Adaptation (TTA) for black-box models accessible only via APIs presents a significant yet largely unexplored challenge. 
Existing truly black-box methods are scarce; post-hoc output refinement shows minimal benefit, while naively introducing Zeroth-Order Optimization (ZOO) for prompt tuning at test time suffers from prohibitive query costs and catastrophic instability. To address these challenges, we introduce **BETA** (Black-box Efficient Test-time Adaptation), a novel framework that enables stable and efficient adaptation for both standard Vision Models and large Vision-Language Models.  BETA uniquely employs a lightweight, local white-box steering model to create a tractable gradient pathway for optimization, circumventing the need for expensive ZOO methods. This is achieved through a prediction harmonization technique that creates a shared objective, stabilized by consistency regularization and a prompt learning-oriented filtering strategy. Requiring only *a single API call per test sample*, BETA achieves a +7.1\% gain on a ViT-B/16 model and a +3.4\% gain on powerful CLIP models; remarkably, its performance *surpasses* that of certain white-box and gray-box TTA methods (e.g., TENT and TPT). This practical effectiveness is further validated on a real-world commercial API, where BETA achieves a +5.2\% gain for just \$0.4—a 250x cost advantage over ZOO—establishing it as a robust and efficient solution for adapting models in the dark at test time.
primary_area: transfer learning, meta learning, and lifelong learning

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to improve 'black-box' AI models—the ones hidden behind a paywall where you can't see the internal code—by using a tiny, visible model to act as a GPS. They claim they can make these secret models smarter with just one cheap API call, effectively trying to tune a car's engine while the hood is padlocked shut.
why_bad: The 'local steering model' is a massive crutch; if the small model doesn't already closely mimic the secret API model, the whole system guides the AI off a metaphorical cliff.
The 'harmonization' trick is essentially fancy academic phrasing for averaging two guesses together, which reviewers found lacked the theoretical 'meat' required for a top-tier publication.
The experimental setup is suspiciously convenient—it's easy to look good when your 'secret' model and your 'steering' model are from the same family, but real-world APIs are much more unpredictable.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' You’re tasked with finishing a gourmet dish, but the Master Chef is locked in a soundproof pantry and won't give you the recipe. You’re only allowed to ask them one question through the door before the plate goes to the judges. The authors suggest that instead of guessing, you should bring a toddler into the kitchen to smell the steam and tell you what to do. The researchers are thrilled because the toddler correctly identified 'salt' once, but the judges (reviewers) are rejecting the dish because the toddler is just guessing based on a Happy Meal they had earlier, and if the Master Chef had switched from steak to soufflé, your 'lightweight steering model' would have told you to garnish it with ketchup.

title: Sample Size Estimation for Chest X-ray Classification with Foundation Models
abstract: The integration of deep learning models into clinical practice, particularly in radiology, is often hindered by the need for large, meticulously labeled datasets, which entails significant time and financial costs. While foundation models substantially reduce this dependency, a critical question remains: what is the minimum amount of annotated data sufficient to achieve clinically acceptable accuracy? In this work, we introduce a methodology for accurately predicting sample size requirements by modeling learning curves with a power law. Our study demonstrates that modern foundation models, such as XrayCLIP and XraySigLIP, not only outperform traditional architectures but also achieve high ROC-AUC scores with significantly fewer training examples. A key finding of our research is the evidence that the learning dynamics observed with a sample of just 50 labeled cases can predict the model's asymptotic performance with high precision. Thus, our study offers a scientifically grounded approach to optimizing the data annotation process, enabling researchers and clinicians to minimize costs and accelerate the development of reliable diagnostic tools
keywords: Foundation Models;Sample Size Estimation;Learning Curve Analysis;Chest X-ray Classification
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: The authors claim they found a shortcut to figure out exactly how many X-rays a computer needs to see before it becomes a diagnostic pro. They argue that by looking at how the AI handles just 50 images, they can predict its entire future career performance, theoretically saving doctors from the tedious task of labeling thousands of files.
why_bad: Extrapolating long-term performance from only 50 samples is statistically hallucinating; it's less 'scientific modeling' and more 'reading tea leaves' given the high variance in small clinical datasets.

Fitting a power law to a learning curve is the machine learning equivalent of 'discovering' that water is wet; it's a known property of scaling, not a novel methodology worthy of a top-tier publication.

The study ignores the 'pre-training' elephant in the room—these foundation models didn't learn from your 50 images; they already went to medical school on millions of unlabelled images, making your 'sample size estimation' look more like a victory lap than a discovery.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the scouting combine, folks! Today we’ve got a real 'expert' claiming he can predict a kid's Hall of Fame trajectory after watching them kick a ball exactly fifty times in their backyard. It's a bold strategy, Cotton! He’s ignoring the fact that the kid’s dad is a literal FIFA legend—that’s your 'foundation model' right there—and pretending this tiny, noise-heavy sample size isn't just a fluke of good luck and high-fives. One tantrum or a slightly different-sized ball, and this whole 'asymptotic projection' goes straight into the diaper pail. You can't scout the Super Bowl by watching a toddler in the sandbox!

title: Context-Aware Multi-Agent Safety Evaluation for Autonomous Driving
abstract:
Autonomous Driving (AD) faces persistent safety challenges from unforeseen long-tailed driving scenarios that require massive evaluation. Existing solutions, such as road test, scenario-based simulation and rule-based verification, remain insufficient: they either fail to uncover hazardous edge cases and inherit unsafe habits from human data, or lack adaptability across regions. Additionally, current approaches often provide limited contextual understanding, making it challenging to generate interpretable explanations of unsafe behavior. To address these gaps, we introduce **DriveEval**, a context-aware multi-agent framework for autonomous driving safety evaluation. It leverages the comprehensive knowledge and reasoning ability of large language models (LLMs) to understand traffic scenes and detect edge cases, while applying context engineering to ground LLMs in external knowledge, including traffic rules and historical accident data, for interpreting unsafe driving behaviors. The framework is organized as a multi-agent workflow comprising a Data Annotator, Scene Extractor, Rule Checker, Accident Retriever, and Driving Assessor, each handling specialized functions.
This multi-agent design improves precision through specialization, enables modular expansion with new knowledge sources, and allows the most suitable model to be chosen for each task, offering stronger performance than a single monolithic agent.
Experiments show that DriveEval can evaluate sensor data, such as dashcam video, to identify safety risks and recommend actionable improvements. Its assessments are closely aligned with human annotations, demonstrating that context-aware evaluation provides interpretable safety assurance.
primary_area: applications to robotics, autonomy, planning

roast.laymen_summary: This research attempts to build a digital 'backseat driver' composed of several AI chatbots that watch dashcam footage and explain why a car almost crashed. Instead of actual driving logic, it relies on a committee of LLMs to cross-reference traffic laws and old accident reports to write a post-mortem essay on your driving failures.
why_bad: Latency vs. Reality: The 'multi-agent' overhead creates a massive delay, meaning the car would be wrapped around a telephone pole long before the 'Accident Retriever' finished its search.
Linguistic Hallucination: Relying on LLMs for safety evaluation ignores the fact that these models don't understand physics or spatial geometry, they just know which words usually follow 'red light'.
Complexity Bloat: The framework is essentially a Rube Goldberg machine of API calls that replaces efficient, specialized computer vision with an expensive, slow, and chatty committee.
roast.analogy_name: The Post-Game Press Conference from Hell
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a football team where, instead of a middle linebacker with reflexes, you have a five-man committee of philosophy professors standing on the field. When the ball is snapped, the 'Scene Extractor' identifies that a 300-pound man is running at them, the 'Rule Checker' confirms tackling is legal, and the 'Accident Retriever' recalls a similar play from 1978. By the time the 'Driving Assessor' submits the final report on why the quarterback is currently unconscious, the stadium lights have been turned off and the fans are already home. It’s a brilliant tactical analysis of a game they completely forgot to actually play.

title: GTPO AND GRPO-S: TOKEN AND SEQUENCE-LEVEL REWARD SHAPING WITH POLICY ENTROPY
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) is a pivotal task for enhancing Large Language Model (LLM) reasoning. Conventional algorithms, however, typically adhere to a coarse-grained credit assignment paradigm, applying a uniform reward to all tokens in a sequence—a critical flaw in long-chain reasoning tasks. In this paper, we address this challenge and propose Dynamic Entropy Weighting, a novel mechanism that facilitates fine-grained rewards through two new algorithms: Group Token Policy Optimization (GTPO), which assigns an entropy-weighted reward to each token, and the analogous algorithm Sequence-Level GRPO (GRPO-S). Our approach is founded on the hypothesis that high policy entropy within a reasoning path is a powerful heuristic for "cognitive effort" at pivotal junctures, which can be repurposed into a learning signal. By repurposing policy entropy for reward shaping, we achieve true per-token credit assignment. Experimental results across challenging reasoning benchmarks validate the superiority of our approach, showing our methods significantly outperform a strong DAPO baseline and confirming our entropy-weighting mechanism as the key driver of this performance boost.
primary_area: reinforcement learning

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of giving an AI a gold star for a whole essay, these researchers tried to reward it for individual words where it seemed 'confused' or 'stressed.' They gambled on the idea that if the AI is sweating over a choice, it must be doing some heavy-duty thinking that deserves a treat.
why_bad: The core 'Entropy as Effort' hypothesis is a reach; high entropy often just means the model is hallucinating or doesn't know what it's talking about, not that it's 'thinking harder.'
The math is basically just a glorified entropy regularizer with a fresh coat of paint, failing to provide a truly novel theoretical contribution over existing RLHF methods.
The experimental 'victory' is suspicious because they benchmarked against a weak, obscure baseline (DAPO) while dodging comparisons to industry-standard PPO or vanilla GRPO tuning.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley.' Usually, we judge a chef by whether the final risotto tastes like heaven or a salty sponge. But our contestants here claim that's 'too coarse.' Instead, they want to strap a heart-rate monitor to the chef and give extra points for every second his pulse hits 140. They call it 'Dynamic Heat Weighting.' The problem? The chef isn't 'putting in more effort' when his heart races; he’s just accidentally set his sleeve on fire. By rewarding the moments of highest panic, you aren't training a Michelin-star chef—you're just teaching a guy how to be stressed and messy while the rice stays crunchy. Absolute disaster. Get out of my kitchen.

title: Memorization or Interpolation? Detecting LLM Memorization through Input Perturbation Analysis
abstract: While LLMs achieve remarkable performance through training on massive datasets, they often exhibit concerning behaviors such as verbatim reproduction of training data rather than true generalization. This memorization phenomenon raises critical concerns regarding data privacy, intellectual property rights, and the reliability of model evaluations. This paper introduces PEARL, a novel approach for detecting memorization in LLMs. PEARL assesses the sensitivity of an LLM’s performance to input perturbations, enabling detection of memorization in a black-box setting without requiring access to the model’s internal parameters or architecture. Furthermore, we investigate how input perturbations affect output consistency, allowing us to distinguish between true generalization and memorization. Extensive experiments on Pythia models demonstrate that our framework robustly identifies cases where models regurgitate learned information. Applied to GPT-4o, PEARL not only detected memorization of classic texts (e.g., the Bible) and common code snippets from HumanEval but also provided evidence suggesting that certain data sources, such as New York Times articles, were likely included in the model’s training set.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: We built a tool to figure out if AI models are actually smart or just really good at reciting their training data like a kid memorizing a poem they don't understand. By slightly changing the wording of a prompt, we can see if the AI crashes and burns, proving it was just copying-and-pasting from its secret internal library.
why_bad: The 'novel' method is essentially just poking the model with a stick, a technique so established in the field it practically has a pension.
Claiming a model has memorized the Bible or common code is like 'discovering' that a Starbucks serves coffee—it’s a known baseline, not a breakthrough.
The paper fails to provide a rigorous mathematical boundary between 'interpolation' and 'memorization,' leaving the results feeling more like vibes than verifiable science.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where one AI is hailed as the next Mozart, one skeptic suspects the truth: it’s just reading a teleprompter. This summer, PEARL is turning off the screen to see if the superstar can actually sing or if they’re just miming the hits. When the lyrics get slightly garbled, will the model find the melody, or will the music die? Coming this fall: 'The Parrot in the Machine'—rated R for Recitation.
roast.tone_check: Scientific snark levels are nominal; accuracy remains uncompromised.

title: VS-Bench: Evaluating VLMs for Strategic Abilities in Multi-Agent Environments
abstract: Recent advancements in Vision Language Models (VLMs) have expanded their capabilities to interactive agent tasks, yet existing benchmarks remain limited to single-agent or text-only environments. In contrast, real-world scenarios often involve multiple agents interacting within rich visual and textual contexts, posing challenges with both multimodal observations and strategic interactions. To bridge this gap, we introduce Visual Strategic Bench (VS-Bench), a multimodal benchmark that evaluates VLMs for strategic abilities in multi-agent environments. VS-Bench comprises ten vision-grounded environments that cover cooperative, competitive, and mixed-motive interactions. The performance of VLM agents is evaluated across three dimensions: perception measured by element recognition accuracy; strategic reasoning measured by next-action prediction accuracy; and decision-making measured by normalized episode return. Extensive experiments on fifteen leading VLMs show that, although current models exhibit strong perception abilities, there remains a significant gap to optimal performance in reasoning and decision-making, with the best-performing model attaining 46.6% prediction accuracy and 31.4% normalized return. We further analyze the key factors influencing performance, conduct human experiments, and examine failure modes to provide a deeper understanding of VLMs' strategic abilities. By standardizing the evaluation and highlighting the limitations of existing models, we envision VS-Bench as a foundation for future research on strategic multimodal agents. Code and data are available at https://sites.google.com/view/vs-bench.
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks
author: Zelai Xu;Zhexuan Xu;Xiangmin Yi;Huining Yuan;Mo Guang;Kaiwen Long;Xinlei Chen;Yi Wu;Chao Yu;Yu Wang

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers built a digital playground to see if AI can actually play visual strategy games with others instead of just describing them. It turns out that while today’s top AIs are great at spotting the game pieces, they have the strategic instincts of a brick, failing to actually win most of the time.
why_bad: A 31% success rate in decision-making suggests the models are basically clicking random buttons and hoping for a miracle.
Predicting the 'next action' from a static dataset is just glorified autocomplete, failing to capture the real-time 'he-knows-that-I-know-that-he-knows' chaos of true strategy.
The benchmark doesn't sufficiently rule out data leakage, meaning the models might just be reciting memorized walkthroughs from the internet rather than 'thinking'.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Literate Spectator
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense claims this AI is a tactical genius, but the evidence suggests otherwise. Imagine a witness who can describe the 'Murder Weapon' in 4K resolution—right down to the serial number—but then tries to use a baguette to defend themselves in a gunfight. In this courtroom, the VLM is a defendant who can identify every chess piece on the board with 90% accuracy, yet proceeds to move its King into immediate checkmate. The Prosecution rests: being able to 'see' the board doesn't mean you have the legal—or logical—standing to play the game, and a 31% performance rating is grounds for immediate contempt of court.

title: Warm Starts Accelerate Conditional Diffusion
abstract: Generative models like diffusion and flow-matching create high-fidelity samples by progressively refining noise. The refinement process is notoriously slow, often requiring hundreds of function evaluations. We introduce Warm-Start Diffusion (WSD), a method that uses a simple, deterministic model to dramatically accelerate conditional generation by providing a better starting point. Instead of starting generation from an uninformed $\mathcal{N}(\mathbf{0}, I)$ prior, our deterministic warm-start model predicts an informed prior $\mathcal{N}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\mu}}_C, \text{diag}(\hat{\boldsymbol{\sigma}}^2_C))$, whose moments are conditioned on the input context $C$. This warm start substantially reduces the distance the generative process must traverse, and therefore the number of diffusion steps required when the context $C$ is strongly informative. WSD is applicable to any standard diffusion or flow matching method, is orthogonal to and synergistic with other fast sampling techniques like efficient solvers, and is simple to implement. We test WSD in a variety of settings, and find that it substantially outperforms standard diffusion in the efficient sampling regime, generating realistic samples using only 4-6 function evaluations, and saturating performance with 10-12.
keywords: diffusion;efficient diffusion;conditional diffusion;inpainting;image inpainting;weather forecasting;probabilistic modelling;probabilistic forecasting
primary_area: generative models

roast.laymen_summary: Standard AI creates images by slowly refining random static, which takes forever; this paper proposes giving the AI a 'head start' by beginning with a blurry, educated guess instead of pure chaos. It's like skipping the first half of a 'How to Draw' tutorial and starting exactly where the circles already look like a face.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is paper-thin; researchers have been using 'informed priors' and 'coarse-to-fine' pipelines since the early days of GANs.
By starting with a deterministic guess, you risk 'mode collapse,' essentially trading the AI's creative soul for a slightly faster render of a generic-looking cat.
The authors conveniently ignore the overhead of training and running the 'warm-start' model, making the speed gains feel more like an accounting trick than a mathematical breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'PhD Kitchen,' where today we're making a five-hour slow-roasted brisket in just six minutes! Normally, diffusion is like roasting meat from raw—it takes time to get that texture right. Our 'Warm Start' method suggests you first zap the meat in a 700-watt microwave until it's a lukewarm, grey slab of 'informed prior,' then hit it with a blowtorch for thirty seconds to make it look 'generative.' Sure, it’s faster, but you're just masking a mediocre base with high-end seasoning, and any critic worth their salt can taste the shortcut. It’s not 'accelerated gourmet'; it’s just fancy leftovers.

title: REDEREF: RECURSIVE DELEGATION AND REFLECTION FOR MULTI-TURN LLM AGENT COLLABORATION WITH DYNAMIC CAPABILITY DISCOVERY
abstract:
Large language models (LLMs) achieve strong single-turn performance, yet real-world deployments demand multi-turn, multi-agent coordination with dynamic routing, reliable credit assignment, and long-horizon memory. We introduce , a lightweight, training-free framework that wraps arbitrary LLM agents with four synergistic components: (i) online Bayesian delegation (Thompson sampling) for
dynamic routing; (ii) calibrated self-reflection via an LLM judge to drive credit assignment and recursive re-routing; (iii) text-appropriate aggregation using selection with evidence checks; and (iv) memory-aware belief updates for long-term adaptation. Across domain-diverse, split-knowledge tasks, attains higher success rates than static non-collaborative baselines, with ablations indicating that the recursive re-routing loop contributes the majority of gains on initially failed tasks while online Bayesian updates improve routing efficiency. These results suggest that an interpretable, probabilistic wrapper can substantially enhance multi-agent LLM coordination—enabling dynamic task routing, emergent specialization, and long-term adaptability with minimal overhead.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs
author: Ankit Shah;Mohammad Parsa Hosseini;Saiyra Qureshi;Alex Huang;Connie Miao;Wei Wei
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of array: line 6 column 152 (char 807)

title: BrainPro: Towards Large-scale Brain State-aware EEG Representation Learning
abstract:
Electroencephalography (EEG) reflects underlying brain states, whose activities are distributed across brain regions and manifest as spatial patterns on the scalp. Learning these spatially structured, state-related patterns requires consistent spatial representations across datasets. However, existing EEG foundation models are typically based on self-attention, which does not preserve location-specific information and struggles to align signals recorded with different channel configurations. Moreover, brain states contain both shared and state-specific regional activity, suggesting that learning neurophysiologically plausible, state-aware representations can complement the shared representations targeted by current models and improve downstream decoding.
To address these limitations, we propose BrainPro, a large EEG model that combines a retrieval-based spatial learning mechanism for cross-layout spatial alignment with a brain state-decoupling module that learns both shared and state-specific representations through parallel encoders and region-aware reconstruction. Pre-trained on a large EEG corpus, BrainPro achieves state-of-the-art performance across nine public BCI datasets spanning emotion, motor, speech, stress, mental disease, and attention tasks. Analyses of spatial filters, channel-drop robustness, and encoder contributions further validate the effectiveness of its spatial alignment and state-aware pathways. These results show that BrainPro achieves improved interpretability of learned spatial patterns and produces representations that benefit diverse EEG decoding tasks.
primary_area: applications to neuroscience & cognitive science

roast.laymen_summary: We built a 'Large EEG Model' that tries to read your mind by actually caring where the sensors are placed on your head instead of treating your brainwaves like a random pile of data. It uses a dual-processing system to separate your general 'vibe' from specific thoughts, but the academic gatekeepers weren't convinced the extra complexity was worth the effort.
why_bad: Architectural Overkill: The 'state-decoupling' module is basically a Rube Goldberg machine—adding massive complexity to solve a 'shared vs. specific' signal problem that simpler models handle just fine with a fraction of the compute.

The 'Retrieval' Crutch: Using a retrieval-based mechanism for spatial alignment is essentially a glorified lookup table; it works in a lab but likely chokes the moment a sensor shifts half an inch in the real world.

Marginal Gains: Despite the 'State-of-the-Art' claims, the performance improvements across the nine datasets are thin enough to be mistaken for statistical noise, making the 'Large' in BrainPro feel more like 'Bloated'.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're pitching a startup for a 'Smart Home' that doesn't just listen for your voice commands, but triangulates your exact coordinates in the kitchen to 'decouple' whether you’re asking for coffee because you’re tired or just because you’re standing near the mug. You’ve built a multi-million dollar 'spatial retrieval' system to determine that when you’re in the bedroom, you’re likely sleeping. It’s peak Silicon Valley: solving a problem everyone else ignored because a $10 motion sensor does the same job. You're trying to sell a Ferrari engine to power a toaster—sure, it's impressive, but we're still just trying to get some bread brown, Steve.

title: Unraveling Syntax: How Language Models Learn Context-Free Grammars
abstract: We introduce a new approach to understanding how language models acquire syntax. While large models achieve impressive results, little is known about their learning dynamics. Our approach starts with the observation that most domains of interest -- such as natural language syntax, coding languages, arithmetic problems -- are captured by context-free grammars (CFGs). In this work we initiate the study of how language modeling for CFGs behaves with respect to the substructure of CFGs, namely the notion of a ``subgrammar". We define subgrammars, and prove a suite of fundamental results showing that the loss of language modeling obeys recurrences with respect to subgrammars. We show empirically that small transformers learn subgrammars in parallel, unlike children-- who first master simple substructures before progressing to more complex constructions. We further explore whether curriculum learning using an inductive bias, by pretraining on a subgrammar, can improve performance, and use alignment analysis to show definitively that such pre-training results in internal representations that are more aligned with the grammar's substructure. Finally we demonstrate that models struggle with deeper recursive structures (a limitation even of large language models), revealing fundamental challenges in how neural networks represent hierarchical syntax.
keywords: language modeling;transformers;context-free grammars (CFG);probabilistic context-free grammars (PCFG);recursion;formal language theory
primary_area: neurosymbolic & hybrid AI systems (physics-informed, logic & formal reasoning, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to figure out if AI learns grammar step-by-step like a toddler, but it turns out the models just try to swallow the entire rulebook at once and still choke on complex sentences. Even when we tried to give them a 'head start' with simpler rules, they couldn't handle deep, nested logic, proving your chatbot is basically just a very fast parrot that hates math.
why_bad: The 'subgrammar' definition is mathematically elegant but practically useless, as it fails to map onto how messy, real-world human language actually functions.

The finding that Transformers learn in parallel rather than sequentially isn't a 'discovery'—it is the literal definition of how backpropagation and attention mechanisms have worked for the last five years.
The 'improved internal alignment' from curriculum learning is a classic academic participation trophy; the model's internal vibes improved, but its actual performance on hard tasks remained mediocre.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' Our chef tonight tried to make a 10-layer French omelet. Instead of mastering the art of cracking an egg first, they threw the eggs, the cheese, the pan, and the waiter into a high-speed blender and hoped an omelet would 'emerge' from the chaos. When that failed, they tried 'Curriculum Cooking'—which just meant staring at a picture of a hen for three hours before turning on the stove. The result? As soon as the recipe required one ingredient to be tucked inside another (you know, basic recursion), the chef had a total meltdown and served a plate of raw ego. It’s not 'hierarchical complexity'; it’s just a soggy mess that can't handle a simple 'if-then' garnish.

title: Demystifying Latent Forgetting in Federated Learning
abstract:
Federated Learning (FL) enables collaborative model training across decentralized, isolated clients in a privacy-preserving manner, but at the cost of limited control over the data and the training procedure. One of the key challenges in FL is the spatial data heterogeneity, which is due to the stratified nature of the underlying data distributions between clients. In addition, FL systems also undergo periods of time in which certain features disappear from the training data pool, resulting in the less studied but critical problem of temporal-spatial data heterogeneity. Such non-uniformity in training data across time introduces a new feature-level latent forgetting that is fundamentally different from the well-studied task-level catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. 
This latent forgetting, if not detected and mitigated timely, can result in poor model performance, especially for certain learning features.
The privacy requirements and temporal-spatial data heterogeneity of FL make the detection and mitigation of latent forgetting challenging.
In this paper, we analyze latent forgetting and propose FedMemo, a privacy-preserving FL framework to control its impact. FedMemo  employs an automated detection mechanism to detect latent forgetting in real time with preserved privacy. FedMemo further introduces a proxy-based 2-step aggregation approach to mitigate the impact of latent forgetting. We evaluate FedMemo in a diverse set of vision and language classification tasks in various FL settings, and show that it outperforms state-of-the-art methods by up to $20.06\%$
primary_area: other topics in machine learning (i.e., none of the above)

roast.laymen_summary: Federated Learning lets AI learn from your phone without seeing your private data, but the model tends to 'forget' specific features if users stop talking about them for a few days. This paper tries to give the AI a memory boost so it doesn't lose its mind just because a trend went out of style in Ohio.
why_bad: The distinction between 'latent forgetting' and standard 'catastrophic forgetting' is mostly semantic window dressing to justify a new acronym.
The 'proxy-based' mitigation strategy is a privacy nightmare in disguise, essentially asking the server to guess what's in the box without looking, which usually fails or leaks data.
Claiming a 20.06% performance jump is the academic equivalent of saying your car gets 500 miles per gallon—it only happens if you're driving off a cliff with the engine off.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Amnesiac Informant
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the prosecution presents 'The Model,' a witness who claims to know everything about the streets. But the moment a local snitch goes on vacation for a week, the witness suddenly can't remember what a crime looks like. The defense introduces 'FedMemo,' a series of 'proxy' sticky notes designed to jog the witness's memory. The Prosecution (Reviewer 2) objects, noting that these notes are based on hearsay, the witness is clearly hallucinating their '20% better' memory, and the 'automated detection mechanism' is just the witness shouting 'I forgot!' whenever they get confused. Sustained; the paper is held in contempt of scientific rigor.

title: Temporal-Oriented Recipe for Transferring Large Vision-Language Model to Video Understanding
abstract: Recent years have witnessed outstanding advances of large vision-language models (LVLMs). In order to tackle video understanding, most of them depend upon their implicit temporal understanding capacity. As such, they have not deciphered important components that contribute to temporal understanding ability, which might limit the potential of these LVLMs for video understanding. In this work, we conduct a thorough empirical study to demystify crucial components that influence the temporal understanding of LVLMs. Our empirical study reveals that significant impacts are centered around the intermediate interface between the visual encoder and the large language model. Building on these insights, we propose a temporal-oriented recipe that encompasses temporal-oriented training schemes and an upscaled interface. Our final model developed using our recipe significantly enhances previous LVLMs on standard video understanding tasks.
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI models treat a video like a fast-moving slideshow rather than a story because the 'connector' between their eyes and their brain is too small. This paper tries to fix that by building a bigger bridge and giving the AI a better study guide for watching clips.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially just making the middleman bigger, which reviewers often dismiss as 'engineering' rather than a scientific breakthrough.
The 'temporal recipe' lacks a truly unique ingredient, feeling more like a collection of existing best practices repackaged with a fancy name.
It fails to prove the model actually understands time; it might just be getting better at guessing what happens next by seeing more frames at once.
roast.analogy_description: Folks, we’re down in the fourth quarter and the coaching staff has a 'revolutionary' plan: they’ve replaced the waterboy's tiny cups with a literal fire hose! They’re claiming this is a 'Temporal-Oriented Hydration Strategy.' Sure, the players are getting more water to their faces at once, but they still don't know the playbook! You can’t just upscale the plumbing between the cooler and the bench and call it a tactical masterclass. The refs—those pesky peer reviewers—are throwing a yellow flag for 'Incremental Gains' because, at the end of the day, a bigger pipe doesn't mean a smarter game plan!

title: Towards a Foundation Model Approach for Causal Graph Learning
abstract: Due to its human-interpretability and invariance properties, Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG) has been a foundational tool across various areas of AI research. %, leading to significant advancements. However, DAG learning remains highly challenging, due to its super-exponential growth in computational cost and identifiability issues, particularly in small-sample regimes. To address these two challenges, we leverage the recent success of transformers and develop a foundation model approach for discovering multiple DAGs across tasks. In particular, we propose Attention-DAG (ADAG), a novel attention-mechanism-based architecture for learning multiple linear Structural Equation Models (SEMs). ADAG learns the mapping from observed data to both graph structure and parameters via a nonlinear attention-based kernel, enabling efficient multi-task generalization of the underlying linear SEMs. By formulating the learning process across multiple domains as a continuous optimization problem, the pre-trained ADAG model captures the common structural properties as a shared low-dimensional prior, thereby reducing the ill-posedness of downstream DAG tasks in small-sample regimes. We evaluate our proposed approach on benchmark synthetic datasets and find that ADAG achieves substantial improvements in both DAG learning accuracy and zero-shot inference efficiency. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first practical approach for pre-training a foundation model for unsupervised DAG learning, representing a step toward more efficient and generalizable down-stream applications in causal discovery.
keywords: Causal Discovery; Inverse Problems; Attention Mechanism
primary_area: causal reasoning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a 'master brain' AI that can instantly guess how different variables cause one another by remembering patterns from previous datasets. Instead of solving each cause-and-effect puzzle from scratch, they hoped a pre-trained Transformer could look at new data and just 'vibe' its way to a correct flowchart.
why_bad: The 'Foundation Model' branding is massive overkill for what is essentially just a fancy way to solve basic linear equations that researchers have handled with simple stats since the 90s.
Their 'zero-shot' claims are shaky because if the new data doesn't perfectly mimic the synthetic training data, the model hallucinates causal links that don't actually exist.

The paper ignores the 'identifiability' elephant in the room: no amount of clever attention mechanisms can magically tell if A causes B or B causes A without the specific intervention data they conveniently left out.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Iron Chef: Over-Engineering.' Our contestants decided that actually cooking an egg to order is too slow, so they pre-fried ten thousand eggs in a factory and froze them into a 'Foundation Omelet.' Their pitch is that whenever a customer walks in, they can just microwave a pre-set shape instead of learning how to use a pan. But here is the catch, Chef: every customer has different allergies and tastes. When the judges asked for a poached egg, the model served a lukewarm, rubbery 'Attention-based' disc and claimed it was 'zero-shot' fine dining. You can't pre-calculate the heat of a kitchen you haven't stepped into yet, and you certainly can't call a microwave a 'Revolutionary Culinary Framework.'

title: A Variational approach to Physics Informed Neural Network for Stochastic Partial Differential Equations
abstract: Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) refer to a deep learning approach to represent the spatial and temporal characteristics of a distributed physical phenomenon, such as thermal fields, using Neural Networks (NNs). The loss function used to train PINNs relies on a term that penalises any violation of the Partial Differential Equation (PDE) that governs the distributed phenomena, in addition to a least-squares-error penalisation of any available observations in the relevant domain. PINNs have been shown to be successful in approximating the solutions to PDEs and have proven effective even in the low data regime. A critical shortcoming is the lack of a systematic treatment of the uncertainty of the approximation. State-of-the-art approaches rely on Bayesian NNs, though these are computationally heavy, both during training and at inference, nor does the associated training procedure derive from first principles. To remedy these limitations, we propose Variational Inference PINNs (VI-PINNs). Our approach derives from first principles: the uncertainty intrinsic to the distributed phenomena is explained by adopting Stochastic PDEs, while relying on standard measurement uncertainty to explain the observational uncertainty. This leads to the formulation of a posterior probability for the distributed phenomenon. Drawing parallels with Bayesian inference in finite spaces and relying on VI techniques to circumvent the otherwise intractable posterior. We derive a training objective that allows us to train two NNs, representing the mean and covariance of the approximation, respectively. The solution may be interpreted as a Bayesian belief about the true distributed phenomenon. Importantly, in the limit, the original PINN framework is recovered. We compare our approach with Bayesian PINNs (B-PINNs). Our results suggest that VI-PINNs are easier to implement, have a lower training time, and yields results that better align with reality, especially when extrapolating outside the measurement range.
keywords: Neural Network;Variational Inference;Steady State Heat Equation;Physics-Informed Neural Network;Partial Differential Equations;Gaussian Process
primary_area: probabilistic methods (Bayesian methods, variational inference, sampling, UQ, etc.)
author: Robbie Slos;Tom Lefebvre;Jolan Wauters;Guillaume Crevecoeur

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach a physics-AI how to admit it’s guessing instead of just confidently making things up. By swapping heavy Bayesian math for a 'good enough' shortcut, we made a system that predicts physical phenomena while simultaneously calculating its own anxiety levels.
why_bad: The 'first principles' derivation is mostly a rebranding of standard Variational Inference, offering more of a marketing spin than a mathematical breakthrough.
The authors compared their speed to Bayesian PINNs, which is like bragging about outrunning a snail—it’s technically true, but ignores much faster modern alternatives.
The assumption that all complex physical uncertainty follows a neat Gaussian bell curve is a massive oversimplification that likely collapses the moment the physics gets actually messy.
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where neural networks are too cocky to admit they're wrong... one algorithm dares to say 'Maybe?' Meet the VI-PINN: it doesn't just predict the weather; it tells you exactly how much it hates its own forecast. It's faster than the old guard, lazier than a Sunday morning, and uses a 'shortcut' that basically involves drawing a circle and hoping for the best. Coming soon to a rejected folder near you: A story of high-stakes math, low-resolution certainty, and the quest to turn 'I think so' into a PhD.

title: Core Advantage Decomposition for Policy Gradients in Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning
abstract: This work focuses on the credit assignment problem in cooperative multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL). Sharing the global advantage among agents often leads to insufficient policy optimization, as it fails to capture the coalitional contributions of different agents. Existing methods mainly assign credits based on individual counterfactual contributions, while overlooking the influence of coalitional interactions. In this work, we revisit the policy update process from a coalitional perspective and propose an advantage decomposition method guided by the cooperative game-theoretic core solution. By evaluating marginal contributions of all possible coalitions, our method ensures that strategically valuable coalitions receive stronger incentives during policy gradient updates. To reduce computational overhead, we employ random coalition sampling to approximate the core solution efficiently. Experiments on matrix games, differential games, and multi-agent collaboration benchmarks demonstrate that our method outperforms baselines. These findings highlight the importance of coalition-level credit assignment and cooperative games for advancing multi-agent learning.
keywords: Deep Learning; Reinforcement Learning; Multi-Agent Systems; Game Theory
primary_area: reinforcement learning

roast.laymen_summary: The paper tries to solve the 'group project problem' in AI, where one person does all the work and everyone else takes the credit. It uses complex game theory to calculate exactly which sub-groups of agents were actually useful, rather than just guessing based on individual effort.
why_bad: The 'random sampling' shortcut is a mathematical house of cards; if your sample size is too small, your 'fair' credit assignment is basically a lottery.
Evaluating every possible coalition is computationally suicidal, making the algorithm useless for any group larger than a small indie band.
The experimental results are suspiciously confined to 'matrix games'—the AI equivalent of bragging that your self-driving car can navigate a straight line in an empty parking lot.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Indiscriminate Heist
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents a bank robbery involving ten suspects. The authors—the defense—claim they can pinpoint exactly who deserves the loot by analyzing every possible sub-combination of the gang. They argue that if the safecracker and the getaway driver had worked alone, they’d be more efficient than if the guy holding the ladder was involved. However, when asked for proof, the defense admits they didn't actually watch the whole security tape. Instead, they 'randomly sampled' three seconds of footage and guessed the rest. Your Honor, this isn't a breakthrough in forensic justice; it's a statistical hallucination that falls apart the moment you add an eleventh accomplice to the lineup. I move to strike this entire testimony from the record.

title: MapQA: A Map-Question-Answering Benchmark for Visual Language Model Reasoning
abstract: Maps are central to how humans make sense of the world, from navigation and environmental monitoring to military planning and historical interpretation. Yet despite rapid progress in large multimodal models (LMMs), these systems continue to struggle with interpreting maps--an essential skill for visual reasoning that goes beyond pattern recognition and text extraction. To close this gap, we introduce MapQA, the first large-scale benchmark specifically designed to evaluate LMMs on map understanding. MapQA contains over 4,200 carefully curated, open-ended question–answer pairs spanning diverse map types, each constructed to require reasoning directly from the map rather than relying on memorized world knowledge. Benchmark questions are generated through a scalable human-in-the-loop process to ensure quality, and evaluated using an LLM-as-a-judge protocol aligned with human judgments. Our experiments show that while humans answer over 91% of questions correctly, state-of-the-art proprietary models achieve barely half that performance, with open-source models typically below 30%. These findings highlight a substantial gap between human and machine map understanding, underscoring the need for benchmarks like MapQA to guide future progress in multimodal reasoning.
keywords: Benchmarks;Datasets;Vision Language Model Benchmarks;Multimodal Model Benchmarks
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers created a 4,200-question test to see if AI can read a map, only to discover that top-tier models have the navigational skills of a confused pigeon. While humans can easily find their way around, even the 'smartest' AI systems are failing to interpret basic visual data that isn't already written in their training manuals.
why_bad: The 'LLM-as-a-judge' evaluation is a circular mess—using a text-based model to grade visual reasoning is like asking a blind man to critique a color palette.
The dataset generation is overly synthetic and templated, meaning the AI is likely just guessing based on sentence structure rather than actually 'looking' at the map.
The paper points out that AI is bad at maps—a known fact—without providing a single architectural tweak to actually fix the problem.
roast.analogy_description: Alright folks, we’re down in the fourth quarter at the Spatial Reasoning Bowl. Team Silicon is at the line of scrimmage with a $100 billion roster, but—oh no!—the quarterback has the map upside down. He’s looking at a river and calling it a touchdown. He’s trying to throw a deep pass to a mountain range because he thinks the contour lines are wide receivers. The referees, who are also AI, are just nodding along because they can’t see the field either. It’s not just a fumble; it’s a total existential crisis in a coordinate grid. The crowd is booing, and the coach is just standing there pointing at a 'Help' sign. Back to you, Jim.

title: Leveraging NLLB for Low-Resource Bidirectional Amharic – Afan Oromo Machine Translation
abstract:
We present a bidirectional machine translation system for Amharic and Afan Oromo, two low-resource Ethiopian languages critical for cultural and linguistic accessibility. Leveraging pre-trained transformer models, we curated a high-quality parallel corpus of 667,021 human-edited sentence pairs, preprocessed through text normalization, non-target language filtering and dividing the data into training, validation, and test sets in a stratified way.. Using the Hugging Face Transformers library, we fine-tuned a sequence-to-sequence transformer architecture, optimized for the linguistic nuances of Ethio-Semitic and Cushitic languages, with tokenized input and dynamic padding for efficient batch processing.
Our model significantly outperforms baselines, including Google Translate and NLLB models (600M, 1.3B, 3.3B parameters), which represent industry and research state-of-the-art for low-resource translation. For Amharic-to-Afan Oromo, it achieves a BLEU score of 42.19, surpassing Google Translate’s 9.6. For Afan Oromo-to-Amharic, it scores 42.82, exceeding NLLB-3.3B’s 5.72. Additional metrics (CHRF++, BERTScore) and low loss values confirm its robustness. These results highlight the efficacy of tailored fine-tuning for low-resource language pairs, advancing cross-lingual communication and digital accessibility in multilingual societies.
keywords: Neural Machine Translation (NMT);No Language Left Behind (NLLB);Natural Language Processing (NLP);Multilingual NLP
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning
author: Ashenafi Mulat Shferw;Rosa Tsegaye Aga

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers took Meta's massive 'No Language Left Behind' AI and gave it a specialized crash course in translating two Ethiopian languages, Amharic and Afan Oromo. While they claim their model wipes the floor with Google Translate, the suspiciously high scores suggest they might have accidentally let the AI see the exam questions before the test.
why_bad: The massive leap in BLEU scores (from 5 to 42) is a massive red flag for data leakage, suggesting the model essentially memorized the test set instead of learning to translate.
Methodological novelty is near-zero; simply fine-tuning an existing NLLB model on a private dataset is a standard industry pipeline, not a publishable scientific contribution.

The evaluation fails to account for the complex morphology of Ethio-Semitic languages, meaning the high scores might just be the result of matching repetitive character patterns rather than actual semantic meaning.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Pre-Signed Confession
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the prosecution finds it 'highly convenient' that the defendant's AI passed its linguistics exam with a 98% while the world’s leading scholars barely managed a 12%. This isn't a breakthrough; it’s a heist. We move to strike this evidence from the record because the researchers essentially handed the witness—the model—a script of the entire trial beforehand. You can't claim you've taught a machine the nuances of Cushitic grammar when it's clearly just reading the answers off the back of its hand. The defense rests on a pile of 'curated data' that smells suspiciously like the test set. Mistrial!
roast.scientific_accuracy_note: While the abstract claims 'tailored fine-tuning,' the lack of architectural innovation or rigorous cross-domain testing makes the results statistically suspect in the context of current LLM benchmarks.

title: GenFlow: Constrained Long-Form Text Generation via Adaptive Workflow Optimization
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) exhibit strong abilities in generating coherent human-like text, yet producing long-form content that satisfies complex constraints remains challenging. Existing approaches either extend generation length through large curated datasets, as in LongWriter, or structure outputs via cognitive-inspired hierarchical planning, as in CogWriter, but often struggle to balance coherence, semantic fidelity, and explicit requirements. In this work, we propose GenFlow, an adaptive framework for constrained long-form text generation. It decomposes writing objectives into constraint-aware sub-plans, uses adaptive decision-making and reward filtering to retain high-quality plans, and optimizes both local and global generation. By embedding constraints directly into the workflow, GenFlow ensures consistency while adapting to evolving requirements. Experimental results on the Qwen2.5 series demonstrate that GenFlow outperforms GPT-4o-mini and CogWriter baselines in constraint satisfaction, coherence, and overall quality.
primary_area: generative models
author: Yifan Zhu;Guanting Chen;Haoran Luo

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built an over-engineered to-do list for AI to help it write long articles without forgetting the instructions halfway through. It breaks the writing process into tiny, micromanaged steps to stop the model from wandering off-script.
why_bad: The 'adaptive framework' is essentially a Rube Goldberg machine of existing techniques that adds more complexity than actual innovation.
Beating GPT-4o-mini is a low-bar participation trophy; the authors avoided the heavy hitters to make their metrics look respectable.
The computational cost of 'optimizing local and global generation' makes the system too slow and expensive for any practical use case outside of a GPU-rich lab.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Micromanaged Stenographer
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents GenFlow: a system that treats writing a simple essay like a high-stakes bureaucratic hearing. Imagine a witness who isn't allowed to speak until a committee of twelve lawyers vets every syllable against a 500-page manual of 'constraints.' Your Honor, the defense claims this 'adaptive workflow' ensures coherence, but in reality, it just creates a witness so paralyzed by sub-plans and 'reward filtering' that the trial will last three decades. We aren't looking at a breakthrough in testimony; we’re looking at a filibuster disguised as a methodology. I move to strike this entire framework from the record for being redundantly tedious.

title: HF-Font: Few-Shot Font Generation via High-Frequency Style Enhancement and Fusion
abstract: Few-shot font generation aims to create new fonts with a limited number of glyph references. It can be used to greatly reduce the workload of manual font design. However, although existing methods have achieved satisfactory performance, they still struggle to capture delicate glyph details, thus resulting in stroke errors, artifacts, and blurriness. To address these problems, we propose HF-Font, a novel framework that generates fonts with higher structural fidelity. Specifically, inspired by the observation that high-frequency information of character images often contains distinct style patterns (e.g., glyph topology and stroke variation), we develop a novel style-enhanced module to improve the style extraction by incorporating high-frequency features from reference images using a high-pass filter. Then, for guiding the generation process, we design a Style-Content Fusion Module (SCFM), which integrates the style features with a component-wise codebook that encodes content semantics. Moreover, we also introduce a style contrastive loss to better transfer high-frequency features. Extensive experiments show that our HF-Font outperforms the state-of-the-art methods in both qualitative and quantitative evaluations, demonstrating its effectiveness across diverse font styles and characters. Our source code will be released soon.
keywords: Style Transfer; Image-to-Image Translation; Font Generation; Deep Generative Model
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build an AI that can clone a person's entire handwriting style after seeing only a handful of letters. They essentially gave the AI a 'sharpen' filter to help it focus on the fine edges and squiggles, hoping to stop the output from looking like a smudged charcoal drawing.
why_bad: The 'High-Frequency Style Enhancement' is essentially just a basic high-pass filter from 1970s signal processing rebranded as a deep learning breakthrough.
The 'Style-Content Fusion Module' is a fancy name for mashing two data vectors together and hoping the neural network figures out the rest by magic.
While the math looks shiny, the AI still hallucinates 'digital dandruff'—artifacts and broken strokes—whenever it encounters a character more complex than a basic 'L'.
roast.analogy_name: The Sharpened Blur: A Font Odyssey
roast.analogy_description: In a world where typography is king, one algorithm dared to dream. It didn't just want to see the letter 'A'—it wanted to see the *soul* of the 'A'. By cranking the 'sharpness' dial to eleven, it promised the world a revolution in forgery. But focus isn't everything. Coming this summer: a high-stakes thriller where a 'Style-Enhanced' hero tries to save the alphabet, only to realize that sharpening a smudge just gives you a very, very pointy smudge. HF-Font: It’s got the frequency, but it’s lost the signal.

title: Bridging the Realism Gap: Generating Formally Verified Vulnerability Datasets from Real-World Code
abstract: The advancement of machine learning for vulnerability detection is critically hampered by the absence of datasets that are simultaneously large-scale, accurately labeled, and realistic. Existing benchmarks impose a trade-off: large, real-world datasets suffer from noisy labels and contamination, while manually curated datasets are too small to train robust models. Synthetic datasets, although formally verifiable, typically lack the structural complexity of production code, resulting in a significant "realism gap". To overcome these limitations, we introduce ApproxVul, a framework and dataset that unites real-world code realism with the mathematical certainty of formal verification. Our framework leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to systematically mutate code snippets from real-world projects, introducing a diverse range of subtle and complex vulnerabilities. Each resulting program is then formally verified to establish ground-truth labels, eliminating label noise. This process yields ApproxVul, a new dataset of over 104,000 compilable and verifiable programs, featuring minimally different vulnerable/safe pairs derived from real-world code. Through comprehensive evaluation, we demonstrate that ApproxVul achieves better cross-dataset generalization than purely synthetic training datasets and slightly outperforms noisy real-world training data. While fine-tuning alone remains insufficient for project-level generalization, ApproxVul's inter-procedural and verifiable structure makes it a crucial stepping stone toward more advanced vulnerability detection approaches.
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks
author: Ahmed Lekssays;Hamza Mouhcine;Issa Khalil

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to train a bug-hunting AI by taking real code, having a second AI intentionally break it, and using math to prove exactly where the holes are. It’s essentially a massive 'Spot the Difference' book for software vulnerabilities, but it still doesn't stop the AI from tripping over its own feet in the real world.
why_bad: The 'formal verification' claim is likely a stretch; verifying 104,000 snippets of 'real-world' code usually requires stripping away so much complexity that the code becomes a toy again.
Using LLMs to inject bugs creates 'AI-flavored' errors that don't accurately mimic the nuanced, logic-defying mistakes made by sleep-deprived human developers.
A 'slight' performance increase over noisy datasets is academic code for 'it barely works,' failing to justify the massive computational overhead of the generation pipeline.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where software is a literal minefield, one team decided to build their own. They took blueprints for a Ferrari, hired a toddler with a sledgehammer to 'redecorate' the engine, and then used a team of physicists to calculate the exact trajectory of the inevitable explosion. It’s the ultimate training simulation for bomb squads... who only ever have to defuse Legos. Coming this fall: The Realism Gap. They proved the math, they built the data, and the AI still can’t find a bug in an open-source library without a map and a flashlight.

title: Softly Constrained Denoisers for Diffusion Models
abstract: Diffusion models struggle to produce samples that respect constraints, a common requirement in scientific applications. Existing approaches have introduced regularization terms in the loss or guidance methods during sampling to enforce such constraints, but they bias the generative model away from the true data distribution. This is a problem, especially when the constraint is misspecified, a common issue when formulating constraints on scientific data. In this paper, instead of changing the loss or the sampling loop, we integrate a guidance-inspired adjustment into the denoiser itself, giving it a soft inductive bias towards constraint-compliant samples. Through experiments, we show that these softly constrained denoisers exploit the constraint knowledge to produce compliant samples, while maintain enough flexibility to deviate from it when there is misspecification with observed data.
primary_area: generative models

roast.laymen_summary: Diffusion models are like creative toddlers who refuse to follow rules, so the authors tried to give them a 'gentle nudge' instead of a strict command. It’s an attempt to make AI respect scientific laws without throwing a tantrum when those laws are slightly wrong.
why_bad: The 'soft' constraint is effectively a 'polite suggestion' that the model ignores the moment things get complicated.
By trying to have it both ways, the model ends up being mediocre at following rules and mediocre at generating realistic data.
The math is essentially a rehash of existing guidance techniques but with a fancy new hat and less empirical backbone.
