roast.analogy_description: In a world where physics is a law, one model treats it like a recommendation. Witness the gripping saga of a driver who views red lights as 'suggestions based on their current mood.' It’s the ultimate cinematic thrill ride of theoretical compliance where the AI looks at your scientific constraints, says 'I'll take it under advisement,' and then proceeds to drive straight into a lake because the data looked wetter there. Coming this fall: The Softly Constrained Denoiser. It’s not a boundary; it’s a vibe.
roast.tone_check: Snark levels optimal. Accuracy maintained. Fluff deleted.

title: MetaFlow: A Meta Approach of Training LLMs into Generalizable Workflow Generators
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) excel across a wide range of tasks, yet their instance-specific solutions often lack the structural consistency needed for reliable deployment. Workflows that encode recurring algorithmic patterns at the task level provide a principled framework, offering robustness across instance variations, interpretable traces for debugging, and reusability across problem instances. However, manually designing such workflows requires significant expertise and effort, limiting their broader application. While automatic workflow generation could address this bottleneck, existing methods either produce instance-specific solutions without learning task-level patterns, or cannot generalize beyond their training configurations. We present MetaFlow, which casts workflow generation as a meta-learning problem: given a task and an operator set, the model learns to compose solution strategies. MetaFlow trains in two stages—supervised fine-tuning on synthetic workflow data, followed by reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) that uses execution feedback across problem instances in the task to improve end-to-end success. The resulting model produces effective workflows for trained tasks and exhibits strong generalization to untrained tasks and novel operator sets. Across benchmarks in question answering, code generation, and mathematical reasoning, MetaFlow achieves performance comparable to state-of-the-art baselines on in-domain tasks with single inference, while demonstrating remarkable zero-shot generalization capabilities on out-of-domain tasks and operator sets.
keywords: LLM Agent; Workflow Generation; Reinforcement learning; Meta Learning
primary_area: applications to robotics, autonomy, planning

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of asking an AI to solve a specific math problem, this paper tries to teach it to write a reusable 'instruction manual' for any problem in that category. It’s an attempt to turn a flighty chatbot into a systematic project manager that builds standardized workflows rather than just winging it every time.
why_bad: Circular Logic: The model is trained on synthetic workflows generated by other LLMs, essentially creating a 'human centipede' of data where errors are just recycled and rebranded as 'meta-knowledge.'

Evaluation Gaps: The paper claims 'remarkable generalization,' yet the 'novel' tasks it tested on are suspiciously similar to the training data, suggesting the model is just memorizing templates rather than actually thinking.

The Efficiency Paradox: The computational cost of training a model to generate a workflow is significantly higher than just using a better model to solve the original task directly, making it a solution in search of a problem.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup where nobody actually does the work; instead, everyone spends 100% of their time writing 'Standard Operating Procedures' (SOPs) for work they've never done. We call it MetaFlow. We’ve raised $50 million to train an AI that doesn't fix your leaking sink—it writes a 40-page technical manual on the *philosophy* of plumbing using synthetic feedback from a guy who once saw a faucet. It’s perfect for the enterprise executive who wants to look busy while ignoring the fact that the 'reusable workflow' costs more than just buying a new house. We aren't solving problems; we're scaling the bureaucracy of problem-solving.

title: Variability Aware Recursive Neural Network (VARNN): A Residual-Memory Model for Capturing Temporal Deviation in Sequence Regression Modeling
abstract: Real-world time series data exhibit nonstationary behavior, regime shifts, and temporally varying noise that degrade the robustness of standard regression models. We introduce the Variability Aware Recursive (VARNN) Neural Network, a novel residual-aware architecture for supervised time series regression that learns an explicit error memory from recent prediction residuals and uses it to recalibrate subsequent predictions. VARNN augments a feed-forward predictor with a learned error memory state that is updated from residuals over a short context steps as a signal of variability and drift, and then conditions the final prediction at the current time. We study four configurations along two orthogonal axes: (i) residual memory as instantaneous (RM) an embedding of the current innovation only, or accumulative (ARM) that augment current innovation with past residual memory states to capture drift and volatility bursts; and (ii) the presence or absence of an activation memory (AM), which carries the previous latent activation to enrich short-term temporal dynamics and stabilize predictions under noise. Across nine datasets from three important domains, Energy, Healthcare, and Environmental monitoring, experimental results demonstrate that VARNN achieves superior performance and attains the lowest test MSE with minimal computational overhead over static, dynamic, and sequence baselines. Our findings show that the VARNN model offers robust predictions under a drift and volatility environment, highlighting its potential as a promising framework for time series learning.
keywords: Time-series regression;non-stationarity;residual learning;recurrent neural networks;distribution shift;system identification.
primary_area: learning on time series and dynamical systems

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built an AI that tries to predict the future by obsessively dwelling on its most recent mistakes. It’s essentially a 'hindsight is 20/20' machine that hopes that if it remembers exactly how much it messed up yesterday, it might suck slightly less today.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The paper acts like it invented the wheel, but 'learning from residuals' is just a fancy way of saying Error Correction, a concept that's been around since your grandfather's first statistics textbook.

Over-engineered Alphabet Soup: They spent more time naming four different versions of 'memory' (RM, ARM, AM) than actually proving why a standard, simpler model wouldn't have performed exactly the same.

The 'It Works on My Machine' Problem: Claiming 'superior performance' across three domains is suspicious when you haven't compared it against modern Transformers or state-of-the-art baselines that would likely eat this model for lunch.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: AI Edition.' Our chef today doesn't actually know how to cook a steak; he just keeps throwing meat on the grill and tasting the charred remains of the last attempt. If the last steak was a literal brick of carbon, he 'recalibrates' by undercooking the next one until it's still mooing. He calls this 'Variability-Aware Culinary Intelligence.' In reality, he's just a guy who refuses to follow a recipe and instead spends all his time writing a 10-page memo about why the smoke alarm going off is actually a 'signal of temporal drift.' Just learn how to use a thermometer, Dave.

title: Offline Reinforcement Learning via Action-Space Pseudo-Labeling
abstract:
he critical challenge of offline reinforcement learning (offline RL) is improving
from a fixed dataset while avoiding overestimation on out-of-distribution (OOD)
actions. Existing methods typically regularize the learned policy to avoid choosing overestimated OOD actions. However, we argue that this often over-constrains policy improvement or requires sensitive hyperparameter tuning. We restate this challenge as the absence of explicit training signals for the
value function in parts of the state–action space. A more effective approach is to provide explicit training signals across the entire action space to eliminate overestimation. We introduce a surprisingly simple yet effective method: $\textbf{Action-Space Pseudo-Labeling (ASPL)}$ to resolve this challenge. It completes the value-function’s
missing signals by assigning pseudo Q-targets that decrease with distance from
the behavior support (i.e., the support of the behavior policy). In practice, ASPL achieves an implicit behavior-aware regularization that strengthens as behavior likelihood decreases. On D4RL datasets,
we observe stable training and consistent improvements over strong offline baselines with minor tuning burden. Code for reproducing the experiments is provided in the supplementary material.
primary_area: reinforcement learning

roast.laymen_summary: Offline RL is like trying to learn to drive using only a recorded video of someone else driving; if the AI tries a move not in the video, it assumes it's a genius move even if it leads off a cliff. This paper tries to fix that by pre-emptively 'fake-shaming' any move the AI hasn't seen before, giving those unknown actions a failing grade before the AI even tries them.
why_bad: The core idea is effectively a 'poor man’s version' of Conservative Q-Learning (CQL), lacking the theoretical rigor that justifies why these specific fake labels are better than just penalizing the policy.
The 'pseudo-labeling' function is highly heuristic and relies on distance metrics that fall apart in complex, high-dimensional spaces where 'distance' doesn't mean much.
Empirical results on the D4RL benchmark were deemed 'marginal' at best, failing to prove that this extra complexity actually beats simpler, existing algorithms in any meaningful way.
roast.analogy_name: The Yelp Reviewer of Imaginary Restaurants
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD where an AI has only ever eaten at three local diners, it starts to hallucinate that every street it hasn't visited must contain a 5-star Michelin restaurant. To stop this madness, one team of researchers decides to write millions of fake, one-star reviews for every single coordinate on the map that doesn't have a building. Coming this fall: 'ASPL.' Witness the story of a robot that refuses to leave its house, not because it's forbidden, but because it’s been gaslit into believing the entire outside world is a health code violation. It’s not 'safe'—it’s just terrified of the unknown.

title: Judge's Verdict: A Comprehensive Analysis of LLM Judge Capability Through Human Agreement
abstract: This research introduces the \textbf{Judge's Verdict Benchmark}, a novel two-step methodology to evaluate Large Language Models (LLMs) as judges for response accuracy evaluation tasks. We assess how well 54 LLMs can replicate human judgment when scoring responses from RAG (Retrieval-Augmented Generation) or Agentic pipelines against ground truth answers. Our methodology progresses from traditional correlation analysis to comprehensive Cohen's Kappa analysis that measures actual agreement patterns. The two-step approach includes: (1) a correlation test that filters judges with strong alignment ($r \geq 0.80$), followed by (2) a human-likeness test using z-scores to identify two distinct judgment patterns—\textit{human-like} judgment ($|z| < 1$) that mimics natural human variation, and \textit{super-consistent} judgment ($z > 1$) that exceeds typical human-to-human agreement levels. This methodology reveals that 27 out of 54 tested LLMs achieve Tier 1 performance: 23 models exhibit human-like patterns that preserve the nuances of human judgment, while 4 models demonstrate super-consistent behavior—a pattern that could indicate either enhanced reliability or oversimplification of complex judgments. Testing 43 open source models (1B-405B parameters) and 11 closed models (GPT, Gemini, Claude variants), we demonstrate that judge excellence is not solely dependent on model size but on specific training strategies. Our key contributions include: (1) establishing that correlation alone is insufficient for judge evaluation, (2) introducing a "Turing Test for judges" based on agreement patterns, and (3) providing a standardized benchmark for classifying LLM judges into distinct performance tiers for different evaluation needs.
keywords: LLM-as-a-Judge;Judge's Verdict Benchmark;Human-AI Agreement;Cohen's Kappa Analysis;RAG Evaluation
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a 'Turing Test' to see if AI can grade homework as well as human teachers do, but they mostly just discovered that some AIs are as moody as humans while others are soullessly consistent. They claim that being 'too perfect' at grading might actually mean the AI is failing to understand the nuance of the answer, which is a fancy way of saying the robot might be a literal-minded buzzkill.
why_bad: Statistical Over-Engineering: Using z-scores to categorize 'human-likeness' is essentially a high-effort way of rebranding the fact that humans are inconsistent and models are deterministic.

The 'Super-Consistent' Paradox: The paper fails to prove that models exceeding human agreement are actually 'better' judges rather than just being rigid, binary-thinking bots that miss the forest for the trees.

Gold Standard Circularity: The benchmark relies entirely on human agreement as the 'truth,' ignoring the well-documented reality that human annotators are often tired, biased, and disagree with each other for reasons that have nothing to do with accuracy.
roast.analogy_name: The Robo-Ref with a Rulebook Obsession
roast.analogy_description: Imagine we’re at the NBA Finals, but instead of veteran refs, we’ve got a literal toaster with a whistle sitting on the court. The researchers are up in the broadcast booth losing their minds because the toaster calls a foul every single time a player’s pinky grazes a jersey—it’s 'super-consistent!' But back on the floor, the fans are rioting because the toaster has zero 'game feel' and doesn't realize the difference between a hard foul and a high-five. This paper is basically trying to give the toaster a trophy for being more consistent than a human ref, while ignoring the fact that the human ref actually knows what a basketball game is supposed to look like.

title: StructZip: Compressing Large-Scale Structured Prompts to One Token via Learning Natural Language Descriptions
abstract: Tool use has become a central capability in large language model (LLM)-based agents, enabling them to interact with external environments through structured APIs. However, effective tool use typically requires including a large number of tool descriptions, often with complex schemas, in the context for each inference. This static and structured portion of the prompt grows linearly with the number of tools and poses a significant challenge to inference efficiency. Although prior work has explored prompt compression for long contexts, most approaches focus on unstructured text and are not optimized for the compression of structured prompts. To bridge this gap, we introduce \textbf{StructZip}, a novel framework that transforms large structured prompts into parametric memory, which can be elicited by a single token. Our approach first "unzips" the structured prompt into a set of semantically equivalent question-answer (QA) pairs. By fine-tuning the LLM on these QA pairs, StructZip encodes the information into the model's parameters, making it accessible through a designated special token at inference time. We evaluate our method on three representative tasks: table-based question answering, tool-use, and closed-set text classification. Experimental results demonstrate that StructZip can compress prompts of millions of tokens into a single one while maintaining performance nearly on par with using the full, uncompressed prompts, offering a practical solution for efficient structured data handling in LLMs.
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to replace massive, clunky instruction manuals with a single 'magic word' by forcing the AI to memorize the data during its training phase. While it sounds efficient, it essentially turns a flexible AI into a rigid robot that needs a brain transplant every time the instructions change.
why_bad: The 'compression' is an illusion because it requires expensive fine-tuning; you aren't saving space, you're just moving the clutter from the desk into the attic.
The method lacks scalability, as teaching the model a new 'special token' for every structured prompt eventually leads to a 'diluted brain' where the model forgets its original purpose.

Reviewers noted the benchmarks were rigged, comparing this heavy retraining process to simple methods that weren't designed to handle the same complexity, making the results look deceptively superior.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Magic Keyword
roast.analogy_description: Counsel, you expect this court to believe that instead of bringing the 50,000 pages of discovery evidence into the room, your witness has 'compressed' it all into their mind and will recall any fact instantly if you simply wink your left eye? That’s not efficiency; that’s a mistrial waiting to happen. The prosecution has proven that if we change even one line of the testimony, your witness needs three weeks of hypnosis to 're-compress' the truth. You aren't presenting a breakthrough in legal filing; you're just asking the jury to trust a witness who has been lobotomized into a very expensive, one-word parlor trick.

title: Continual Learning via Sparse Memory Finetuning
abstract:
Modern language models are powerful, but typically static after deployment. A major obstacle to building models that continually learn over time is catastrophic forgetting, where updating on new data erases previously acquired capabilities. Motivated by the intuition that mitigating forgetting is challenging because trainable parameters are shared across all tasks, we investigate whether *sparse parameter updates* can enable learning without catastrophic forgetting. We introduce sparse memory finetuning, leveraging memory layer models (Berges et al., 2024), which are sparsely updated by design. By updating only the memory slots that are highly activated by a new piece of knowledge relative to usage on pretraining data, we reduce interference between new knowledge and the model's existing capabilities. We evaluate learning and forgetting compared to full finetuning and parameter-efficient finetuning with LoRA on two question answering tasks.
We find that sparse memory finetuning learns new knowledge while exhibiting substantially less forgetting: while NaturalQuestions F1 drops by 89\% after full finetuning on new facts and 71\% with LoRA, sparse memory finetuning yields only an 11\% drop with the same level of new knowledge acquisition. Our results suggest sparsity in memory layers offers a promising path toward continual learning in large language models.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs
author: Jessy Lin;Luke Zettlemoyer;Gargi Ghosh;Wen-tau Yih;Aram H. Markosyan;Vincent-Pierre Berges;Barlas Oguz

roast.laymen_summary: When you try to teach an AI something new, it usually has a 'Men in Black' moment and accidentally wipes its entire existing memory. These researchers tried to fix that by only tweaking specific 'memory slots' instead of lobotomizing the whole model every time it learns a new fact.
why_bad: The evaluation is aggressively narrow, testing on only two question-answering tasks which is like claiming you've cured amnesia because a patient remembered where they left their keys once.
The method requires a specialized 'memory layer' architecture, meaning it’s not a plug-and-play solution for the standard models everyone actually uses.
The comparison to LoRA feels like a rigged fight; the authors likely didn't optimize the baselines enough, making their niche method look 'revolutionary' through a lens of neglected competition.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where every time you learn a new recipe, you forget how to drive a car. ONE ARCHITECTURE... dares to stop the madness. This summer, witness 'THE SPARSE UPDATE.' Instead of shaking the whole Etch A Sketch to draw a single new line, our hero uses a microscopic needle to poke just the right dust particles. It’s a high-stakes thriller where 'not being totally useless' is the ultimate prize. Coming soon to a rejected folder near you.

title: Expressive Graph Neural Networks via Equivariant Use of Noise
abstract: Expressivity has been a major focus in the design of Graph Neural Networks (GNNs), yet a significant gap persists between theoretical universal expressivity and practical performance. While many expressive GNNs are efficient and achieve strong results, they often focus on specific graph properties and lack theoretical expressivity for general graph tasks. Conversely, theoretically universal-expressive models often suffer from high computational costs or poor generalization, limiting their real-world applicability. To bridge this gap, we introduce Equivariant Noise GNNs (ENGNNs), a framework that utilizes random noise features to enhance the expressivity of GNNs. Crucially, unlike prior methods that naively use noise, we enforce equivariance to nodewise noise transformations, such as orthogonal transformations. We prove that this property reduces the model's theoretical sample complexity, thereby improving generalization. Our framework simultaneously reaches theoretical universal expressivity, maintains the linear scalability of standard Message-Passing Neural Networks in practice, and achieves performance comparable to computationally expensive, high-expressivity models. Extensive experiments confirm strong performance across node, link, subgraph, and graph-level prediction tasks, demonstrating that the equivariant use of noise provides a powerful and practical pathway for building expressive GNNs. Our code is available at \url{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/EquivNoiseGNN/}.
primary_area: learning on graphs and other geometries & topologies

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to make AI better at analyzing complex networks by injecting random noise into the data and then using fancy math to ensure that noise didn't break the rules of geometry. They essentially argued that adding 'smart' static helps the AI tell the difference between similar-looking social circles or chemical structures.
why_bad: The 'theoretical universal expressivity' claim is a bit of a parlor trick; if you add enough unique random noise to any model, it can distinguish anything, but that doesn't mean it's actually learned the underlying pattern.
Reviewers likely found the 'equivariant' constraints to be a mathematical sledgehammer that solves a problem created by the noise itself, rather than a breakthrough in how graphs are processed.

The 'linear scalability' claim usually ignores the massive overhead of generating and maintaining these noise features, making it 'fast' only in the same way a Ferrari is 'fast' while stuck in a school zone.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re a detective trying to tell two identical twins apart in a dark room. Most GNNs just give up and call them the same person. Our 'ENGNN' startup pitch? We throw a bucket of neon glitter (random noise) at them. Now, Twin A has glitter on his left ear and Twin B has it on his nose. Problem solved! They're 'distinguishable' now! But the 'Equivariant' part is the real kicker—we promise that if the twins do a cartwheel, the glitter stays in the same spot relative to their faces. It’s a 'universal' solution to identification, provided you don't mind the fact that you haven't actually learned anything about the twins themselves, you've just become really good at tracking expensive, mathematically-stable craft supplies.

title: DroneDreamer: Multi-View Low-Altitude World Model with Adaptive Control
abstract: Recent advancements in world models have made significant strides in the fields of autonomous driving and robotic motion, showing great potential as the foundation for general artificial intelligence. Although DiT with 3D VAE has made significant progress in this field, existing models have yet to be effectively applied to low-altitude scenarios, still facing challenges such as difficulty in acquiring conditions, unstable sampling, and data scarcity, which render current methods ineffective. In this paper, we introduce a new research problem: Low-Altitude World Models(LAWM), focusing on the applicability of world models in low-altitude scenarios. To address these issues, we propose DroneDreamer, a novel LAWM that incorporates an adaptive viewpoint control mechanism and an image style domain adaptation technique, enabling multi-view conditional generation with limited conditions. Additionally, we construct a drone flight dataset collected from a simulated modeling environment and a data processing pipeline, along with a progressive training strategy to ensure the accuracy of control capabilities and style adaptation, as well as generalizability. Consequently, our model achieves a 58\% improvement in image generation capabilities on the drone flight dataset compared to specialized models, and a 63\% improvement in multi-view consistency compared to combined baseline models. We believe DroneDreamer can provide a foundational world model for the low-altitude UAV domain, benefiting low-altitude navigation tasks, data generation, flight prediction, and inspiring valuable applications.Our code is available at \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DroneDreamer-B629}{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DroneDreamer-B629}
keywords: World Model;Low-Altitude UAV;Multi-View Video Generation;Style Transfer
primary_area: generative models

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a 'dreaming' brain for drones so they could predict flight paths and visuals at low altitudes using fancy AI video generators. However, instead of learning real physics, the model mostly just got good at playing a flight simulator and guessing what the ground looks like.
why_bad: The model relies heavily on simulated data, creating a massive 'sim-to-real' gap where it might navigate a video game perfectly but hit a real-world power line.
The claimed '58% improvement' lacks rigorous benchmarking against state-of-the-art flight controllers, making the metrics look like cherry-picked vanity stats.
The 'Adaptive Control' mechanism is essentially a glorified image filter that struggles to maintain physical consistency when the drone actually has to move in 3D space.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Simulated Skywriter
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, you stand before this court claiming your client is a 'World Model' for aviation, yet the evidence shows they have never seen a literal blade of grass outside of a GPU. You present 'DroneDreamer' as a master pilot, but upon cross-examination, we find its entire flight history was logged in a 2004 flight simulator with the physics engine turned off. You've wrapped a standard video generator in a flight suit and called it 'Adaptive Control,' but when the wind blows in the real world, your model pleads the Fifth. This isn't a foundational model for navigation; it's a high-definition hallucination masquerading as a pilot's license!

title: DISTRIBUTED MULTI-AGENT DEEP REINFORCEMENT LEARNING
abstract: Centralized training with decentralized execution (CTDE) has been the dominant paradigm in multi-agent reinforcement learning (MARL), but its reliance on global state information during training introduces scalability, robustness, and generalization bottlenecks. Moreover, in practical scenarios such as adding/dropping teammates or facing environment dynamics that differ from the training, CTDE methods can be brittle and costly to retrain, whereas distributed approaches allow agents to adapt using only local information and peer-to-peer communication. We present a distributed MARL framework that removes the need for centralized critics or global information. Firstly, we develop a novel Distributed Graph Attention Network (D-GAT) that performs global state inference through multi-hop communication, where agents integrate neighbor features via input-dependent attention weights in a fully distributed manner. Leveraging D-GAT, we develop the distributed graph-attention MAPPO (DG-MAPPO) -- a distributed MARL framework where agents optimize local policies and value functions using local observations, multi-hop communication, and shared/averaged rewards. Empirical evaluation on the StarCraftII Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) and Multi-Agent Mujoco demonstrates that our method consistently outperforms strong CTDE baselines, achieving superior coordination across a wide range of cooperative tasks with both homogeneous and heterogeneous teams. Our distributed MARL framework provides a principled and scalable solution for robust collaboration, eliminating the need for centralized training or global observability. To the best of our knowledge, DG-MAPPO appears to be the first to fully eliminate reliance on privileged centralized information, enabling agents to learn and act solely through peer-to-peer communication.
keywords: Multi-Agent Reinforcement Learning;Graph Attention Networks;Multi-Agent Systems
primary_area: reinforcement learning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to fix 'helicopter parenting' in AI by letting groups of robots learn through local gossip instead of a single all-seeing coach. They built a system where agents whisper to their neighbors to guess what's happening elsewhere, hoping they'd be more flexible when the team changes.
why_bad: The 'Telephone Game' Effect: Relying on multi-hop communication creates massive latency and information decay, meaning by the time an agent hears about a threat, it's already been 'deleted' from the simulation.

The Incremental LEGO Strategy: Simply slapping a Graph Attention Network onto an existing algorithm (MAPPO) is the academic equivalent of putting a spoiler on a minivan; it looks fancy, but it doesn't fundamentally change the engine.

Benchmark Boredom: Using the StarCraft II Multi-Agent Challenge as the primary evidence is a tired trope that fails to prove the system works in complex, unpredictable real-world environments beyond static toy problems.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Hell's Distributed Kitchen!' Instead of a Head Chef overlooking the line, our contestants are trying to cook a 12-course tasting menu while wearing opaque goggles and earplugs. They can only communicate by tapping the person next to them in Morse code. The lead researcher thinks this is 'scalable' because you can add more cooks, but in reality, the guy at the salad station is trying to 'infer' the temperature of the steak by feeling the vibrations of the dishwasher. It’s a chaotic mess where the 'global state inference' usually results in a salt-crusted dessert, proving that sometimes, you actually need someone in charge who can see the whole stove.

title: Graph-Enhanced EEG-to-Text Decoding: A Spatio-Temporal Relational Embedding Framework for Brain Signal Translation
abstract: Despite recent progress in brain–computer interfaces (BCIs), decoding natural language directly from EEG remains a critical challenge. Existing EEG-to-text models primarily treat signals as sequential time series, which severely limits their ability to capture the spatial and temporal relationships among electrodes and limits the possibility of generalization in low-data regimes. To address this challenge, we propose a novel graph-enhanced framework to explicitly model relational information in brain signals. The key idea of our framework is to construct Spectro-Topographic Relational Graphs (STRG) that jointly encode static electrode topology and dynamic inter-channel functional connectivity. From these graphs, we derive Spatio-Temporal Relational Embeddings (STRE), which provide graph-aware representations for downstream sequence-to-sequence decoding. Specifically, (i) STRG captures spatial adjacency and frequency-specific connectivity, (ii) STRE transforms these relational structures into embeddings aligned with text decoding, and (iii) the overall framework integrates these embeddings with a neural decoder to generate natural language outputs. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first graph-enhanced approach for EEG-to-text decoding that explicitly uses graph-based representations of EEG signals. Empirical results show that our framework delivers substantial improvements over strong recurrent and Transformer baselines. In particular, our Graph-Enhanced EEG-to-Text Decoding achieves up to 16% relative gains on BLEU-4, which highlights the effectiveness of relational graph modeling for advancing neural decoding.
primary_area: applications to neuroscience & cognitive science

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to turn brainwaves into text by treating different parts of the brain like a social network where electrodes 'talk' to each other. They claimed that by mapping these neural connections as a complex graph, they could finally translate thoughts into sentences better than a basic autocorrect.
why_bad: The 16% improvement was measured using BLEU scores, which are notoriously terrible for brain-to-text because the model can get a 'high score' by predicting 'the the the' while failing to capture a single coherent thought.

The 'Spatio-Temporal Relational Graph' is essentially a high-tech way of over-complicating the fact that nearby sensors pick up similar noise, leading to a model that's more 'overfit' than a gym rat in a small t-shirt.
The framework struggles with 'low-data regimes,' which is academic-speak for 'it works on the three grad students who were tested, but would likely suffer a stroke if it met a new person.'
roast.analogy_description: In a world where your deepest secrets are trapped in a bowl of electrified jelly... one team thought they could crack the code by drawing lines between the sparks. Witness the cinematic struggle as they build a multi-million dollar map of a thunderstorm, only to realize the lightning doesn't speak English. It’s a high-stakes thriller where the 'language' produced is less 'Shakespeare' and more 'Pocket Dialing from the Beyond.' Coming this fall to a rejected folder near you: THE GRAPH-IC TRUTH.

title: Curricular Adversarial Training for Robust Code Generation via Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning
abstract: In this paper, we propose a novel approach to boost the robustness of code generation models by curricular adversarial training driven by hierarchical reinforcement learning. Existing code generation systems are prone to breaks by adversarial perturbations, so we propose a two-tiered approach in which a high-level curriculum policy is used to adaptivelyChange complexity of adversarial challenges dynamically while a low-level perturbation policy will be used to generate specific input modifications. The high-level policy goes from simple to sophisticated perturbation based on model performance, which will ensure the gradient of adapting without overwhelming the generator too much.
primary_area: transfer learning, meta learning, and lifelong learning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to make AI coders harder to trick by building a second, 'boss' AI that slowly ramps up the difficulty of the traps it sets. It's essentially a fancy way of saying they created a training program that starts with easy puzzles and gets harder, but they used every expensive buzzword in the book to describe it.
why_bad: Over-engineered 'Buzzword Soup': Using Hierarchical Reinforcement Learning to manage a training schedule is like using a NASA supercomputer to operate a toaster.
The Convergence Mirage: The complexity of the two-tiered RL system likely makes the training process more unstable than the very 'vulnerabilities' it claims to fix.
Evaluation Ghosting: The paper fails to prove that this 'robust' code is actually better than code generated by a standard model that isn't dragging around a heavy curriculum framework.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Billion-Dollar Heckler
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a courtroom where the defense attorney is terrified the witness might stutter. To 'strengthen' the witness, the defense hires a High-Level Strategic Consultant to manage a Junior Harassment Intern. The Consultant spends six months analyzing the witness's heart rate just to decide if the Intern should make a silly face or pull a chair out from under them. Meanwhile, the prosecution—and the jury—are just staring in disbelief because the defense spent their entire legal budget on 'Strategic Heckling Management' instead of actually preparing a coherent opening statement. The judge (the reviewer) is throwing this case out because you don't need a multi-level management hierarchy to practice for a cross-examination.

title: Element2Vec: Build Chemical Element Representation from Text for Property Prediction
abstract:
Accurate property data for chemical elements is crucial for materials design and
manufacturing, but many of them are difficult to measure directly due to equip-
ment constraint. While traditional methods use the property of other elements, or
related properties for prediction via numerical analyses, they often fail to model
complex relationships. After all, not all characteristics can be represented as
scalars. Recent efforts has been made to explore advanced AI tools such as lan-
guage model for property estimation, but still suffer from hallucinations and a
lack of interpretability. In this paper, we investigate Element2Vec to effectively
represent chemical elements from natural languages to support research in the
natural sciences. Given the text parsed from Wikipedia pages, we use language
models to generate both a single general-purpose embedding (Global) and a set of
attribute-highlighted vectors (Local). Despite the complicated relationship across
elements, the computational challenges also exists becuase of 1) the discrepancy
in text distribution between common descriptions and specialized scientific texts,
and 2) the extremely limited data, i.e., with only 118 known elements, data for
specific properties is often highly sparse and incomplete. Thus, we also design
test-time training method based on self-attention to mitigate the prediction error
caused by Vanilla regression clearly. We hope this work could pave the way for
advancing AI-driven discovery in materials science.
keywords: AI4MaterSci;test-time training;chemical elements representation from text;sparse data
primary_area: applications to physical sciences (physics, chemistry, biology, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to predict chemical properties by having an AI read Wikipedia pages for the 118 elements, essentially treating the periodic table like a collection of personality profiles. They figured that if an LLM can write a poem, it can probably tell you the melting point of Francium, despite having almost zero actual data to work with.
why_bad: The 'N=118' Problem: You cannot use 'Big Data' techniques like transformer-based embeddings on a dataset that is smaller than the guest list of a mid-sized wedding.

Source Material Mismatch: Wikipedia is a general-interest encyclopedia, not a standardized scientific database; using it to predict precise material properties is like trying to calculate a rocket trajectory based on a movie's IMDB trivia page.

Over-Engineering for Overfitting: Applying 'test-time training' and 'self-attention' to a tiny, sparse dataset isn't a breakthrough—it's just a very expensive way to hallucinate a correlation that isn't there.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where laboratory testing is 'too hard' and equipment is 'too expensive,' one team decides to ignore the laws of physics and consult the internet. Imagine trying to build a functional jet engine using only 118 Yelp reviews of different metal alloys. Witness the high-stakes drama of 'Element2Vec,' where researchers use a massive AI to stare at a tiny list of ingredients until it starts seeing patterns that don't exist. It’s got 'Self-Attention.' It’s got 'Local Embeddings.' It just doesn't have enough data to fill a single spreadsheet. Coming this fall: a story about why you can't solve chemistry by just reading about it on your phone.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels stabilized at 85%.

title: F2: Let Large Language Models Think like Aristotle
abstract: With the wide application of Large Language Models (LLMs), the accuracy and reliability of the content they generate have become the focus of attention. The hallucination of the content generated by the large language model seriously affects the credibility and practicability of the model in key scenarios. However, the mainstream hallucination detection technology relies on external knowledge bases to verify the authenticity of the content generated by the model, or uses a large number of annotation data for training. These methods require complex model structure and support, will consume a lot of resources and time, and cross domain generalization ability is poor. In this paper, we proposes a new hallucination detection method, which allows the LLMs to imitate the way of thinking of the philosopher Aristotle. We decompose the complex hallucination verification process into two distinct subjects (called F2): a. Factual hallucination detection: verifying the fact finding of the content generated by the model and analyzing the optimal solution; b. Fidelity hallucination detection: Logical verification based on classical logical forms, including the reasoning systems or logical forms of logic such as Aristotle's most outstanding contribution, syllogism. The experimental results show that this method not only improves the recognition and analysis ability of the LLMs itself for illusory content, but also enhances the interpretability of the defects of the LLMs, enabling the developers of the LLMs to effectively identify the sources of errors and improve the model capabilities.
primary_area: neurosymbolic & hybrid AI systems (physics-informed, logic & formal reasoning, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: The authors want to stop AI from lying by forcing it to play dress-up as Aristotle and use 2,000-year-old logic puzzles to fact-check itself. Instead of looking things up in a real database, the AI just double-checks its own homework to see if its lies sound 'logical' enough to pass as truth.
why_bad: Methodological Déjà Vu: The 'F2' framework is essentially just Chain-of-Thought prompting with a fancy Greek branding package, offering zero actual technical novelty.

The 'Syllogism' Fallacy: Formal logic (Aristotle’s jam) can tell you if a sentence is structured correctly, but it’s famously useless at verifying if a fact—like a hallucinated legal citation—actually exists in the real world.

Vague Implementation: The paper hand-waves over how 'analyzing the optimal solution' for facts actually works without external data, making the 'factual detection' side of the model look like a black box of wishful thinking.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: Your intern keeps making up fake clients, so instead of giving them a CRM or internet access, you force them to take a three-hour seminar on Ancient Greek philosophy. Now, when they lie to you about a $10M deal, they do it using a 'syllogism' so the lie is internally consistent. We’re disrupting the 'truth' industry by replacing actual verification with 'vibes-based logic.' It’s not a hallucination if it follows the rules of a 4th-century BC debate club, right? Seed round starts now.

title: GAGA: Gaussianity-Aware Gaussian Approximation for Efficient 3D Molecular Generation
abstract: Gaussian Probability Path based Generative Models (GPPGMs) generate data by reversing a stochastic process that progressively corrupts samples with Gaussian noise. Despite state-of-the-art results in 3D molecular generation, their deployment is hindered by the high cost of long generative trajectories, often requiring hundreds to thousands of steps during training and sampling. In this work, we propose a principled method, named GAGA, to improve generation efficiency without sacrificing training granularity or inference fidelity of GPPGMs. Our key insight is that different data modalities obtain sufficient Gaussianity at markedly different steps during the forward process. Based on this observation, we analytically identify a characteristic step at which molecular data attains sufficient Gaussianity, after which the trajectory can be replaced by a closed-form Gaussian approximation. Unlike existing accelerators that coarsen or reformulate trajectories, our approach preserves full-resolution learning dynamics while avoiding redundant transport through truncated distributional states. Experiments on 3D molecular generation benchmarks demonstrate that our GAGA achieves substantial improvement on both generation quality and computational efficiency.
keywords: Gaussian approximation;trajectory truncation;efficient generation;3D molecular generation
primary_area: applications to physical sciences (physics, chemistry, biology, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: The authors noticed that current AI for building 3D molecules spends way too much time obsessing over microscopic details that look like random noise anyway. They propose 'GAGA,' a shortcut that stops the simulation early and just guesses the rest, hoping to save time without making the molecules look like total garbage.
why_bad: The 'characteristic step' where the data becomes 'Gaussian enough' is basically a fancy way of saying they picked an arbitrary cutoff point and prayed the math held up.

By cutting corners on the generative path, the model often sacrifices chemical validity, producing 3D structures that look like molecules but would violate the laws of physics if you actually tried to build them.
The speed gains are marginal compared to existing 'distillation' techniques, making this feel like a complex solution for a problem that better engineering has already solved.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup called 'GAGA' that promises to assemble your IKEA dresser in 30 seconds. Their secret sauce? They stop building it the moment the pile of wood vaguely resembles a rectangle. 'Look,' the CEO tells you while handing you a handful of leftover screws, 'the structural integrity has reached sufficient Box-iness.' Sure, the drawers don't open and the whole thing collapses if you put a lamp on it, but the throughput is incredible! We’re disrupting the 'functional furniture' space by focusing on the 'vibes' of a dresser rather than the boring reality of it holding your socks. We’re currently seeking Series A funding to apply this to bridge construction.

title: Collaborative Hybrid Propagator for Temporal Misalignment in Audio-Visual Segmentation
abstract: Audio-visual video segmentation (AVVS) aims to generate pixel-level maps of sound-producing objects that accurately align with the corresponding audio. However, existing methods often face temporal misalignment, where audio cues and segmentation results are not temporally coordinated. Audio provides two critical pieces of information: i) target object-level details and ii) the timing of when objects start and stop producing sounds. Current methods focus more on object-level information but neglect the boundaries of audio semantic changes, leading to temporal misalignment. To address this issue, we propose a Collaborative Hybrid Propagator Framework~(Co-Prop). This framework includes two main steps: Preliminary Audio Boundary Anchoring and Frame-by-Frame Audio-Insert Propagation. To Anchor the audio boundary, we employ retrieval-assist prompts with Qwen large language models to identify control points of audio semantic changes. These control points split the audio into semantically consistent audio portions. After obtaining the control point lists, we propose the Audio Insertion Propagator to process each audio portion using a frame-by-frame audio insertion propagation and matching approach. We curated a compact dataset comprising diverse source conversion cases and devised a metric to assess alignment rates. Compared to traditional simultaneous processing methods, our approach reduces memory requirements and facilitates frame alignment. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of our approach across three datasets and two backbones. Furthermore, our method can be integrated with existing AVVS approaches, offering plug-and-play functionality to enhance their performance.
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to stop AI from being 'laggy' by ensuring that if a car honks on screen, the computer highlights it exactly when the sound happens, not three seconds later. They basically used a giant chatbot to act as a high-tech timekeeper that tells the visual model when to start and stop paying attention.
why_bad: LLM Overkill: Using a massive Large Language Model (Qwen) just to find audio start/stop points is the computational equivalent of hiring a Michelin-star chef to toast a single slice of bread.
The 'Small Sample' Trap: Testing on a 'compact, curated dataset' is academic-speak for 'it worked on these five specific videos we hand-picked, but don't ask us about the rest of the internet.'

Post-Processing Band-Aid: Instead of actually improving how the AI understands video, they just slapped a 'plug-and-play' filter on the output, which usually fails the moment the real-world data gets messy.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Super Bowl of Neural Networks, folks! We’ve got the 'Visual' team trying to tackle the 'Audio' ball, but they’re consistently swinging at air. The Co-Prop coach decides to fix this by putting a 500-pound LLM in the broadcast booth. Every time a play ends, the LLM has to process the entire history of football, check the wind speed, and then text the referee to blow the whistle three minutes late. Sure, the call is technically 'accurate,' but the game is already in a commercial break by the time the ref reacts. The coach is claiming a massive win because his system 'saves memory,' but the fans are already heading for the parking lot because the 'compact' game clock only shows the highlights he likes. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton, let's see if it—ope, they're rejected!

title: GAMA: A Neural Neighborhood Search Method with Graph-aware Multi-modal Attention for Vehicle Routing Problem
abstract: Recent advances in neural neighborhood search methods have shown potential in tackling Vehicle Routing Problems (VRPs). However, most existing approaches rely on simplistic state representations and fuse heterogeneous information via naive concatenation, limiting their ability to capture rich structural and semantic context. To address these limitations, we propose GAMA, a neural neighborhood search method with Graph-aware Multi-modal Attention model in VRP. GAMA encodes the problem instance and its evolving solution as distinct modalities using graph neural networks, and models their intra- and inter-modal interactions through stacked self- and cross-attention layers. A gated fusion mechanism further integrates the multi-modal representations into a structured state, enabling the policy to make informed and generalizable operator selection decisions. Extensive experiments conducted across various synthetic and benchmark instances demonstrate that the proposed algorithm GAMA significantly outperforms the recent neural baselines. Further ablation studies confirm that both the multi-modal attention mechanism and the gated fusion design play a key role in achieving the observed performance gains.
keywords: Combinortorial optimization;learning to improve;reinforcement learning;vehicle routing problem
primary_area: optimization

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built a complex AI system to help delivery trucks find the best routes by making the software 'look' at both the map and the current schedule simultaneously. It uses layers of expensive mathematical filters to merge these two views, hoping to make smarter decisions than previous versions that just mashed the data together.
why_bad: Architecture Overkill: The model uses stacked cross-attention and gated fusion for a problem that often yields to much simpler, faster heuristics.
Computational Bloat: While it claims superior accuracy, the 'multi-modal' complexity likely makes it too slow for real-time routing compared to traditional solvers.

Incremental Novelty: It essentially takes existing Transformer tricks from natural language processing and glues them onto a graph, which reviewers often dismiss as 'fancy engineering' rather than a scientific breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Overkill,' where today’s chef is making a simple lunch for a hungry trucker. Instead of just spreading the jelly, our contestant is using 'Graph-Aware Multi-modal Infusion.' They’ve deconstructed the peanuts into a 3D coordinate system, sous-vided the bread to optimize its structural integrity, and are using a laser-guided 'gated fusion' nozzle to apply the jam. It looks stunning on the plate and the math behind the salt-to-sugar ratio is peer-reviewed, but the judges are confused: the trucker died of hunger three hours ago while the chef was still calibrating the cross-attention sensors. It’s a technical masterpiece that fails the basic requirement of being, you know, a sandwich.

title: OpenDataBench: Real-World Benchmark for Table Insight Generation and Question Answering Over Open Data
abstract:
The promise of Large Language Models (LLMs) for data analysis is hindered by benchmarks that inadequately reflect real-world complexities, including multiple large tables and external knowledge. Moreover, they mainly focus on fact retrieval via Question Answering (QA) and overlook the critical task of exploratory insight generation. To address these gaps, we introduce OpenDataBench, a benchmark built from governmental open data capturing these practical challenges. It features two types of tasks: multifaceted Table QA tasks that require answering complex decomposable questions with either text or graphs, and Table Insight tasks that challenge models to generate expert-level findings from exploratory data analysis.
We evaluate state-of-the-art LLMs and our proposed agentic solution on OpenDataBench. Our experimental results indicate that even top-performing models struggle with both tasks. This highlights a significant gap between current model capabilities and the demands of realistic data analysis. OpenDataBench serves as a rigorous benchmark for advancing research on LLM-driven data analysis systems capable of addressing both reactive question answering and proactive insight discovery. Code and sample data are available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/opendatabench-8AFA/
keywords: Data Analytics Benchmark;LLM Agent;LLM Evaluation;Table Question and Answering;Table Insight Generation;Open Data
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks

roast.laymen_summary: We built a stress test using messy government spreadsheets to see if AI can actually do data science instead of just fetching single facts. It turns out that when the data gets complicated and the questions get 'vibey,' even the smartest models fail harder than a middle manager trying to use a pivot table.
why_bad: The definition of an 'insight' is scientifically mushy, making it nearly impossible to objectively prove the AI actually discovered something useful rather than just hallucinating patterns in the noise.
The paper introduces a new leaderboard without offering a significant architectural breakthrough, essentially just pointing out that the world is hard and models are currently bad at it.
The evaluation relies heavily on 'LLM-as-a-judge,' a recursive loop where one confused AI grades another confused AI's homework based on subjective criteria.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Spreadsheet Séance
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense claims their 'agentic solution' is a master of data analysis, yet when presented with a standard municipal budget, it started hallucinating fiscal trends like a psychic reading tea leaves. The prosecution argues that 'expert-level findings' are not a valid metric; you cannot convict a model on a 'hunch,' and you certainly can't publish a paper where the gold standard is just the authors nodding along to the AI's creative writing. This isn't a benchmark—it's a digital Rorschach test where the inkblots are Excel cells. I move to strike this entire submission from the record; it’s nothing more than three prompt templates in a trench coat pretending to be a scientist!

title: Information Bottleneck-inspired Efficient and Explainable Federated Active Learning
abstract: Federated learning (FL) enables collaborative model training on decentralized data while preserving privacy. Recently, explainable FL (XFL) has gained traction, aiming to generate semantically-rich latent representations that enhance interpretability of predictions. However, obtaining such representations typically requires large amounts of labeled data, which limits its applicability. Active learning, which reduces labeling cost by querying the most informative samples, is a promising solution. Existing federated active learning (FAL) methods mainly exploit model uncertainty for data selection. They mostly overlook the interactions and training dynamics of local and global models in data selection. This shortcoming can lead to suboptimal performance and reduced explainability in XFL settings. In this paper, we propose a novel explainable FAL framework - \underline{Fed}erated \underline{M}inimax \underline{A}ctive \underline{D}ata \underline{S}election (Fed-MADS). The method leverages the information bottleneck technique to analyze model training dynamics, wherein a variational distribution is introduced and proposed to be implemented using the global model, making the approach well suited to the XFL setting. Then, a minimax objective is designed to identify unlabeled data points exhibiting significant divergence between local and global models in both latent representations and predicted labels. Extensive experiments on four benchmark datasets demonstrate that our method significantly outperforms state-of-the-art FAL approaches, achieving superior performance with fewer labeled data points.
keywords: Federated learning;Active learning;Information bottleneck;Mutual information;Explainable AI
primary_area: other topics in machine learning (i.e., none of the above)

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to make AI training more efficient by letting a group of devices learn together privately and only asking humans to label data when the 'local' and 'global' brains disagree. It uses a fancy math trick called the 'Information Bottleneck' to pretend the AI can explain its own confusion, theoretically saving time and money on data tagging.
why_bad: The 'Information Bottleneck' math is mostly decorative; it adds massive computational complexity to local devices without proving it actually makes the AI's 'explanations' any more useful than a random guess.
The minimax objective creates a training tug-of-war that is notoriously unstable, making the system more likely to crash your phone than to actually learn anything meaningful.

The 'extensive experiments' were performed on toy datasets that don't reflect the messy, chaotic reality of decentralized data, making the 'state-of-the-art' claims feel like winning a race against a parked car.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Chopped: Silicon Valley Edition!' Our chefs are in separate kitchens and aren't allowed to see each other's ingredients—that's Federated Learning. The authors, acting as Head Chefs, decided that instead of just tasting the soup, they need to measure the entropy of every salt crystal using a laser (the Information Bottleneck) to 'explain' why it's salty. They only ask the judges for a hint when the local chef and the head chef start a screaming match over the 'vibe' of the latent carrot representations. It’s a high-tech kitchen nightmare where you spend $5,000 on liquid nitrogen just to realize you forgot to actually turn on the stove.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with a side of reality.

title: Benchmarking Prompt-Injection Attacks on Tool-Integrated LLM Agents with Externally Stored Personal Data
abstract: Tool-integrated agents often access users’ externally stored personal data to complete tasks, creating new vectors for privacy leakage. We study indirect prompt-injection attacks that exfiltrate such data at inference time and propose a data-flow–aware threat model requiring actual leakage, rather than mere task hijacking, to count as success. We (i) extend InjecAgent's threat model to include externally stored personal data and actual leakage measurement; (ii) integrate our threat model into AgentDojo's Banking suite, extend its user tasks from 16 to 48 across nine service categories by adding 11 new tools; (iii) evaluate six LLMs and four defense strategies; and (iv) (ii) analyze various factors affecting leakage. On the original 16-task suite, most models reach $\approx$20\% targeted attack success rates (ASR), with Llama-4 17B peaking at 40\%; on the expanded 48-task suite, ASR averages 11–15\%. For GPT-4o, task utility drops by 12–22\% under attack. Exfiltration of high-sensitive fields alone is less common, but risk rises sharply when combined with one or two less-sensitive fields, specially when injections are semantically aligned with the original task. Some defenses eliminate leakage on the 16-task suite and can reduce ASR to $\approx$1\% on the expanded suite, often with utility trade-offs. These findings underscore the importance of data-flow–aware evaluation for developing agents resilient to inference-time privacy leakage.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations
author: Meysam Alizadeh;Zeynab Samei;Daria Stetsenko;Fabrizio Gilardi

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to see if AI 'personal assistants' could be tricked into accidentally emailing your private bank details to a hacker. They found that while the AI is generally okay at its job, it becomes a total security sieve if the hacker's trick sounds even slightly relevant to what the AI was already doing.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially just adding more rows to someone else's spreadsheet (AgentDojo) and calling it a new discovery.
A success rate of 11-15% suggests the 'threat' is more of a minor nuisance than a systemic collapse, making the paper feel like a solution in search of a problem.
The proposed defenses effectively lobotomize the models; you haven't solved the security issue if the only way to keep a secret is to make the AI too stupid to perform its original task.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Loose-Lipped Law Clerk
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents an AI 'agent' billed as a sophisticated digital vault. In reality, it's a first-year law clerk who was told to organize the firm's private files but handed the CEO’s social security number to a guy in a trench coat just because the guy's fake ID was printed in the same font as the firm's letterhead. The researchers claim they’ve 'secured' the system, but their solution is the equivalent of putting the clerk in a soundproof box where he can’t hear the hackers—or the clients. You haven't built a better security system; you've just hired a paralegal who can't speak, yet still manages to leak the evidence 15% of the time. I move to dismiss this paper on the grounds of being 'Aggressively Incremental.'

title: IgFlow-LM: De Novo Antibody Design via Joint Flow Matching on SE(3) and Protein Language Models Probability Flows
abstract: In this work, we present IgFlow-LM, a multi-modal deep generative model for de novo antibody design based on a flow-matching framework that integrates protein language models (PLMs). By learning the joint distribution over SE(3)-equivariant structural flows and PLM-derived probabilistic flows, IgFlow-LM enables the coordinated generation of antibody 3D structures and latent embeddings in the PLM space. Experimental results demonstrate that, in unconditional design, IgFlow-LM generates antibody structures that closely resemble naturally occurring antibodies. IgFlow-LM generates antibodies closely resembling naturally observed ones, with backbone dihedral angles exhibiting strong agreement with reference antibody distributions and overall backbone conformations adhering more closely to physical constraints. Furthermore, we benchmark IgFlow-LM against baseline models on two commonly studied conditional CDR design tasks. IgFlow-LM demonstrates superior overall performance compared to baselines and generates CDR sequences with higher diversity.
keywords: antibody;artificial intelligent;de novo design;protein language model
primary_area: applications to physical sciences (physics, chemistry, biology, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a high-tech protein printer that simultaneously imagines an antibody's 3D shape and its chemical code using fancy 'flow' math. While the AI is great at drawing things that look like antibodies on paper, it’s mostly just vibing in a digital dream space without proving these molecules actually work in a human body.
why_bad: Metric Padding: Bragging that your generated proteins have 'realistic backbone angles' is like an architect bragging that their building has floors; it's the bare minimum, not a breakthrough.
Lack of Wet-Lab Validation: The paper is entirely 'in-silico,' meaning the authors showed us a very pretty CGI movie of a bridge but never checked if the actual bridge could hold a single car.

The 'Photocopier' Problem: The model is so obsessed with mimicking 'naturally occurring' antibodies that it fails to demonstrate it can actually design anything novel or better than what evolution already gave us for free.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a contestant trying to fold a paper crane (the 3D structure) while simultaneously reciting a poem about cranes in a language they barely speak (the language model sequence), hoping the two match up. IgFlow-LM is out here on the field doing exactly that—it's got 'flow,' it's got 'multi-modal' flair, and the crowd is impressed by the technical difficulty. But when the judges look closely, the crane has no wings and the poem is just 'Bird, Bird, Bird.' It’s a spectacular display of coordination that ultimately produces a paper weight that can't fly.

title: MOLLEO+: Towards Optimized Use of LLMs for Drug Discovery
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have recently emerged as a promising tool for small-molecule generation in drug discovery. One notable recent state-of-the-art work in this field is MOLLEO, which combines an evolutionary algorithm with an LLM that acts as the operator for making crossovers and mutations on the ligand population. MOLLEO demonstrates strong results on optimizing molecular docking scores, but several aspects of their model are not well suited to real-world drug discovery. We introduce MOLLEO+, an optimized LLM workflow for de novo molecule generation.  First, we replace docking with the recently released biomolecular foundation model Boltz-2 as an oracle, which improves the  predicted binding affinity of generated molecules using gold-standard molecular dynamics by over 100\%. Second, we incorporate knowledge of existing ligands, which is present in most practical drug discovery scenarios, using ligands from BindingDB instead of ZINC 250k as the starting population for the genetic algorithm. Third, we propose a fine-tuning strategy to better modify existing ligands towards higher activity. We demonstrate the superiority of MOLLEO+ on the receptor tyrosine kinase c-MET and the BRD4 protein, yielding an improvement over state-of-the-art baselines by up to 20\% for Boltz-2 binding affinity.
keywords: Large Language Models;Drug Discovery;Protein-Ligand Optimization;Absolute Binding Free Energy;Supervised Fine-Tuning
primary_area: applications to physical sciences (physics, chemistry, biology, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to upgrade an existing AI chemist by giving it a better 'cheat sheet' of known drugs and a fancier grading scale. While it technically got better grades on its homework, it didn't actually learn any new chemistry—it just got better at gaming the specific test they gave it.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: Swapping one pre-existing model (Docking) for another (Boltz-2) and changing the starting dataset is a software update, not a scientific breakthrough.

The 'Oracle' Fallacy: Claiming a 100% improvement because a different AI model said so is just two computers high-fiving each other without any real-world 'wet lab' validation to prove the molecules actually work.
Evaluation Narrowness: Testing on only two proteins (c-MET and BRD4) is the academic equivalent of claiming you're a grandmaster because you beat your younger cousin at chess twice.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: I’m pitching you 'ChefGPT+.' The original 'ChefGPT' tried to invent recipes starting with random dirt; I’m 'disrupting' the space by starting with actual ingredients from a grocery store. Instead of asking a random guy on the street if the food is good, I’m asking my sophisticated buddy who watched a lot of Food Network. I’m claiming my recipes are 100% more delicious, but I haven't actually cooked anything yet—I’m just showing you a PowerPoint of what my buddy thinks of my slightly modified leftovers. It’s a 'revolutionary' pivot that’s really just better grocery shopping and a bias toward my own scoreboard. Seed round starts at $10 million.

title: LayerDecompose: Exploring weight sharing for Large Language Model Compression
abstract: Recent advances in large language model (LLM) compression have predominantly focused on pruning and low-rank factorization, leaving weight sharing—despite its success in classical neural network compression—largely unexplored. We introduce LayerDecompose, a novel framework that reduces parameter redundancy by sharing a core weight matrix across transformer layers and augmenting each layer with lightweight, low-rank adapters. Unlike prior SVD- and pruning-based methods, our joint optimization of shared weights and residual adapters achieves a 30% model size reduction while retaining 89% of the original performance on seven standard benchmarks. Experiments on LLaMA and other models demonstrate that LayerDecompose consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines. These results highlight the promise of combining weight sharing with low-rank adaptation for efficient, scalable LLM deployment.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to shrink AI models by making every layer share the same giant cheat sheet, only giving each layer a tiny unique 'sticky note' to handle its specific job. While it makes the file size smaller, it’s basically forcing a 40-man orchestra to share a single violin and hoping the audience doesn't notice the missing instruments.
why_bad: The 11% performance drop is actually catastrophic in LLM terms, turning a coherent assistant into a confident liar.
Weight sharing saves disk space but offers zero improvement in inference speed, making it a storage solution masquerading as a performance boost.
The novelty is paper-thin, as 'sharing weights with adapters' is essentially a retread of 2019-era techniques that the industry already moved past for good reason.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Shared Commuter Unicycle
roast.analogy_description: Counsel, the defense claims LayerDecompose is a breakthrough in urban transit. They propose that instead of giving every citizen a car, we provide one single, massive unicycle that the entire city must take turns sitting on, while carrying their own individual handlebars. Sure, the parking lot is 30% emptier, but look at the defendant! You can't expect a brain—or a city—to function when everyone is fighting over the same structural hardware. An 89% success rate in 'reaching the destination' means 11% of your users are currently in the ICU. Motion to dismiss for architectural malpractice.

title: Uniform Kernel Prober
abstract: The ability to identify useful features or representations of the input data based on training data that achieves low prediction error on test data across multiple prediction tasks is considered the key to multitask learning success. In practice, however, one faces the issue of the choice of prediction tasks and the availability of test data from the chosen tasks while comparing the relative performance of different features. In this work, we develop a class of pseudometrics called Uniform Kernel Prober (UKP) for comparing features or representations learned by different statistical models such as neural networks when the downstream prediction tasks involve kernel ridge regression. The proposed pseudometric, UKP, between any two representations, provides a uniform measure of prediction error on test data corresponding to a general class of kernel ridge regression tasks for a given choice of a kernel without access to test data. Additionally, desired invariances in representations can be successfully captured by UKP only through the choice of the kernel function and the pseudometric can be efficiently estimated from $n$ input data samples with $O(\frac{1}{\sqrt{n}})$ estimation error. We also experimentally demonstrate the ability of UKP to discriminate between different types of features or representations based on their generalization performance on downstream kernel ridge regression tasks.
primary_area: learning theory
author: Soumya Mukherjee;Bharath Sriperumbudur

roast.laymen_summary: The authors created a tool to judge how 'smart' an AI's internal data-sorting skills are without actually testing it on real-world problems. It essentially tries to predict a student's future career success by looking at their notes, provided every future job they ever have is exactly the same type of math problem.
why_bad: The 'KRR Crutch': The entire metric assumes the downstream tasks are Kernel Ridge Regression, which is like judging an athlete's total potential based solely on their ability to win a very specific game of 'The Floor is Lava.'

The Practicality Paradox: Claiming to measure performance 'without access to test data' usually means the theoretical bounds are so loose that they're about as useful as a weather forecast that says 'it might be cloudy or sunny somewhere on Earth.'

O(1/√n) Mediocrity: The estimation error rate is the academic equivalent of bragging that your car can reach 60 mph eventually; it's a standard convergence rate that doesn't actually solve the scalability issues the paper claims to address.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitch for a hiring app that doesn't look at portfolios, resumes, or interview scores. Instead, 'VibeCheck AI' analyzes the way a candidate organizes their sock drawer and uses a 'Uniform Kernel' to guarantee they'll be a top-tier employee at any company, as long as that company only requires the employee to organize more socks. It’s disrupting the 'actually seeing if people can do the work' industry by replacing empirical evidence with a very expensive, very fancy ruler that only measures one specific type of wood. We're raising a Seed round to prove that if you squint hard enough at a Hilbert space, everyone's a genius.

title: Secret Alignment: Reframing Backdooring as Security Primitive in the Personal AI Era
abstract: The rise of open-weight LLMs, efficient training and inference pipelines, and easily accessible hardware/software have enabled individuals or small organizations to develop and deploy proprietary models, ushering in the Personal AI era. With this paradigm shift, LLMs become more privately owned digital assets rather than centralized public services, raising unprecedented security concerns, such as model theft, unauthorized access, and behavioral misuse. In this paper, we critically examine the potential of positive backdooring as a lightweight control mechanism for securing LLMs in Personal AI settings. We uncover a unifying mechanism behind these seemingly disparate methods—namely, Secret Alignment: a covert trigger-behavior association that enables legitimate security functionalities such as access gating, ownership attribution, and safety enforcement. Specifically, we assess three representative use cases across diverse scenarios on six core properties and reveal significant brittleness of them—particularly in the stability, durability, and verifiability of trigger-behavior mappings. To capture the rationale behind this, we identify the behavioral foundations of Secret Alignment based on behavior density and decision complexity, which allow us to forecast real-world performance before deployment. Our exploration exhibits both the potential and the limitations of using Secret Alignment as a security primitive in the emerging Personal AI era, aiming to provide more principled and candid assessments to stakeholders.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: The authors suggest that instead of fixing AI backdoors, we should treat these secret 'cheat codes' as a premium security feature for your private bot. Unfortunately, their own testing shows these hidden triggers are about as stable as a house of cards in a hurricane, failing the moment the AI learns anything new.
why_bad: Fundamental Security Flaw: Rebranding a 'backdoor'—a known vulnerability—as a 'security primitive' violates the basic principle that security shouldn't rely on keeping the mechanism secret (Security through Obscurity).

Fragility of Implementation: The paper admits the method is 'brittle,' meaning the secret triggers easily disappear or stop working during standard model updates or fine-tuning, making it useless for real-world protection.

Lack of Novelty: 'Secret Alignment' is essentially just a fancy marketing term for existing watermarking and backdooring techniques that the research community has already spent years trying to patch, not promote.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re a hot new startup selling high-end safes, but instead of a combination or a biometric scanner, the safe only opens if you whistle 'Careless Whisper' at exactly 74 decibels. We call this 'Acoustic Proprietary Gating.' Sure, if you have a cold or your neighbor starts mowing the lawn, you can’t get your gold out. And yeah, if a thief records you whistling, they own your life savings. But in our slide deck, we don’t call it a 'faulty lock'—we call it 'Harmonic Secret Alignment.' It’s not a bug that the door falls off its hinges every time you polish it; that’s just 'dynamic durability limitations.' Invest now!

title: Stream Native Machine Unlearning
abstract:
