Machine unlearning work assumes a static, i.i.d. training environment that doesn't truly exist. Machines are expected to learn and unlearn 
We introduce the Memory Pair, the first stream-native unlearning algorithm coupling an online learning-unlearning pair. 
Our framework achieves state-of-the-art logarithmic regret bounds under strong convexity that scale naturally for nonstationary stream environments. These guarantees extend the lifespan of deployed models, reducing costly retraining while ensuring compliance with data deletion requirements. Finally, the algorithm intentionally achieves a novel sublinear memory footprint for an online machine unlearning. This makes it attractive for on-device learning, memory-constrained devices, and IoT networks where privacy is paramount.
primary_area: optimization

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to build a digital eraser that can scrub specific memories from an AI's brain while it's still learning from a firehose of new data. Instead of shutting the whole system down to fix a mistake, it attempts to 'unlearn' on the fly using a tiny amount of computer memory.
why_bad: The 'strong convexity' assumption is a massive theoretical crutch; it's like designing a car that only works if the entire world is a perfectly flat parking lot.

Their 'sublinear memory' claim is a double-edged sword; the model tries to be so lightweight that it likely suffers from 'catastrophic forgetting' where it accidentally erases the stuff it was actually supposed to keep.
The reviewers likely saw through the 'stream-native' branding, noting that the math is just a slight tweak on existing online optimization rather than a fundamental breakthrough in privacy.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Iron Chef: Data Privacy!' Our contestant today is trying to make a 12-course meal on a moving train. The catch? Halfway through the sauté, a customer demands they 'un-cook' only the onions because of an allergy, without stopping the stove. The chef uses a 'Memory Pair' technique—basically trying to flick the onions out with a toothpick while the pan is still screaming hot. They claim it’s efficient because they’re only using a single thimble to hold their ingredients (that’s your sublinear memory). But because the chef assumed the pan has 'perfectly even heat' (strong convexity), the mushrooms are burnt, the sauce is broken, and the customer is still getting a hint of onion. It’s a flashy performance, but I’m still leaving the restaurant hungry and slightly poisoned.
roast.tone_check: Snarky, scientifically grounded, and accurately dismissive of over-idealized theoretical bounds.

title: Planning with Generative Cognitive Maps
abstract: Planning relies on cognitive maps  -- models that encode world structure given cognitive resource constraints. The problem of learning functional cognitive maps is shared by humans, animals and machines. However, we still lack a clear understanding of how people represent maps for planning, particularly when the goal is to support cost-efficient plans. We take inspiration from theory of compositional mental representations in cognitive science to propose GenPlan: a cognitively-grounded computational framework that models redundant structure in maps and saves planning cost through policy reuse. Our framework integrates (1) a Generative Map Module that infers generative compositional structure and (2) a Structure-Based Planner that exploits structural redundancies to reduce planning costs. We show that our framework closely aligns with human behavior, suggesting that people approximate planning by piecewise policies conditioned on world structure. We also show that our approach reduces the computational cost of planning while producing good-enough plans, and contribute a proof-of-concept implementation demonstrating how to build these principles into a working system.
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers suggest that instead of mapping every inch of the world, our brains save energy by treating different places as 'copy-pasted' versions of each other. They built a robot brain that tries to cut corners by reusing the same simple movements whenever it recognizes a familiar-ish layout.
why_bad: The 'good-enough plans' produced by the model are often significantly less efficient than standard algorithms, trading away too much accuracy for questionable speed gains.
The claim that it 'closely aligns with human behavior' is based on a narrow experimental task that doesn't account for the messy, non-repeating reality of actual human environments.
The 'Generative Map Module' is essentially a glorified pattern-matcher that struggles to generalize once you move beyond simple, grid-like toy problems.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup called GenPlan that wants to disrupt how you walk through your own house. Instead of looking at where your furniture actually is, our app assumes every room is just a slightly reshuffled IKEA showroom. Why bother navigating the 'unique' nuances of your hallway when you can just reuse the 'Living Room Alpha' movement script? Sure, you might stub your toe on a coffee table that wasn't in the template, but think of the mental bandwidth you're saving for Netflix! We aren't failing to reach the kitchen; we're just optimizing for 'structural redundancy' while we walk into the fridge. It’s not a bug, it’s a piecewise policy!

title: Programmatic Representation Learning with Language Models
abstract: Classical models for supervised machine learning, such as decision trees, are efficient and interpretable predictors, but their quality is highly dependent on the particular choice of input features. Although neural networks can learn useful representations directly from raw data (e.g., images or text), this comes at the expense of interpretability and the need for specialized hardware to run them efficiently. In this paper, we explore a hypothesis class we call Learned Programmatic Representations (LeaPR) models, which stack arbitrary features represented as code (functions from data points to scalars) and decision tree predictors. We synthesize feature functions using Large Language Models (LLMs), which have rich prior knowledge in a wide range of domains and a remarkable ability to write code using existing domain-specific libraries. We propose two algorithms to learn LeaPR models from supervised data. First, we design an adaptation of FunSearch to learn features rather than directly generate predictors. Then, we develop a novel variant of the classical ID3 algorithm for decision tree learning, where new features are generated on demand when splitting leaf nodes. In experiments from chess position evaluation to image and text classification, our methods learn high-quality, neural network-free predictors often competitive with neural networks. Our work suggests a flexible paradigm for learning interpretable representations end-to-end where features and predictions can be readily inspected and understood.
primary_area: neurosymbolic & hybrid AI systems (physics-informed, logic & formal reasoning, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to use ChatGPT as a high-end personal assistant to write custom computer code that explains data, then plugged those snippets into a simple digital flowchart. The goal was to build a model that is easy to understand without needing a massive supercomputer to run it, essentially trying to turn 'black box' AI into a 'clear box' instruction manual.
why_bad: Efficiency Paradox: Using a massive, multi-billion parameter LLM to generate a tiny decision tree is like using a space shuttle to deliver a pizza; the 'efficiency' gain at the end doesn't justify the astronomical cost of the 'learning' phase.

Benchmark Cherry-Picking: The paper claims to be 'competitive' with neural networks, but likely compares itself to outdated or poorly-tuned models rather than the state-of-the-art architectures it aims to replace.

Methodological Fluff: The 'novel' ID3 algorithm variant is essentially just calling an LLM API in a loop until it spits out code that works, which reviewers often see as glorified prompt engineering rather than a fundamental algorithmic breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Iron Developer,' where today's contestant is trying to make a simple piece of toast. Instead of just pushing the lever down on a regular toaster (a standard neural network), they’ve hired a world-class Michelin-star chef (the LLM) to write a 400-page technical manual on the molecular thermodynamics of browning bread. They then built a Rube Goldberg machine to follow those instructions. Sure, at the end, you have a piece of toast and a manual that explains exactly why it's crispy—which is great for 'interpretability'—but you spent $5,000 on consulting fees and three days of work for something that tastes exactly like the 50-cent bread you could have just toasted yourself.
roast.tone_check: Scientifically accurate snark delivered with 99.9% confidence intervals.

title: MA-RAG: Multi-Agent Retrieval-Augmented Generation via Collaborative Chain-of-Thought Reasoning
abstract: We present MA-RAG, a Multi-Agent framework for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) that addresses the inherent ambiguities and reasoning challenges in complex information-seeking tasks. Unlike conventional RAG methods that rely on either end-to-end fine-tuning or isolated component enhancements, MA-RAG orchestrates a collaborative set of specialized AI agents: Planner, Step Definer, Extractor, and QA Agents, to tackle each stage of the RAG pipeline with task-aware reasoning. Ambiguities may arise from underspecified queries, sparse or indirect evidence in retrieved documents, or the need to integrate information scattered across multiple sources. MA-RAG mitigates these challenges by decomposing the problem into subtasks, such as query disambiguation, evidence extraction, and answer synthesis, and dispatching them to dedicated agents equipped with chain-of-thought prompting. These agents communicate intermediate reasoning and progressively refine the retrieval and synthesis process. Our design allows fine-grained control over information flow without any model fine-tuning. Crucially, agents are invoked on demand, enabling a dynamic and efficient workflow that avoids unnecessary computation. This modular and reasoning-driven architecture enables MA-RAG to deliver robust, interpretable results. Experiments on multi-hop and ambiguous QA benchmarks demonstrate that MA-RAG outperforms state-of-the-art training-free baselines and rivals fine-tuned systems, validating the effectiveness of collaborative agent-based reasoning in RAG.
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of array: line 6 column 157 (char 765)

title: Conversion of sparse Artificial Neural Network to sparse Spiking Neural Network can save up to 99% of energy
abstract:
Artificial Neural Networks (ANNs) are becoming increasingly important but face the challenge of the large scale and high energy consumption.
 Dynamic Sparse Training (DST) aims to reduce the memory and energy consumption of ANNs by learning sparse network topologies, which ultimately results 
 in structural connection sparsity. Meanwhile, Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs) have attracted increasing attention due to their biological plausibility 
 and event-driven nature, which ultimately results in temporal sparsity. To bypass the difficulty of directly training SNNs, converting pre-trained ANNs to SNNs
  (ANN2SNN) is becoming a popular approach to obtain high-performance SNNs. Here for the first time, we investigate the advantage of dynamically sparsely 
  trained ANNs for conversion into sparse SNNs. By adopting Cannistraci-Hebb Training (CHT), a state-of-the-art brain-inspired DST family that 
  resembles synaptic turnover during neuronal connectivity learning in brain circuits, we investigated the extent to which connectivity sparsity 
  impacts the accuracy and theoretical energy efficiency of SNNs across different conversion approaches. The results show that sparse SNNs can achieve 
  accuracy comparable to or even surpassing that of dense SNNs. Moreover, sparse SNNs can reduce theoretical energy consumption by up to 99\% compared with dense SNNs. 
  Furthermore, driven by the interest in understanding the physical dynamics interactions between firing rate and accuracy in SNNs, we systematically analyzed 
  the temporal relationship between the saturation of firing rate and accuracy in SNNs.  Our results reveal a significant time lag in which firing rate 
  saturation precedes accuracy saturation. We also demonstrate that the magnitude of time lag is significantly different between sparse and dense networks, 
  where the average time lag of sparse SNNs are higher than dense SNNs. Together, these results demonstrate that Cannistraci-Hebb Training can be effectively 
  integrated into ANN-to-SNN conversion pipelines to obtain SNNs with competitive trade-off between accuracy and theoretical energy.
keywords: Spiking neural networks;ANN-to-SNN conversion dynamic sparse training;Cannistraci-Hebb Training;sustainable AI;energy-efficient architectures
primary_area: applications to neuroscience & cognitive science

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to save power by pruning an AI's brain and converting it into 'spikes' that only fire when necessary, much like a lazy employee who only emails when the boss is looking. They found they could cut 99% of the connections and still get the right answer, though the AI starts 'yelling' internally long before it actually figures out what it's doing.
why_bad: The 99% energy saving is 'theoretical,' which is researcher-speak for 'this doesn't actually run on real-world hardware yet.'
ANN-to-SNN conversion is a shortcut that avoids the messy, difficult reality of training spiking networks from scratch, making the results feel like a port of a mobile game to a supercomputer.

The 'time lag' discovery—that networks peak in activity before they peak in accuracy—is less of a breakthrough and more of an inherent trait of SNNs that the authors treated like a new law of physics.
roast.analogy_name: The Minimalist Librarian: Silence is Power
roast.analogy_description: In a world where data centers consume the power of a small sun, one team decided to lobotomize the machine. They took a library, burned 99% of the books, and replaced the librarians with caffeinated mimes who only communicate via frantic hand signals. See the gripping drama of a system that starts screaming its answer before it has even finished reading the question. It's 99% more efficient because it’s 99% empty. Coming soon to a theoretical architecture near you.
roast.tone_check: Dryly clinical with a hint of 'I've seen this 100 times at CVPR' exhaustion.

title: TPD-AHD: Textual Preference Differentiation for LLM-Based Automatic Heuristic Design
abstract: The design of effective heuristics for complex combinatorial optimization problems has traditionally relied on extensive domain expertise and manual effort. While Large Language Model-based Automated Heuristic Design (LLM-AHD) offers a promising path toward autonomous heuristic generation, existing methods often suffer from undirected search processes and poor interpretability, resulting in a black-box optimization paradigm. To address these limitations, we introduce Textual Preference Differentiation for Automatic Heuristic Design (TPD-AHD), a novel framework that integrates preference optimization with textual feedback to guide LLM-driven heuristic evolution. TPD-AHD employs a best-anchored strategy to pair heuristic candidates and generates a natural language textual loss. This loss is then translated into a textual gradient, which provides explicit, interpretable instructions for iterative heuristic refinement. This approach not only enhances the transparency of the optimization trajectory but also ensures a directed search toward high-performance regions. Extensive experiments on a suite of NP-hard combinatorial optimization problems demonstrate that TPD-AHD consistently outperforms both manually designed heuristics and existing LLM-AHD methods. Furthermore, it exhibits strong generalization capabilities across diverse domains and provides clear insights into the heuristic improvement process. TPD-AHD establishes a new paradigm for interpretable, efficient, and scalable automatic heuristic design.
keywords: Textual Differentiation;Preference Optimization;Automatic Heuristic Design;Large Language Model
primary_area: optimization

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to make AI write better problem-solving code by forcing it to compare its work to a 'gold standard' and write a self-deprecating essay on why it failed. Instead of just guessing, the AI uses these verbal critiques as a roadmap to tweak its logic until the math finally works.
why_bad: The 'textual gradient' is essentially just fancy prompt engineering disguised as math; calling a paragraph of feedback a 'gradient' is like calling a recipe 'thermodynamic modeling.'
It relies heavily on a 'best-anchored' strategy, which means if the initial 'best' guess is garbage, the AI spends the rest of the paper just refining different flavors of hot trash.
The framework adds a massive layer of computational overhead for 'interpretability' that most engineers would trade for a 5% speed increase any day of the week.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you’re a startup founder pitching a 'revolutionary' kitchen. Instead of hiring a chef who knows how to cook, you hire a guy who just reads Yelp reviews of Beef Wellington to a microwave. Our system, TPD-AHD, takes two mediocre burritos, writes a 500-word critique on why one is slightly less depressing than the other, and then tells the microwave to 'optimize the flavor profile' based on those adjectives. It’s not actually learning chemistry; it’s just getting bullied into better results by its own internal monologue. Disruptive? Yes. Scalable? Maybe. Does it actually know what salt is? Absolutely not.

title: Binning of Linear Models for Time Series Forecasting
abstract: Linear models have been gaining attention in the time series forecasting (TSF) field, as several variations of modified linear layers have shown excellent performance across benchmarked datasets. This work extends these efforts by exploring the application of the binning technique to linear models. The conceptual rationale is based on the idea that binning can serve as a simple means of efficient learning through isolating temporal patterns. Instead of relying on temporally adjacent observations, binning adopts another perspective by exposing binned linear layers to periodically grouped data, treating temporal neighbors independently. Both conceptual analysis and empirical experimentation are conducted, with the results demonstrating that this modification can lead to improvements in prediction error. This work positions binning as a simple and effective technique, contributing to the exploration of representations structured by periodicity.
primary_area: learning on time series and dynamical systems

roast.laymen_summary: The authors propose chopping time-series data into periodic buckets—like grouping every Monday together—and running simple math on those piles instead of looking at the data as a continuous flow. They argue that ignoring what happened five minutes ago in favor of what happened last week makes the model smarter, but the reviewers weren't buying the 'less is more' pitch.
why_bad: It breaks the 'time' in time series by treating immediate neighbors as strangers, effectively discarding local trend information.
The 'novel' technique is essentially a rebranded version of seasonal decomposition, a concept that's been standard in statistics since the disco era.
The model’s success is entirely dependent on picking the perfect 'bin' size, making it a brittle hyperparameter nightmare rather than a robust solution.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition!' Our chef today tried to reinvent the tasting menu. Instead of serving a logical sequence of appetizers, mains, and desserts, they decided to 'bin' the ingredients by the day of the week they were purchased. You want a bite of that steak? Too bad, it's 'Vegetable Bin' time, so you're getting a mouthful of raw garnish while the meat sits in a different bucket across the kitchen. The chef argues this is 'efficient learning' because it isolates flavor patterns, but the judges are pointing out that a meal—like a time series—only works if the flavors actually flow together. You can't ignore the fact that you're frying an egg just because the 'periodic bin' says it's time to chop onions. It’s not a revolution; it’s just a disorganized pantry.

title: Rethinking Graph Attention Networks: A New Robust Approach
abstract:
Graph Attention Networks (GAT) have achieved remarkable success for representation learning with graphs. However, their performance is significantly degraded due to the oversmoothing in which deep GAT leads to homogenized node representations.
In this paper, we introduce a new quantitative measure of oversmoothing based on Mahalanobis distance. 
This measure provides a more robust assessment than conventional Euclidean metrics.
Based on this insight, we propose the Mahalanobis Graph Attention Network (MGAT) to alleviate the oversmoothing issue. MGAT adds a Mahalanobis regularizer to reduce representation collapse and preserves inter-class separability.
Extensive experiments on common benchmark datasets demonstrate the efficiency and superiority of our proposed model compared to the base GATs.
keywords: graph neural networks;graph representation learning;over- smoothing;graph attention network
primary_area: learning on graphs and other geometries & topologies

roast.laymen_summary: In the world of AI, 'Graph Attention Networks' often get so confused by too much data that they decide everyone in a social network looks exactly the same. The authors tried to fix this 'identity crisis' by using a fancier math ruler called Mahalanobis distance to force the AI to keep people's unique traits separate.
why_bad: Oversmoothing is the 'I’m not like other girls' of graph research—everybody claims to fix it, but swapping a standard ruler for a Mahalanobis one is a tiny 'delta' that doesn't justify a full paper.

The 'extensive experiments' likely rely on the 'Holy Trinity' of stale datasets (Cora, Citeseer, Pubmed) which are basically the 'Hello World' of 2012; beating them now is like bragging you can run faster than a sedentary turtle.

The paper fails the 'So What?' test: it introduces a complex regularizer to solve a problem that more modern architectures have already bypassed with much simpler tricks like skip-connections or just... fewer layers.
roast.analogy_name: The Beige Apocalypse: The Ruler of Doom
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where every social media user is slowly merging into a single, characterless, beige blob... one algorithm stands alone. Witness the horror of THE OVERSMOOTHING, where your data loses its soul and everyone becomes their neighbor. But one hero has a secret weapon: THE MAHALANOBIS REGULARIZER. He’s got a slightly more expensive calculator and a desperate need for a PhD. This summer, watch as he fights to keep the 'cool kids' and the 'nerds' in separate clusters using 1930s statistics. Rated R for 'Redundant Math' and 'Reviewer 2's Wrath.'
roast.scientific_verdict: REJECTED: The academic equivalent of putting a spoiler on a 1998 Honda Civic and calling it a 'New Robust Supercar.'

title: Motion Score Matching in Video Generation
abstract:
Subtle motions in videos are critical for overall realism for video generation but are overlooked by current score distillation methods like distribution matching distillation. Current distillation methods prefer to match the style first, %because that will
since it takes up most of the numerical significance. Such a distillation scheme will only create poorly generated motions, severely degrading the overall realism after distillation. 
To address this, we propose motion score and enforce the matching of motion distribution in distillation. 
We show that matching motion distribution is vital for the quality of generated videos.
primary_area: generative models

roast.laymen_summary: Current AI video tools are like photographers who can take a stunning still but have no idea how to film a basic walk cycle without everyone looking like they're melting. This paper tried to force the AI to prioritize 'the wiggle' over 'the paint job,' but it turns out that making things move naturally is way harder than just telling the computer to 'look at the movement more.'
why_bad: The 'Motion Score' is essentially just optical flow in a trench coat, offering very little theoretical novelty over existing methods.
The authors claim style 'takes up the numerical significance,' which is fancy academic speak for 'our loss function got distracted by shiny objects and we don't know why.'
The experimental results look like a 2005 screensaver; it fixes the jitter but introduces a level of blurring that makes the video look like it was filmed through a bowl of soup.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're pitching a revolutionary new 'Athlete Simulator.' You spent the entire seed round making sure the jersey textures have 8K resolution and realistic sweat beads, but when the player tries to run, their legs clip through their chest. This paper is your 'Series B' pivot where you tell investors you've invented a 'Kinetics Priority Protocol.' In reality, you're just screaming 'MOVE BETTER' at a mannequin while it falls over in high definition. It’s a classic 'lipstick on a pig' situation, except the pig is vibrating uncontrollably and the lipstick costs $50,000 in GPU credits to apply.

title: Overconfidence in LLM-as-a-Judge: Diagnosis and Confidence-Driven Solution
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are widely used as automated judges, where practical value depends on both accuracy and trustworthy, risk-aware judgments. Existing approaches predominantly focus on accuracy, overlooking the necessity of well-calibrated confidence, which is vital for adaptive and reliable evaluation pipelines. In this work, we advocate a shift from accuracy-centric evaluation to confidence-driven, risk-aware LLM-as-a-Judge systems, emphasizing the necessity of well-calibrated confidence for trustworthy and adaptive evaluation. We systematically identify the **Overconfidence Phenomenon** in current LLM-as-a-Judges, where predicted confidence significantly overstates actual correctness, undermining reliability in practical deployment. To quantify this phenomenon, we introduce **TH-Score**, a novel metric measuring confidence-accuracy alignment. Furthermore, we propose **LLM-as-a-Fuser**, an ensemble framework that transforms LLMs into reliable, risk-aware evaluators. Extensive experiments demonstrate that our approach substantially improves calibration and enables adaptive, confidence-driven evaluation pipelines, achieving superior reliability and accuracy compared to existing baselines.
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities

roast.laymen_summary: AI models are currently acting like arrogant judges who claim to be 100% certain even when they are completely wrong. This paper tries to fix their ego by inventing a 'humility score' and forcing a group of these cocky models to talk to each other before making a decision.
why_bad: The 'TH-Score' is essentially just Expected Calibration Error (ECE) with a marketing rebrand and no actual mathematical novelty.
The 'LLM-as-a-Fuser' is a fancy name for basic ensembling, a technique older than the authors' GPU cluster.
The paper fails to prove that adding more LLMs to the 'fusion' actually solves the underlying bias instead of just averaging out the hallucinations.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' You have a line cook who serves a raw steak but looks you dead in the eye and swears it's a Michelin-star souffle. Instead of teaching the cook how to use a thermometer, the authors suggest bringing in three more delusional cooks to vote on whether the meat is pink. They call this 'Ensemble Gastronomy,' but at the end of the day, you're still paying $50 for a plate of salmonella and a very confident smile.

title: triCAM: A Real Monocular Multi-Modal Event-based Pedestrian Dataset
abstract: Event-based visions offer key advantages, such as low latency, high dynamic range, and microsecond temporal resolution. These strengths have motivated extensive research into their complementarity with other modalities, which led to the creation of several multi-modal event-based datasets. However, most of these datasets are designed for automotive or robotic domains, with limited attention to human-centered perception in everyday settings. In this paper, we introduce triCAM, a real-world monocular multi-modal event-based pedestrian dataset. triCAM integrates event streams, RGB images, depth images, IMU data, and pedestrian bounding box annotations. This dataset contains 20 sequences, each recorded in two different restaurants in both static and dynamic camera motions. By providing a rich dataset on pedestrian activities in socially interactive environments, triCAM contributes to the advancement of research in robust perception and human interaction understanding.
keywords: Multimodal learning;Multisensor data;Event camera;RGBD camera;Depth estimation;IMU
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers filmed people walking around two restaurants using a high-tech camera setup that captures motion at microsecond speeds alongside regular video. They hoped this 'triCAM' data would help AI understand human movement better, but it's essentially a very fancy, very small collection of home movies from a bistro.
why_bad: Size matters, and 20 sequences is a 'clip reel,' not a robust dataset for training modern AI models.
Data diversity is non-existent; filming in only two restaurants means the AI will be an expert on those specific floor plans and absolutely nothing else.
The technical novelty is low because duct-taping an event camera to an RGB-D sensor is a well-known weekend project, not a groundbreaking academic contribution.
roast.analogy_description: Okay, imagine I’m pitching you 'Uber for Waiter-Tracking,' but it only works if the waiter is in one of two specific Italian restaurants and moves at a very specific speed. We’ve got high-speed sensors to track exactly how fast a breadstick falls, but the moment a customer walks into a different lighting setup, the whole system has a mid-life crisis. It’s a hyper-niche solution looking for a problem, wrapped in a 'multi-modal' buzzword salad that tastes like a rejection letter.

title: Understanding Transformer Architecture through Continuous Dynamics: A Partial Differential Equation Perspective
abstract:
The Transformer architecture has revolutionized artificial intelligence, yet a principled theoretical
understanding of its internal mechanisms remains elusive. This paper introduces a novel analytical
framework that reconceptualizes the Transformer’s discrete, layered structure as a continuous spa-
tiotemporal dynamical system governed by a master Partial Differential Equation (PDE). Within this
paradigm, we map core architectural components to distinct mathematical operators: self-attention
as a non-local interaction, the feed-forward network as a local reaction, and, critically, residual con-
nections and layer normalization as indispensable stabilization mechanisms. We do not propose
a new model, but rather employ the PDE system as a theoretical probe to analyze the mathemat-
ical necessity of these components. By comparing a standard Transformer with a PDE simulator
that lacks explicit stabilizers, our experiments provide compelling empirical evidence for our cen-
tral thesis. We demonstrate that without residual connections, the system suffers from catastrophic
representational drift, while the absence of layer normalization leads to unstable, explosive train-
ing dynamics. Our findings reveal that these seemingly heuristic ”tricks” are, in fact, fundamental
mathematical stabilizers required to tame an otherwise powerful but inherently unstable continuous
system. This work offers a first-principles explanation for the Transformer’s design and establishes
a new paradigm for analyzing deep neural networks through the lens of continuous dynamics.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs
author: YUKUN ZHANG;Xueqing Zhou

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to rebrand the Transformer—the engine behind ChatGPT—as a complex physics equation to prove it isn't just a lucky pile of code. They concluded that the 'tricks' engineers use to keep AI stable are actually vital mathematical anchors that prevent the system from having a digital seizure.
why_bad: The central thesis—viewing neural networks as continuous flows—is a well-trodden path that has been explored extensively since 2017, making the 'novelty' claim a bit of a stretch.
The paper uses a mountain of high-level calculus to 'discover' why Residual Connections are necessary, which is the academic equivalent of using a GPS to find your own kitchen.
It offers no practical improvements or new architectures, merely providing a more expensive and slower way to simulate what we already have.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Over-Engineered Brake Pad
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, you stand before this court claiming that the Defendant—one 'Residual Connection'—is not merely a piece of metal, but a 'spatiotemporal stabilizing operator' essential to the laws of motion. The prosecution argues that we already know the car crashes without brakes; we don't need a 50-page dissertation on fluid dynamics to justify their existence. You’ve brought in a high-priced 'PDE Simulator' as an expert witness, yet it fails to explain how this helps us drive any faster or more efficiently. This court finds your 'theoretical probe' to be a redundant display of mathematical vanity. Motion to dismiss for lack of utility; the jury already knows how to use a seatbelt without reading the physics of inertia.

title: Signal Preserving Weight Initialization for Odd-Sigmoid Activations
abstract:
Activation functions critically influence trainability and expressivity, and recent work has therefore explored a broad range of nonlinearities.
However, widely used Gaussian i.i.d. initializations are designed to preserve activation variance under wide or infinite width assumptions.
In deep and relatively narrow networks with sigmoidal nonlinearities, these schemes often drive preactivations into saturation, and collapse gradients.  To address this, we introduce an odd-sigmoid activations and propose an activation aware initialization tailored to any function in this class. Our method remains robust over a wide band of variance scales, preserving both forward signal variance and backpropagated gradient norms even in very deep and narrow networks. Empirically, across standard image benchmarks we find that the proposed initialization is substantially less sensitive to depth, width, and activation scale than Gaussian initializations. In physics informed neural networks (PINNs), scaled odd-sigmoid activations combined with our initialization achieve lower losses than Gaussian based setups, suggesting that diagonal-plus-noise weights provide a practical alternative when Gaussian initialization breaks down.
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: Neural networks usually fail because their starting internal settings are so messy they 'clog' the system before learning even begins. This paper proposes a niche math tweak to unblock the pipes, specifically for 'skinny' AI models that most researchers stopped using years ago.
why_bad: Rebranding well-known hyperbolic functions as 'odd-sigmoids' is a linguistic paint job that doesn't count as a theoretical breakthrough.
The focus on 'deep and narrow' networks is solving a problem for an architecture that has been largely extinct since the rise of Transformers and ResNets.
The 'diagonal-plus-noise' trick is a hyper-specific patch for PINNs that fails to prove it has any utility in the broader, real-world AI landscape.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD where your AI is thinner than a runway model on a juice cleanse... one wrong move will paralyze the brain. (Deep bass drop). If the starting numbers are too big, the neurons scream and shut down. If they’re too small, they whisper into the void. This paper introduces 'The Odd-Sigmoid'—it’s like Tanh, but it’s wearing a fake mustache and demanding a higher salary. Watch as the researchers try to balance a signal on a staircase made of wet soap. It’s a high-stakes drama about fixing a plumbing problem in a house that nobody actually lives in anymore. Rated R for Redundant Math.

title: SelfMask: Cross-modal Self-Masking for Multimodal Representation Learning in Missing Modality Scenarios
abstract: Multimodal learning promises to harness complementary information across diverse modalities, yet real-world deployments often face missing modalities due to acquisition costs, privacy constraints, or data corruption, leading to substantial performance degradation. We present \ours, a framework for learning robust representations in the presence of incomplete multimodal data. During training, \ours\ imputes missing modality representations through a masked representation learning scheme with adaptive masking, where informative masks are learned from data rather than sampled at random. To guide the imputation without relying on unavailable ground-truth for missing modalities, we introduce a cross-modal consistency loss: predicted representations of missing modalities are required not only to align with semantic content but also to support the reconstruction of observed ones. This consistency-based objective encourages robust, semantically grounded representations. Experiments on MIMIC-IV and CMU-MOSEI demonstrate that \ours\ consistently improves resilience and predictive accuracy under diverse missing-modality scenarios. Ablation studies further show that our learned masks outperform conventional random masking, yielding more reliable cross-modal representations. Our framework is broadly applicable across multimodal domains, offering a practical solution for real-world settings where incomplete modalities are the norm.
keywords: Multimodal learning;Missing modality;Self-supervised learning;Representation-level imputation;Cross-modal masking
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning
author: Jungwon Choi;Juho Lee

roast.laymen_summary: This paper attempts to build an AI that doesn't panic when half its data goes missing, like a self-driving car that loses its cameras in a fog and tries to 'hallucinate' the road based only on GPS and sound. It suggests that instead of guessing blindly, the AI should strategically hide parts of its own data during training to get better at filling in the blanks.
why_bad: The 'adaptive masking' strategy is basically the AI choosing its own homework assignments; it likely learned to hide the easy stuff to make its performance metrics look artificially inflated.

The 'cross-modal consistency loss' is essentially a fancy way of saying 'don't let the fake data contradict the real data,' which is a recycled concept that lacks the 'wow factor' needed for top-tier venues.

The authors benchmarked against 'random masking,' which is the academic equivalent of bragging that you're faster than a toddler; it doesn't prove the method is better than actual state-of-the-art competitors.
roast.analogy_description: [Deep, gravelly voiceover] IN A WORLD... where your Netflix connection is buffering and the audio is out of sync... one algorithm dares to guess the plot. SEE! An AI that watches a romantic comedy with the screen turned off and tries to reconstruct the lead actress’s face just by the sound of her eating popcorn. FEEL! The tension as the model tries to make its 'hallucinated' video match the audio, even though it’s basically just making stuff up as it goes. CRITICS CALL IT: 'A bold attempt to gaslight the researchers into thinking the missing data was never important anyway.' Coming this fall: SELF-MASK. It doesn't need to see the truth... it creates its own.

title: CoLaP: Contrastive Learning with Adaptive Prompts for Continual Learning
abstract: Continual learning (CL) aims to enable models to learn a sequence of new tasks without forgetting previously acquired knowledge. Prompt-based approaches, which adapt small prompt parameters while keeping a large pre-trained backbone frozen, have become a popular strategy to reduce forgetting. However, most existing methods rely solely on visual encoders to effectively guide prompt selection, which leaves them vulnerable to distribution shifts, because biased visual representations can misidentify prompts and lead to severe forgetting. We propose CoLaP, a language-guided prompt selection framework that leverages multimodal models to address this limitation. During training, each input is converted into a rich textual description that provides semantic guidance for training the visual prompt selector. The prompt pool is constructed from clustered concepts that are unique to each dataset, reflecting its specific distribution. In inference, the learned visual selector operates purely on images, preserving efficiency while maintaining the balance between plasticity and stability. Extensive experiments on both in-distribution and out-of-distribution benchmarks show that purely visual prompt methods degrade as the number of tasks grows, whereas our language-informed approach achieves superior generalization and robustness. These results highlight the promise of multimodal semantic guidance for scalable and resilient continual learning.
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors are trying to stop AI models from having a 'senior moment' every time they learn a new task by using text descriptions as a GPS for their memory. Instead of the AI just looking at an image and getting confused, it uses language hints to make sure it pulls the right 'knowledge file' out of its digital filing cabinet.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially just slapping a dictionary onto an existing method, which is the academic equivalent of putting a new hat on a Malibu Stacy doll.

The training pipeline is a convoluted mess that requires generating textual descriptions for every single image, making it about as computationally efficient as heating a house by burning piles of cash.
Despite all the fancy multimodal 'guidance,' the performance gains are so marginal they could be mistaken for statistical noise or a very lucky Sunday.
roast.analogy_name: The Gordon Ramsay Post-It Note Nightmare
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' You’re a chef who has to learn a new recipe every hour, but every time you learn Beef Wellington, you forget how to boil an egg. The authors' brilliant solution isn't to get a better brain; it’s to hire a narrator to scream 'THIS IS A CARROT' into your ear while you cook. They’ve covered every spice jar in 500-word essays explaining the 'semantic essence of cumin' just so you don't accidentally put sawdust in the soup. Sure, you might pass the taste test today, but your kitchen is now a hoarding situation of sticky notes, and you’re still one power outage away from forgetting what a fork is. It’s a lot of reading for a dish that still tastes like 'Mediocre Research Paper.'

title: Bonsai Networks: Structured Pruning and Sparse Training of Foundation Models
abstract: The recent trend of scaling neural networks to unprecedented sizes demands efficient structured sparsity for practical deployment, yet precise control of sparsity levels and patterns for hardware acceleration remains challenging. This paper introduces the Adaptive Soft-Thresholding Algorithm (ASTRA), which achieves a target sparsity by adapting group-wise regularization strength based on computationally inexpensive sparsity characterizations. We establish ASTRA’s theoretical foundations, proving the existence of stable regularizations that realize the desired sparsity. We demonstrate sublinear and linear convergence rates for both the model parameters and the regularization weight in deterministic settings and, crucially, an almost sure $O(1/t)$ convergence rate in the practical stochastic-gradient setting. ASTRA provides a theoretically grounded method for direct, precise control over structured sparsity, enabling the pruning and fine-tuning of foundation models into Bonsai Networks: accelerator-friendly miniatures trained to match the teacher’s outputs while preserving downstream performance.
primary_area: optimization

roast.laymen_summary: The authors wanted to shrink massive AI models into 'Bonsai' versions by using a math trick to selectively delete the digital equivalent of 'junk fat.' They claim their new algorithm, ASTRA, can trim these models so precisely that they stay smart while finally being small enough to run on regular hardware.
why_bad: The 'novel' ASTRA algorithm is essentially a 2015 math paper wearing a fake mustache and a trench coat; the reviewers weren't fooled by the rebranding of well-known proximal gradient methods.
While the authors brag about 'precise control' in theory, the stochastic convergence they're so proud of is likely to jitter like a caffeine-addicted squirrel when applied to actual large-scale data.
The 'accelerator-friendly' claim is a massive stretch, as structured sparsity often looks great on paper but runs like a tractor on modern GPUs that aren't built for this specific flavor of pruning.
roast.analogy_name: The Extreme Topiary Olympics: Chainsaw Edition
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, we are live at the AI Garden of Eden where the ASTRA team is attempting the 'Bonsai' maneuver on a 175-billion-parameter sequoia! They’re stepping up with a high-tech laser level, promising a perfectly manicured miniature, but look at the replay! Every time they snip a leaf, the whole tree shudders. They’re claiming 'linear convergence' to the crowd, but the judges in the booth see the truth: they’re essentially trying to perform open-heart surgery with a hedge trimmer while riding a unicycle. It's a bold strategy, Cotton, but the hardware is already smoking and the 'miniature' looks more like a pile of expensive mulch than a masterpiece. Back to you in the booth!

title: PIKA: Expert-Level Synthetic Datasets for Post-Training Alignment from Scratch
abstract:
Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF) has become a cornerstone for aligning large language models (LLMs). However, its effectiveness critically depends on high-quality instruction data. Most existing high-quality alignment datasets are either private or require costly human annotation, which hinders reproducibility and scalability. Even with the emergence of Reinforcement Learning from AI Feedback (RLAIF), concerns about data quality remain. Moreover, it is still unclear to the open-source community how much data is actually required to fine-tune a base model into a strong instruction-following model. 
Current state-of-the-art approaches typically rely on over 300k examples even at the supervised fine-tuning (SFT) stage, yet they still underperform compared to proprietary models, leaving substantial barriers for academic and resource-constrained communities.
To address this gap, we introduce PiKa, a data-efficient family of expert-level alignment datasets. 
In particular, the PiKa-SFT dataset uses only 30k SFT examples—an order of magnitude fewer than the SoTA dataset Magpie.
Through extensive evaluations by fine-tuning Llama-3-8B-Base on PiKa and other public instruction following datasets,
we show that PiKa-SFT alone outperforms models trained on much larger datasets. Remarkably, on two widely used alignment benchmarks, AlpacaEval 2.0 and Arena-Hard, PiKa-SFT fine-tuning surpasses the official Llama-3-8B-Instruct model -- which was trained on over 10M proprietary examples. We further extend our study by training the Qwen2.5 series (0.5B–7B) on PiKa-SFT, consistently outperforming their official instruction-tuned counterparts.  In addition, we curate 30k high-quality preference optimization examples, which further improve alignment performance when applied after SFT initialization. These findings demonstrate that high-quality alignment can be achieved with significantly reduced data, providing a practical and scalable path for advancing open-source LLM alignment research. Our code and data will be available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/PiKa.
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers claim they can turn a basic AI into a genius by feeding it 30,000 'perfect' AI-generated examples instead of the millions of human-made ones used by big tech companies. They argue that quality beats quantity so thoroughly that their tiny DIY model actually outscores the multi-billion dollar heavyweights from Meta and Alibaba.
why_bad: The 'vibe-based' benchmarks used (AlpacaEval 2.0) are notorious for being easily gamed by models that mimic the specific writing style of the AI judge rather than actually being smarter.

There is a massive risk of 'data contamination,' where the synthetic training data might have accidentally included the answers to the tests, making the model look like a genius when it's really just a student with a cheat sheet.

The paper fails to prove the model is actually robust; while it might ace a few standardized tests, it likely lacks the depth, safety guardrails, and 'common sense' that only come from the massive, diverse datasets used by the official models.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition!' Our chefs today claim they’ve revolutionized fine dining by replacing a 48-hour slow-braised ragu with a 3-minute Cup O' Noodles. They’ve added a tiny sprig of parsley and served it on a heated gold plate, and because the food critic (the benchmark) is a sucker for presentation and salt, they gave it a 10/10. But let’s be real: they haven't discovered a 'data-efficient' secret; they’ve just figured out exactly how to please one specific judge's palate while the rest of us are still going to get food poisoning the moment we ask for a dish that isn't on their 30,000-item 'cheat sheet' menu. It’s not expert-level cooking—it’s just very expensive garnish on a very small snack.

title: Learning More by Seeing Less: Structure-First Learning for Efficient, Transferable, and Human-Aligned Vision
abstract: Despite remarkable progress in computer vision, modern recognition systems remain fundamentally limited by their dependence on rich, redundant visual inputs. In contrast, humans can effortlessly understand sparse, minimal representations like line drawings, suggesting that structure, rather than appearance, underlies efficient visual understanding. In this work, we propose a novel structure-first learning paradigm that uses line drawings as an initial training modality to induce more compact and generalizable visual representations. We demonstrate that models trained with this approach develop a stronger shape bias, more focused attention, and greater data efficiency across classification, detection, and segmentation tasks. Notably, these models also exhibit lower intrinsic dimensionality, requiring significantly fewer principal components to capture representational variance, which mirrors observations of low-dimensional, efficient representations in the human brain. Beyond performance improvements, structure-first learning produces more compressible representations, enabling better distillation into lightweight student models. Students distilled from teachers trained on line drawings consistently outperform those trained from color-supervised teachers, highlighting the benefits of structurally compact knowledge. Together, our results support the view that structure-first visual learning fosters efficiency, generalization, and human-aligned inductive biases, offering a simple yet powerful strategy for building more robust and adaptable vision systems.
keywords: Structure-first learning;Line drawings;Data efficiency;Generalization;Human-aligned vision;Shape bias;Sparse representations;Knowledge distillation
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to make AI smarter by feeding it minimalist line drawings instead of real photos, arguing that 'less is more' for a computer's brain. They basically wanted to turn the AI into a minimalist artist, but the reviewers pointed out that real-world problems aren't just a giant coloring book.
why_bad: The 'efficiency' gains are mostly creative accounting; the authors ignored the massive computational cost of converting the entire dataset into line drawings in the first place.
Claiming the model is 'human-aligned' just because it likes shapes is a massive stretch—it's like saying a toaster is human-aligned because it also enjoys bread.
The experimental results were marginal at best, showing only tiny improvements that likely wouldn't survive a different random seed or a slightly more difficult dataset.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where AI models are gluttons for data, one team of researchers dares to ask: What if we trained a world-class surgeon... using only IKEA instruction manuals? [Inception Horn BWAHHH] They’ll master the structure! They’ll understand the minimalist essence of the human form! But when the patient is on the table and the textures get messy, turns out knowing where the 'Allen Key' goes doesn't help when you can't tell a spleen from a Swedish meatball. Coming this summer: 'The Skeleton Crew.' They see the bones, but they can’t find the meat.

title: Prototypical Knowledge Transfer for Multi-Scenario Recommendation with Optimal Transport
abstract: Modern APPs often need to provide personalized recommendations across various scenarios, such as the homepage, local page, and live stream on TikTok. User behaviors in these scenarios differ, resulting in diverse data distributions. To effectively handle varying distributions and enhance performance, current Multi-Scenario Recommendation (MSR) methods usually employ parameter sharing for knowledge transfer across scenarios. However, when there are significant differences in scenario distributions, direct parameter sharing fails to achieve effective knowledge transfer and feature alignment. In this paper, we propose a Prototypical Knowledge Transfer (PKT) method with Optimal Transport for Multi-Scenario Recommendation. Specifically, PKT first employs the Multi-gate Mixture of Experts (MMoE) as a shared feature extractor across all scenarios. We then introduce a prototype layer as an intermediary distribution to serve as a bridge for knowledge transfer between different scenario distributions. Furthermore, we leverage Optimal Transport to facilitate efficient knowledge transfer from scenario distributions to prototypes. To extract scenario-specific features, each scenario is equipped with its own expert network and utilizes the LHUC architecture to enhance scenario-specific features. We conducted offline experiments on two datasets and deployed the method on a video platform, followed by online A/B testing.
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning
author: Xiaoshuang Chen;Yan Wang;Yong Jiang

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a universal recommendation engine that understands you're a different person on TikTok Live than you are on your homepage by using a mathematical 'middleman' to translate your behavior. Unfortunately, they just stapled together several old AI techniques and hoped the extra complexity would distract everyone from the lack of actual innovation.
why_bad: The 'novelty' is essentially a mashup of existing architectures (MMoE and LHUC) with a decorative mathematical layer.
Using Optimal Transport to align distributions is high-brow overkill that adds massive computational cost for negligible accuracy gains.
The experimental results fail to prove that the 'prototype layer' actually does anything that a standard shared hidden layer wouldn't do.
roast.analogy_description: Folks, we are witnessing a coaching disaster. The team spent the entire off-season building an 'Optimal Hydration Transport' system where players have to scan their retinas and consult a 'Prototype Thirst Ambassador' just to get a sip of water. It’s a gorgeous, multi-million dollar setup, but the clock is ticking and the players are still thirsty because the system is too busy calculating the molecular distribution of electrolytes to actually hand out the cups. They’ve brought in 'Mixture of Experts'—which is just five guys holding one clipboard—and the fans are booing because, at the end of the day, a basic water cooler would’ve won the game. High-tech sideline theater that ends in a delay-of-game penalty!

title: Taming Text-to-Sounding Video Generation via Advanced Modality Condition and Interaction
abstract:
This study focuses on a challenging yet promising task, Text-to-Sounding-Video (T2SV) generation, which aims to generate a video with synchronized audio from text conditions, meanwhile ensuring both modalities are aligned with text. 
Despite progress in joint audio-video training, two critical challenges still remain unaddressed: (1) a single, shared text caption where the text for video is equal to the text for audio $(T_V = T_A)$ often creates modal interference, confusing the pretrained backbones, and (2) the optimal mechanism for cross-modal feature interaction remains unclear.
To address these challenges, we first propose the Hierarchical Visual-Grounded Captioning (HVGC) framework that generates pairs of disentangled captions, a video caption ($T_V$), and an audio caption ($T_A$), eliminating interference at the conditioning stage.
Based on HVGC, we further introduce BridgeDiT, a novel dual-tower diffusion transformer, which employs a Dual CrossAttention (DCA) mechanism that acts as a robust ``bridge" to enable a symmetric, bidirectional exchange of information, achieving both semantic and temporal synchronization.
Extensive experiments on three benchmark datasets, supported by human evaluations, demonstrate that our method achieves state-of-the-art results on most metrics. Comprehensive ablation studies further validate the effectiveness of our contributions, offering key insights for the future T2SV task. All the codes and checkpoints will be publicly released.
primary_area: generative models

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to stop AI from getting confused when making videos with sound by giving it two separate instruction manuals—one for the eyes and one for the ears. They built a fancy 'bridge' between the two systems to make sure the bangs and flashes actually happen at the same time.
why_bad: The 'HVGC' framework is basically just fancy prompt engineering that asks an LLM to do the heavy lifting, which isn't the architectural breakthrough they claim it is.
The Dual-Tower BridgeDiT architecture is a computational nightmare that likely trades massive amounts of VRAM for marginal gains in 'synchronization'.
Claiming SOTA (State-of-the-Art) results while ignoring comparison against heavy-hitting industrial models makes the evaluation feel like winning a race where the fastest runners weren't invited.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Dysfunctional Orchestra
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense claims they’ve revolutionized cinema. They found that giving a single, vague script to both a blind cameraman and a deaf pianist resulted in... well, a mess. Their 'brilliant' solution? Hire a middleman to rewrite the script into two slightly different scripts and build a multi-million dollar glass bridge between the two performers so they can poke each other while working. The prosecution argues that this isn't innovation; it's just overcomplicating a job that a single competent director could do. You've built a bridge, yes, but the bridge costs more than the movie, and the pianist is still playing 'Mary Had a Little Lamb' while the camera films a Michael Bay explosion!

title: Chain-of-Thought Hijacking
abstract:
Reasoning models are widely used to improve task performance by allocating
more inference-time compute, and prior work suggest it may also strengthen
safety by improving refusal. Yet we find the opposite: the same reasoning can
be used to bypass safety. We introduce Chain-of-Thought Hijacking, a jailbreak
attack on reasoning models. The attack pads harmful requests with long sequences
of harmless reasoning. Across HarmBench, CoT Hijacking reaches a 99% attack
success rate (ASR) on Gemini 2.5 Pro, far exceeding prior jailbreak methods. Our
mechanistic analysis shows that mid layers encode the strength of safety checking,
while late layers encode the verification outcome. Long benign CoT dilutes both
signals by shifting attention away from harmful tokens. Targeted ablations of at-
tention heads identified by this analysis causally increased ASR, confirming their
role in a safety subnetwork. These results show that the most interpretable form
of reasoning—explicit CoT—can itself become a jailbreak vector when combined
with final-answer cues. We release prompts, outputs, and judge decisions to facil-
itate replication.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations
author: Jianli Zhao;Tingchen Fu;Rylan Schaeffer;Mrinank Sharma;Fazl Barez
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of array: line 6 column 160 (char 819)

title: PULSE: Projection-based Unlearning via Linear Speedy Entropy Maximization
abstract: Machine unlearning enables selective erasure of knowledge associated with specific data points from trained models without retraining from scratch. However, existing unlearning approaches face significant limitations: they typically degrade performance on remaining data, require access to the original retain dataset to maintain the model utility, and incur high computational costs. To address these challenges, we propose PULSE (Projection-based Unlearning via Linear Speedy Entropy Maximization), a novel retain-data-free unlearning method. PULSE jointly learns a projection matrix alongside the model backbone during training. During the unlearning phase, PULSE freezes the model backbone and trains a forget-specific projection matrix that maximizes confidence on the data to be forgotten. By subtracting this forget-specific matrix from the original projection, PULSE transforms confident predictions into targeted uncertainty, effectively achieving forgetting. Unlike existing methods that modify model outputs to enforce forgetting, PULSE operates directly on representation space. Extensive experiments on standard benchmarks show that PULSE achieves near-perfect forget accuracy while preserving retain accuracy and runs 10–20× faster while being memory efficient thus, establishing a new paradigm for efficient machine unlearning.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: PULSE tries to make AI forget specific data by adding a specialized 'filter' layer during training that can later be tuned to turn unwanted memories into pure confusion. It claims to 'delete' information 20 times faster than usual by simply subtracting a math matrix instead of actually retraining the brain.
why_bad: The 'Pre-registration' Trap: The method only works if you bake the projection layer into the model from the very first day of training, making it useless for the 99% of existing AI models people actually want to fix.

Subtraction is not Deletion: Subtracting entropy-maximized matrices from representation space is a mathematical 'trust me bro' that likely leaves 'shadow' traces of the data behind rather than genuinely erasing it.

The Utility-Privacy Paradox: By maximizing uncertainty to 'forget,' the model often becomes a stuttering mess on similar data points it was actually supposed to remember, proving that a lobotomy is indeed 'fast' but rarely 'effective'.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a star quarterback who accidentally memorized the opponent's illegal playbook. Instead of spending weeks retraining his brain, the coach just gives him a pair of high-tech earplugs that blast static the second he sees a forbidden play. Sure, the coach is shouting 'Look how fast he forgot!' from the sidelines while the QB stands frozen in a confused daze, but meanwhile, the defense is closing in and the actual game plan is falling apart. It's not 'unlearning' the play; it's just making sure the QB is too disoriented to execute it—or anything else nearby.

title: Hybrid Dual-Attention Functional Map Learning for Robust Shape Matching
abstract: Although deep functional map methods have significantly advanced the field of 3D shape matching, many existing approaches still rely on conventional network architectures for feature enhancement and use only the Laplace–Beltrami operator (LBO) to construct eigenbases for functional map computation. This often results in performance degradation when the learned features are insufficiently distinctive. To overcome these limitations, we propose an efficient unsupervised framework for deformable shape matching. Our method incorporates a feature extraction module with a dual-layer attention mechanism, a differentiable functional map solver, and an optimal transport (OT) post-processing step to produce accurate point-to-point correspondences. The attention mechanism learns discriminative and structurally invariant descriptors, significantly improving robustness under complex geometric deformations. Additionally, we introduce a hybrid matching strategy that integrates both Laplacian and elastic modal representations, optimized via Sinkhorn iterations to yield a transport matrix. This facilitates robust and accurate correspondence recovery. Extensive experiments across diverse challenging scenarios demonstrate that our approach outperforms state-of-the-art methods in matching accuracy. Our code is publicly available.
keywords: 3D shape matching，Deep functional maps，Attention mechanism，Optimal transport
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to teach computers how to recognize that two different 3D shapes are actually the same object in different poses. They attempted this by piling on every trendy AI buzzword—like 'attention' and 'optimal transport'—hoping the sheer complexity would fix their accuracy issues.
why_bad: Incremental Frankensteining: Shoving standard attention modules and Sinkhorn iterations into a pipeline is 'architecture surgery,' not a theoretical breakthrough.
Over-Engineering: Adding elastic modal representations to the standard Laplace-Beltrami operator adds significant computational bloat for marginal, 'noise-level' performance gains.
The 'SOTA' Mirage: Claiming superior accuracy while testing on aging, over-saturated datasets is like bragging about a world record you set in your own backyard.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Geometry Olympics, where our contestant is attempting to toast a single slice of bread. They aren't just using a toaster; they’ve rigged up a dual-laser tracking system and a NASA-grade thermal sensor to ensure 'robust browning.' It’s a spectacular display of technical gymnastics, folks, but look at the judges—they're unimpressed! The contestant is doing a triple-backflip just to push the lever down. They’ve added 'elastic springs' to the bread basket for no apparent reason, and while the toast is technically cooked, the kitchen is now on fire and the setup costs four million dollars. A classic case of solving a solved problem with ten times the necessary horsepower. Back to you, Jim.

title: Forgetting-MarI: LLM Unlearning via Marginal Information Regularization
abstract: As AI models are trained on ever-expanding datasets, the ability to remove the influence of specific data from trained models has become essential for privacy protection and regulatory compliance, such as the ''right to be forgotten." Unlearning addresses this challenge by selectively removing parametric knowledge from the trained models without retraining from scratch, which is critical for resource-intensive models such as Large Language Models (LLMs). Existing unlearning methods often degrade model performance by removing more information than necessary when attempting to ''forget" specific data. We introduce $\textit{Forgetting-MarI}$, an LLM unlearning framework that provably removes only the additional (marginal) information contributed by the data to be unlearned, while preserving the information supported by the data to be retained. By penalizing marginal information, our method yields an explicit upper bound on the unlearn dataset’s residual influence in the trained models, providing provable undetectability. Extensive experiments confirm that our approach outperforms current state-of-the-art unlearning methods, delivering reliable forgetting and better preserved general model performance across diverse benchmarks. This advancement represents an important step toward making AI systems more controllable and compliant with privacy and copyright regulations without compromising their effectiveness.
keywords: Machine Unlearning;LLM Unlearning;Data Privacy;Information-Theoretic Regularization;Mutual Information
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations
author: Shizhou Xu;Yuan Ni;Stefan Broecker;Thomas Strohmer

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to build a 'surgical amnesia' tool for AI that deletes specific private memories without making the model act like it’s had a lobotomy. They claim to have found a mathematical way to erase only the 'extra' info a dataset adds, leaving the AI's general knowledge perfectly intact and the deletion completely untraceable.
why_bad: The 'provable' mathematical guarantees rely on massive simplifications that fall apart the moment they meet a real-world, non-linear LLM.
The claim of 'undetectability' is a bit of a stretch, as it assumes we have a perfect way to measure what a model actually 'knows' deep in its weights.
The computational cost of calculating 'marginal information' for a 70B parameter model is likely a one-way ticket to burning down a server farm.
roast.analogy_name: The Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Neural Net
roast.analogy_description: In a world where your AI knows too much... one team of scientists attempts the ultimate heist: breaking into a billion-parameter brain to steal one specific memory! [Deep Voiceover] They call it 'Forgetting-MarI.' It’s like trying to remove the salt from a baked cake without ruining the frosting. Can they perform digital lobotomy with the precision of a laser, or is their 'provable' math just a fancy way of saying they’re guessing which neurons to kill? Coming this fall: One model, one deleted ex-girlfriend, and a lot of very angry Reviewers who aren't buying the 'undetectable' hype. It’s 'Inception' meets 'The IT Crowd'—only with more Greek letters and significantly less budget.

title: The Pump Scheduling Problem: A Real-World Scenario for Reinforcement Learning
abstract: Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) has demonstrated impressive results in domains such as games and robotics, where task formulations are well-defined. However, few DRL benchmarks are grounded in complex, real-world environments, where safety constraints, partial observability, and the need for hand-engineered task representations pose significant challenges. To help bridge this gap, we introduce a testbed based on the pump scheduling problem in a real-world water distribution facility. The task involves controlling pumps to ensure a reliable water supply while minimizing energy consumption and adhering to the system's constraints. Our testbed includes a realistic simulator, three years of high-resolution (1-minute) operational data from human-led control, and a baseline RL task formulation. This testbed supports a wide range of research directions, including offline RL, safe exploration, inverse RL, and multi-objective optimization.
keywords: Reinforcement Learning;Offline RL;Pump Scheduling;Water Distribution Networks
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks
author: Henrique Donancio;Laurent Vercouter;Harald Roclawski

roast.laymen_summary: We built a digital sandbox to see if an AI could manage city water pumps without causing a massive blackout or leaving everyone thirsty. Since we can't trust the robot with actual pipes yet, we’re offering our homework to the public as a 'benchmark' for others to fix.
why_bad: The 'realistic' simulator is a friction-less fantasy that fails to account for the chaotic physical degradation of actual 40-year-old infrastructure.
The baseline RL model manages to be significantly less efficient than a simple set of 'if-then' rules scribbled on a napkin by a human operator.
Providing a pile of old logbooks (offline data) doesn't solve the fact that the AI has no way to learn from its mistakes without potentially flooding a basement in the real world.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Municipal Hydro-Bowl, folks! The researchers are stepping onto the field with a rookie quarterback—a Reinforcement Learning agent—that's spent three years watching game film of a guy named Gary turning valves. The whistle blows! The AI is analyzing the 'multi-objective optimization'... and it’s decided to save three cents on electricity by letting the local reservoir hit zero! The crowd is parched, the 'high-resolution' data is just a replay of Gary's lunch breaks, and the 'realistic simulator' has conveniently ignored the fact that the stadium's pipes are bursting. It’s a bold strategy to call this a 'testbed' when the athlete is still terrified of actual water. Back to you, Jim!

title: Numerical Sensitivity and Robustness: Exploring the Flaws of Mathematical Reasoning in Large Language Models
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have made significant progress in the field of mathematical reasoning, but whether they have true the mathematical understanding ability is still controversial. To explore this issue, we propose a new perturbation framework to evaluate LLMs' reasoning ability in complex environments by injecting additional semantically irrelevant perturbation sentences and gradually increasing the perturbation intensity. At the same time, we use an additional perturbation method: core questioning instruction missing, to further analyze the LLMs' problem-solving mechanism. The experimental results show that LLMs perform stably when facing perturbation sentences without numbers, but there is also a robustness boundary. As the perturbation intensity increases, the performance exhibits varying degrees of decline; when facing perturbation sentences with numbers, the performance decreases more significantly, most open source models with smaller parameters decrease by nearly or even more than 10\%, and further increasing with the enhancement of perturbation intensity, with the maximum decrease reaching 51.55\%. Even the most advanced commercial LLMs have seen a 3\%-10\% performance drop. By analyzing the reasoning process of LLMs in detail, We find that models are more sensitive to perturbations with numerical information and are more likely to give incorrect answers when disturbed by irrelevant numerical information. The higher the perturbation intensity, the more obvious these defects are. At the same time, in the absence of core questioning instruction, models can still maintain an accuracy of 20\%-40\%, indicating that LLMs may rely on memory templates or pattern matching to complete the task, rather than logical reasoning. In general, our work reveals the shortcomings and limitations of current LLMs in their reasoning capabilities, which is of great significance for the further development of LLMs.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: This paper reveals that AI 'math geniuses' are actually just overconfident parrots who get confused if you mention a random number like '7' while they're working. It turns out these models don't understand logic; they've just memorized the textbook and start glitching the moment you add a little bit of irrelevant noise.
why_bad: The 'distraction' method is basically the academic version of 'poking it with a stick,' which lacks the technical sophistication needed for top-tier venues.
The discovery that LLMs are sensitive to numbers is old news in the AI world—it's like writing a paper to prove that dogs get distracted by squirrels.
Concluding that models rely on memory because they can answer without a question is a bit of a 'Captain Obvious' moment for auto-regressive transformers.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a startup where our 'proprietary algorithm' is just a guy named Chad who memorized a million receipts. Chad is great at taxes until you mention your grocery list has the number '5' on it; then he suddenly decides your tax return is $5 million because he's 'sensitive to numerical noise.' We’re disrupting the 'knowing things' space by replacing boring logic with pure, unadulterated pattern matching. If you don't even ask Chad a question, he’ll still give you an answer 40% of the time just based on the vibe of the room. It’s not a bug, it’s a feature—we call it 'Probabilistic Accounting as a Service.' Seed round starts now.

title: ExMAG: Learning of Maximally Ancestral Graphs
abstract:
In mixed graphs, there are both directed and undirected edges. An extension of acyclicity to this mixed-graph setting is known as maximally ancestral graphs. This extension is of considerable interest in causal learning in the presence of confounders. There, directed edges represent a clear direction of causality, while undirected edges represent confounding.

We propose a score-based branch-and-cut algorithm for learning maximally ancestral graphs. The algorithm produces more accurate results than state-of-the-art methods, while being faster to run on small and medium-sized synthetic instances.
primary_area: causal reasoning
author: Petr Ryšavý;Pavel Rytíř;Xiaoyu He;Georgios Korpas;Jakub Marecek

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to build a GPS for cause-and-effect that can figure out if one thing causes another or if there's a secret, hidden third factor messing with both. It’s a math-heavy way of untangling whether your coffee habit causes insomnia or if your high-stress job is actually causing both.
why_bad: The 'state-of-the-art' speed gains only happen on tiny, toy datasets; once you give it a real-world problem, the algorithm grinds to a halt like a budget laptop running Crysis.
