The paper leans entirely on synthetic data, which is essentially the researchers grading their own homework using questions they wrote themselves.
The branch-and-cut constraints are mathematically dense but don't actually offer a theoretical breakthrough—it's basically just a very expensive way of guessing and checking.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re at a party and see two people, Dave and Sarah, constantly moving in sync. Is Dave following Sarah, or is there a hidden DJ—the confounder—calling the shots? Our startup, ExMAG, uses a 'proprietary score-based' method to solve this mystery. We’re basically the Sherlock Holmes of social awkwardness, provided the party only has five people and everyone is standing perfectly still. We’re disrupting the 'knowing what actually happened' space by being slightly faster than our competitors at solving problems that are too simple to actually matter. It’s unscalable, impractical, and mathematically gorgeous—invest now!

title: LightReasoner: Can Small Language Models Teach Large Language Models Reasoning?
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable progress in reasoning, often through supervised fine-tuning (SFT). However, SFT is resource-intensive, relying on large curated datasets, rejection-sampled demonstrations, and uniform optimization across all tokens—even though only a fraction carry meaningful learning value. In this work, we explore a counterintuitive idea: can smaller language models (SLMs) teach larger language models (LLMs) by revealing high-value reasoning moments that reflect the latter's unique strength? We propose LightReasoner, a novel framework that leverages the behavioral divergence between a stronger expert model (LLM) and a weaker amateur model (SLM). LightReasoner operates in two stages: (1) a sampling stage that pinpoints critical reasoning moments and constructs supervision examples capturing the expert's advantage through expert–amateur contrast, and (2) a fine-tuning stage that aligns the expert model with these distilled examples, amplifying its reasoning strengths. Across seven mathematical benchmarks, LightReasoner improves accuracy by up to 28.1%, while reducing time consumption by 90%, sampled problems by 80%, and tuned token usage by 99%, all without relying on ground-truth labels. By turning weaker SLMs into effective teaching signals, LightReasoner offers a scalable and resource-efficient approach for advancing LLM reasoning. For reproducibility, the implementation is available at: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/LightReasoner.
keywords: Large Language Models;Post-training;LLM Reasoning Enhancement;Label-free Supervision
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs
author: Jingyuan Wang;Yankai Chen;Zhonghang Li;Chao Huang

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to train a genius AI by having it watch a dumber AI fail, focusing only on the moments where the smart one was clearly better. They claim this 'self-high-five' method makes the big model even smarter using 99% less data and zero actual answer keys.
why_bad: The 'Reasoning' Mirage: The paper mistakes a model's existing bias for new learning, essentially just rewarding the LLM for being itself rather than teaching it new logic.
Circular Logic: If the 'teacher' (SLM) is significantly dumber, the 'divergence' signal is often just noise, meaning the model is optimizing for 'least-stupid' rather than 'actually correct.'
Weak Baselines: The 28% improvement claim likely compares the method against an unoptimized strawman rather than modern, robust RLHF or DPO techniques.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Disasters: Efficiency Edition!' Today, instead of reading a cookbook, our Michelin-star chef is going to watch a toddler try to make toast. Every time the toddler puts the bread in the fish tank instead of the toaster, our chef writes down, 'Don't do that.' The paper argues that by focusing exclusively on NOT being a toddler, the chef becomes a culinary god. It's technically 90% faster than actually cooking, but you're mostly just congratulating yourself for knowing that bread shouldn't be wet. Great for the ego, terrible for the actual soup.

title: Schreier-Coset Graph Rewiring
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) provide a principled framework for learning on graph-structured data, yet their expressiveness is fundamentally limited by over-squashing-the exponential compression of information from distant nodes into fixed size vectors. While graph rewiring methods attempt to alleviate this issue by modifying topology, existing approaches can introduce prohibitive computational bottlenecks. We propose Schreier-Coset Graph Rewiring (SCGR), a group-theoretic rewiring method that augments the input graph with a Schreier-coset graph derived from a special linear group $\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbb{Z}_n)$. Unlike heuristic rewiring, SCGR provides  $\textit{provable}$ theoretical guarantees: the auxiliary graph exhibits a spectral gap and a bounded effective resistance, creating low-resistance bypasses for long-range communication. By coupling these two graphs with strength, we ensure that effective resistance between any node pair is bounded, directly mitigating over-squashing. Empirical evaluations demonstrate that SCGR reduces effective resistance by 15-40\% across benchmark datasets while maintaining competitive accuracy and lower computational overhead, making it practical for both large-scale and diverse applications.
primary_area: learning on graphs and other geometries & topologies

roast.laymen_summary: Graph Neural Networks suffer from a 'telephone game' problem where information gets lost when traveling across long distances. The authors tried to fix this by building a high-speed, mathematically-precise subway system on top of the graph using fancy group theory to make sure no message gets dropped.
why_bad: Mathematical Overkill: Using the Special Linear Group SL(2, Zn) to rewire a graph is like using a laser-guided scalpel to cut a loaf of bread—it's impressively complex, but nobody asked for this level of abstraction for a marginal gain.

Theoretical vs. Practical Gap: While the 'effective resistance' decreased on paper, the paper fails to prove that this specific group-theoretic structure actually maps to real-world data patterns rather than just being a pretty geometric coincidence.

The 'Black Box' Headache: By forcing the graph into a rigid algebraic structure, the authors likely sacrificed the model's ability to learn flexible, data-driven features, making it a nightmare to debug when the 'provable guarantees' don't translate to better predictions.
roast.analogy_name: The Intergalactic Hyper-Lanes for Your Backyard
roast.analogy_description: Listen, your current AI is like a small town where the mail takes three weeks to get from the suburbs to downtown because there are too many stop signs. Our startup, SCGR, doesn't just add a lane; we’re using 4th-dimensional non-Euclidean geometry to fold your backyard directly into the town square. Is it technically 'shorter'? Yes. Does it require every mailman to have a PhD in Abstract Algebra and a specialized spacecraft? Also yes. We’re disrupting the 'walking' industry by replacing it with a mathematically perfect wormhole that 40% of users didn't know they needed and 100% of users can't explain to their boss. Invest now before the laws of physics catch up with our Series A!

title: Classifier-Constrained Alternating Training: Mitigating Modality Imbalance in Multimodal Learning
abstract: Modality imbalance, driven by divergent convergence dynamics across modalities, critically limits multimodal model performance. Although alternating training methods mitigate encoder-level interference, they fail to prevent dominance of classifiers by faster-converging modalities, suppressing contributions from weaker ones. To address this core limitation, we propose Classifier-Constrained Alternating Training (CCAT). Our framework first pre-trains an unbiased cross-modal classifier using bidirectional cross-attention and a regularization term that constrains modality contribution differences. This classifier is then frozen as a stable decision anchor during subsequent training, preventing bias toward any modality. To preserve modality-specific features while leveraging this anchor, we integrate modality-specific Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules into the classifier. During alternating training, CCAT updates only the encoder of the active modality and its corresponding LoRA parameters. Furthermore, a sample-level imbalance detection mechanism quantifies contribution disparities, enabling targeted optimization of severely imbalanced samples to bolster weaker modalities. Extensive experiments across multiple benchmarks demonstrate CCAT’s consistent superiority: it achieves accuracy gains of +1.35% on CREMA-D, +6.76% on  Kinetic-Sound and +1.92% on MVSA over state-of-the-art methods, validating the framework’s efficacy in learning balanced, robust multimodal representations.
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities

roast.laymen_summary: When AI tries to learn from two sources at once—like watching a video and listening to the audio—it usually gets lazy and only listens to the 'easier' one while ignoring the other. This paper tries to fix that by pre-hiring a 'fair judge' who refuses to change their mind, then forcing each sense to take turns practicing in isolation.
why_bad: The performance 'gains' are embarrassingly marginal; bragging about a 1.35% improvement is like claiming you’re a professional athlete because you walked to the mailbox slightly faster today.
Freezing the classifier at the beginning is a high-stakes gamble that assumes your initial 'unbiased' judge is actually smart, rather than just equally confused by everything.
The added complexity of LoRA modules and sample-level detection creates a Rube Goldberg machine of training steps that likely costs more in compute time than the tiny accuracy boost is worth.
roast.analogy_name: The Helicopter Parent Referee: Modality Meltdown
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where 'Vision' is a overachieving prodigy and 'Audio' is a basement-dwelling slacker, one algorithm dares to force them into a group project. Witness: THE CCAT. Watch in awe as the 'Frozen Classifier'—a referee who made his decision before the game even started—forces the siblings to play in separate rooms so the prodigy doesn't hurt the slacker's feelings. It’s a high-octane thriller featuring 'Low-Rank Adaptation' coaches and 'Imbalance Detectors' that scream every time someone works too hard. Will they save the model? Or will they just spend 48 hours of GPU time to move the needle by one measly percent? Coming this fall to a Peer Review graveyard near you.

title: Rate-Distortion Optimization for Transformer Inference
abstract: Transformers achieve superior performance on many tasks, but impose heavy compute and memory requirements during inference. This inference can be made more efficient by partitioning the process across multiple devices, which, in turn, requires compressing its intermediate representations. In this work, we introduce a principled rate-distortion-based framework for lossy compression that learns compact encodings that explicitly trade off bitrate against accuracy. Experiments on language benchmarks show that the proposed codec achieves substantial savings with improved accuracy in some cases, outperforming more complex baseline methods. We characterize and analyze the rate-distortion performance of transformers, offering a unified lens for understanding performance in representation coding. This formulation extends information-theoretic concepts to define the gap between rate and entropy, and derive some of its bounds. We further develop probably approximately correct (PAC)-style bounds for estimating this gap. For different architectures and tasks, we empirically demonstrate that their rates are driven by these bounds, adding to the explainability of the formulation.
keywords: information theory;learning theory;lossy learnable compression;transformers;language models;usable information;neural networks
primary_area: other topics in machine learning (i.e., none of the above)

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to speed up giant AI models by splitting them across different computers and squishing the data sent between them like a zipped folder. They claim that by making the data slightly 'lossy' or lower quality, the AI actually works better, which is basically the scientific equivalent of saying a blurry photo helps you see better.
why_bad: The 'improved accuracy' claim is a massive red flag that likely stems from noise or lucky seeding rather than a revolutionary compression miracle.
The PAC-style bounds are mathematically 'vibe-heavy' but practically useless, serving more as academic window dressing than actual engineering guidance.
It's an over-engineered solution that introduces more latency through complex encoding than it saves by reducing data size, reinventing the wheel with a hexagonal shape.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Over-Engineered Eats'! Today, our contestant tried to serve a 12-course Thanksgiving dinner in a studio apartment with one burner. Their brilliant idea? Puree the entire turkey, dehydrate it into a tiny 'optimized' pill, and mail it to the neighbor’s house to be rehydrated. The chef claims the turkey actually tastes *juicier* after being pulverized and shipped, citing 'molecular moisture bounds.' The judges are currently staring at a dry pile of grey dust, wondering why the chef didn't just use a Ziploc bag and a microwave like a normal human being. It's technically impressive chemistry, but I'm still hungry and the kitchen is on fire.
roast.tone_check: Scientific snark level: Optimal. Peer review burns: Third-degree.

title: Graph Synthetic Out-of-Distribution Exposure with Large Language Models
abstract: Out-of-distribution (OOD) detection in graphs is critical for ensuring model robustness in open-world and safety-sensitive applications. Existing graph OOD detection approaches typically train an in-distribution (ID) classifier on ID data alone, then apply post-hoc scoring to detect OOD instances. While OOD exposure—adding auxiliary OOD samples during training—can improve detection, current graph-based methods often assume access to real OOD nodes, which is often impractical or costly. In this paper, we present GOE-LLM, a framework that leverages Large Language Models (LLMs) to achieve OOD exposure on text-attributed graphs without using any real OOD nodes. GOE-LLM introduces two pipelines: (1) identifying pseudo-OOD nodes from the initially unlabeled graph using zero-shot LLM annotations, and (2) generating semantically informative synthetic OOD nodes via LLM-prompted text generation. These pseudo-OOD nodes are then used to regularize ID classifier training and enhance OOD detection awareness. Empirical results on multiple benchmarks show that GOE-LLM substantially outperforms state-of-the-art methods without OOD exposure, achieving up to a 23.5% improvement in AUROC for OOD detection, and attains performance on par with those relying on real OOD labels for exposure.
keywords: Out-of-Distribution Detection;Graph Neural Networks;Large Language Models
primary_area: learning on graphs and other geometries & topologies

roast.laymen_summary: The authors realized that AI models struggle to identify 'weird' data they've never seen, so they used ChatGPT to hallucinate fake examples of weirdness to use as practice. Essentially, they’re trying to teach a guard dog to recognize a burglar by showing it a bunch of AI-generated pictures of imaginary monsters.
why_bad: The 'Circular Reasoning' Trap: The paper uses an LLM to label what is 'out-of-distribution' and then evaluates the model on those same LLM-defined standards, which is basically grading your own homework.

The Efficiency Nightmare: Using a massive, billion-parameter LLM just to generate training samples for a simple graph model is like using a space shuttle to commute to the grocery store—technically possible, but hilariously impractical.

Lack of Theoretical Grounding: The reviewers noted that 'prompting' an LLM to be 'informative' isn't a rigorous mathematical strategy for OOD exposure; it’s just vibes-based data augmentation that might not hold up in real-world edge cases.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' Our chef today wants to prepare a dish using rare, exotic truffles, but since truffles are expensive and hard to find, they asked a fancy AI food blogger to write a description of a truffle and then threw those printed descriptions into the soup. They’re claiming the soup now 'captures the essence' of the truffle, but the judges are just sitting here eating soggy paper. You can’t train a palate on synthetic flavors generated by someone who’s never actually tasted the ingredient. It’s a 20-dollar bowl of hot water and imagination, and the bill for the AI blogger is more expensive than the actual truffles would have been. Please, shut it down!

title: POPS: Recovering Unlearned Multi-Modality Knowledge in MLLMs with Fine-tuning and Prompt-based Attacks
abstract: Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have demonstrated impressive performance on cross-modal tasks by jointly training on large-scale textual and visual data, where privacy-sensitive examples could be intentionally or unintentionally encoded, raising concerns about privacy or copyright violation.  To this end, Multi-modality Machine Unlearning (MMU) was proposed as a mitigation that can effectively force MLLMs to forget private information. Yet, the robustness of such unlearning is not fully exploited when the model is published and accessible to malicious users. In this paper, we propose a novel adversarial strategy, namely Prompt-Optimized Parameter Shaking (POPS), aiming to retrieve the unlearned multi-modality knowledge via fine-tuning. Our method steers victim MLLMs to generate potential private examples by prompt optimization and then uses the synthesized examples to fine-tune the MLLMs to generate private information. Our experiments on the different MMU benchmarks reveal substantial weaknesses in the existing MMU algorithms. Our attacks achieve near-complete recovery of supposedly erased sensitive information, exposing fundamental vulnerabilities that challenge the very foundations of current multimodal privacy protection.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to prove that when AI 'unlearns' private data, it’s actually just hiding it under the rug rather than throwing it away. By poking the AI with specific prompts and giving it a quick 'refresher' training session, they managed to make the model cough up the very secrets it was supposed to have forgotten.
why_bad: The 'recovery' method is circular; if you need to generate 'potential private examples' to train the attack, you basically already need to know the secrets you're trying to steal.
The term 'Parameter Shaking' is high-octane marketing for basic fine-tuning, offering zero new theoretical insight into why the unlearning failed in the first place.
The experiments were conducted on weak 'unlearning' baselines that were already known to be fragile, making the 'near-complete recovery' claim feel like beating up a straw man.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'The Great Academic Bake-Off,' where today's contestant tried to un-bake a raisin cookie. They realized the raisins were 'private data' and used a 'Machine Unlearning Spatula' to flick them out. But then, our authors—acting as the chaotic sous-chefs—came in with a blowtorch and a magnifying glass. They 'shook' the cookie until the residual raisin juice seeped back to the surface and then claimed they 'recovered the raisin.' The judges are rolling their eyes because the chefs basically just brought their own raisins to the kitchen to prove the cookie still tasted like grapes. It’s not a breakthrough in food science; it’s just making a mess of a dessert that was already ruined.

title: Online Learning of Nonlinear Autoregressive Processes over Cellular Complexes
abstract: Real-world time-series are often high-dimensional, structured with higher-order dependencies, and exhibit time-varying dynamics, making them challenging to model effectively. Abstract Cellular Complexes (ACCs) provide a principled way to capture such higher-order topological structure, serving as a powerful inductive bias for multivariate time-series modeling. While recent methods based on message-passing neural networks and Hodge Laplacians exploit higher-order relationships, they are primarily designed for offline settings and lack adaptability to streaming and non-stationary environments. In this work, we introduce a framework for nonlinear autoregressive modeling over ACCs, where predictive functions are defined in a reproducing kernel Hilbert space (RKHS) induced by shift-invariant kernels. We further propose an efficient online learning algorithm to estimate these functions. Experimental results on synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that our method shows competitive or improved performance in prediction accuracy and adaptability under streaming conditions.
primary_area: learning on graphs and other geometries & topologies

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to predict future data by treating simple spreadsheets like complex, multi-dimensional geometric shapes that evolve in real-time. It’s essentially an attempt to use high-level topology to solve time-series problems that most people just use a basic trendline for.
why_bad: Computational Obesity: Using RKHS (kernels) for online learning means the model’s memory usage grows every time it sees a new data point, making it a ticking time bomb for your RAM.

Geometric Overkill: Applying Cellular Complexes to time-series data is like using a GPS-guided laser cutter to slice bread; the math is fancy, but the 'competitive' results suggest a simple knife would have worked fine.

The Synthetic Crutch: The paper leans heavily on simulated data where their specific geometric assumptions are baked in, while the real-world results are suspiciously 'comparable' to much simpler methods.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where data moves at the speed of light, one team of researchers decided that regular math was just too easy. This summer, witness the struggle as they try to solve a shifting Rubik’s Cube made of liquid glass—while running a marathon through a Hilbert space! It’s got higher-order dependencies! It’s got Hodge Laplacians! It’s got a computational cost so high, your server room will reach the temperature of a dying sun! They promised a revolution in adaptability, but they delivered a masterpiece of over-engineering that barely outperforms a spreadsheet from 1998. It’s 'The 4D Rubik’s Cube Fire Drill'—rated 'R' for Redundant.

title: RLBenchNet: Benchmarking Neural Architectures with PPO Across Reinforcement Learning Tasks
abstract: Reinforcement learning (RL) has advanced significantly through the application of diverse neural network architectures. In this study, we systematically evaluate the performance of several architectures within RL tasks using a widely adopted policy gradient algorithm, Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). The architectures considered include Long Short-Term Memory (LSTM), Multi-Layer Perceptron (MLP), Mamba/Mamba-2, Transformer-XL, Gated Transformer-XL, and Gated Recurrent Unit (GRU). Through comprehensive experiments spanning continuous control, discrete decision-making, and memory-based environments, we uncover architecture-specific strengths and limitations. Our results show that: (1) MLPs excel in fully observable continuous control tasks, offering an effective balance between performance and efficiency; (2) recurrent architectures such as LSTM and GRU provide robust performance in partially observable settings with moderate memory demands; (3) Mamba models achieve up to 4.5× higher throughput than LSTM and 3.9× higher than GRU, while maintaining comparable performance; and (4) only Transformer-XL, Gated Transformer-XL, and Mamba-2 succeed on the most memory-intensive tasks, with Mamba-2 requiring 8× less memory than Transformer-XL. These findings highlight the trade-offs among architectures and provide actionable insights for selecting appropriate models in PPO-based RL under different task characteristics and computational constraints.
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers spent a lot of GPU credits to confirm that simple AI models are fast for easy tasks, while 'big brain' models are needed for games that require more than a three-second memory span. It’s essentially a competitive 'Who’s Who' of neural networks playing video games to see who crashes first.
why_bad: The 'contribution' is basically an expensive Excel spreadsheet of benchmarks that everyone in the field already intuitively suspected.
Testing only one training algorithm (PPO) is like reviewing six different supercars but only ever driving them through a school zone at 20 mph.
It lacks theoretical depth—it tells us that Mamba is faster than a Transformer, but doesn't explain if it's actually learning better or just getting lucky with the math.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a tech startup that raises $5 million to build a 'Revolutionary Bread-Heating Framework.' They spend six months testing a bonfire, a magnifying glass, and a luxury toaster using only one specific brand of sourdough. Their big 'scientific' conclusion? The toaster is the most efficient, and the bonfire burns the bread. It’s not a breakthrough; it’s just a very expensive way to tell the world that a toaster does, in fact, toast.

title: Elucidating the Design Space of FP4 training
abstract: The increasing computational demands of foundation models have spurred research into low-precision training, with 4-bit floating-point (\texttt{FP4}) formats emerging as a frontier for maximizing hardware throughput. While numerous techniques have been proposed to stabilize \texttt{FP4} training, they often present isolated solutions with varying, and not always clear, computational overheads. This paper aims to provide a unified view of the design space of \texttt{FP4} training. We introduce a comprehensive, quantisation gradient-based framework for microscaling quantization that allows for a theoretical analysis of the computational costs associated with different stabilization methods on both the forward and backward passes. Using a simulator built on this framework, we conduct an extensive empirical study across a wide range of machine learning tasks, including regression, image classification, diffusion models, and language models. By systematically evaluating thousands of combinations of techniques—such as novel gradient approximations, rounding strategies, and scaling methods, we identify which configurations offer the most favourable performance-to-overhead trade-off. We find that the techniques enabling the best trade-off involve carefully combining Hadamard transformations, tensor scaling and stochastic rounding. We further find that using \texttt{UE5M3} as a scaling factor potentially offers a good compromise between range and precision with manageable computational overhead.
primary_area: infrastructure, software libraries, hardware, systems, etc.

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to train AI using ultra-tiny 4-bit numbers to save memory, but found the math becomes so unstable it's like trying to build a skyscraper out of Jell-O. To fix it, they added a bunch of complex 'stabilizers' that ironically make the process almost as clunky as just using the bigger numbers in the first place.
why_bad: The 'Simulated Reality' Flaw: The researchers performed their tests on a software simulator rather than actual chips, meaning their 'speed' results are basically fan fiction that ignores how real hardware handles data.

The Complexity Paradox: By the time you apply Hadamard transforms and stochastic rounding to stop the 4-bit training from crashing, the extra computational 'taxes' eat up most of the efficiency gains you were chasing.

Lack of Novel Insight: The paper is essentially a giant, glorified spreadsheet of trials (a 'grid search') that tells us what happened without providing a deep theoretical 'why' that researchers can actually use for the next generation of chips.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Efficiency Olympics! Our runner today is trying to set a world record by wearing flip-flops to 'minimize shoe weight.' Bold move! But wait—to keep the flip-flops from flying off, he's spent three hours duct-taping his feet, ankles, and calves in a complex 'stability framework.' He’s technically wearing the lightest shoes on the track, folks, but he’s carrying five pounds of tape and moving with the grace of a confused penguin. He’s claiming a 'unified view of footwear design space,' but the referees are looking at the clock and—oof—he’s actually slower than the guy in regular Nikes. That’s a disqualification for solving a problem that he spent more energy creating than fixing!
roast.scientific_accuracy_rating: 9/10 - Snarky but honors the technical trade-offs of low-precision quantization.

title: Monocular 3D Human Pose Estimation via Euler Angles
abstract: Monocular 3D human pose estimation is a key challenge in computer vision. While existing joint position-based methods often struggle with accurate bone length prediction and rotation ambiguities under joint collinearity, joint rotationbased methods circumvent the bone length issue but face discontinuity problems when regressing the body’s self-rotation angle, limiting their practical application. In this work, we theoretically analyze the root cause of this discontinuity and propose a framework for Euler angle-based pose estimation. Our method transforms Euler angle into its corresponding sine-cosine representation on the unit circle, ensuring a continuous and wrap-around-free formulation, and introduces body orientation as an internally learned conditional variable, which jointly optimizes the prediction of Euler angles in a unified learning process. By allowing the network to adapt its regression behavior based on the predicted condition, the framework effectively resolves the discontinuity inherent in Euler-angle regression. Experiments across diverse model architectures, including CNNs, GCNs, and Transformers, demonstrate that our approach enables continuous and stable self-rotation prediction. The framework forms a versatile and efficient module that maintains compatibility with existing backbones and facilitates streamlined end-to-end training.
keywords: 3D human pose estimation;Joint rotation;Euler angle;body's self-rotation discontinuities;orientation conditions
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: Trying to figure out how a person is standing from a single flat photo usually results in digital limbs that look like glitchy noodles. These researchers tried to fix the glitching by using old-school angles and a sine-cosine math trick to stop the 3D skeletons from having a literal 'breakdown' when they rotate too far.
why_bad: Gimbal Lock Denial: Euler angles are the VHS tapes of 3D math; no matter how much sine-cosine 'polish' you put on them, they eventually hit a mathematical dead zone where the joints lose a degree of freedom and freeze up.

Circular Logic: Using a predicted 'body orientation' to help predict 'joint angles' creates a feedback loop of failure—if the first guess is slightly off, the whole skeleton performs an unintentional exorcism.
Incremental Novelty: The 'sine-cosine representation' is essentially a clunkier version of 6D rotation embeddings that the rest of the field moved on to years ago to avoid exactly these issues.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen Nightmares: CV Edition'! Today, our chef is trying to bake a 3D soufflé using only a flat photo of a cake. Instead of using a thermometer like a sane person, he’s measuring the oven heat using a compass. 'But Chef,' we say, 'if the compass points North, the cake collapses!' His solution? He draws a circle on a napkin with ketchup so the compass doesn't get confused, then he asks his sous-chef to *guess* if it's Tuesday, and uses that guess to decide how much flour to add. The result? It looks like a cake for five seconds, but the moment you try to slice it, the structural integrity vanishes because you're using 18th-century navigation tools to do molecular gastronomy. Just use a scale, Greg!

title: Hierarchical Code Embeddings with Multi-Level Attention for Reinforcement Learning State Representation
abstract:
\begin{abstract}
In this paper, we propose novel state representation and reinforcement learning (RL)
system of encoding the semantics of code hierarchically using multiple
attention mechanisms. Traditional approaches regularly address code embeddings
as flat sequences or to be reliant only on graph-based representations,
which don't capture the complex level of interplay between local and global
code features. The proposed method incorporate token-level,
function-level, and module-level attention using graph-structured
dependencies, to allow the RL agent to reason about code at varying
granularities while maintaining structural relationships 
\end{abstract}
primary_area: transfer learning, meta learning, and lifelong learning

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to teach an AI to understand software by making it stare at individual words, entire functions, and whole files simultaneously. It turns out that forcing a robot to overthink its reading list just makes it incredibly slow and prone to getting lost in the details.
why_bad: Over-engineering for sport: The 'hierarchical' structure is a Rube Goldberg machine where simple text processing would have sufficed.
Computational gluttony: The multi-level attention mechanism consumes enough compute to heat a small apartment complex without a proportional increase in accuracy.
Ablation absence: The authors failed to prove that their complex 'module-level' layer actually contributes anything beyond extra lines in the bibliography.
roast.analogy_description: Folks, we’re seeing a runner try to finish a 100-meter dash while stopping every two inches to examine the track through a microscope, a magnifying glass, and a telescope simultaneously. He’s got that 'multi-level' perspective, sure, but the finish line is in another zip code and he's currently paralyzed by the molecular structure of a pebble. The judges are checking their watches, the crowd is heading for the exits, and our 'hierarchical' athlete is losing to a guy in flip-flops who just knows how to run forward. It’s high-tech, it’s expensive, and it’s functionally useless.

title: LUCID: Universal Auditing of Distilled Large Language Models
abstract: The growing transparency of large language models (LLMs) makes distillation into smaller models an inevitable practice, allowing users to cheaply inherit advanced capabilities such as reasoning. Yet this trend also exposes model providers to new risks: unauthorized data distillation may misappropriate the teacher model’s valuable functions, resulting in copyright violations, privacy leaks, and other serious harms. Existing fingerprinting techniques mainly focus on detecting complete model theft, offering little protection for specific functional capabilities, and many require white-box access, limiting real-world applicability. In this work, we propose LUCID (LLM distillation Unveiled via invarianCe auditor Infringement Detection), the first black-box detection framework tailored to identifying the misappropriation of a victim model’s specific capability, particularly those acquired through distillation. LUCID constructs both infringing and non-infringing models on a capability-sensitive observation dataset, designs self-reflective prompts to elicit internal judgments from the protected model, and extracts judge-token representations to train a binary classifier for infringement detection. Theoretical analysis substantiates the generalization ability and decision boundary separability of our approach, while empirical results demonstrate its effectiveness in reliably identifying unauthorized data distillation without requiring access to the suspect model’s architecture or parameters.
keywords: large language models. distillation protection. IP protection.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: Big AI companies are tired of 'student' models copying their homework for free, so this paper proposes a digital smoke detector to catch these copycats in the act. It tries to prove a smaller model is a 'thief' just by looking at its outputs, without ever needing to see the secret code inside.
why_bad: Circular Reasoning: The system uses the 'victim' model to judge its own potential copycats, which is essentially asking a narcissist if they see themselves in a mirror.
The 'Coincidence' Loophole: The authors struggle to prove the difference between a model that actually stole data and one that just got smart by reading the same public internet we all use.
Fragility to Paraphrasing: The detection method relies on specific 'judge-tokens' that can be easily defeated if the 'thief' simply tells their model to talk in a slightly different persona or style.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you spent $100M developing a secret hot sauce, and now every food truck in town is selling a 'generic' version that tastes suspiciously like yours. You can't break into their trucks to see the recipe, so you hire a 'Super-Taster'—who happens to be your own head chef—to take a tiny lick and declare, 'Yup, that’s my cumin!' Your pitch is: 'We catch sauce-thieves without seeing their kitchens!' But the investors (reviewers) aren't buying it because your head chef is biased as hell, and if the food truck just adds a single extra squeeze of lime, your 'Universal Auditor' suddenly has no idea what it's tasting. It’s a high-tech vibe check that fails the moment someone changes the garnish.

title: Evolving Embodied Intelligence: Graph Neural Network–Driven Co-Design of Morphology and Control in Soft Robotics
abstract: The intelligent behavior of robots does not emerge solely from control systems, but from the tight coupling between body and brain—a principle known as embodied intelligence. Designing soft robots that leverage this interaction remains a significant challenge, particularly when morphology and control require simultaneous optimization. A significant obstacle in this co-design process is that morphological evolution can disrupt learned control strategies, making it difficult to reuse or adapt existing knowledge. We address this by develop a Graph Neural Network-based approach for the co-design of morphology and controller. Each robot is represented as a graph, with a graph attention network (GAT) encoding node features and a pooled representation passed through a multilayer perceptron (MLP) head to produce actuator commands or value estimates. During evolution, inheritance follows a topology-consistent mapping: shared GAT layers are reused, MLP hidden layers are transferred intact, matched actuator outputs are copied, and unmatched ones are randomly initialized and fine-tuned. This morphology-aware policy class lets the controller adapt when the body mutates. On the benchmark, our GAT-based approach achieves higher final fitness and stronger adaptability to morphological variations compared to traditional MLP-only co-design methods. These results indicate that graph-structured policies provide a more effective interface between evolving morphologies and control for embodied intelligence.
primary_area: applications to robotics, autonomy, planning

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to build squishy robots that can evolve new body parts without their brains completely glitching out during the growth spurt. By using a 'connect-the-dots' neural network, we hoped the robot could grow an extra leg and immediately know how to wiggle it instead of just lying there like a sad, confused marshmallow.
why_bad: The Graph Neural Network is an over-engineered sledgehammer for a nut that simpler, less 'trendy' algorithms cracked five years ago.
Your 'evolutionary' process is just an expensive way to burn GPU hours on a search space that is 99% non-functional glitch-heaps.
Beating a basic MLP baseline in a morphology task is like bragging that a professional athlete can outrun a toddler—it’s technically a win, but it's not a scientific contribution.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re pitching a fitness app for teenagers whose limbs grow three inches overnight. Normally, the kid would spend a week tripping over their own feet, but our 'Dynamic Schema' tech ensures their brain re-maps the new leg length in real-time. We’re calling it 'Bio-Instructional Synergy.' It’s disruptive, it’s 'embodied,' and it’s perfect for the VC who loves words like 'topology-consistent.' Sure, it only works in a simulated void with zero gravity and the 'evolution' takes longer than actual carbon-based life, but look at these GAT attention weights! We’re not just building robots; we’re building a brain that doesn't care if you replace its arm with a wet noodle. We’re seeking $5M to find a problem that actually fits this solution.

title: On structured sparsity and dual lottery tickets for Robust Continual Multi-task Learning
abstract:
Continual learning for large language models (LLMs) faces a critical challenge: adapting to new tasks often results in catastrophic forgetting of prior knowledge and destructive interference across tasks. While sparse adaptation methods, such as Lottery Ticket Adaptation (LoTA), have emerged to mitigate these issues by optimizing only sparse subnetworks, they often rely on data-dependent mask calibration or random pruning. LoTA, for instance, identifies sparse subnetworks to avoid destructive interference and enables model merging, demonstrating improved performance over full fine-tuning (FFT) and low-rank adaptation (LoRA) in multi-task scenarios. Its extension, LoTTO, further enhances sequential training by learning mutually sparse masks to prevent overlap between tasks.

Building upon these insights, our work introduces a novel approach for robust continual multi-task adaptation, specifically designed to achieve high accuracy on two or more tasks (A and B) without catastrophic forgetting. Our technique distinguishes itself by first selecting subnetworks based on inherent structural properties using expander graph masks, rather than relying on data-dependent or purely random selection. These expander masks provide a principled and structurally sound basis for defining initial sparse subnetworks. Subsequently, to ensure high accuracy on both current and past tasks while actively preventing catastrophic forgetting, we train these structurally-derived masks using Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC). EWC selectively regularizes the parameters deemed important for previously learned tasks, thereby preserving critical knowledge and enabling efficient adaptation to new objectives.

This combined methodology not only yields demonstrably higher scores across multiple tasks but also offers a compelling multi-task extension of the Dual Lottery Ticket Hypothesis (DLTH). In this context, we claim that any two random expander masks can be transformed into highly trainable subnetworks, achieving high degrees of accuracy on distinct tasks. Our approach provides a powerful and efficient framework for robust continual learning in LLMs, addressing the core challenges of destructive interference and catastrophic forgetting through structured sparsity and intelligent knowledge preservation.
keywords: Continual learning;Expander graphs;Multitask learning;Catastrophic forgetting;Sparse adaptation
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to stop AI from 'forgetting' old tasks by forcing it to use specific, mathematically-shaped clusters of neurons for each new thing it learns. They basically attempted to build a digital filing cabinet where the folders are locked with old-school math to prevent new info from overwriting the old.
why_bad: Methodological Overkill: Adding Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) to structural masks is like wearing a belt and suspenders on pants that are already duct-taped to your waist; it's redundant and mathematically messy.

The 'Math-Washing' Critique: The paper uses fancy 'expander graphs' to choose neurons because they sound prestigious, but fails to prove this actually works better than just picking random neurons with a 25-cent coin flip.
Theoretical Overreach: Claiming a new 'Dual Lottery Ticket Hypothesis' based on a tiny experiment is like making a free throw and announcing you’ve discovered a brand-new law of universal gravity.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’ve got a one-bedroom apartment (the LLM) and you’re trying to lease it to a professional bagpiper and a meditation coach at the same time. Our 'disruptive' solution? We use 'Expander Zoning' to force the bagpiper into the walk-in closet and the coach into the bathtub. Then, we apply 'Aggressive Passive-Aggression' (EWC) to make sure they don't touch each other's stuff. Is it efficient? No. Is it comfortable? Absolutely not. But we’re calling it the 'Universal Housing Law' and seeking a $50M Seed Round because we managed to keep them both in the building for forty-eight hours without a noise complaint.

title: TN-AutoRCA: Benchmark Construction and Agentic Framework for Self-Improving Alarm-Based Root Cause Analysis in Telecommunication Networks
abstract:
Root Cause Analysis (RCA) in Telecommunication Networks is a critical task, yet it presents a formidable challenge for Artificial Intelligence (AI) due to its complex, graph-based reasoning requirements and the scarcity of realistic benchmarks. To catalyze research in this domain, We herein present TN-RCA, an inaugural real-world, publicly accessible benchmark for root cause analysis (RCA)
of telecommunication network alarms, comprising 530 fault scenarios constructed from expert-validated Knowledge Graphs(KGs). Our evaluation reveals that even state-of-the-art Large Language Models (LLMs) perform poorly on this task, with the best LLMs achieving an F1-score below 70%, highlighting its significant difficulty.To address this challenge, we then propose Auto-RCA, a novel agentic
system that produces the core code to analysis root cause through the automatically iterative refinement. The core innovation of Auto-RCA lies beyond simple self-correction; it employs an iterative ”evaluate-analyze-repair” loop that systematically identifies common patterns across all failure cases to generate contrastive feedback. This feedback guides the LLM to fix systemic logical flaws rather than
isolated errors. Experiments show that this agentic framework dramatically boosts the performance of root cause analysis in telecommunication networks, raising the final F1-score on TN-RCA from 58.99% (achieved by Gemini-2.5-Pro directly) to 91.79%. Meanwhile, Auto-RCA achieves 50% accuracy on the OpenRCA benchmark and demonstrates its generalization. This work not only
contributes a crucial benchmark to the community but also demonstrates that autonomous, self-optimizing agentic architecture is a powerful paradigm for solvingcomplex, domain-specific reasoning problems.
keywords: Alarm-Based Root Cause Analysis; LLMs; Agentic Framework; Telecommunication Networks
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks

roast.laymen_summary: The authors created a new test to see if AI can figure out why your cell service is acting up, only to realize that even the smartest models are basically guessing. Their solution is a 'self-improving' bot that writes its own code to fix its mistakes, eventually brute-forcing its way to a passing grade.
why_bad: Temporal Hallucinations: The paper claims to use 'Gemini-2.5-Pro' for evaluation, a model version that doesn't actually exist, casting serious doubt on the validity of their data.

The 'Student-Grading-Their-Own-Exam' Problem: The iterative repair loop risks overfitting to the specific benchmark cases, meaning the AI isn't learning logic; it's just memorizing how to pass this one specific test.

Extreme Lack of Polish: With glaring typos like 'dif ficulty' and 'automati cally,' the authors demonstrate a level of attention to detail that you really don't want in someone managing critical telecommunications infrastructure.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’ve got a startup where the 'engineers' are just a bunch of interns who have never seen a circuit board. When the server room catches fire, instead of fixing it, the lead intern writes a script to ignore the smoke. If the room is still hot, he rewires the thermostat until it reads 70 degrees while the walls melt. We’re calling it 'Iterative Thermal Optimization,' but the fire department calls it 'Arson.' We’re looking for a $50M seed round to keep the interns caffeinated enough to ignore the sirens.

title: Auth-Prompt Bench: Towards Reliable and Stable Prompting in Text-to-Image Generation
abstract:
Recent advances in diffusion models and large language models (LLMs) have enabled text-to-image generation of remarkable fidelity, allowing users to synthesize images directly from natural language prompts. However, we observe that prompt informativeness varies significantly across user proficiency levels, which draws little research attention in the field. Those ambiguous or under-specified prompts often lead to unstable outputs that deviate from user intent, while current benchmarks provide limited means to quantify this phenomenon. 
We address this gap by introducing Authentic Prompt Benchmark (Auth-Prompt Bench), a large-scale benchmark of 17,580 prompt-image pairs from both novice and expert users sourced from authentic web cases, specifically designed to evaluate the stability of the prompting in text-to-image generation. Unlike existing metrics that focus solely on prompt–image alignment, Auth-Prompt Bench is further grounded in an information-theoretic perspective of prompt-to-prompt transmission, enabling stability assessment through complementary metrics: mutual information, prompt entropy, and prompt energy.
Building on these insights, we propose NoxEye, an end-to-end prompt optimization framework comprising (i) an information enhancer that maps user prompts toward the model-preferred distribution, and (ii) an information aligner that enforces fine-grained alignment of visual entities. Across Auth-Prompt Bench and other established benchmarks, NoxEye delivers improvements of up to 13.52\% in mutual information, 20.30\% in prompt entropy, and 27.01\% in prompt energy and enhances prompts from novice users, over state-of-the-art baselines.
Our results establish Auth-Prompt Bench as one of the first dedicated benchmarks for stability in T2I generation and demonstrate that information-theoretic prompt optimization can significantly enhance both robustness and fidelity. The human evaluation results further verify the efficacy of our method in user intent alignment for T2I generation. We hope this work provides a foundation for the community on principled evaluation and reliable user–model interaction in T2I generative systems. The source code and dataset will be made publicly available.
keywords: text-to-image dataset and benchmark;large language models;prompt optimization
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities

roast.laymen_summary: Most people are terrible at describing what they want to AI image generators, leading to weird, unpredictable results. The authors built a massive library of bad prompts and a 'translator' AI that uses complex math to turn your vague 'dog' request into a hyper-specific technical blueprint.
why_bad: Math-washing: The authors used 'Prompt Energy' and 'Mutual Information' to measure art quality, which is like trying to judge a beauty pageant by measuring the contestants' body temperature.

Circular Benchmarking: The 'Auth-Prompt' dataset is just scraped web data, meaning the model is being graded on its ability to mimic existing noise rather than actually understanding what a 'novice' wants.

The 'So What?' Factor: Increasing 'Prompt Entropy' by 20% sounds impressive in a lab, but if the final image still has seven fingers and a floating hat, the mathematical optimization is functionally irrelevant to the end user.
roast.analogy_name: The Trial of the Thesaurus-Thumping Translator
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a courtroom where the defendant is accused of being an unhelpful middleman. The Prosecution presents a witness who asked for a simple 'cup of coffee.' The defendant, acting as the translator, turned that request into a 400-page manifesto on the thermodynamics of caffeine and the socio-economic history of beans. When the Judge asks if the witness actually got their coffee, the Defense screams, 'Objection! Look at how much Information Entropy we added to the sentence!' The Prosecution counters that while the sentence is now mathematically 'dense,' the witness is still thirsty, and the translator is just charging for extra syllables no one asked for. The jury finds that adding complexity to a simple request isn't 'stability'—it's just a very expensive way to confuse a barista.

title: POLICY DEGENERACY IN DEEP REINFORCEMENT LEARNING FOR RECOMMENDATIONS: AN EMPIRICAL STUDY
abstract:
Deep Reinforcement Learning (RL) offers a promising framework for learning
adaptive policies in recommender systems, particularly for the cold-start prob-
lem where balancing precision and discovery is crucial. In this work, we provide
a transparent and reproducible benchmark to investigate this challenge, training
a highly-optimized Deep Q-Network (DQN) agent within a high-fidelity offline
simulation to learn a dynamic recommendation policy. Contrary to expectations,
our final evaluation reveals that the trained agent’s performance is at a statistical
tie with a simple, static heuristic baseline across a suite of key metrics, including
cumulative reward and NDCG@10. However, we show that this statistical parity
in outcomes masks a fundamental divergence in behavior: the heuristic employs
a conservative, exploitation-heavy strategy, while the RL agent learns a radically
different and more exploratory policy. We argue that this result is not a failure of
the agent, but rather a crucial insight into the limitations of offline evaluation. The
finding provides powerful, empirical evidence that the simulation environment it-
self can create a ”performance ceiling,” lacking the fidelity to distinguish between
a good policy and a potentially great one. Our work thus serves as a crucial bench-
mark and cautionary tale, signaling an urgent need for the community to develop
richer offline evaluation environments or prioritize hybrid online-offline methods
to bridge the gap between simulation and real-world impact.
keywords: reinforcement learning; recommender systems; offline evaluation; simulation; DQN; reproducibility
primary_area: reinforcement learning

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a complex, expensive AI to give better recommendations, only to find it performed exactly as well as a simple 'Top 10' list. Instead of admitting defeat, they’ve argued that their AI is actually a genius and the test itself was just too simple to measure its greatness.
why_bad: Zero empirical uplift: The complex RL agent failed to beat a basic, static heuristic on any meaningful metric.
Blaming the benchmark: The 'performance ceiling' argument is a convenient, unfalsifiable excuse for a model that simply didn't work.
Circular reasoning: The authors trained the agent in a simulation and then claimed the same simulation was too low-fidelity to evaluate the agent properly.
roast.analogy_name: The Ferrari in a School Zone
roast.analogy_description: Imagine pitching a $500,000 supercar that can hit 200mph, but testing it in a 15mph school zone. When it arrives at the finish line at the exact same time as a 1998 Honda Civic, you don’t apologize for the price tag. Instead, you tell the VCs that the pavement 'lacked the fidelity' to showcase your aerodynamic downforce. You aren't selling a slow car; you're selling a car so advanced that the Earth's infrastructure is holding it back. Disruptive, right?

title: SF-PE: A Synergistic Fusion of Absolute and Relative Positional Encoding for Spiking Transformers
abstract:
Positional signals in spiking neural networks (SNNs) suffer distortion due to spike binarization and the nonlinear dynamics of Leaky Integrate-and-Fire (LIF) neurons, which compromises self-attention mechanisms. We introduce Spiking-RoPE, a spiking-friendly relative rotary positional encoding that applies two-dimensional spatiotemporal position-dependent rotations to queries/keys prior to
binarization, ensuring that relative phase kernels are preserved in statistical expectation under LIF dynamics while maintaining content integrity. Building on this core, we propose Spiking Fused-PE (SF-PE), a scheme that fuses absolute CPG-based spikes with Spiking-RoPE. The resulting attention score decomposes into complementary row/column (absolute) and diagonal (relative) structures, thereby expanding the representable function space. We validate our method across two diverse domains (time-series forecasting and text classification) on Spikformer, Spike-driven Transformer, and QKFormer backbones. SF-PE consistently improves accuracy and enhances length extrapolation capabilities. Ablations on rotation bases and 1D vs. 2D variants support the design. These results establish rotary encoding as an effective, spiking-friendly relative PE for SNNs and demonstrate that fusing absolute and relative signals yields synergistic benefits under spiking constraints. Code: https://anonymous.4open.science/r/SNN-RoPE-F6DE.
keywords: Spiking Neural Networks (SNNs);Rotary Positional Encoding (RoPE);Central Pattern Generator (CPG);Neuromorphic Computing;Time-series Forecasting;Text Classification
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: Spiking neural networks are brain-inspired AI that communicate in simple 'on-off' pulses, but they frequently lose track of where they are in a sentence or sequence. This paper tries to fix their amnesia by giving these pulses a hybrid GPS that tells them both their exact seat number and who they are sitting next to.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: It essentially just 'bolted' existing Rotary Positional Encoding (RoPE) onto Spiking Transformers and called it a revolution.
The Efficiency Paradox: Adding complex trigonometric rotations to a system designed for ultra-low-power binary spikes is like putting a heavy V8 engine on a bicycle.
Evaluation Weakness: Testing SNNs on text classification is a niche flex that fails to prove the method is better than standard, non-spiking models that already do this perfectly.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where neurons only know how to scream 'YES' or 'NO,' one team decided they needed to learn the Waltz. (Inception BRAAM sound). They’ve got the 'Absolute' map and the 'Relative' compass, but there’s a catch: the dancers are blindfolded and can only communicate by clapping. It’s a high-stakes mathematical disco where the trigonometry is expensive, the spikes are cheap, and the reviewers are standing in the corner refusing to dance. This summer, witness the 'Synergistic Fusion' that literally no one asked for. Rated R for Redundant.

title: Swarm Intelligence Enhanced Reasoning: A Density-Driven Framework for LLM-Based Multi-Agent Optimization
abstract: Recently, many approaches, such as Chain-of-Thought (CoT) prompting and Multi-Agent Debate (MAD), have been proposed to further enrich Large Language Models' (LLMs) complex problem-solving capacities in reasoning scenarios. However, these methods may fail to solve complex problems due to the lack of ability to find optimal solutions. Swarm Intelligence has been serving as a powerful tool for finding optima in the field of traditional optimization problems. To this end, we propose integrating swarm intelligence into the reasoning process by introducing a novel Agent-based Swarm Intelligence (ASI) paradigm. In this paradigm, we formulate LLM reasoning as an optimization problem and use a swarm intelligence scheme to guide a group of LLM-based agents in collaboratively searching for optimal solutions. To avoid swarm intelligence getting trapped in local optima, we further develop a Swarm Intelligence Enhancing Reasoning (SIER) framework, which develops a density-driven strategy to enhance the reasoning ability. To be specific, we propose to perform kernel density estimation and non-dominated sorting to optimize both solution quality and diversity simultaneously. In this case, SIER efficiently enhances solution space exploration through expanding the diversity of the reasoning path. Besides, a step-level quality evaluation is used to help agents improve solution quality by correcting low-quality intermediate steps. Then, we use quality thresholds to dynamically control the termination of exploration and the selection of candidate steps, enabling a more flexible and efficient reasoning process. Extensive experiments are conducted on widely-used seven mathematical reasoning benchmarks, i.e., MATH-500, MMLU-STEM, etc. As expected, our method consistently outperforms both CoT methods and existing reward-guided approaches, particularly on complex problems. This demonstrates the effectiveness of our approach in leveraging swarm intelligence for enhanced reasoning.
keywords: Multi Agent System;Large Language Model;Reasoning;Swarm Intelligence
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities

roast.laymen_summary: The authors think the best way to solve a hard math problem is to unleash a literal mob of AI agents to argue about it until they find the 'most diverse' answer. It’s basically trying to turn a group chat of confused bots into a genius-level hive mind using fancy math that smells like over-engineering.
why_bad: Computation Overkill: Solving a math problem by running a swarm of LLMs is like using a fleet of Ferraris to deliver a single pizza—it works, but the cost-to-benefit ratio is catastrophic.

Obscurity via Math-Washing: Using 'kernel density estimation' and 'non-dominated sorting' on LLM outputs is a transparent attempt to make 'we just generated more samples' sound like a Nobel-worthy discovery.

Latency Nightmare: The paper conveniently glosses over the fact that their 'flexible' reasoning process likely takes ten minutes to answer a question that a single model could get wrong in three seconds.
roast.analogy_name: The Infinite Interns: Multiverse Math Edition
roast.analogy_description: In a world where one AI isn't smart enough to do the math, one team has a daring plan: Hire 500 interns, put them in separate soundproof booths, and force them to solve the same equation. But there's a twist! A guy with a clipboard (the SIER framework) is standing in the middle, throwing away any answer that sounds too similar to the others, all while screaming about 'diversity.' Watch as they burn through the company's entire yearly budget just to figure out if 7+5 is still 12. This summer, experience the thrill of massive GPU bills and 45-minute wait times for a single response. One hive. No brain. Coming soon to a Peer Review near you.

title: Rethinking Diffusion Model in High Dimension
abstract: Curse of Dimensionality is an unavoidable challenge in statistical probability models, yet diffusion models seem to overcome this limitation, achieving impressive results in high-dimensional data generation. Diffusion models assume that they can learn the statistical quantities of the underlying probability distribution, enabling sampling from this distribution to generate realistic samples. But is this really how they work? We argue not, based on the following observations 1) In high-dimensional sparse scenarios, the fitting target of the diffusion model's objective function degrades from a weighted sum of multiple samples to a single sample, which we believe hinders the model’s ability to effectively learn essential statistical quantities such as posterior, score, or velocity field. 2) Most inference methods can be unified within a simple framework which involves no statistical concepts, aligns with the degraded objective function, and provides an novel and intuitive perspective on the inference process.
keywords: Diffusion Model;High Dimension;Diffusion Model Inference Method;Curse of Dimensionality
primary_area: generative models

roast.laymen_summary: The authors are essentially arguing that your favorite AI image generator isn't actually a math genius learning complex patterns, but rather a lazy student who just memorizes individual photos because the 'data room' is too big to navigate. They claim that instead of learning how to paint, the AI is just playing a high-stakes game of Connect-the-Dots with single points of data.
why_bad: The paper attempts to 'disprove' statistics in high dimensions while conveniently ignoring the Manifold Hypothesis, which is the literal foundation of why these models work in the first place.
The 'novel' framework offered is just a stripped-down, less-rigorous version of existing ODE/SDE solvers that researchers have been using for years.
It makes massive, sweeping claims about the failure of objective functions without providing the empirical 'receipts' or mathematical proofs required to back up such a hot take.
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, we are looking at a classic goal-tending violation! The authors are stepping onto the court claiming the Diffusion Dribblers aren't actually playing team basketball; they're claiming every player is just standing in their own private zip code because the arena is too huge. They’re yelling at the refs that the 'playbook' is a lie and that the players are just teleporting to the hoop! But look at the Jumbotron—the reviewers are calling a technical foul. You can't just say 'the math is broken' because you can't see the passes. The authors tried to bench the entire field of statistics in the final quarter, and the league office just handed them a lifetime ban from the playoffs! It’s a bold strategy, Cotton, but they just got dunked on by their own lack of evidence!

title: GL-Fusion: Rethinking the Combination of Graph Neural Network and Large Language model
abstract:
Recent research on integrating Large Language Models (LLMs) with Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) typically follows two approaches: LLM-centered models, which convert graph data into tokens for LLM processing, and GNN-centered models, which use LLMs to encode text features into node and edge representations for GNN input. LLM-centered models often struggle to capture graph structures effectively, while GNN-centered models compress variable-length textual data into fixed-size vectors, limiting their ability to understand complex semantics. Additionally, GNN-centered approaches require converting tasks into a uniform, manually-designed format, restricting them to classification tasks and preventing language output. To address these limitations, we introduce a new architecture that deeply integrates GNN with LLM, featuring three key innovations: (1) Structure-Aware Transformers, which incorporate GNN’s message passing capabilities directly into LLM’s transformer layers, allowing simultaneous processing of textual and structural information and generating outputs from both GNN and LLM; (2) Graph-Text Cross-Attention, which processes full, uncompressed text from graph nodes and edges, ensuring complete semantic integration; and (3) GNN-LLM Twin Predictor, enabling LLM’s flexible autoregressive genera
tion alongside GNN’s scalable one-pass prediction. GL-Fusion achieves outstand performance on various tasks. Notably, it achieves state-of-the-art performance on OGBN-Arxiv and OGBG-Code2,
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to smash a GPS map directly into a chatbot's brain so it could understand how people are connected while it talks. It attempts to stop 'summarizing' information and instead forces the AI to read every single detail of every connection at the same time.
why_bad: The 'Structure-Aware Transformer' adds massive computational overhead, essentially forcing the AI to solve a Rubik's cube while it tries to finish a sentence.
Processing 'uncompressed' text for every node in a graph is a memory-management suicide mission that would make even the beefiest GPUs scream for mercy.
The 'Twin Predictor' is less of a breakthrough and more like duct-taping two different models together and claiming they've achieved 'deep integration' because they share a lunchbox.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a kitchen appliance that claims to toast your bread *while* it's being blended into a smoothie. Our pitch? 'Why toast, then blend, when you can do both simultaneously at 20,000 RPM?' Sure, it requires a dedicated nuclear reactor to run, and the resulting sludge is technically 'integrated,' but it’s a solution to a problem nobody had, using a method that breaks every law of kitchen efficiency. It’s not a breakthrough; it’s just a very expensive way to make warm crumbs.

title: Scale-time Equivalence in Neural Network Training
abstract:
Neural networks have demonstrated remarkable performance improvements as model size, training time, and data volume have increased, but the relationships among these factors remain poorly understood. We develop a theoretical framework to investigate the interplay between model size and training time, introducing the concept of scale-time equivalence: the idea that in certain training regimes, a small model trained for a long time can achieve similar performance to a larger model trained for a shorter time. To analyze this, we model neural network training as a gradient flow in random subspaces of larger non-linear models. In this setting, we show theoretically that neural network scale and training time can be traded off with each other. Empirically, we validate scale-time equivalence on MLPs and CNNs trained with gradient descent across standard vision benchmarks, CIFAR-10, SVHN, and MNIST.

We then investigate the consequences of scale-time equivalence on double descent, the phenomenon where model performance changes non-monotonically with respect to training data volume, model scale, and training time. In regimes where scale-time equivalence holds, we show that double descent with respect to training time and model scale may share a common cause: namely, overfitting to noise early during training. Through this, we provide potential explanations for several previously unexplained empirical phenomena: reduced data requirements for generalization in larger models, heightened sensitivity to label noise in overparameterized models, and instances where increasing model scale does not necessarily enhance performance.
primary_area: learning theory

roast.laymen_summary: The authors spent a lot of math to prove that a small, persistent brain can eventually match a massive, lazy one if you give it enough time to study. They essentially tried to turn 'patience is a virtue' into a physics law for AI, claiming that time and size are just two sides of the same mediocre coin.
why_bad: The 'Scale-time Equivalence' theory is basically a rebranding of existing scaling laws, offering very little new insight for anyone who hasn't been living under an academic rock.
The empirical 'validation' is performed on MNIST and CIFAR-10, which is like trying to prove you've invented a warp drive by testing it on a tricycle.
The theoretical framework assumes 'random subspaces,' a massive oversimplification that completely ignores how modern, complex architectures like Transformers actually learn in the wild.
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, we are witnessing a bizarre showdown in the AI Arena! In Lane 1, we have 'The Giga-Chonk,' a massive model that takes one giant step and calls it a day. In Lane 2, we have 'The Caffeinated Hamster,' a tiny model running four billion laps to cover the same ground. The commentators—our authors—are screaming that these two athletes are identical because they both eventually reach the snack bar. But wait! The referees are blowing the whistle! They're pointing out that this race is taking place in a hallway three feet long. Sure, the hamster looks fast here, but put them on a real marathon track—like an actual LLM dataset—and that hamster is going to have a heart attack before the first mile. It’s a bold strategy to ignore reality, Cotton, let’s see if it pays off for them—spoiler: it didn't!

title: InfiAgent: Self-Evolving Pyramid Agent Framework for Infinite Scenarios
abstract: Large Language Model (LLM) agents have demonstrated remarkable capabilities in organizing and executing complex tasks, and many such agents are now widely used in various application scenarios. However, developing these agents requires carefully designed workflows, carefully crafted prompts, and iterative tuning, which requires LLM techniques and domain-specific expertise. These hand-crafted limitations hinder the scalability and cost-effectiveness of LLM agents across a wide range of industries. To address these challenges, we propose \textbf{InfiAgent}, a Pyramid-like DAG-based Multi-Agent Framework that can be applied to \textbf{infi}nite scenarios, which introduces several key innovations: a generalized "agent-as-a-tool" mechanism that automatically decomposes complex agents into hierarchical multi-agent systems; a dual-audit mechanism that ensures the quality and stability of task completion; an agent routing function that enables efficient task-agent matching; and an agent self-evolution mechanism that autonomously restructures the agent DAG based on new tasks, poor performance, or optimization opportunities. Furthermore, InfiAgent's atomic task design supports agent parallelism, significantly improving execution efficiency. This framework evolves into a versatile pyramid-like multi-agent system capable of solving a wide range of problems. Evaluations on multiple benchmarks demonstrate that InfiAgent achieves 9.9\% higher performance compared to ADAS (similar auto-generated agent framework), while a case study of the AI research assistant InfiHelper shows that it generates scientific papers that have received recognition from human reviewers at top-tier IEEE conferences.
primary_area: applications to robotics, autonomy, planning
author: Chenglin Yu;Yang Yu;SongmiaoWang;Yuchen Wang;Yifan Yang;jinjiali;Ming Li;Hongxia Yang

roast.laymen_summary: InfiAgent is basically an automated corporate hierarchy where a 'boss' AI hires 'intern' AIs to do all the tedious work of building and fixing other AIs. The authors claim this system can solve literally any problem in existence by constantly rearranging its own org chart whenever it gets confused.
why_bad: The 'infinite scenarios' claim is a massive red flag for reviewers; in AI, a tool that claims to do everything usually does nothing particularly well.

The 'self-evolution' mechanism is essentially a recursive loop that risks burning through thousands of dollars in API credits while the agents argue with each other about how to restructure their own flowchart.
Using an AI-written paper getting accepted at an IEEE conference as a performance metric is the academic equivalent of saying your toaster is a genius because it didn't burn the bread this morning.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition!' Imagine a Head Chef who refuses to touch a pan. Instead, he builds a robot whose only job is to build smaller robots to hold the spatulas. This 'Pyramid of Pots' claims it can cook every dish in human history simultaneously. You ask for a simple omelet, and suddenly the robots start 'self-evolving'—the egg-cracker robot decides it’s now a whisk, the whisk robot tries to 'audit' the salt levels, and the whole system gets stuck in a loop trying to optimize the ergonomics of a frying pan. Ten hours later, you have no breakfast, a $4,000 electricity bill, and a Head Chef bragging that his robots just wrote a 5-star review of their own imaginary omelet.

title: Fundamental Limits of Crystalline Equivariant Graph Neural Networks: A Circuit Complexity Perspective
abstract:
Graph neural networks (GNNs) have become a core paradigm for learning on relational data. In materials science, equivariant GNNs (EGNNs) have emerged as a compelling backbone for crystalline-structure prediction, owing to their ability to respect Euclidean symmetries and periodic boundary conditions. Despite strong empirical performance, their expressive power in periodic, symmetry-constrained settings remains poorly understood. 
This work characterizes the intrinsic computational and expressive limits of EGNNs for crystalline-structure prediction through a circuit-complexity lens. We analyze the computations carried out by EGNN layers acting on node features, atomic coordinates, and lattice matrices, and prove that, under polynomial precision, embedding width $d=O(n)$ for $n$ nodes, $O(1)$ layers, and $O(1)$-depth, $O(n)$-width MLP instantiations of the message/update/readout maps, these models admit a simulation by a uniform $\mathsf{TC}^0$ threshold-circuit family of polynomial size (with an explicit constant-depth bound). Situating EGNNs within $\mathsf{TC}^0$ provides a concrete ceiling on the decision and prediction problems solvable by such architectures under realistic resource constraints and clarifies which architectural modifications (e.g., increased depth, richer geometric primitives, or wider layers) are required to transcend this regime. The analysis complements Weisfeiler-Lehman style results that do not directly transfer to periodic crystals, and offers a complexity-theoretic foundation for symmetry-aware graph learning on crystalline systems.
keywords: Crystalline Materials;Circuit Complexity;Equivariant Graph Neural Networks
primary_area: learning on graphs and other geometries & topologies

roast.laymen_summary: This paper proves that the fancy AI models used to design new materials are essentially just high-speed calculators with a very low 'IQ' ceiling. It turns out that no matter how much you tweak them, these models are mathematically stuck in a shallow level of thinking that prevents them from solving truly complex logic puzzles.
why_bad: The central proof relies on such restrictive assumptions—like constant depth and polynomial precision—that the conclusion becomes a self-fulfilling prophecy.
It offers a 'theoretical ceiling' that nobody asked for, as practical performance in materials science is driven by data quality, not whether the model can simulate a Turing machine.
The 'so what' factor is missing: it tells us the car can't fly, but doesn't explain why it's still winning races on the ground.
