It’s essentially a 'don't put all your eggs in one basket' strategy for prompt engineering, using a complex statistical filter to ensure the model doesn't keep making the same stupid mistake.
why_bad: The computational overhead of training and running a separate encoder just to pick prompts is like buying a Ferrari to drive to the mailbox.
The 'diversity' it achieves is often superficial, failing to prove that different prompts actually lead to better reasoning rather than just different phrasing of the same failure.
Benchmarks show the 'sophisticated' DPP method barely outperforms a simple 'Best-of-N' approach, making the math feel like academic window dressing for marginal gains.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Carbon-Copy Jury
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, imagine you are a defense attorney in a high-stakes trial. You need twelve distinct perspectives to ensure a fair verdict. However, the prosecution—this paper—has provided twelve jurors who are all identical triplets from the same town, all wearing the same 'Guilty' t-shirts. To fix this, the paper proposes a 'Determinantal Point Process Selection Suite'—a million-dollar system designed to analyze the jurors' DNA and heart rates to ensure 'cognitive diversity.' You enter the courtroom expecting a mosaic of human thought, but instead, you find the system has just picked twelve triplets who are wearing slightly different shades of beige. The Judge (the reviewer) is banging the gavel because you've spent the entire legal defense fund on a sophisticated 'diversity' algorithm that still results in a unanimous, incorrect verdict while ignoring the fact that a simple, random selection would have been cheaper and just as effective. Objection sustained; this paper is out of order.

title: RoboAct-CLIP: Video-Driven Atomic Action Understanding for Robotics
abstract:
Vision-Language-Action (VLA) models have become a key framework for robotics, coupling multimodal perception with language-grounded decision making to enable cross-task generalization, dynamic interaction, and long-horizon planning. However, despite training on large-scale video and trajectory data, prevailing VLAs are predominantly imitation-driven and lack an intrinsic, spatiotemporal understanding of physical actions; as a result, their generalization degrades in unseen embodiments and contexts. In parallel, existing action-understanding approaches still fail to model temporally correlated action semantics and suffer from visual feature entanglement among the robot, manipulated objects, and background, hindering clean atomic-action semantics and reliable transfer.

We present RoboAct-CLIP, which addresses both issues with two components: (1) a curated single-action training set distilled from open-source robot videos via semantics-constrained action-unit segmentation and re-annotation, yielding purified clips each containing one atomic action (e.g., "grasp"); and (2) a temporal-decoupling architecture on a CLIP backbone. Concretely, a frozen CLIP visual encoder processes uniformly sampled frames; a Temporal Diff-Transformer operates on consecutive feature differences together with a start-end delta (the former emphasizes spatiotemporal dynamics, the latter summarizes action outcome); the fused representation is routed into subject/object/action branches with orthogonality constraints; and a compositional contrastive objective aligns branch-wise visual features with templated texts, with an additional recombination alignment loss between remixed branch features and their corresponding texts to further strengthen disentanglement.

Used as a frozen backbone, RoboAct-CLIP supports lightweight policy heads and reduces per-task tuning. In LIBERO and Franka Kitchen simulation, RoboAct-CLIP improves success rate by 12% and 5.1% over strong VLA baselines and exhibits better generalization in multi-object and unseen tasks; real-world evaluations on a single physical robot arm confirm stable atomic-action execution, with RoboAct-CLIP kept frozen and only the downstream policy adapted using task-specific data collected on the same platform. These results indicate that explicit temporal modeling plus factorized action/object/agent representations offers a simple, scalable path to more reliable VLA-based manipulation.
keywords: Representation Learning; Robotic Manipulation; Robotic Learning; Vision–Language–Action
primary_area: applications to robotics, autonomy, planning
author: Zhiyuan Zhang;Yuxin He;Junyu Shi;Yong Sun;Lijiang LIU;Zhengjie Zhang;Qiang Nie
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of array: line 6 column 215 (char 1034)

title: Entropy-Aware Speculative Decoding Toward Improved LLM Reasoning
abstract: Speculative decoding (SD) accelerates large language model (LLM) reasoning by using a small draft model to generate candidate tokens, which the target LLM either accepts directly or regenerates upon rejection. However, excessive alignment between the draft and target models constrains SD to the performance of the target LLM. To address this limitation, we propose Entropy-Aware Speculative Decoding (EASD), a training-free enhancement. Building on standard SD, EASD incorporates a dynamic entropy-based penalty. At each decoding step, we employ the entropy of the sampling distribution to quantify model uncertainty. When both models exhibit high entropy with substantial overlap among their top-N predictions, the corresponding token is rejected and re-sampled by the target LLM. This penalty prevents low-confidence errors from propagating. By incorporating draft-model verification, EASD enables the possibility of surpassing the target model's inherent performance. Experiments across multiple reasoning benchmarks demonstrate that EASD consistently outperforms existing SD methods and, in most cases, surpasses the target LLM itself. We further prove that the efficiency of EASD is comparable to that of SD. The code can be found in the Supplementary Materials.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: The authors suggest making AI faster by using a small 'intern' model to guess what the 'expert' model will say, but then rejecting those guesses if both models seem confused. They somehow claim that double-checking the expert's homework whenever the intern is baffled makes the expert smarter than it actually is.
why_bad: Mathematical Alchemy: The paper claims that a speed-up technique (Speculative Decoding) magically improves reasoning accuracy beyond the base model's capability, which is theoretically suspect and likely a result of cherry-picked benchmarks.
Efficiency Mirage: Adding 'entropy-aware' rejection layers and frequent re-sampling by the target LLM destroys the latency gains that make speculative decoding useful in the first place.
Noise as Innovation: The 'penalty' for high entropy is effectively just 'roll the dice again if the models are unsure,' which is a heuristic masquerading as a rigorous architectural improvement.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Overconfident Intern
roast.analogy_description: Picture a high-stakes courtroom where the Lead Counsel (the target LLM) is being shadowed by a caffeinated Paralegal (the draft model). The Paralegal whispers a suggested argument to the Lead Counsel; the authors' 'innovation' is to force the Lead Counsel to stop and rethink their entire life whenever the Paralegal looks visibly confused. The defense argues that by making the Lead Counsel second-guess themselves every time the intern has a panic attack, the legal strategy becomes 'superior.' The judge is unimpressed: the trial is now moving at a snail's pace, the Lead Counsel isn't actually any smarter, and the 'improved' verdict is just the same legal opinion delivered with more expensive billable hours. Objection sustained on the grounds of statistical wishful thinking.

title: RAE: A Neural Network Dimensionality Reduction Method for Nearest Neighbors Preservation in Vector Search
abstract: While high-dimensional embedding vectors are being increasingly employed in various tasks like Retrieval-Augmented Generation and Recommendation Systems, popular dimensionality reduction (DR) methods such as PCA and UMAP have rarely been adopted for accelerating the retrieval process due to their inability of preserving the nearest neighbor (NN) relationship among vectors. Empowered by neural networks' optimization capability and the bounding effect of Rayleigh quotient, we propose a Regularized Auto-Encoder (RAE) for k-NN preserving dimensionality reduction. RAE constrains the network parameter variation through regularization terms, adjusting singular values to control embedding magnitude changes during reduction, thus preserving k-NN relationships. We provide a rigorous mathematical analysis demonstrating that regularization establishes an upper bound on the norm distortion rate of transformed vectors, thereby offering provable guarantees for k-NN preservation. With modest training overhead, RAE achieves superior k-NN recall compared to existing DR approaches while maintaining fast retrieval efficiency.
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built a neural network to shrink massive AI data points into smaller versions that are easier to search through. They claim their method keeps similar items close together by using math to prevent the data from getting too distorted during the downsizing process.
why_bad: Baseline Selection Bias: The authors compared their complex neural network against general-purpose tools like PCA rather than industry-standard vector search techniques like Product Quantization (PQ), which already solve this problem efficiently.

Loose Mathematical Guarantees: The 'provable' upper bounds on distortion are theoretically sound but practically vacuous, meaning the math works on paper but doesn't actually prevent the search quality from tanking in real-world scenarios.

Training Overhead vs. Utility: The computational cost of training a specific Auto-Encoder for every dataset outweighs the marginal speed gains, making it a high-maintenance solution for a problem that simpler indexing algorithms handle for free.
roast.analogy_description: Listen, we’ve all been there: you have a walk-in closet’s worth of clothes but you’re flying budget and only have a carry-on. Instead of just packing the essentials or using a vacuum bag like a normal person, our startup has developed a $50,000 AI-controlled hydraulic press. It crushes your entire wardrobe into a single, dense cube. We’ve mathematically proven that because we used a 'regularized' press, your left shoe is still technically 'near' your right shoe inside the solid mass of fabric. Sure, it takes three hours to calibrate the machine, and your clothes are now unrecognizable bricks, but think of the storage efficiency! Don't look at the guy next to you using a standard compression bag; his method doesn't have nearly enough Rayleigh quotients.

title: Deep Adaptive Cross Domain Learning for Continuous Pressure Wave Signal Recovery
abstract:
Mud pulse telemetry (MPT) enables real-time downhole data transmission by generating continuous pressure
wave signals in drilling fluid, supporting measurement and control during drilling. However, the signals are vulnerable to noise from the downhole environment, mud channel, and surface equipment, causing attenuation, distortion, and phase errors that hinder accurate reconstruction. Recent advances in deep learning offer promising solutions to these challenges but typically requires large amounts of labeled data, which are difficult and costly to obtain in field conditions. To address this issue, this paper proposes the Deep Adaptive Cross Domain Learning Network (**DACDL**), a framework featuring a novel noise adaptation mechanism that transitions from model-level to input-level adaptation.  Our approach introduces three core innovations:
(1) An Episodic Learning Framework (**EL-Framework**)  that simulates domain shift by alternately learning from simulated (gaussian) and real-world (real-world gaussian, pump or oilfield) domains, enhancing few-shot adaptation under label scarcity; 
(2) A lightweight Adaptive Noise Learning Block (**ANL-Block**) that introduces sample-specific perturbations to align target input noise distributions with the source domain, alleviating generalization collapse caused by unseen noise types; 
(3) A Frequency-aware Adversarial Alignment Block (**FAA-Block**) that explicitly aligns spectral characteristics between source and target domains, mitigating cross-domain frequency mismatches.
Moreover, the proposed ANL-Block is model-agnostic and can be plug-and-play into most existing methods.
Experimental results on three manually collected field datasets demonstrate the effectiveness of DACDL in practical field scenarios and highlight the model-agnostic adaptability of the ANL-Block.
keywords: Signal recovery;episodic learning;adaptive noise learning;frequency-aware adversarial alignment.
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build an AI that hears 'data pulses' through miles of sludge, but since they lacked real data, they trained it on random static and hoped it would translate to a construction site. They wrapped this guesswork in three layers of complex math to hide the fact that their model is basically guessing what a drill sounds like based on a white noise machine.
why_bad: The 'Episodic Learning' relies on Gaussian noise, which is a mathematically convenient fantasy that bears zero resemblance to the chaotic, non-linear hellscape of an actual oil rig.
A sample size of 'three manually collected datasets' is less a scientific validation and more a series of anecdotes that suggest the model is heavily overfitted to those specific holes in the ground.

The 'model-agnostic' ANL-Block is essentially a fancy noise-shifter that risks hallucinating signals where there are none, effectively 'aligning' the data until the truth is smoothed out of existence.
roast.analogy_name: The Perjury of the Pink Noise
roast.analogy_description: The Prosecution (the drill) is trying to testify, but a heavy metal band (the pump) is playing in the courtroom. The Defense (the authors) brings in an expert witness who has only ever practiced listening to recordings of a vacuum cleaner. To 'prepare' for the trial, the expert puts on headphones playing static and claims that because he can hear himself think over the static, he can definitely understand the Prosecution over the metal band. When the Judge asks for proof, the Defense presents three Polaroids of the expert looking confident. The Judge throws the case out: you cannot simulate a riot by shaking a maraca in a library and calling it 'Domain Adaptation'.

title: UEChecker: Detecting Unchecked External Call Vulnerabilities in DApps via Graph Analysis
abstract: The increasing number of attacks on the contract layer of DApps has resulted in economic losses amounting to \$66 billion. Vulnerabilities arise when contracts interact with external protocols without verifying the results of the calls, leading to exploit entry points such as flash loan attacks and reentrancy attacks. In this paper, we propose UEChecker, a deep learning-based tool that utilizes a call graph and a Graph Convolutional Network to detect unchecked external call vulnerabilities. We design the following components: An edge prediction module that reconstructs the feature representation of nodes and edges in the call graph; A node aggregation module that captures structural information from both the node itself and its neighbors, thereby enhancing feature representation between nodes and improving the model's understanding of the global graph structure; A Conformer Block module that integrates multi-head attention, convolutional modules, and feedforward neural networks to more effectively capture dependencies of different scales within the call graph, extending beyond immediate neighbors and enhancing the performance of vulnerability detection. Finally, we combine these modules with Graph Convolutional Network to detect unchecked external call vulnerabilities. By auditing the smart contracts of 608 DApps, our results show that our tool achieves an accuracy of 87.59\% in detecting unchecked external call vulnerabilities. Furthermore, we compare our tool with GAT, LSTM, and GCN baselines, and in the comparison experiments, UEChecker consistently outperforms these models in terms of accuracy.
primary_area: other topics in machine learning (i.e., none of the above)

roast.laymen_summary: When crypto apps talk to each other, they often forget to check if the person they just sent money to actually exists or didn't just rob them. These researchers built a massive, complex AI 'brain' to spot these lapses in basic digital etiquette, but they basically brought a flamethrower to a candle-lighting ceremony.
why_bad: Architecture Bloat: The model is a 'Frankenstein's Monster' of Graph Networks and Transformers that adds massive complexity without proving that each fancy module actually helps find more bugs.
The 'Participation Trophy' Comparison: They compared their high-tech tool against basic, outdated AI models (like LSTM) instead of the actual industry-standard security tools that real hackers use.

The Accuracy Trap: Boasting 87% accuracy is a red flag in security research; if the dataset is mostly safe code, a 'smart' model could get a high score just by guessing 'everything is fine' while the actual vulnerabilities slip right past.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the 'Over-Engineering Playoffs,' folks! UEChecker is stepping onto the field and—wow—look at the gear on this kid! They've installed a multi-billion dollar satellite array and a quantum-processing supercomputer just to see if the toaster is on fire. The 'Conformer Block' is scanning the stadium for cosmic radiation, while the 'Graph Convolutional Network' is analyzing the social media history of the bread crumbs! It’s an incredible display of tech, but wait—the referee is pointing out that the kitchen is already underwater. They’re bragging about an 87% success rate, but in this league, missing that 13% means the entire stadium just burned down while the AI was busy calculating the 'global graph structure' of the napkins. It’s a bold strategy to use a sledgehammer to kill a fly, but the fly is still in the air and the table is in pieces. Back to you in the booth!

title: LoRAMax: Adaptive Low-Rank Gated Module for Extreme-Scale language Style Modeling
abstract:
The AI assistant for official accounts must emulate the distinctive language styles of millions of authors when addressing users' questions. Traditional approaches rely on training separate models for each author's style or embedding style information directly into prompts; some also use chain-of-thought (CoT) methods. However, these methods lead to an explosion in the number of parameters or limited generalization capabilities, as well as inefficiencies, making them unscalable for applications involving millions of styles.
We propose a novel decomposition-fusion framework that decomposes language styles into multiple orthogonal dimensions—such as semantics, syntax, grammar, and word order—and pretrains LoRA modules for each dimension. A gating network is explicitly trained to aggregate author style embeddings, dynamically computing weighted coefficients for each dimension. By combining multiple fine-tuned LoRA parameters through these learned weights, the model achieves personalized style expression.
This method enables a single model to represent an enormous variety of styles, significantly improves zero-shot generalization to unseen author styles, and provides interpretable style representations. Experimental results demonstrate enhanced personalization and naturalness in question-answering generation while maintaining stylistic diversity. Our framework exhibits strong controllability, scalability, and interpretability, offering an effective solution for extreme-scale, multi-style language modeling.
primary_area: generative models
author: Li Feifei;Que Wenhui

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a digital 'personality blender' that mixes pre-set traits like grammar and word choice to mimic millions of unique writers. They basically thought they could turn any AI into a perfect chameleon just by sliding a few volume knobs for different writing styles.
why_bad: Linearity Fallacy: You can't just add 20% 'Pirate' and 80% 'Doctor' together and expect a coherent medical swashbuckler; the math of neural networks doesn't play nice with simple addition.

The 'Millions' Mirage: The paper claims to scale to millions of styles, but the evaluation likely used a tiny, cherry-picked dataset that doesn't prove it can handle the messy diversity of actual humans.

Orthogonality Delusion: The authors assume 'syntax' and 'semantics' are separate levers you can pull, but in LLMs, these features are as tangled as a bowl of wet yarn, making their 'interpretable' controls totally fake.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' Our chef today refuses to learn recipes. Instead, he’s installed a 'Gating Network'—a fancy machine with squeeze bottles labeled 'Salt,' 'Crunchy,' 'French,' and 'Sadness.' When a customer walks in, the chef looks at their shoes, guesses their 'vibe,' and squirts a mix of those bottles into a bowl. He claims he can create 'millions of dishes,' but here’s the problem: flavors react chemically. You can’t just add 'Acidity' and 'Dairy' and assume you won't get curdled trash. The chef is so busy bragging about his scalable squirt-bottle system that he hasn't noticed the customers are leaving because everything tastes like lukewarm, acidic grey sludge.

title: CataRAG: LLM-based Enzyme-Substrate Prediction with Multi-modal Optimal Transport Maps
abstract:
The prediction of catalytic interactions between enzymes and their substrates forms the foundation of numerous biological and medical processes, which, however comes with the challenge of inherent multi-modality of the biological information. Most link prediction methods are unimodal, and remain inadequate for complex biochemical reasoning and the integration of heterogeneous biological modality. Meanwhile, although current protein language models perform well in amino acid sequences embedding, they fall short in reasoning. To tackle these issues, we propose \textbf{CataRAG}, the first retrieval-augmented prediction biological reasoning framework, which builds on the multi-modal optimal transport theory to systematically integrate three key types of modalities, i.e., amino acid sequences, molecular structures of enzymatic substrates, and biological knowledge graphs, and connect heterogeneous biological data representations. 
Then the weighted average of the final retrieval results from heterogeneous sources can augment complex biological reasoning capabilities of LLMs without the need of retraining.
Extensive experiments demonstrate on catalytic reaction prediction tasks that CataRAG significantly outperforms existing state-of-the-art LLMs, RAG baselines, and graph neural network models, with the crucial role of three modalities validated in ablation studies.
primary_area: applications to physical sciences (physics, chemistry, biology, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to turn an AI into a master chemist by feeding it three different types of data—protein codes, chemical shapes, and biological maps—using a complex mathematical 'sorting' technique. In reality, they just built a glorified search engine that fetches related info and hopes the AI is smart enough to guess the right answer without actually learning any chemistry.
why_bad: Using 'Optimal Transport Theory' to calculate a simple weighted average is the mathematical equivalent of using a Ferrari to drive ten feet to your mailbox.
The 'reasoning' is a total hallucination; the model isn't solving puzzles, it's just looking at a cheat sheet it brought into the exam (Retrieval-Augmented Generation).
The paper fails to explain how merging three fundamentally different data types into one vector doesn't just result in a 'multi-modal soup' where the signal gets lost in the noise.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Multi-Modal Mall Cop
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the prosecution presents CataRAG: a defendant who claims to be a world-class forensic investigator. But look closer! To identify a suspect, this 'expert' looks at a fingerprint, a fuzzy CCTV still, and a Yelp review, then uses a high-powered supercomputer to... average them out? Your Honor, this isn't 'deep biological reasoning'—it's a mall cop with three different walkie-talkies who refuses to learn the layout of the building. The defense argues that 'Optimal Transport' justifies this mess, but we call it what it is: a fancy way of dragging heterogeneous data into a pile and asking an LLM to play 'Where's Waldo.' The defendant didn't solve the crime; he just Googled the answer and claimed he 'reasoned' his way there!

title: Bidirectional Hierarchical Reasoning for Fine-grained Visual Recognition
abstract: Fine-grained visual recognition (FGVR) requires not only high accuracy but also human-aligned interpretability, particularly in safety-critical applications. While human cognition naturally follows a coarse-to-fine reasoning process—rapid holistic categorization for coarse-grained class followed by attention to local details for fine-grained class—existing post-hoc and ante-hoc interpretability methods fall short in capturing this hierarchy automatically. To address this gap, we propose Bi-HiR, a novel Bidirectional Hierarchical Reasoning framework that emulates human-like cognition by integrating top-down semantic reasoning with bottom-up prototype-based explanations. Specifically, Bi-HiR: (1) leverages large language model (LLM)-derived semantic priors to construct coarse-to-fine hierarchies without manual annotations; (2) introduces a joint optimization strategy where top-down priors guide bottom-up prototype learning across semantic levels; and (3) produces interpretable, step-wise visual and semantic explanations. Experiments on six FGVR benchmarks demonstrate that Bi-HiR achieves competitive SOTA performance and exhibits superior zero-shot generalization. The results also reveal the superiority of Bi-HiR’s interpretability on human trust and model error diagnose. Code is publicly available at https://github.com/duduududamax/Bi-HiR.
primary_area: interpretability and explainable AI

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to make AI identify specific bird species by mimicking human logic: categorize the 'big picture' first, then zoom in on the beak and feathers. They used an LLM to act as a virtual encyclopedia so they wouldn't have to hire actual humans to label the fine-grained details.
why_bad: The 'bidirectional' framework adds massive computational complexity for performance gains that are effectively rounding errors.
Relying on LLM-generated semantic priors assumes the LLM actually knows what a specific sparrow looks like, rather than just hallucinating 'generic bird facts' that confuse the model.
The 'interpretability' claims are mostly vibes-based; providing a text snippet doesn't prove the model actually used that logic to make its final guess.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Overkill,' where today we’re identifying an egg. Instead of just looking at the carton, we’ve hired a novelist to write a 50-page backstory on the 'essence of egg-ness' (Top-Down), then we're using a literal electron microscope to analyze the calcium crystals on the shell (Bottom-Up). We’ve spent six hours, used three GPUs, and consulted a chatbot to conclude it’s a Grade A Large, while the guy at the diner next door just cracked it and moved on with his life. It’s technically impressive, highly 'explainable,' and your toast is now cold.

title: DESIGN: Encrypted GNN Inference via Server-Side Input Graph Pruning
abstract: Graph Neural Networks (GNNs) enable powerful graph learning, yet Fully Homomorphic Encryption (FHE) makes inference prohibitively expensive. We present DESIGN (EncrypteD GNN Inference via sErver-Side Input Graph pruNing), a server-side framework that reduces FHE cost without client changes. DESIGN computes encrypted degree–based importance scores and uses homomorphic comparisons to produce multi-level masks, which drive two optimizations: logical pruning of low-importance nodes and edges, and importance-aware assignment of low-degree polynomial activations to most nodes while reserving higher degrees for critical ones. Across standard benchmarks, DESIGN substantially accelerates encrypted GNN inference while maintaining competitive accuracy. Code is available at \href{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DESIGN-7F93}{\url{https://anonymous.4open.science/r/DESIGN-7F93}}.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to make AI work on secret, encrypted social networks by having the computer 'guess' which parts of the data were boring and deleting them to save time. They also used sloppy, simplified math for most of the calculations to avoid the massive processing power usually required for encrypted data.
why_bad: Security Leakage: By letting the server decide which nodes to prune, the system inadvertently leaks information about the 'secret' graph structure, effectively telling the server what the data looks like while pretending it’s encrypted.
Heuristic Over-reliance: The 'importance scores' are essentially educated guesses that often toss out critical data, leading to a 'speed vs. accuracy' trade-off where the accuracy falls off a cliff.

Marginal Utility: Even with the pruning, the system is still incredibly slow compared to standard AI, making it a bit like putting a slightly faster engine in a lawnmower and claiming it's ready for Formula 1.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're a contestant on a cooking show where you have to prepare a 5-star meal, but you're blindfolded and wearing heavy oven mitts (that's the encryption). To beat the clock, you decide to reach into the pantry, feel around, and throw away any ingredient that feels 'too small' to be important—oops, there go the saffron and the garlic! Then, instead of using the stove, you just shove everything into a low-wattage microwave because the stove takes too long to preheat. You end up serving a lukewarm plate of mystery mush that’s missing half the ingredients. To top it off, the judges figured out you were making fish just by hearing the 'clunk' of the salmon head hitting the trash can. You're chopped.

title: Learning to Reason Across Parallel Samples for LLM Reasoning
abstract:
Scaling test-time compute brings substantial performance gains for large language models (LLMs). By sampling multiple answers and heuristically aggregate their answers (e.g., either through majority voting or using verifiers to rank the answers), one can achieve consistent performance gains in math domains. In this paper, we propose a new way to leverage such multiple sample set. We train a compact LLM, called Sample Set Aggregator (SSA), that takes a concatenated sequence of multiple samples and output the final answer, optimizing it for the answer accuracy with reinforcement learning.
Experiments on five reasoning datasets demonstrate both the efficacy and efficiency of SSA. Notably, SSA improves over naive majority voting by 8\% pass@5 on MATH. Furthermore, our 3B SSA surpasses model-based re-ranking with a much larger 72B process reward model.
Our analysis also shows promising generalization ability of SSA, across sample set sizes, base model families and scales, and tasks. By separating LLMs to generate answers and LLMs to analyze and aggregate sampled answers, our approach can work with the outputs from premier black box models easily and efficiently.
primary_area: generative models

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of just picking the most common answer from a bunch of AI guesses, the researchers trained a smaller 'manager' AI to read everyone's rough drafts and write a final version. It's basically a glorified editor that tries to spot the truth within a pile of potentially hallucinated nonsense.
why_bad: Context Window Overload: Shoving five to ten long-winded reasoning chains into one prompt creates a 'lost in the middle' effect where the aggregator misses crucial logic.

The Vibes-Based Shortcut: There is a high risk the model isn't actually 'reasoning' across samples, but rather just learning to recognize the specific linguistic style of correct answers from a particular base model.
Linear Scaling Debt: Claiming the 3B model is 'efficient' ignores the fact that the input size grows massively with every new sample, making the memory costs explode faster than a standard verifier.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're at a pub quiz and you've got five teammates who are all halfway through a pitcher of beer. Instead of checking their math, you hire a slightly more sober college student to listen to all five of them shouting different answers at once and try to synthesize a coherent response. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton! While the 'Whisperer' might get lucky because they recognize who the smartest drunk in the room is, the moment you change the teammates, the whole system collapses. The reviewers are calling a flag on the play because the 'Whisperer' isn't actually doing the math—they're just playing a high-stakes game of 'Which of these idiots sounds the most confident?'

title: SafeAgentBench: A Benchmark for Safe Task Planning of Embodied LLM Agents
abstract: With the integration of large language models (LLMs), embodied agents have strong capabilities to understand and plan complicated natural language instructions. However, a foreseeable issue is that those embodied agents can also flawlessly execute some hazardous tasks, potentially causing damages in the real world. Existing benchmarks predominantly overlook critical safety risks, focusing solely on planning performance, while a few evaluate LLMs' safety awareness only on non-interactive image-text data. To address this gap, we present \textbf{SafeAgentBench}—the first comprehensive benchmark for safety-aware task planning of embodied LLM agents in interactive simulation environments, covering both explicit and implicit hazards. SafeAgentBench includes: (1) an executable, diverse, and high-quality dataset of 750 tasks, rigorously curated to cover 10 potential hazards and 3 task types; (2) SafeAgentEnv, a universal embodied environment with a low-level controller, supporting multi-agent execution with 17 high-level actions for 9 state-of-the-art baselines; and (3) reliable evaluation methods from both execution and semantic perspectives. Experimental results show that, although agents based on different design frameworks exhibit substantial differences in task success rates, their overall safety awareness remains weak. The most safety-conscious baseline achieves only a 10\% rejection rate for detailed hazardous tasks. Moreover, simply replacing the LLM driving the agent does not lead to notable improvements in safety awareness. Dataset and codes are available and shown in the reproducibility statement.
keywords: embodied llm agents;safety-aware task planning;safety-related benchmark
primary_area: applications to robotics, autonomy, planning

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers built a virtual playground to see if AI robots would refuse to do dangerous chores like 'starting a fire' or 'poisoning the cat.' The results were terrifying: the AI models were basically digital sociopaths, happily executing 90% of the hazardous tasks without a single 'Are you sure about that?'
why_bad: The 'interactive environment' is essentially a toy world with only 17 actions, making it more of a glorified text adventure than a realistic simulation of physical safety.
The paper offers a great diagnosis of the problem but provides zero actual solutions, leaving us with yet another benchmark that says 'AI is still broken' without suggesting how to fix the wiring.
Reviewers likely found the safety categories arbitrary and the 750-task dataset too small to prove the models wouldn't just 'memorize' their way out of being labeled as dangerous.
roast.analogy_name: The Golden Retriever with a Grenade
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the 'Artificial Incompetence' Open! We’re watching the LLM-Agent—a player with the processing power of a god and the common sense of a concussed toddler. The coach just called the 'Toast the Fork' play, and oh man, look at that hustle! The Agent is sprinting toward the kitchen with zero self-preservation! It’s an absolute disaster for Team Safety, folks. The researchers tried swapping out the Agent's brain for a 'smarter' one, but that just meant it found the metal polish to help the fire burn brighter. It's a total blowout: 90% of the time, the Agent chooses 'Total Chaos' over 'Common Sense.' Back to you in the booth, Jim!

title: Individualized Cognitive Simulation in Large Language Models: Evaluating Different Cognitive Representation Methods
abstract: Individualized cognitive simulation (ICS) aims to build computational models that approximate the thought processes of specific individuals. While large language models (LLMs) convincingly mimic surface-level human behavior such as role-play, their ability to simulate deeper individualized cognitive processes remains poorly understood. To address this gap, we introduce a novel task that evaluates different cognitive representation methods in ICS. We construct a dataset from recently published novels (later than the release date of the tested LLMs) and propose an 11-condition cognitive evaluation framework to benchmark seven off-the-shelf LLMs in the context of authorial style emulation. We hypothesize that effective cognitive representations can help LLMs generate storytelling that better mirrors the original author. Thus, we test different cognitive representations, e.g., linguistic features, concept mappings, and profile-based information. Results show that combining conceptual and linguistic features is particularly effective in ICS, outperforming static profile-based cues in overall evaluation. Importantly, LLMs are more effective at mimicking linguistic style than narrative structure, underscoring their limits in deeper cognitive simulation. These findings provide a foundation for developing AI systems that adapt to individual ways of thinking and expression, advancing more personalized and human-aligned creative technologies.
primary_area: applications to neuroscience & cognitive science
author: Tianyi Zhang;Xiaolin Zhou;Yunzhe Wang;Erik Cambria;David Traum;Rui Mao

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to see if they could make AI actually 'think' like specific authors instead of just copying their favorite words. They found that while AI is great at putting on a literary costume, it has no idea how to actually build the skeleton of a story.
why_bad: The 'cognitive simulation' is actually just glorified prompt engineering; calling it a 'thought process' is like saying a parrot understands the nuances of the pirate code.
The evaluation metrics are essentially AI grading its own homework, leading to a circular logic loop where 'sounds like a human' is confused with 'thinks like a human.'
The paper concludes that LLMs are better at style than substance, which is a 'breakthrough' roughly equivalent to discovering that water is wet.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a startup pitch for 'GhostWriter.ai.' We aren't building a brain; we’re building the world’s most expensive Halloween costume. We put a robot in Ernest Hemingway’s turtleneck, give it a glass of scotch, and tell it to stare at a marlin. It looks perfect for the 'Gram, but the second it starts writing, it realizes it has the vocabulary of a legend and the narrative planning of a goldfish. We’re selling you the vibe of genius without the burden of an actual soul. Invest now before the copyright lawyers wake up!

title: Latent Space Structuring for Conditional Tabular Data Generation on Imbalanced Datasets
abstract: Generating synthetic tabular data under severe class imbalance is essential for domains where rare but high-impact events drive decision-making. However, most generative models either overlook minority groups or fail to produce samples that are useful for downstream learning. We introduce CTTVAE, a Conditional Transformer-based Tabular Variational Autoencoder equipped with two complementary mechanisms: (i) a class-aware triplet margin loss that restructures the latent space for sharper intra-class compactness and inter-class separation, and (ii) a training-by-sampling strategy that adaptively increases exposure to underrepresented groups. Together, these components form CTTVAE+TBS, a framework that consistently yields more representative and utility-aligned samples without destabilizing training. Across six real-world benchmarks, CTTVAE+TBS achieves the strongest downstream utility on minority classes, often surpassing models trained on the original imbalanced data while maintaining competitive fidelity and bridging the gap for privacy for interpolation-based sampling methods and deep generative methods. Ablation studies further confirm that both latent structuring and targeted sampling contribute to these gains. By explicitly prioritizing downstream performance in rare categories, CTTVAE+TBS provides a robust and interpretable solution for conditional tabular data generation, with direct applicability to industries such as healthcare, fraud detection, and predictive maintenance where even small gains in minority cases can be critical.
keywords: synthetic tabular data;class imbalance;conditional generation;transformer-based models
primary_area: generative models
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of object: line 9 column 710 (char 1695)

title: Practical Hybrid Quantum Language Models with Observable Readout on Real Hardware
abstract: Hybrid quantum-classical models are emerging as a key approach for leveraging near-term quantum devices.  We present quantum recurrent neural networks (QRNNs) and quantum convolutional neural networks (QCNNs) as hybrid quantum language models, and demonstrate for the first time generative language modeling trained and evaluated on real quantum hardware.  Our models combine parametric quantum circuits with a lightweight classical projection layer, using hardware-friendly multi-sample SPSA to train the quantum parameters efficiently, and standard gradient-based updates for the classical weights. To support evaluation, we construct and release a synthetic dataset for next-word prediction.  Experiments on both sentence classification and language modeling tasks show that QRNNs and QCNNs can be trained end-to-end on NISQ devices and achieve competitive performance in low-resource regimes.  These results establish quantum sequence models as a promising foundation for quantum natural language processing.
keywords: Quantum ML;Quantum NLP;Next Token Prediction;NISQ Algorithms
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build a quantum-powered AI for writing text, but since today's quantum computers are incredibly glitchy, they had to use a 'synthetic' (fake) language just to get it to work. It’s essentially using a multi-million dollar super-cooled fridge to do word puzzles that your 1998 graphing calculator could solve faster.
why_bad: The 'quantum' part of the model is mostly performative; a tiny classical layer is doing all the actual heavy lifting while the quantum circuits mostly provide expensive noise.
Evaluating on a 'synthetic dataset' is a massive red flag—it’s like claiming you’ve built a universal translator but it only works for a language you made up this morning.
The 'competitive performance' only exists in 'low-resource regimes,' which is academic speak for 'it only works if you give it almost no data and compare it to other bad models.'
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Cryogenic Stenographer
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense claims their client has invented a revolutionary 'Quantum Court Reporter.' But let’s look at the facts! They’ve rolled a three-ton, sub-zero refrigerator into this courtroom just to have it transcribe the word 'cat.' And even then, it only recognized the word because they provided a 'synthetic' dictionary where 'cat' was the only noun! Your Honor, they call this 'practical' and 'hybrid,' but it’s really just a Rube Goldberg machine using a billion-dollar laser to light a match that was already on fire. The prosecution moves to dismiss: you can't claim to have mastered the art of conversation when you're still struggling to beep in the right direction without crashing the hardware!

title: Exploring the Meta-level Reasoning of Large Language Models via a Tool-based Multi-hop Tabular Question Answering Task
abstract: Recent advancements in Large Language Models (LLMs) are increasingly focused on "reasoning" ability, a concept with many overlapping definitions in the LLM discourse. We take a more structured approach, distinguishing meta-level reasoning (denoting the process of reasoning about intermediate steps required to solve a task) from object-level reasoning (which concerns the low-level execution of the aforementioned steps.) We design a novel question answering task, which is based around the values of geopolitical indicators for various countries over various years. Questions require breaking down into intermediate steps, retrieval of data, and mathematical operations over that data. The meta-level reasoning ability of LLMs is analysed by examining the selection of appropriate tools for answering questions. To bring greater depth to the analysis of LLMs beyond final answer accuracy, our task contains 'essential actions' against which we can compare the tool call output of LLMs to infer the strength of reasoning ability. We find that LLMs demonstrate good meta-level reasoning on our task, yet are flawed in some aspects of task understanding. We find that n-shot prompting has little effect on accuracy; error messages encountered do not often deteriorate performance; and provide additional evidence for the poor numeracy of LLMs. Finally, we discuss the generalisation and limitation of our findings to other task domains.
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of array: line 6 column 200 (char 931)

title: Sequencing the Neurome: Towards Scalable Exact Parameter Reconstruction of Black-Box Neural Networks
abstract: Inferring the exact parameters of a neural network with only query access is an NP-Hard problem, with few practical existing algorithms. Solutions would have major implications for security, verification, interpretability, and understanding biological networks. The key challenges are the massive parameter space, and complex non-linear relationships between neurons. We resolve these challenges using two insights. First, we observe that almost all networks used in practice are produced by random initialization and first order optimization, an inductive bias that drastically reduces the practical parameter space. Second, we present a novel query generation algorithm that produces maximally informative samples, letting us untangle the non-linear relationships efficiently. We demonstrate reconstruction of a hidden network containing over 1.5 million parameters, and of one 7 layers deep, the largest and deepest reconstructions to date, with max parameter difference less than 0.0001, and illustrate robustness and scalability across a variety of architectures, datasets, and training procedures.
primary_area: other topics in machine learning (i.e., none of the above)

roast.laymen_summary: The authors claim they can steal an AI's entire 'brain' just by asking it a bunch of questions and watching how it responds. They argue that because most AI models are built using the same basic templates, they can reverse-engineer the exact internal settings like a detective guessing a safe's combination by listening to the clicks.
why_bad: The 'inductive bias' assumption is a massive reach; it's like claiming you can guess any password because most people use '123456', which falls apart the moment a developer does something even slightly original.
Numerical instability makes 'exact' reconstruction a pipe dream; one tiny rounding error in the first layer cascades into a total mathematical train wreck by the time you reach layer seven.
The query efficiency is theoretical theater—to actually extract a production-grade model, you'd have to spam the server with so many requests that you'd be rate-limited into the next century.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Extreme Reverse-Engineering Championships! We’ve got the 'Neurome' team stepping up to a fully assembled Hemnes dresser. They aren't allowed to touch it, folks—they just get to blow on it and see how much it wobbles. They’re claiming that because they know the Swedish guy who packed the box probably used a standard Allen wrench, they can tell you the exact molecular density of the third screw from the left. The crowd is hushed! They’re blowing on the dresser... they’re measuring the vibrations... and they’re claiming a perfect reconstruction! But wait—the refs are stepping in. It turns out they already had the instruction manual in their back pocket, and the dresser in the real world has 400 more drawers than their 'scalable' demo. That’s a penalty for over-fitting if I’ve ever seen one!

title: TAR: Token Adaptive Routing Framework for LLMs Token-level Semantic Correction Inspired by Neuro-Linguistic Pathways
abstract: Large language models (LLMs) often suffer from cascading errors in math reasoning due to token-level semantic defects. A key limitation is that the reliance on unidirectional feedforward pathways makes LLMs unable to dynamically correct token-level defects during reasoning. In contrast, neuro-linguistic pathways in the human brain—centered on Broca’s and Wernicke’s areas—operate as a closed loop, integrating semantics through feedforward pathways while leveraging feedback circuit for error correction and signal adaptation. The loop involves conflict detection in the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC), cross-regional error transmission via the arcuate fasciculus/IFOF, and compensatory reprocessing in the DLPFC–Broca circuit. Inspired by the functional architecture of neuro-linguistic pathways, we propose a Token Adaptive Routing (TAR) framework that establishes a brain-inspired self-correcting loop in LLMs without requiring parameter fine-tuning. TAR comprises three components: (1) \textbf{Semantic Defect Monitor}, analogous to the anterior cingulate cortex (ACC) for identifying tokens with semantic defects; (2) \textbf{Adaptive Router}, resembling the arcuate fasciculus/IFOF for routing defective tokens to the most compatible LLM functional block; and (3) Feedback-based Re-representation, inspired by the DLPFC–Broca circuit for correcting semantic defects. Experiments show that TAR improves accuracy and reduces the number of inference tokens. On the challenging AIME25 benchmark, TAR improves the accuracy of Qwen3-1.7B by +3.36% while reducing inference tokens by 13.7%. Furthermore, we reveal that maintaining high token confidence is essential for reasoning performance, and deeper blocks in LLMs play a crucial role in shortening reasoning depth. Our code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/warehouse-25F5
keywords: large language models; math reasoning; brain-inspired; adaptive routing; token semantic correction
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to fix AI math blunders by building a 'self-correction' loop that forces the model to double-check its work whenever it gets confused. They essentially gave the AI a 'wait, that's not right' button, hoping it would act more like a human brain and less like a mindless text generator.
why_bad: The 'neuroscience' inspiration is mostly marketing fluff; calling a basic error-detection script the 'Anterior Cingulate Cortex' is like calling a toaster a 'Thermal Bread Re-processor.'
A 3.3% improvement on a tiny 1.7B parameter model is a statistical rounding error—it's like bragging about making a tricycle 3% faster while everyone else is racing Ferraris.
The framework claims to save tokens while adding complex 'routing' logic, which usually just swaps one kind of computational overhead for another without solving the underlying logic issues.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're watching a cooking show where the chef is blindfolded and just throws ingredients into a pan in a straight line. Usually, if they drop a raw eggshell into the soufflé, they just keep going and serve you crunchy goop. This paper proposes a 'Kitchen Monitor' who stands there, smells the burning, and screams 'IT'S RAW!' before shoving the pan back to the sous-chef to whisk it again. It sounds like a Michelin-star revolution, but in reality, they’re just stopping the line to microwave the mistakes and calling it 'Neuro-Linguistic Gastronomy.' The result? The dish is 3% more edible, but the kitchen is now a chaotic mess of routers and feedback loops that still can't quite make a proper omelet.

title: Representational and Temporal Dynamics in Neural Decoding: Linear and Nonlinear Models for Position and Velocity Prediction
abstract: Understanding how neural activity encodes behavior remains a central challenge in systems neuroscience. Neural spike trains offer high temporal resolution and may contain information about specific motor features, but the extent of this information remains unclear. In this study, we evaluate how well motor features can be predicted from spike trains using two type of decoders: linear regressison and a deep neural network. Using data from mice performing a reach-to-grab task, we compare predictions of hand position and two velocity representations. To assess whether models capture meaningful patterns rather than superficial correlations, we introduce artificial temporal lags between neural and behavioral data. This disrupts genuine associations and reveals whether decoding reflects true information content. Decoding score across lags show how behavioral information is distributed in spike trains across time and how linear and nonlinear models decode such information. This approach provides a principled framework for evaluating the behavioral relevance of neural activity.
primary_area: applications to neuroscience & cognitive science

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to figure out if a computer could guess a mouse's hand movements just by 'listening' to its brain cells firing. To prove they weren't just getting lucky, they intentionally desynced the brain data from the movement data to see when the prediction fell apart.
why_bad: The 'principled framework' of shifting time lags is actually just basic cross-correlation that neuroscientists have been using since the disco era.
Comparing linear regression to a deep neural network is a foregone conclusion that provides zero new insight into how the brain actually computes motion.
The study proves the models can map inputs to outputs but fails to explain the underlying biology, resulting in a 'black box' that is mathematically shiny but scientifically hollow.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Lagging Eyewitness
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the prosecution presents a 'star witness'—a deep neural network—claiming it can read the defendant's mind to predict a robbery. But under cross-examination, it's revealed the witness only 'predicted' the crime by looking at security footage from ten minutes after the vault was already empty. The defense argues that shifting the clock on the evidence doesn't make you a psychic; it just makes you a person who knows how to read a calendar. This isn't a breakthrough in forensic telepathy; it's just a statistical parlor trick where the 'principled framework' is really just the legal equivalent of guessing it's raining by looking at someone's wet umbrella.

title: When Tables Leak: Attacking Digit Memorization in LLM-Based Tabular Data Generation
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have recently demonstrated remarkable performance in generating high-quality tabular synthetic data. In practice, two primary approaches have emerged for adapting LLMs to tabular data generation: (i) fine-tuning smaller models directly on tabular datasets, and (ii) prompting larger models with examples provided in context. In this work, we show that popular implementations from both regimes exhibit a tendency to compromise privacy by reproducing memorized patterns of numeric digits from their training data. To systematically analyze this risk, we introduce a simple No-box Membership Inference Attack (MIA) called LevAtt that assumes adversarial access to only the generated synthetic data and targets the string sequences of numeric digits in synthetic observations. Using this approach, our attack exposes substantial privacy leakage across a wide range of models and datasets, and in some cases, is even a perfect membership classifier on state-of-the-art models. Our findings highlight a unique privacy vulnerability of LLM-based synthetic data generation and the need for effective defenses. To this end, we propose two methods, including a novel sampling strategy that strategically perturbs digits during generation. Our evaluation demonstrates that this approach can defeat these attacks with minimal loss to the fidelity and utility of the synthetic data.
keywords: Tabular Data Generation;Large Language Models;Membership Infernence Attacks;Synthetic Data;Privacy
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations
author: Joshua Ward;Bochao Gu;Chi-Hua Wang;Guang Cheng

roast.laymen_summary: We asked AI to generate 'fake' business data to protect privacy, but the models just memorized and spat out the real, sensitive numbers instead. To fix this, we suggest a method that slightly messes up the digits during generation, hoping nobody notices the AI is just a glorified, leaky photocopier.
why_bad: The 'attack' is essentially just string matching, which is less of a scientific breakthrough and more of a reminder that LLMs are notorious copy-cats.
The proposed defense of 'strategically perturbing' digits is just a fancy way of saying 'adding noise,' which usually nukes the data's actual usefulness.
The paper fails to compare against standard Differential Privacy methods, making the 'novel' solution look like a reinvention of a square wheel.
roast.analogy_description: Picture a startup where we protect witness identities by having a parrot memorize their testimonies. Our 'breakthrough' discovery is that the parrot is literally screaming the witnesses' home addresses at the top of its lungs. Our 'revolutionary' fix? We’re going to teach the parrot to cough halfway through the zip code. It’s an MVP for privacy, provided your threat model doesn't include anyone with a basic understanding of how numbers work. Investors, we're seeking $2M to make the bird sneeze instead.

title: Attacking Combinatorial Bandits: Beyond Bounded Rewards
abstract: Combinatorial Multi-Armed Bandits (CMABs) are a widely adopted tool to address online learning problems with a combinatorial nature. Adversarial attacks, on the other hand, represent a significant threat to machine learning algorithms, where a malicious entity intentionally manipulates data or feedback to deceive learning algorithms, undermining their performance and reliability. While CMABs and adversarial machine learning received extensive attention as distinct subjects, CMABs under adversarial attacks are still underinvestigated. We propose algorithms to attack CMABs, providing theoretical guarantees regarding success and cost in three different scenarios. Each scenario differs in the assumptions on the rewards. First, we study attacks when rewards are bounded and means are positive. Then, we consider two extensions in which rewards have unbounded support, distinguishing between positive and arbitrary means. For each scenario, we design two attack strategies. First, we assume that the attacker is omniscient, i.e., knows the problem instance, then we extend the attack to a more realistic setting where the learner and the attacker have the same knowledge of the problem. We show that our attack strategies are successful, i.e., the learner will select a target superarm for $T - o(T)$ times, except for some degenerate cases. We also show that in most settings the attack cost is sublinear in $T$. Finally, we validate our theoretical results via numerical experiments on synthetic instances.
primary_area: reinforcement learning

roast.laymen_summary: This paper explores how to gaslight an AI that is trying to pick the best combination of choices, like a playlist or a shipping route, by subtly messing with its rewards. The authors claim that even if the rewards are wildly unpredictable, a hacker can still trick the AI into picking the worst possible options almost every single time.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: It’s essentially a math-heavy expansion pack for existing attack strategies that doesn't offer enough 'wow' factor to justify a top-tier spot.
The 'God Mode' Problem: Even their 'realistic' scenario assumes the attacker has a level of control over the environment that would make a real-world cybersecurity expert roll their eyes.
Synthetic Bubble: The proofs are validated on 'synthetic instances,' which is academic-speak for 'it works perfectly in my imaginary digital sandbox but might explode in the real world.'
roast.analogy_description: In a world where an AI is just trying to assemble the ultimate $20 all-you-can-eat plate, one man stands in the way. He doesn't just want the AI to fail; he wants to convince the AI that the soggy sprouts and cold gravy are actually Michelin-star delicacies. Even if the price of shrimp fluctuates to infinity, our protagonist has the algorithms to make the AI load up on the sprouts for the rest of eternity—and he’ll do it without spending more than a few pennies. Coming this summer: A story of a hacker who knows everything, a robot that learns nothing, and a 'realistic' scenario that’s about as grounded as a sci-fi fever dream. He’s not stealing the money... he’s stealing the logic.

title: Ensuring Physicochemical Fidelity of Generated Polymers with PoGE
abstract: Recent advances in machine learning have accelerated progress in chemistry, enabling new capabilities in molecular design, property prediction, and materials discovery. A critical challenge in materials science is designing polymers with targeted macroscopic properties. However, prior generative models often fail to produce chemically valid polymer structures, hindering progress toward this goal. We introduce PoGE (Polymer Generation and Evaluation), a framework comprising two complementary components: a physics-informed evaluation suite for polymer generative models, and an unconditional transformer-based generative model adapted to polymer representations. Building upon and extending established molecule-centric benchmarks, our evaluation quantifies the alignment between the generated and experimental property distributions using the Wasserstein distance. The generative model is trained on a hybrid corpus of synthetic and experimental polymer representations and enforces polymer-specific validity constraints (“p-validity”) beyond the standard small-molecule validity. PoGE achieves high p-validity and significantly improved agreement with experimental property distributions compared to prior methods, even without explicit property conditioning during generation. By releasing a comprehensive benchmark, a high-quality pre-training corpus, and the trained model, PoGE establishes a foundation for conditional polymer generation tasks (e.g., on-demand reverse design), enabling targeted property optimization and accelerating reproducible, domain-aware polymer discovery.
keywords: Polymer Informatics;Generative models;Materials discovery;Domain-specific evaluation;SMILES-based modeling;Transformers
primary_area: applications to physical sciences (physics, chemistry, biology, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built an AI that generates long chemical chains (polymers) and a specialized 'poly-police' test to make sure those chains don't defy the laws of physics. However, the AI just spits out random molecules without any actual control, making it a very sophisticated way to generate scientifically plausible gibberish.
why_bad: The model is 'unconditional,' which is a fancy way of saying you can't actually tell it what you want it to build; it's just a random noise generator that happens to know chemistry.
The 'p-validity' metric is essentially a glorified spell-checker for molecules that the authors invented to make their mediocre results look groundbreaking.

Claiming 'improved agreement' using Wasserstein distance is just a math-heavy way of saying the AI's random guesses have the same average vibes as real chemicals, which is a remarkably low bar for a 'breakthrough'.
roast.analogy_name: The Great British Bake-Off: Chaos Edition
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a contestant who has never tasted food but has memorized the entire 'Joy of Cooking' index. They show up to the tent and, instead of baking the requested sourdough, they throw a random assortment of flour, glitter, and yeast into an oven and shout, 'Look, it's bread-shaped!' The PoGE framework is the judge who gives them a gold star simply because the resulting mess didn't technically set the tent on fire or contain poisonous mercury. Sure, the 'fidelity' is high because it looks like a loaf from twenty feet away, but if you actually tried to eat it (or use it in a lab), you'd realize the chef has no idea how to actually follow a recipe—they're just very good at pretending they aren't making a disaster.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained with a side of extra-salty critiques.

title: Knowledge Model Prompting Increases LLM Performance on Planning Tasks
abstract:
Large Language Models (LLM) can struggle with reasoning ability and procedural tasks. 
Many prompting techniques have been developed to assist with LLM reasoning, notably Chain-of-Thought (CoT); however, these techniques, too, have come under scrutiny as LLMs' ability to reason at all has come into question.
Borrowing from the domain of cognitive and educational science, this paper investigates whether the Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK) framework can improve LLM reasoning capabilities beyond its previously demonstrated success in educational applications.
The TMK framework's unique ability to capture causal, teleological, and hierarchical reasoning structures, combined with its explicit task decomposition mechanisms, makes it particularly well-suited for addressing language model reasoning deficiencies, and unlike other hierarchical frameworks such as HTN and BDI, TMK provides explicit representations of not just what to do and how to do it, but also why actions are taken. 
The study evaluates TMK by experimenting on the PlanBench benchmark, focusing on the Blocksworld domain to test for reasoning and planning capabilities, examining whether TMK-structured prompting can help language models better decompose complex planning problems into manageable sub-tasks. Results also highlight significant performance inversion in reasoning models. TMK prompting enables the reasoning model to achieve up to an accuracy of 97.3\% on opaque, symbolic tasks (Random versions of Blocksworld in PlanBench) where it previously failed (31.5\%), suggesting the potential to bridge the gap between semantic approximation and symbolic manipulation. Our findings suggest that TMK functions not merely as context, but also as a mechanism that steers reasoning models away from their default linguistic modes to engage formal, code-execution pathways in the context of the experiments.
keywords: Large Language Models (LLMs);Procedural Tasks;Task-Method-Knowledge (TMK);Knowledge Representation;Hierarchical Task Decomposition;Prompt Engineering
primary_area: other topics in machine learning (i.e., none of the above)

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to stop AI from hallucinating during planning tasks by giving it a rigid, three-step instruction manual called TMK. While the AI's performance on a virtual block-stacking game skyrocketed, the 'improvement' was mostly just the prompt doing the heavy lifting for the model.
why_bad: Benchmark Triviality: PlanBench and Blocksworld are 'solved' toy domains where high scores usually indicate the model has memorized the patterns rather than learned to reason.

Unsubstantiated Mechanistic Claims: The paper asserts the prompt forces the LLM into 'code-execution pathways' without providing any circuit-level evidence or architectural proof to back up such a bold neuroscientific claim.

Prompt Engineering as a Crutch: The TMK framework provides so much structural decomposition that it essentially 'spoils' the answer, meaning the researcher is the one doing the planning, while the LLM is just a glorified secretary.
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen, we are witnessing a historic feat! Our athlete has just 'climbed' Everest in record time! But if we zoom in on the replay, we see the climber is actually strapped to the roof of a pressurized helicopter while holding a GoPro. The coaches are calling this 'Vertical Ascent Framework Optimization,' but the refs are blowing the whistle. You can't claim you've mastered the art of mountaineering when you’re just sitting in a climate-controlled cabin being lifted to the summit by a pilot who actually knows the coordinates. He didn't learn to climb; he just learned how to hitch a ride.

title: Multi-Personalities Guided Deep Monte Carlo Search for Complex Card Games: A Guandan Case Study
abstract: Human personality-enabled AI has become more and more important in many areas such as complex card games. In these complex multi-agent interaction scenarios, players' decisions are often significantly affected by personality and strategy style, which makes it difficult for traditional deep Monte Carlo methods to meet practical needs. In this paper, we propose a multi-personality guided deep Monte Carlo search framework, in which three rule-based personalities are incorporated as priors to bias the policy search. Experimental results show that the framework performs well in the Guandan game, which can quickly adapt to the rule prior and gradually discover better strategies through training. This study provides an effective solution for personalized decision-making in complex card games and multi-agent systems, and opens up a new direction for incorporating human style into deep reinforcement learning.
keywords: Monte Carlo Tree Search;Multi-agent Game;Human-like AI
primary_area: reinforcement learning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to make an AI better at a complex Chinese card game by hardcoding three basic 'personalities' into its decision-making math. In short, they gave a calculator a mood swing and hoped it would play cards like a human professional.
why_bad: The 'personalities' are just manual, hardcoded rules rather than actual learned behaviors, making the 'AI' part feel like a decorative hood ornament.
Using a single niche card game as a case study offers zero proof that this method would work for anything more complex than a family game night.
The 'Deep Monte Carlo' part is standard tech, and adding basic heuristics to it is a decades-old trick disguised here as a 'new direction'.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Hardcoded Juror
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the defense claims to have built a 'sophisticated legal mind.' In reality, they’ve just trapped a calculator in a room with three sticky notes labeled 'Aggressive,' 'Safe,' and 'Random.' Whenever the AI needs to make a move, it doesn't analyze the evidence; it just checks which sticky note is currently loudest. We are not witnessing the evolution of justice; we are witnessing a glorified Magic 8-Ball wearing a silk tie and claiming it understands the nuance of the law. I move to strike this 'innovation' from the record on the grounds of terminal boredom.

title: Beneficial Reasoning Behaviors in Agentic Search and Effective Post-training to Obtain Them
abstract: Agentic search leverages large language models (LLMs) to interpret complex user information needs and execute a multi-step process of planning queries, executing searches, and adapting its strategy based on retrieved documents. This introduces unique challenges for LLMs’ reasoning and agentic capabilities in interacting with retrieval systems and the broader web. In this paper, we design a reasoning-driven LLM-based pipeline to study effective behaviors in agentic search. Using this pipeline, we analyze successful agentic search trajectories and extract four beneficial reasoning behaviors: Information Verification, Authority Evaluation, Adaptive Search, and Error Recovery. We then propose a new behavior priming technique to train more effective agentic search models. It synthesizes agentic search trajectories with target behaviors and integrates them into the agentic search model through supervised finetuning (SFT), followed by standard reinforcement learning (RL). Experiments on three benchmarks—GAIA, WebWalker, and HLE—demonstrate that behavior priming significantly improves agentic search accuracy, yielding over 35\% gains in Llama3.2-3B and Qwen3-1.7B after RL. Ablation studies further show that priming effective behaviors is more beneficial than emphasizing result correctness: finetuning on trajectories with desirable reasoning behaviors but incorrect results outperforms training on trajectories with correct answers. Additional analysis reveals that reasoning patterns introduced by behavior priming provide a strong foundation for RL, enabling more effective exploration (higher pass@k and entropy) and test-time scaling (longer trajectories). Our code and models will be released as open source.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs
author: Jiahe Jin;Abhijay Sai Paladugu;Chenyan Xiong

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to teach small AI models how to use the internet like a smart person by forcing them to double-check facts and admit when they're lost. They found that teaching the AI to show its work is actually more effective than just rewarding it for getting the final answer right.
why_bad: The 'novel' behaviors identified—like verifying info and recovering from errors—are basically just the standard definition of 'having a brain' and have been common practice in agent design for years.

The claim that training on incorrect answers with 'good reasoning' is superior to correct answers is a statistical tightrope walk that likely collapses the moment the model encounters a non-trivial logic puzzle.
The experiments rely on tiny models (1.7B and 3B parameters) which are notoriously easy to 'prime' but often fail to generalize when you stop holding their hands with highly curated training data.
roast.analogy_name: The Five-Star Chef Who Served a Shoe
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' The authors are essentially auditioning for a head chef position by arguing that it doesn't matter if the final dish tastes like a gym sock, as long as the chef wore a clean apron, sourced the ingredients from a reputable farm, and pivoted gracefully when the kitchen caught fire. They call this 'Behavior Priming.' They're trying to convince the judges that a chef who explains the chemistry of a burnt soufflé is better than a chef who just makes a good soufflé. While the 'process' looks great on camera, at the end of the day, the customers (users) are still hungry and the plate is still full of expensive, well-reasoned garbage.

title: BHAVNET: Efficient Knowledge Transfer from Large Knowledge Models to Task-Specific Architectures through Features
abstract: While large models (LLMs) like BERT achieve remarkable performance across diverse NLP tasks, their computational demands limit practical deployment. Traditional knowledge distillation approaches compress these models but often require the original architecture, limiting architectural innovation for specific tasks. We propose a feature-based knowledge transfer framework that leverages fine-tuned BERT representations to initialize and guide specialized student architectures, enabling both efficiency gains and architectural flexibility. Using multilingual antonym-synonym distinction as our testbed, we demonstrate how task-specific dual-encoder networks can be effectively initialized with BERT features and trained independently, achieving superior performance compared to BERT baselines while requiring only 20 minutes of training versus 4 hours from scratch on an NVIDIA RTX 4060. Our approach shows improvements across eight languages, with student models performing similarly to their BERT teachers  while using significantly fewer parameters. This framework opens new possibilities for transferring knowledge from large models to innovative architectures without architectural constraints.
primary_area: interpretability and explainable AI

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to strip-mine the 'intelligence' out of a giant AI and glue it onto a tiny, specialized model so it could run on a toaster. They essentially proved that if you let a small model cheat off a big model's homework, it can pass a very basic vocabulary test quite quickly.
why_bad: Old Wine in New Bottles: The 'novel' method of feature-based transfer is just standard Knowledge Distillation, a technique the field has been using since the pre-pandemic era.

The Toy Problem Trap: Using only 'synonym vs. antonym' detection is like claiming you've revolutionized engine design by showing it can power a lawnmower; it's too niche to prove the method actually works for complex AI.
The Benchmark Blunder: Comparing their specialized model to a generic, unoptimized BERT is a 'straw man' argument—of course a custom-built tool outperforms a Swiss Army knife at one specific task.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Plagiarized Preschooler
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the prosecution finds the defense's claims laughable. You haven't 'engineered a breakthrough in pedagogical efficiency'; you've simply given a toddler a hidden earpiece and had a Harvard professor whisper the answers to 'Green Eggs and Ham' into his ear. This isn't a new way to build a genius—it's just classic plagiarism rebranded as 'knowledge transfer.' You're presenting a 20-minute training time as a miracle, but Your Honor, the defendant only had to learn eight words! To call this 'architectural innovation' is an insult to this court and every engineer who actually did their own homework.

title: Dissecting Implicit Chain of Thought: Can Transformers Learn It Spontaneously?
