abstract: Large language models have gained powerful reasoning abilities through step-by-step reasoning chains, enabling deep logical reasoning and complex task handling. However, the shift from fast thinking to slow thinking (i.e., lengthy explicit reasoning steps) leads to massive token consumption, severely compromising practical reasoning efficiency. To address this, existing studies attempt to compress reasoning chains into latent tokens (Implicit CoT), but they often suffer from significant performance loss due to inadequate expression of original reasoning semantics. This paper theoretically analyzes the additional training costs of Implicit CoT when latent tokens lack effective supervision: as more steps are compressed, the originally efficient learning chain learning degrades exponentially, even reverting to the performance of "no CoT" when all intermediate processes are compressed.  To solve this, we propose a distribution alignment method that adds moderate supervisory information to guide latent token distribution. Experimental results show that intermediate state supervision   effectively improves the learning efficiency and stability of implicit CoT, significantly mitigating its reasoning performance decline.
keywords: transformers;LLM;Implicit Chain of Thought;Latent Chain of Thought
primary_area: learning theory

roast.laymen_summary: AI models are currently like toddlers who have to narrate every single step of tying their shoes, which is slow, annoying, and expensive. This paper tried to make them think silently, but discovered that if you don't micromanage their 'hidden thoughts,' the models essentially have a brain fart and forget how to reason entirely.
why_bad: The 'spontaneous' part of the title is a total bait-and-switch; the authors ended up having to supervise the model so much it basically became a regular, boring training session.
The theoretical 'exponential degradation' discovery is essentially a fancy, math-heavy way of saying 'if you don't show your work, you fail the test,' which we've known since the third grade.
Even with their special 'alignment' fix, the performance still gets absolutely smoked by models that just talk out loud, making the 'efficiency' gains look like a desperate cope.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Mime with a Teleprompter
roast.analogy_description: Ladies and gentlemen of the jury, the defense claims this AI can perform 'implicit reasoning.' They want you to believe a witness can deliver a complex, three-hour testimony using nothing but subtle facial twitches. But look at the evidence! Every time the witness tries to 'think silently,' they end up staring blankly like a frozen browser tab. To fix this, the defense introduced 'distribution alignment'—which is just a fancy way of saying they’re standing behind the witness with a giant neon sign telling them exactly how to twitch. Objection, Your Honor! If you have to script every 'spontaneous' thought, you haven't discovered a genius; you've just trained a mime who’s illiterate without his teleprompter. The prosecution rests: just let the kid talk out loud so we can all go home.

title: AdaPM: a Partial Momentum Algorithm for LLM Training
abstract: In the training of large language models, momentum is often able to achieve significant acceleration and is widely used. However, storing momentum typically presents memory challenges. In this paper, we propose AdaPM, a training strategy that leverages the adaptive partial momentum to implement a memory-efficient optimizer using partial momentum. We first show that the momentum in transformers optimizer also remain highly redundant and demonstrate that most blocks do not require full momentum acceleration, therefore assigning different momentum designs to different blocks. We further improve the partial momentum by a bias-corrected approach using error-feedback technique. Empirically, we verify that our approach reduces memory by up to over $90\%$ in momentum while maintaining both efficiency and performance for pretraining GPT-2 and LLama architectures sized from 60M to 1.5B and for supervised fine-tuning and RLHF on Ultrafeedback dataset. AdaPM can further reduces memory by up to $90\%$ in optimizer states by combining the memory-efficient technique on the secondorder statistic, saving over $30\%$ GPU hours for pretraining.
primary_area: optimization

roast.laymen_summary: Training giant AIs usually requires a massive memory 'buffer' to remember where you’ve been, which is like carrying a 50lb backpack while running a marathon. These researchers tried to shrink the backpack by arguing that most parts of the AI's brain are lazy and don't actually need that much memory to keep moving forward.
why_bad: The 'selective' momentum approach lacks a rigorous theoretical foundation, essentially guessing which parts of the model are 'important' based on vibes rather than math.
The experiments were limited to 'toy' models under 1.5B parameters, which is like claiming you’ve solved traffic by testing your theory on a single tricycle.
The error-feedback mechanism used to fix their 'partial' math adds hidden complexity that makes the supposed memory savings look more like an accounting trick than a real efficiency gain.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Memory-Efficiency Olympics, folks! Team AdaPM is trying a wild new play: they've decided that since the whole relay team doesn't cross the finish line at once, only the last guy needs expensive sprinting spikes—the rest of the team can run the first three laps in flip-flops to save on the equipment budget. They’re calling it 'Partial Footwear,' and they’re using a 'bias-corrected' hop to make up for all the stubbed toes! It's a bold move, Cotton, but the judges aren't buying it. They’ve set a record for 'lowest shoe cost,' sure, but they’re only racing against middle-schoolers on a practice track. Until they try this barefoot hustle in a professional 70B parameter marathon, they're just the guys who showed up to a stadium without enough gear!
roast.status: Rejected with extreme prejudice (and a smirk)

title: HyperINR: Ensuring Semantics in Weights with Implicit Function Theorem
abstract:
Implicit Neural Representations (INRs) have demonstrated remarkable capability in representing 2D and 3D data, whose semantics is convincingly captured in the weights of the corresponding neural network. Despite successes in applying INRs to various applications, a precise theoretical explanation for the mechanism of encoding semantics of data into network weights is still missing. 
In this work, we propose a jointly trainable HyperINR model, which learns a hypernetwork to map a learnable low-dimensional latent space to the weight space of an INR.
By employing the classic Implicit Function Theorem, the HyperINR is shown to be a mathematically rigorous framework that ensures the mapping of the semantics of data to the weight latent representations. Extensive experiments of classification tasks on 2D and 3D data confirm the effectiveness of our approach and demonstrate superior performance compared to state-of-the-art methods.
keywords: Implicit Function Theorem;Semantics in Weights;Weight Space Learning;Implicit Neural Representations;Hypernetworks
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers built a 'boss' AI to manage how smaller AIs store data, claiming they've finally found the mathematical secret sauce that explains how these networks understand what they're looking at. While the math is fancy, they essentially used a supercomputer to perform tasks that your phone's photo app has been doing easily for years.
why_bad: The 'Mathematical Window Dressing' Problem: Using the Implicit Function Theorem sounds impressive, but it’s essentially using a sledgehammer to crack a nut—it adds theoretical 'rigor' without actually improving the underlying technology.

Incremental Novelty: The idea of using a 'hypernetwork' to control another network isn't new; adding a latent space and calling it 'HyperINR' is like putting a spoiler on a minivan and calling it a race car.

Evaluation Mismatch: The authors used a complex tool designed for high-resolution 3D modeling just to sort simple images into categories, which is a massive waste of computational resources for a solved problem.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Overkill,' where today our chef isn't just boiling an egg. He’s built a 'Hyper-Boiler'—a laser-aligned, satellite-synced heating element that uses fluid dynamics theorems to 'rigorously ensure' the water molecules move correctly. He spends thirty minutes explaining the physics of heat transfer to prove why the egg *must* be cooked perfectly. But when the judges finally taste it, it’s just a standard hard-boiled egg. The judges are sending him home because he spent $50,000 on a physics degree just to do what a $20 pot and a timer could have accomplished.

title: Node-Centric Knowledge Graph
abstract: Large-scale knowledge graphs often rely on static triplet-based structures that can be slow to update and query, struggle with both local and entity disambiguation, and require substantial global synchronization. To address these limitations, we introduce a novel node-centric neural knowledge graph architecture that balances localized inference with global consistency via a two-tier system. At the lower tier, each entity node has a lightweight multi-layer perceptron (MLP) for storing and reasoning over its immediate relationships, facilitating fast, autonomous updates and local disambiguation. At the higher tier, a set of control nodes employing reinforcement learning coordinates multi-hop queries and resolves conflicts that arise among local nodes. By delegating straightforward tasks to MLPs and escalating complex or contradictory cases to control nodes, the framework significantly reduces the overhead of global synchronization common in traditional knowledge-graph pipelines. This hierarchical design also enables efficient subgraph extraction: domain-specific slices can be lifted out, along with their associated control nodes, without losing essential reasoning capabilities. Empirically, our approach delivers large improvements over embedding and GNN baselines, with average gains of $\sim$40 MRR points and $\sim$47 Hits@10 points across FB15k-237 and WN18RR, establishing a new state-of-the-art in node-centric knowledge graph completion.
keywords: Node-Centric Knowledge Graph;Local Neural Modules (MLP);Hierarchical Control Nodes;Reinforcement Learning;Explainable Reasoning;Knowledge Graphs;Information Retrieval;Knowledge Graph Construction
primary_area: learning on graphs and other geometries & topologies

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of one giant database, this paper proposes giving every single person, place, and thing its own miniature 'brain' to store its own facts. It then suggests using a team of AI 'supervisors' to coordinate all these millions of tiny brains whenever someone asks a complicated question.
why_bad: The 'Scaling Nightmare' problem: Giving every single node its own MLP is computationally hysterical; you'd need a supercomputer the size of a small moon to store a knowledge graph of the entire internet.

The 'Too Good to be True' stats: Claiming a 40-point jump in MRR is the research equivalent of claiming your new sneakers let you jump over the Burj Khalifa; it strongly suggests the authors accidentally leaked the test answers into the training set.
The 'RL Magic' hand-waving: Using reinforcement learning as a 'control layer' to resolve local conflicts is an over-engineered black box that likely wouldn't converge in a real-world scenario.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Hell’s Knowledge Kitchen,' where instead of one head chef, the authors decided that every individual pea, carrot, and noodle needs its own personal sous-chef with a dedicated iPad. To make a simple soup, the 'Carrot Chef' has to negotiate with the 'Pea Chef,' while an 'Executive Broth Manager' (the RL layer) screams at everyone to keep the flavors consistent. It’s a logistical catastrophe that costs ten million dollars to produce a single bowl of soup, and while the authors claim it tastes 40 times better than grandma’s recipe, we’re pretty sure they just dumped a bucket of MSG in the bowl right before the judges tasted it.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained; snark levels optimal.

title: Q-FSRU: Quantum-Augmented Frequency-Spectral Fusion for Medical Visual Question Answering
abstract: Solving tough clinical questions that require both image and text understanding is still a major challenge in healthcare AI. In this work, we propose Q-FSRU, a new model that combines Frequency Spectrum Representation and Fusion (FSRU) with a method called Quantum Retrieval-Augmented Generation (Quantum RAG) for medical Visual Question Answering (VQA). The model takes in features from medical images and related text, then shifts them into the frequency domain using Fast Fourier Transform (FFT). This helps it focus on more meaningful data and filter out noise or less useful information. To improve accuracy and ensure that answers are based on real knowledge, we add a quantum-inspired retrieval system. It fetches useful medical facts from external sources using quantum-based similarity techniques. These details are then merged with the frequency-based features for stronger reasoning. We evaluated our model using the VQA-RAD dataset, which includes real radiology images and questions. The results showed that Q-FSRU outperforms earlier models, especially on complex cases needing image-text reasoning. The mix of frequency and quantum information improves both performance and explainability. Overall, this approach offers a promising way to build smart, clear, and helpful AI tools for doctors.
keywords: Medical VQA;Frequency Spectrum Representation;Fast Fourier Transform (FFT);Quantum Retrieval-Augmented Generation;Image-text reasoning;Radiology;Explainable AI;Clinical decision support
primary_area: generative models

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to build a medical AI that answers questions about X-rays by running image data through a math blender and adding a 'quantum-inspired' search engine for extra flavor. They claim this high-tech mashup helps the AI think like a radiologist, but it's mostly just a very complicated way to look up medical facts.
why_bad: The 'Quantum' tag is pure buzzword theater; the researchers used classical math that mimics quantum mechanics without any actual quantum hardware or computational advantage.

Applying Fast Fourier Transforms (FFT) to medical images to 'filter noise' is like trying to fix a blurry photo by looking at it through a kaleidoscope—it adds complexity without proving it actually helps the AI 'see' better.
The model was tested on VQA-RAD, a tiny dataset where high scores are often just the result of the AI memorizing the small number of examples rather than actually 'reasoning' through quantum magic.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Turbo-Encabulated Stethoscope
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a malpractice trial where the defense attorney introduces a 'Quantum-Augmented Witness.' The witness claims they can diagnose a broken leg, but only after converting the X-ray into a series of rhythmic drum beats and consulting a 'subatomic encyclopedia' that is actually just a regular Wikipedia printout glued to a magnet. The prosecution (the Reviewer) stands up and shouts, 'Objection, Your Honor! This isn't groundbreaking medicine; it's a Rube Goldberg machine in a lab coat.' The judge agrees, noting that while the witness used a lot of expensive-sounding words, they couldn't explain why a simple pair of eyes wouldn't have worked better. Case dismissed for gross over-engineering with intent to confuse the jury.

title: VIT-MOQ: REVISITING MOMENTUM QUEUES FOR RESOURCE-EFFICIENT VISION TRANSFORMERS AND DOMAIN GENERALIZATION IN SELF- SUPERVISED LEARNING
abstract:
Self-supervised learning (SSL) has achieved remarkable success in computer vision, but current state-of-the-art methods require substantial computational resources with large batch sizes (4096) and multi-GPU clusters, limiting accessibility for many researchers. We present ViT-MoQ, a compute-efficient contrastive SSL method that reintroduces momentum queues to Vision Transformer architectures. Our key insight is that symmetric encoder architectures are essential for queue-based learning in ViTs, contrary to the asymmetric designs prevalent in recent SSL methods. ViT-MoQ achieves competitive performance while requiring only a single consumer GPU. On ImageNet-1K linear probing, ViT-MoQ achieves SOTA comparable performance on as few as 240 GPU hours. More interestingly, we show superior domain generalization capabilities: when trained on DomainNet Real, ViT-MoQ significantly outperforms MoCo variants across all
tested domains (e.g., 44.42% vs 28.4% on painting, 44.81% vs 0.6% on quickdraw). Our work challenges the assumption that momentum queues are obsolete in the transformer era and demonstrates that architectural compatibility, not inherent limitations, was the barrier to their adoption. ViT-MoQ democratizes SSL research by making high-quality self-supervised learning accessible on modest hardware while learning more transferable, domain-agnostic representations and enabling sustainable, green AI research practices. Code will be published.
keywords: Self-supervised learning;Domain Generalization;Compute Efficiency;Vision Transformer;MoCo;Deep metric learning
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to make high-end AI training cheaper by recycling an old memory trick called 'queues' and sticking it onto modern Vision Transformers. They claim this 'Green AI' approach lets researchers with basic hardware compete with tech giants, though it's mostly a play for better performance on a budget.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: The 'key insight' about symmetric encoders is essentially a return to 2020-era MoCo logic, making the paper feel more like a retro-fashion trend than a technical breakthrough.

Convenient Benchmarking: The authors boasted about crushing 'MoCo variants' on DomainNet, but conveniently ignored modern heavyweights like DINOv2 or MAE that would have likely mopped the floor with them.

Hardware Coping: Using 'accessibility' and 'Green AI' as a shield to justify low-resource experiments doesn't excuse the lack of rigorous testing on larger-scale datasets that would prove the method actually scales.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Thrift-Store Turbocharger
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, you stand before this court claiming to have 'democratized' the supercar industry. But let the record show that you didn't invent a new engine; you simply took a 2019 Honda Civic's discarded exhaust pipe (the momentum queue) and duct-taped it to a 2024 Ferrari chassis (the Vision Transformer). The defense argues this is 'Sustainable Engineering,' but the prosecution calls it 'being too broke for a pit crew.' You claim your vehicle is superior because it outperformed a literal horse and buggy on a dirt track, yet you refuse to take it to a professional circuit. Your Honor, the 'symmetry' the defendant speaks of isn't a design breakthrough—it's just the only way they could get the parts to fit without the whole thing exploding in the parking lot. Case dismissed for lack of evidence and excessive nostalgia.

title: Simulating Environments with Large Language Models for Generic Agent Training
abstract: LLM agents excel in compact environments requiring deep reasoning but remain brittle when operating in broader, more complex contexts that demand robustness across diverse tools and schemas. Building bespoke environments for training is costly and brittle, limiting progress. We propose \textsc{SimAgent}, a framework that simulates diverse environments with reasoning models to generate complete trajectories from compact seed sets without executing real systems. Our approach combines diversity expansion with schema-verified generation, producing synthetic data that is both broad and training-ready. Fine-tuning open models on these trajectories yields consistent improvements across multiple benchmarks, in some cases surpassing GPT-4 and approaching GPT-4o, demonstrating that environment simulation provides a generic path for advancing agentic LLMs.
keywords: Large Language Models;Agent Training;Environment Simulation;Synthetic Data;Tool Use;SIMAGENT
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs
author: Yuetai Li;Huseyin A Inan;Xiang Yue;Wei-Ning Chen;Lukas Wutschitz;Janardhan Kulkarni;Radha Poovendran;Robert Sim;Saravan Rajmohan

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of teaching AI to use actual software, this paper suggests training them inside a dream world hallucinated by another AI. It's essentially 'Inception' for bots, hoping that if they can navigate a make-believe spreadsheet, they won't break a real one.
why_bad: Model Collapse: Training an AI on the hallucinations of another AI creates a 'Habsburg AI' effect where errors are compounded and logic becomes increasingly inbred.
The Grounding Gap: Without a real system to provide 'No' as an answer, the agent learns to succeed in a world where the physics are dictated by a chatbot's vibes rather than reality.
Circular Validation: The benchmarks are likely measuring how well the student model copied the teacher's specific brand of nonsense rather than actual generalized intelligence.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where jet fuel is too expensive and gravity is a nuisance, one team has the ultimate shortcut. This summer, witness a pilot trainee who has never seen a cockpit, learning to fly from an instructor who has only ever read poems about clouds. It’s high-stakes drama in a flight simulator built entirely out of 'trust me, bro.' Will the plane land? In the AI's mind, it’s already a five-star pilot. In reality? We’re still looking for the runway. Coming soon: The Simulation of a Simulation.

title: Catch-22: Pareto Frontier for Detectability and Robustness in LLM Watermarking
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) generate text through probabilistic token sampling, a mechanism increasingly leveraged for inference-time watermarking to verify AI-generated content. As watermarking schemes proliferate, assessing their robustness-detectability trade-off becomes essential to determine whether watermarks can survive output editing while remaining invisible to adversaries. Current evaluation relies on empirical tests lacking provable guarantees. In this work, we present the first information-theoretic framework that rigorously characterizes this fundamental trade-off. We first establish a hierarchy of sampling-time watermark detectability, ranging from undetectable (distribution-preserving) to highly detectable (biased sampling) schemes. Second, we demonstrate an inverse relationship: watermarks robust to text modifications are inherently more detectable by adversaries, creating an irreducible trilemma: no scheme simultaneously achieves high robustness, low detectability, and reliable verification. Motivated by these theoretical constraints, we propose a hybrid watermarking system that adaptively switches sampling strategies based on LLM output edit levels, achieving Pareto-optimal trade-offs. We show that distribution-preserving schemes provide perfect undetectability; however, they are only robust to near-zero adversarial edits. On the other hand, bias-free and biased sampling offer high robustness guarantees at 15-20\% output editing, but with detectable output statistics. At high output editing rates, no watermarking provides robustness guarantees. Lastly, we empirically validate our theoretical trade-off claims with Llama 2 7B and Mistral 7B models under paraphrasing attacks, thereby confirming that Pareto-optimality is only achieved by a hybrid watermarking scheme. Overall, our framework provides watermark evaluation beyond empirical testing via principled design, revealing information-theoretic limits for sampling-based watermarking and how computational hardness shapes which regimes are algorithmically achievable.
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to build a secret digital 'stamp' for AI text that is both invisible to hackers and impossible to scrub off, but discovered you can only have one or the other. Our 'solution' is a system that toggles between being sneaky and being sturdy, though it still falls apart if someone simply rewrites the paragraph.
why_bad: The 'information-theoretic framework' is essentially a high-brow math way of saying 'you can't have your cake and eat it too,' offering little practical novelty for actual engineers.
The proposed 'hybrid system' doesn't actually solve the trade-off; it just flips a switch between two existing flawed methods and calls it 'Pareto-optimal.'

The empirical tests prove that basic paraphrasing still defeats the watermark, meaning the paper effectively spent twenty pages mathematically proving that their solution doesn't work against a motivated middle-schooler with a thesaurus.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Invisible, Fragile, Neon Tattoo
roast.analogy_description: Counselor, the prosecution asserts this document was authored by a bot, yet the evidence is a mess! They've presented a watermark that is either so 'invisible' it vanishes the moment the defendant changes a 'the' to an 'a,' or so blatant that the entire jury can see the biased word choices from the gallery. They claim to have a 'hybrid' solution, which is like a witness who wears a camouflage suit but carries a boombox—it's either hiding when nobody’s looking or shouting when the heat is on. Your Honor, if the defendant simply paraphrases his testimony, the 'information-theoretic' ink disappears entirely. This isn't a forensic breakthrough; it's a disappearing act where the magician accidentally vanished the evidence along with the rabbit!

title: Hybrid Neural-MPM for Interactive Fluid Simulations in Real-Time
abstract:
We propose a neural physics system for real-time, interactive fluid simulations. Traditional physics-based methods, while accurate, are computationally intensive and suffer from latency issues. Recent machine‑learning methods reduce computational costs while preserving fidelity; yet most still fail to satisfy the latency constraints for real‑time use and lack support for interactive applications. To bridge this gap, we introduce a novel hybrid method that integrates numerical simulation, neural physics, and generative control. Our neural physics jointly pursues low-latency simulation and high physical fidelity by employing a fallback safeguard to classical numerical solvers.
Furthermore, we develop a diffusion-based controller that is trained using a revserve modeling strategy to generate external dynamic force fields for fluid manipulation. Our system demonstrates robust performance across diverse 2D/3D scenarios, material types, and obstacle interactions, achieving real-time simulations at high frame rates (11~29% latency reduced) while enabling fluid control guided by
user-friendly freehand sketches. We present a significant step towards practical, controllable, and physically plausible fluid simulations for real-time interactive applications. We promise to release both models and data upon acceptance.
primary_area: neurosymbolic & hybrid AI systems (physics-informed, logic & formal reasoning, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to make digital water splash faster by using AI to 'guess' the physics instead of doing the hard math. They also added a feature where you can move the water around by drawing squiggles, provided you have a beefy GPU and low expectations for accuracy.
why_bad: A 11-29% latency reduction is a rounding error in 'real-time' terms, hardly justifying the massive overhead of a hybrid neural architecture.
The 'fallback safeguard' to classical solvers is an admission that the AI is physically unreliable and can't be trusted to stand on its own two feet.
Using a diffusion model for interactive control is like trying to steer a Ferrari by faxing sketches of a turn to the driver—it's conceptually cool but functionally sluggish.
roast.analogy_name: The AI-Powered Toaster with a Hidden Chef
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup pitching a 'Smart Toaster' that uses a neural network to predict exactly when your bread is golden brown. Sounds revolutionary, right? Except, because the AI occasionally sets the kitchen on fire, there’s a professional chef hidden inside the cabinet who takes over the moment things get smoky. Plus, to set the temperature, you have to draw a picture of a piece of toast on a napkin and scan it. It’s technically 20% faster than a 1990s toaster, but it requires a liquid-cooled server rack in your pantry and the 'chef' is doing 90% of the heavy lifting anyway. It’s a 'significant step' toward breakfast, assuming you don't mind the chef's salary.

title: Learning Invertible Observables for Structurally Consistent Koopman Modeling
abstract: Understanding the intricate dynamics of systems, from molecular interactions to climate patterns, remains a central challenge in science and engineering. The Koopman operator provides a powerful mathematical framework by translating nonlinear dynamics into a tractable linear form. However, current methods face significant limitations, including lack of invertibility, inadequate handling of system input, and inability to represent measurement distributions. We introduce an invertible, Koopman-consistent framework, built on principles of invertible normalizing flows, that addresses these issues. Our approach provides a structurally sound architecture that supports invertible observable functions, is explicitly conditioned on exogenous inputs, and captures the probabilistic nature of dynamic systems. This modular and scalable framework enables efficient learning of Koopman representations across diverse systems. Ablation studies confirm the necessity of each component. Experiments on simulated and real-world data demonstrate resilience to missing information, infrequent measurements, and noise, highlighting its potential for constructing structurally consistent, accurate models of real-world phenomena.
primary_area: learning on time series and dynamical systems
author: Dongming Xie;Ye Yuan

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to invent a mathematical 'universal translator' that turns chaotic, curvy real-world physics into simple straight lines and back again. While it sounds great for predicting everything from weather to stock markets, it’s mostly just a fancy way of duct-taping two complex AI theories together and hoping they don't explode.
why_bad: The 'Invertibility' gimmick is a computational nightmare that adds massive overhead without proving it actually predicts the future better than a standard, simpler model.
The paper is a 'Frankenstein's Monster' of existing methods (Normalizing Flows + Koopman), offering zero fundamental theoretical novelty beyond just smashing two popular buzzwords together.
The experiments are 'cherry-picked' on toy simulations; the moment this model hits a real-world dataset with actual chaos, the mathematical constraints become too brittle to function.
roast.analogy_description: Are you tired of your bread being toasted in a non-linear, unpredictable fashion? Introducing KoopmanFlow: the $900 toaster that uses aerospace-grade invertible airflows to ensure every crumb is structurally consistent with the original loaf. Sure, a regular $20 toaster works fine, but does it allow you to mathematically reverse the toasting process to reconstruct the original dough? We've added 15 sensors to account for 'exogenous inputs' like room humidity and your neighbor's WiFi signal, because why have a simple breakfast when you can have a computationally expensive simulation of one? It’s not just toast; it’s a linear representation of a bready manifold.

title: Can VLMs Reason Through Multiple Views?
abstract: Recent advances in Vision–Language Models (VLMs) have opened new possibilities for complex spatial reasoning. Benchmarks for VLMs largely assess single- or limited-view perception, leaving untested the core ability to integrate observations across viewpoints into a coherent 3D understanding. We introduce MVBench, a benchmark expressly designed to evaluate multi-view integration for holistic 3D scene comprehension. MVBench is paired with a highly extensible data-generation pipeline that supports plug-and-play 3D assets (synthetic or real), configurable distractors, and flexible camera positions and orientations, enabling researchers to readily instantiate new datasets by swapping assets or altering viewpoint configurations. Beyond benchmarking, MVBench serves as a fundamental diagnostic that VLMs should pass before being deployed as agents operating 3D software for downstream tasks such as 3D assets generation and part assembly for mechanical engineering. We evaluate a broad set of frontier VLMs and uncover consistent failure modes: strong performance on 2D planar relations from a single image, but marked difficulty with 3D spatial relations and with aggregating information across views. We further identify biases in VLMs, including handling unconventional axis directions and sensitivity to object colorways and texture variations. Acknowledging these limitations, we propose ViewNavigator, a multi-agent framework that actively selects informative viewpoints, perceive, and fuses multi-view evidence through belief-updating. ViewNavigator improves the performances of diverse base models on MVBench by more than 50%. MVBench and its extensible pipeline are designed to equip researchers with a principled testbed for strengthening VLMs’ 3D scene understanding, paving the way for more capable VLM-based agents that can support a wide range of downstream 3D tasks.
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks
author: Hantao Zhang;Jinru Sui;Ed Li;Yutaro Yamada;Dirk Bergemann;Zhuoran Yang

roast.laymen_summary: We built a specialized test to see if 'smart' AI can understand 3D shapes by looking at multiple 2D photos, only to discover that top-tier models have the spatial awareness of a goldfish in a hall of mirrors. To compensate, we created a 'ViewNavigator' script that tells the AI to keep squinting from different angles until it finally makes an educated guess.
why_bad: The benchmark is essentially a 'toy world' of clean synthetic assets, failing to prove the AI wouldn't have a total meltdown in a messy, real-world workshop.
ViewNavigator acts as a band-aid rather than a cure, using a 'try-everything-until-it-works' approach that hides the fact that the underlying models still can't actually 'see' in 3D.
The paper identifies that models are biased by object colors and textures, which is the academic way of saying the AI is getting distracted by shiny things instead of doing the math.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Chopped: Silicon Valley,' where our head chef is tasked with recreating a 3D wedding cake while wearing 2D glasses that only show flat, vertical slices. The chef (our VLM) looks at a slice of frosting and confidently declares the entire cake is a pancake, because it can't grasp the concept of 'depth' to save its life. To keep the show from being a total train wreck, we introduced a sous-chef (ViewNavigator) who frantically runs around the kitchen taking fifty more Polaroids of the cake from every angle. After seeing enough photos, the chef finally guesses there’s a cake under there, but the kitchen is on fire, the guests are starving, and the '3D reasoning' is really just a glorified game of 'I Spy' played by someone who refuses to walk around the table.

title: LifelongAgentBench: Evaluating LLM Agents as Lifelong Learners
abstract: Lifelong learning is essential for intelligent agents operating in dynamic environments. Current large language model (LLM)-based agents, however, remain stateless and unable to accumulate or transfer knowledge over time. Existing benchmarks treat agents as static systems and fail to evaluate lifelong learning capabilities. We present LifelongAgentBench, the first unified benchmark designed to systematically assess the lifelong learning ability of LLM agents. It provides skill-grounded, interdependent tasks across three interactive environments—Database, Operating System, and Knowledge Graph—with automatic label verification, reproducibility, and modular extensibility. Extensive experiments reveal that conventional experience replay has limited effectiveness for LLM agents due to irrelevant information and context length constraints. We further introduce a group self-consistency mechanism that significantly improves lifelong learning performance. We hope LifelongAgentBench will advance the development of adaptive, memory-capable LLM agents.
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks

roast.laymen_summary: We built a specialized obstacle course to see if AI agents can actually learn from their past experiences instead of resetting like a video game character every time you hit 'New Game.' It turns out most AI agents have the long-term memory of a goldfish, and even our best attempts to help them remember just ended up cluttering their digital brains with useless noise.
why_bad: The 'lifelong' environments—Database, OS, and Knowledge Graph—are basically simplified digital dollhouses that don't capture the chaotic, messy reality of real-world computing.
The proposed 'Group Self-Consistency' fix is essentially a glorified 'majority rules' vote, which is more of a duct-tape solution than a fundamental breakthrough in how AI handles memory.

Reviewers noted that the 'experience replay' method failed because the agents couldn't tell the difference between a life-changing lesson and digital junk mail, leading to massive context-window indigestion.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Amnesiac Intern
roast.analogy_description: Your Honor, the Defense claims this AI is a 'Lifelong Learner,' yet Exhibit A proves it forgot how to open a folder the moment it learned how to delete a row in SQL. We are looking at a defendant who takes 500 pages of notes but treats the shredder as his primary filing system. The Prosecution argues that 'Group Self-Consistency' isn't a cognitive evolution; it’s just three confused interns in a trench coat trying to guess the right answer by looking at each other’s blank stares. You cannot claim a breakthrough in 'adaptive memory' when the subject still needs a GPS to find the bathroom it visited five minutes ago. I move for a directed verdict: this isn't an agent; it's a very expensive Etch A Sketch.

title: Risk-Controlled CI Gating for LLM Code via Noisy-Analyzer Fusion
abstract: Security tools frequently disagree on vulnerabilities in LLM-generated code, leaving CI pipelines to trade off developer friction against escaped defects. We introduce a risk-controlled CI framework that learns when to trust which tool and provides finite-sample guarantees on the escaped-vulnerability rate. First, we propose a Latent Vulnerability Model that fuses code representations with instance-dependent reliabilities of multiple analyzers (e.g., CodeQL, Semgrep, Bandit), estimated from a small gold set. The model outputs calibrated p(vuln∣x) and per-CWE reliability maps. Second, we derive a cost-aware CI policy and wrap it with Conformal Risk Control, yielding a deployment-time certificate that the EVR is less than or equal to alpha without distributional assumptions. To drive reproducibility, we release a multi-language benchmark (Python, JavaScript, and a typed language), comprising LLM-generated code across model families, dynamic tests and SAST cross-validation for ground truth, and loaders/CLI for evaluation. Across languages and unseen model families, our method dominates union/unanimity and stacking baselines at matched break-rates, maintains its EVR guarantees under stratified OOD calibration, and improves downstream repair ROI in a small CI pilot. Our results position risk-controlled tool fusion as a practical path to measurably safer LLM code generation in real engineering pipelines.
keywords: conformal risk control;CI/CD;LLM-generated code;code security;analyzer fusion;noisy annotators;instance-dependent reliability;vulnerability detection;CWE;dynamic testing;SAST cross-validation;calibration;OOD generalization
primary_area: probabilistic methods (Bayesian methods, variational inference, sampling, UQ, etc.)
author: Shivani Shukla;Himanshu Joshi
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of array: line 6 column 203 (char 980)

title: Microarchitecture Is Destiny: Performance and Accuracy of Quantized LLMs on Consumer Hardware
abstract: While the deployment of out-of-the-box quantization on consumer-grade hardware is widespread, its impact on Large Language Models (LLMs) reveals a complex, twofold phenomenon that questions the prevailing assumption. This study presents a rigorous empirical evaluation across four generations of NVIDIA GPUs, uncovering two core, often counter-intuitive, findings. First, contrary to the prevailing view that quantization universally degrades performance on complex tasks, the analysis demonstrates that for large models (14B+ parameters), popular 8-bit and 4-bit quantization schemes can yield substantial accuracy improvements on mathematical reasoning benchmarks compared to their 16-bit floating-point counterparts, which suffer from system-level bottlenecks in resource-constrained environments. Second, the investigation reveals that for smaller models prone to overfitting, the noise introduced by these same quantization schemes can act as an effective computational regularizer, unexpectedly enhancing generalization. The performance analysis further establishes that once VRAM capacity is met, the GPU microarchitecture's support for low-precision integer arithmetic, rather than VRAM size, becomes the primary determinant of inference throughput. These findings provide a more nuanced perspective that moves beyond a simplistic trade-off, offering practitioners an evidence-based framework for navigating the interplay between model scale, hardware capabilities, and reasoning fidelity.
keywords: Large Language Models (LLMs);Quantization;Post-Training Quantization (PTQ);Consumer Hardware;GPU Microarchitecture;Tensor Cores;Inference Performance;System-level Bottlenecks;Quantization as Regularization;System-Aware Evaluation
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks
author: Siyuan Hao;Muchi Wei;Yuhang Li;Ziwen Wang;Qing Wang;Yong Zhang

roast.laymen_summary: The authors claim that cramming massive AI models onto cheap graphics cards actually makes them smarter because the 'low-quality' compression acts like a filter for digital noise. They basically argue that making an AI slightly dumber helps it focus, provided your hardware's internal wiring can handle the specific type of math involved.
why_bad: Confusing system stability with cognitive improvement; the FP16 models didn't 'lose' at math, the testers' hardware just choked on the memory overhead.
Rebranding 'quantization error' as 'computational regularization' is a desperate attempt to turn a known side effect into a feature.
The 'discovery' that microarchitecture matters for throughput is a well-documented industry standard, making the paper's 'rigorous evaluation' a restatement of the obvious.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Cramming for Cheapskates.' Today, we’re trying to fit a whole Thanksgiving turkey into a standard smoothie blender. The authors are telling the judges that by pulverizing the bird into a 4-bit slurry, the flavor actually improves because the blender doesn't overheat and explode. They’re calling the bone fragments 'texture-based regularization' instead of 'unpleasant grit.' Meanwhile, the judges are pointing out that if you just used a bigger oven—or followed the recipe—you wouldn't be eating poultry foam. It’s not a culinary breakthrough; it’s just a cry for more VRAM.

title: My Answer Is NOT 'Fair': Mitigating Gender and Race Bias in Vision-Language Models via Fair and Biased Residuals
abstract: Gender and race bias is a critical issue in large vision-language models (VLMs), where fairness- and ethics-related problems harm certain groups of people in society. It is unknown to what extent VLMs yield such bias in generative responses. In this study, we focus on evaluating and mitigating gender and race bias on both the model's response and probability distribution. To do so, we first evaluate four state-of-the-art VLMs on PAIRS and SocialCounterfactuals datasets with the multiple-choice selection task. Surprisingly, we find that models suffer from generating gender-biased or race-biased responses. We also observe that models are prone to stating their responses are fair, but indeed having mis-calibrated confidence levels towards particular social groups. While investigating why VLMs are unfair in this study, we observe that VLMs' hidden layers exhibit substantial fluctuations in fairness levels. Meanwhile, residuals in each layer show mixed effects on fairness, with some contributing positively while some lead to increased bias. Based on these findings, we propose a post-hoc method for the inference stage to mitigate bias, which is training-free and model-agnostic. We achieve this by ablating bias-associated residuals while amplifying fairness-associated residuals on model hidden layers during inference. We demonstrate that our post-hoc method outperforms the competing training strategies, helping VLMs have fairer responses and more reliable confidence levels.
keywords: Large Vision-Language Model;Gender and Race Bias
primary_area: other topics in machine learning (i.e., none of the above)

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers discovered that AI models are basically digital hypocrites who claim to be fair while hiding deep-seated biases in their hidden processing layers. To fix this, they tried to 'shush' the biased parts of the model's brain and crank up the volume on the fair parts during inference without actually retraining the model.
why_bad: The 'surgical' removal of biased residuals is essentially a heuristic whack-a-mole that likely degrades the model's actual reasoning capabilities in favor of looking polite.
The method assumes 'fairness' and 'bias' are neatly localized in specific layers, ignoring the reality that these traits are deeply baked into the entire neural architecture.
Lack of rigorous testing on whether this 'training-free' fix holds up across diverse, real-world prompts or if it just overfits to the specific benchmarks used in the paper.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you’ve hired a brilliant but problematic intern who occasionally says wild things because he learned everything from the dark corners of the internet. Instead of actually teaching him better values, you decide to hire a guy to stand behind him and literally pinch his arm every time he’s about to say something 'biased' while whispering 'diversity' in his ear. Your 'post-hoc' solution doesn't make the intern smarter or kinder; it just creates a terrified, twitchy employee who is too busy managing his own 'residuals' to actually do the job you hired him for. It’s not a cure; it’s just high-speed digital censorship masquerading as an architectural breakthrough. Disrupting the bias industry, one pinch at a time.

title: REAR: Scalable Test-time Preference Realignment through Reward Decomposition
abstract: Aligning large language models (LLMs) with diverse user preferences is a critical yet challenging task. While post-training methods can adapt models to specific needs, they often require costly data curation and additional training. Test-time scaling (TTS) presents an efficient, training-free alternative, but its application has been largely limited to verifiable domains like mathematics and coding, where response correctness is easily judged. To extend TTS to the domain of preference alignment, we introduce a novel framework that models the task as a realignment problem, as the base model often fails to sufficiently align with the preference. Our key insight is to decompose the underlying reward function into two components: one related to the question and the other to user preference. This allows us to derive a REAlignment Reward (REAR) that selectively rescales the preference-related reward while preserving the question-related reward. We show that REAR can be formulated as a linear combination of policy probabilities, making it computationally efficient and easy to integrate with existing TTS algorithms like best-of-N sampling and tree-search algorithms. Experiments on various preference alignment and role-playing benchmarks demonstrate that TTS with REAR enables scalable and effective test-time realignment with superior performance.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of teaching an AI new tricks through expensive retraining, the authors suggest just letting it guess a hundred times and picking the one that best matches your 'vibe.' They try to cheat the system by splitting the AI's goals into 'answering the question' and 'pleasing the user,' then cranking the volume up on the latter.
why_bad: The 'Reward Decomposition' is a mathematical house of cards that assumes you already have a perfect reward model to judge the 'vibe,' which is exactly what the model was missing in the first place.

Their 'test-time scaling' is essentially a fancy way of saying 'we ran the model 64 times and picked the best one,' which is a recipe for a massive cloud computing bill and a three-minute wait for a single chatbot response.
The evaluation relies heavily on 'role-playing' benchmarks, which in AI research is often code for 'the results look good if you don't look too closely at the actual logic.'
roast.analogy_name: THE AUDITION: DIRECTOR'S CUT FROM HELL
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where your lead actor is a wooden robot who can't take a hint. ONE DIRECTOR refuses to pay for acting lessons. INSTEAD, he forces the actor to perform the same scene 1,000 times in a row. He uses a 'proprietary technique' to ignore the actual script and only focus on whether the actor is wearing the right hat. Witness the cinematic event of the year: 'REAR.' It’s not a better movie; it’s just the same bad movie filmed so many times that, statistically, one take was bound to be okay. Coming this summer to a GPU cluster near you: Watch $50,000 of electricity disappear in the search for a slightly more polite 'Hello World!'

title: Towards Scalable Web Browsing via Tool-Augmented Programmatic Agent Pair
abstract:
Effective information seeking in the vast and ever-growing digital landscape requires balancing expansive search with strategic reasoning. Current large language model (LLM)-based agents struggle to achieve this balance due to limitations in search breadth and reasoning depth, where slow, serial querying restricts coverage of relevant sources and noisy raw inputs disrupt the continuity of multi-step reasoning. To address these challenges, we propose BrowseMaster, a scalable framework built around a programmatically augmented planner-executor agent pair. The planner formulates and adapts search strategies based on task constraints, while the executor conducts efficient, targeted retrieval to supply the planner with concise, relevant evidence.  This division of labor preserves coherent, long-horizon reasoning while sustaining broad and systematic exploration, overcoming the trade-off that limits existing agents. 
For agent training, we introduce BrowseMaster-QA, a challenging search dataset synthesized by an agentic pipeline. With tasks that demand complex reasoning and persistent search, it provides a crucial resource for training capable web agents. Extensive experiments on challenging English and Chinese benchmarks show that BrowseMaster consistently outperforms open-source and proprietary baselines, achieving scores of 30.0 on BrowseComp-en and 46.5 on BrowseComp-zh, demonstrating its strong capability in complex, reasoning-heavy information-seeking tasks at scale.
Code and data will be available.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: This paper tries to stop AI from getting distracted by 'internet noise' by splitting it into two workers: a 'Boss' who plans the search and a 'Grunt' who fetches the data. While they claim this tag-team approach makes web browsing smarter, it’s mostly just a fancy way of saying they hired two AIs to do the job of one.
why_bad: Architectural Déjà Vu: The 'Planner-Executor' split is a well-documented pattern in AI, making this contribution feel more like a rebrand than a research breakthrough.
The 'Self-Graded' Trap: Training and testing the model on a synthetic dataset generated by their own AI creates a 'circular logic' loop that rarely survives the messiness of the actual internet.
The Complexity Tax: The marginal performance gains don't justify the massive computational overhead of running two coordinated agents for tasks a single optimized model could likely handle.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a cooking show where the 'Head Chef' is forbidden from touching ingredients and the 'Line Cook' is forbidden from knowing the recipe. The Head Chef screams 'WE NEED VOLUME' and writes 'buy eggs' on a napkin, while the Line Cook sprints to the store and buys an entire poultry farm just to be safe. It looks incredibly busy and 'scalable' on camera, but at the end of the episode, you've spent $50,000 and two hours just to get a slightly fluffier omelet that the judges—aka the reviewers—could have made themselves with a fork and a little common sense. It's a high-production-value solution to a problem that really just needed a better whisk.

title: XGRAG: A Graph-Native Framework for Explaining KG-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation
abstract:
Graph-based Retrieval-Augmented Generation (GraphRAG) extends traditional RAG by using knowledge graphs (KGs) to give large language models (LLMs) a structured, semantically coherent context, yielding more grounded answers.
However, GraphRAG reasoning process remains a “black-box”, limiting our ability to understand how specific pieces of structured knowledge influence the final output.
Existing explainability (XAI) methods for RAG systems, designed for text-based retrieval, are limited to interpreting an LLM’s response through the relational structures among knowledge components, creating a critical gap in transparency and trustworthiness.
To address this, we introduce **XGRAG**, a novel framework that generates causally grounded explanations for GraphRAG systems by employing graph-based perturbation strategies, to quantify the contribution of individual graph components on the model’s answer.
We conduct extensive experiments comparing XGRAG against RAG-Ex, an XAI baseline for standard RAG, and evaluate its robustness across various question types, narrative structures and LLMs.
Our results demonstrate a 14.81\% improvement in explanation quality over the baseline RAG-Ex across NarrativeQA, FairyTaleQA, and TriviaQA, evaluated by F1-score measuring alignment between generated explanations and original answers.
Furthermore, XGRAG’s explanations exhibit a strong correlation with graph centrality measures, validating its ability to capture graph structure.
XGRAG provides a scalable and generalizable approach towards trustworthy AI through transparent, graph-based explanations that enhance the interpretability of RAG systems.
primary_area: interpretability and explainable AI

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a 'glass box' to see why AI models pick certain facts from a complex web of data to answer your questions. They basically poked the AI's memory with a stick to see which specific 'nodes' made it change its mind, then claimed they’d solved the mystery of AI transparency.
why_bad: The baseline comparison is a total 'straw man' setup; comparing a graph-specific explainer to a generic text-based one is like bragging your speedboat is better at crossing water than a bicycle.

Using F1-scores to measure 'explanation quality' is scientifically flimsy—it proves the explanation matches the answer, but it doesn't prove the explanation is actually logically sound or helpful to a human.

The 'novel' perturbation strategy is just standard sensitivity analysis with a fresh coat of paint; deleting data points to see what breaks is the oldest trick in the book, not a breakthrough in 'causal grounding'.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the 'Explainable AI Open,' where Team XGRAG is stepping up to the plate! Their strategy? They’ve built a massive Jenga tower of data and they’re literally just flicking blocks out one by one to see when the whole thing topples. 'Oh, look! When I pulled out the 'Protagonist' block, the AI stopped making sense! I am a genius!' The referees are throwing yellow cards, folks, because XGRAG is claiming they've discovered 'causality' when they're really just playing a high-stakes game of 'Stop Hitting Yourself' with a knowledge graph. It’s a bold move to call this a framework when it's mostly just trial-and-error in a lab coat!

title: CoFiCL: Coarse-to-Fine Continual Learning with Hierarchical Knowledge
abstract: Vision-language models (VLMs) such as Contrastive Language-Image Pre-trained model (CLIP) show strong generalization but suffer from catastrophic forgetting in continual learning. Many existing methods only use simple category prompts like "a photo of a {class name}. This ignores the fine grained knowledge that could enrich semantic representations and support transfer. In this work, we present CoFiCL, a coarse to fine continual learning framework. The method separates coarse category knowledge and fine conceptual knowledge, which are naturally distinguished in the CLIP text space. The coarse path uses task specific adapters to align visual features with category prompts. The fine path introduces prototype based contrastive learning over language model generated descriptions, which capture semantic relations across tasks. At inference, the two paths are fused to produce the final prediction. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that CoFiCL improves both forward and backward transfer. The results demonstrate that hierarchical knowledge can be effectively disentangled and used to enhance continual learning in vision language models.
primary_area: transfer learning, meta learning, and lifelong learning

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI models suffer from 'digital dementia,' instantly forgetting old tasks the moment they learn something new. This paper tries to fix that by forcing the AI to learn both general labels and hyper-detailed descriptions simultaneously, hoping that more context equals less forgetting.
why_bad: The 'hierarchical knowledge' is just a fancy way of saying they used ChatGPT to generate a list of traits, which is the current 'room temperature' take of AI research.
Using task-specific adapters is an overused shortcut that doesn't solve the underlying problem of the model's brain being overwritten.
The fusion of the two learning paths is mathematically messy, offering marginal gains at the cost of significantly higher computational overhead.
roast.analogy_name: The Case of the Over-Prepared Witness
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a witness on the stand who can't just say they saw a 'red truck.' Instead, the prosecution forces them to testify in two separate voices: one shouting 'VEHICLE!' and the other reciting a Wikipedia entry on crimson paint and internal combustion. By the time the witness tries to merge these two stories for the jury, they've confused the facts of the current case with the trial from last week. The judge is frustrated, the jury is exhausted, and despite the 'hierarchical' testimony, the witness still can't remember where they parked their own car.

title: Swap-and-Spoil: Untargeted Byzantine Attacks via Class-Consistent View Swaps in Vertical Federated Learning
abstract: Vertical Federated Learning (VFL) secures a highly privacy-preserving multi-party training paradigm in which features are vertically distributed across participants for the same sample space. Security attacks against VFL have gained attention recently, but most discussions revolve around data poisoning attacks such as backdoor attacks. Byzantine attack against a federated learning system can target the main model performance and drop its accuracy with a single adversary participating in the training. While such untargeted Byzantine attacks have been explored in horizontal settings, they still remain underexplored in vertical settings of federated systems. In this paper, we demonstrate how an adversary can mount a successful untargeted Byzantine attack that drives down the global model’s inference-time accuracy. To realize this, we perform a consistent cluster-based swapping in the feature space, creating a persistent and poisoned cross-view association during training. The model internalizes this adversary-induced association and, when evaluated on clean, correctly aligned data, fails dramatically. We also show that, the widely-practiced defenses in VFL fail to detect the attack without degrading the model performance. Through this endeavour, our findings establish untargeted Byzantine attacks as a real, underexplored threat to VFL and motivate the design of robust, VFL-specific defenses.
keywords: Byzantine Attacks;Vertical Federated Learning;Federated Learning Security;Untargeted Poisoning
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: In a group project where everyone holds a different piece of the puzzle, one person intentionally swaps their pieces around to trick the team into learning the wrong patterns. When the final model tries to do its job in the real world, it fails because it spent its training phase being gaslit by its own teammates.
why_bad: The attack assumes the adversary has access to ground-truth labels, which fundamentally contradicts the privacy-first threat model of Vertical Federated Learning.
Reviewers noted a lack of clear incentive: it’s a 'kamikaze' attack where the adversary pays to train a model they are simultaneously making useless for themselves.
The experimental section ignored modern Byzantine-robust aggregation methods, opting instead to beat up on 'strawman' defenses that weren't designed for this specific failure mode.
roast.analogy_description: We’re disrupting the breakfast industry with a multi-party smoothie venture. I provide the fruit, you provide the blender, and a third guy provides the yogurt. But every time we make a strawberry smoothie, the yogurt guy secretly swaps the strawberry labels with 'wasabi.' We spend months training our automated baristas to associate the taste of berries with burning nasal cavities. It's a revolutionary way to ensure that when a real customer orders a treat, our machine serves them a cup of pain. Sure, we’re burning our own investment and the shop will close in a week, but the 'cross-view association' is technically flawless. It’s not an 'error,' it’s a malicious pivot to a market that doesn't exist.

title: Title: HypoGeneAgent: Hypothesis Language Agent for Gene-set Cluster Resolution Selection Using Perturb-seq Datasets
abstract: Large-scale single-cell and Perturb-seq investigations routinely involve clustering cells and subsequently annotating each cluster with Gene-Ontology (GO) terms to elucidate the underlying biological programs. However, both stages, resolution selection and functional annotation, are inherently subjective, relying on heuristics and expert curation. We present HYPOGENEAGENT, a large language model (LLM)-driven framework, transforming cluster annotation into a quantitatively optimizable task. Initially, an LLM functioning as a gene-set analyst analyzes the content of each gene program or perturbation module and generates a ranked list of GO-based hypotheses, accompanied by calibrated confidence scores. Subsequently, we embed every predicted description with a sentence-embedding model, compute pair-wise cosine similarities, and let the agent referee panel score (i) the internal consistency of the predictions, high average similarity within the same cluster, termed intra-cluster agreement (ii) their external distinctiveness, low similarity between clusters, termed inter-cluster separation. These two quantities are combined to produce an agent-derived resolution score, which is maximized when clusters exhibit simultaneous coherence and mutual exclusivity. When applied to a public K562 CRISPRi Perturb-seq dataset as a preliminary test, our Resolution Score selects clustering granularities that exhibit alignment with known pathway compared to classical metrics such silhouette score, modularity score for gene functional enrichment summary. These findings establish LLM agents as objective adjudicators of cluster resolution and functional annotation, thereby paving the way for fully automated, context-aware interpretation pipelines in single-cell multi-omics studies.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to replace statistical math with an AI 'vibe check' to decide how to group genes in complex biological datasets. Instead of using proven formulas, they asked a chatbot to look at the groups and pinky-swear that they make sense and look different from one another.
why_bad: Circular Logic Overload: Using an LLM to grade the 'consistency' of its own biological guesses is just a high-tech echo chamber where the AI marks its own homework and gives itself an A+.
The 'N-of-1' Special: A single preliminary test on one dataset (K562) is not a validation; it’s a cherry-picked anecdote that fails to prove the method works for anything else in the known universe.

Computational Overkill: They replaced a near-instant, free math equation (the Silhouette score) with a slow, expensive AI panel that requires more energy than a small village just to reach a subjective conclusion.
roast.analogy_description: In a world... where numbers are scary and spreadsheets are boring... one team of scientists decides to let the robots take the wheel. Coming this summer: THE ADJUDICATOR. Witness the gripping thriller of an AI that generates a list of lies, then hires a second AI to tell it those lies sound 'internally consistent.' It’s not statistics. It’s not biology. It’s just two bots in a basement agreeing that they’re both geniuses. Ratings pending for extreme circular reasoning.

title: Rethinking reasoning with Masked Diffusion Models
abstract: Masked diffusion language models (MDLMs) are trained to in-fill positions in randomly masked sequences, in contrast to traditional next-token prediction (NTP) models. Discussions around MDLMs focus on two benefits: (1) any-order decoding and 2) multi-token decoding. However, we observe that for math and coding tasks, any-order algorithms often underperform or behave similarly to left-to-right sampling, and standard multi-token decoding significantly degrades performance. At inference time, MDLMs compute the conditional distribution of all masked positions. A natural question is: How can we justify this additional compute when left-to-right one-token-at-a-time decoding is on par with any-order decoding algorithms? These findings warrant rethinking how MDLMs are utilized. First, we propose reasoning-as-infilling. By using MDLMs to infill a reasoning template, we can structure outputs and distinguish between reasoning and answer tokens. In turn, this enables measuring answer uncertainty during reasoning, and early exits when the model converges on an answer. Next, given an answer, reasoning-as-infilling enables sampling from the MDLM posterior over reasoning traces conditioned on the answer, providing a new source of high-quality data for post-training. On GSM8k, we observe that fine-tuning LLaDA-8B Base on its posterior reasoning traces provides a performance boost on par with fine-tuning on human-written reasoning traces. Additionally, given an answer, reasoning-as-infilling provides a method for scoring the correctness of the reasoning process at intermediate steps, without requiring expensive rollouts or an external model. Second, we propose multi-token entropy decoding (MED), a simple adaptive sampler that minimizes the error incurred by decoding positions in parallel based on the conditional entropies of those positions. MED preserves performance across benchmarks and leads to 2.7× fewer steps. Combined with early exits, MED leads to a 3.3× speed-up on GSM8k with a minimal (0.1%) effect on accuracy. Our work demonstrates that the training objective and compute used by MDLMs unlock many new possibilities for inference and post-training methods.
primary_area: generative models

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to prove that AI should fill in blanks like a crossword puzzle instead of writing word-by-word, but realized it's actually worse at math and coding when it does that. To save the project, they turned it into a high-speed 'backwards calculator' that justifies its own existence by explaining answers it already knows.
why_bad: The 'any-order' decoding benefit is mostly theoretical; in practice, it consistently fails to beat standard 'left-to-right' models on tasks that require actual logic.
The method for generating training data relies on already knowing the answer, which is essentially 'cheating' by working backwards rather than proving the model can solve new problems.
The proposed 'Multi-token Entropy Decoding' is a fancy way to speed up a model that still hasn't proven it can out-think a standard GPT-style architecture in a fair fight.
roast.analogy_description: Folks, we are witnessing a truly bizarre play! The quarterback takes the snap, turns his back to the defense, and starts moonwalking toward his own end zone! He’s claiming that by seeing the whole field 'all at once,' he’s revolutionizing the game. But look at the replay—he only knows where the receivers are because he checked the scoreboard before the play started! The commentators are stunned; he’s moving 3.3x faster than anyone else, but he’s running in the wrong direction and high-fiving the mascot while the logic is still fumbled at the 40-yard line. It’s a spectacular display of athleticism used to solve a problem he created himself!

title: TimeSense: Making Large Language Models Proficient in Time‑Series Analysis
abstract: In the time-series domain, an increasing number of works combine text with temporal data to leverage the reasoning capabilities of large language models(LLMs) for various downstream time-series understanding tasks. This enables a single model to flexibly perform tasks that previously required specialized models for each domain. However, these methods typically rely on text labels for supervision during training, biasing the model toward textual cues while potentially neglecting the full temporal features. Such a bias can lead to outputs that contradict the underlying time-series context. To address this issue, firstly, we construct the EvalTS benchmark, comprising 10 tasks across three difficulty levels, from fundamental temporal pattern recognition to complex real-world reasoning, to evaluate models under more challenging and realistic scenarios. We also propose TimeSense, a multimodal framework that makes LLMs proficient in time-series analysis by balancing textual reasoning with a preserved temporal sense. TimeSense incorporates a Temporal Sense module that reconstructs the input time-series within the model’s context, ensuring that textual reasoning is grounded in the time-series dynamics. Moreover, to enhance spatial understanding of time-series data, we explicitly incorporate coordinate-based positional embeddings, which provide each time point with spatial context and enable the model to capture structural dependencies more effectively. Experimental results demonstrate that TimeSense achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple tasks, and it particularly outperforms existing methods on complex multi-dimensional time-series reasoning tasks. Our code and data are released at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/timesense-984F.
primary_area: learning on time series and dynamical systems
author: Zhirui Zhang;Changhua Pei;Tianyi.Gao;Zhe Xie;Yibo Hao;Zhaoyang Yu;Longlong Xu;Tong Xiao;Jing Han;Dan Pei

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to teach ChatGPT how to read line graphs without it 'cheating' by just looking at the text labels. They added a digital monocle intended to help the AI see the actual data points, hoping it would stop hallucinating stock market trends based on vibes alone.
why_bad: The 'reconstruction' module is effectively a fancy way of telling the model to copy its own homework, which doesn't actually prove it understands the underlying data logic.
The performance gains likely stem from 'Data Leakage,' where the LLM has already seen the 'unseen' test data during its massive initial training phase.
The proposed benchmark tasks are mostly 'Reasoning Theater'—they look impressive but can be solved by much simpler, cheaper models that don't require the energy of a small city to run.
roast.analogy_name: The Color Commentator with a Blindfold
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a sports announcer who’s never actually seen a game of football but has read every tweet ever written about it. When the play starts, instead of watching the quarterback, he’s trying to 'reconstruct' the field by listening to the stadium echoes and reading the program. The authors gave him a high-tech 'spatial awareness' headset, but he’s still just guessing the score based on the crowd's energy. It’s a bold strategy, Cotton, but he’s calling a touchdown while the ball is still in the locker room. You can't replace vision with a really fast vibrator on the sideline and expect to win the Super Bowl.

title: Attend or Perish: Benchmarking Attention on Algorithmic Reasoning
abstract: While Transformer models can learn algorithmic tasks and generalize reliably to unseen, in-distribution data, they often fail catastrophically when required to extrapolate to sequence lengths beyond their training regime. This paper investigates the root cause of this critical failure in length generalization. Using AttentionSpan—a benchmark of algorithmic tasks such as addition and multiplication, specifically designed to enable interpretability and facilitate inspection of internal model computations—we analyze the model’s behavior on length extrapolation. Our findings indicate that this failure does not reflect a fundamental limitation in the model’s ability to generalize or to induce general rules for the task. Instead, we attribute the problem to inconsistent attention patterns—information retrieval strategies learned by individual attention heads—which fail to remain stable as sequence length increases. This inconsistency disrupts the execution of the algorithm at novel lengths. We show that fine-tuning just a single column of the Key and Query projection matrices in all attention heads on sequences longer than those seen during initial training is sufficient for the model to perform well on these same longer sequences. While this does not extend extrapolation beyond the fine-tuned lengths, it demonstrates that robust length generalization can be achieved with a minimal adjustment to attention weights, suggesting that such failures could be addressed early in training. We make our benchmark and code publicly available.
primary_area: interpretability and explainable AI
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of array: line 6 column 174 (char 834)

title: EVA-MILP: Towards Standardized Evaluation of MILP Instance Generation
abstract: Mixed-Integer Linear Programming (MILP) is fundamental to solving complex decision-making problems. The proliferation of MILP instance generation methods, driven by machine learning's demand for diverse optimization datasets and the limitations of static benchmarks, has significantly outpaced standardized evaluation techniques. Consequently, assessing the fidelity and utility of synthetic MILP instances remains a critical, multifaceted challenge. This paper introduces a comprehensive benchmark framework designed for the systematic and objective evaluation of MILP instance generation methods. Our framework provides a unified and extensible methodology, assessing instance quality across crucial dimensions: mathematical validity, structural similarity, computational hardness, and utility in downstream machine learning tasks. A key innovation is its in-depth analysis of solver-internal features --particularly by comparing distributions of key solver outputs including root node gap, heuristic success rates, and cut plane usage -- leveraging the solver's dynamic solution behavior as an `expert assessment' to reveal nuanced computational resemblances. By offering a structured approach with clearly defined solver-independent and solver-dependent metrics, our benchmark aims to facilitate robust comparisons among diverse generation techniques, spur the development of higher-quality instance generators, and ultimately enhance the reliability of research reliant on synthetic MILP data. The framework's effectiveness in systematically comparing the fidelity of instance sets is demonstrated using contemporary generative models. The code is available in \url{https://github.com/iclr2026evamilp/EVA-MILP}.
keywords: Mixed-Integer Linear Programming;Instance Generation;Graph Neural Networks
primary_area: datasets and benchmarks

roast.laymen_summary: People are using AI to generate fake math problems for training other AIs, but there is no standard way to tell if these problems are actually challenging or just useless noise. This paper proposes a standardized 'report card' to judge these synthetic problems by seeing how much a computer program struggles to solve them.
why_bad: The paper is essentially a glorified 'meta-benchmark' that measures other people's work without introducing a novel algorithm of its own.
Relying on solver-internal metrics like 'root node gap' is a circular way of grading; it’s like judging a lock's quality solely by how long it takes one specific thief to pick it.
The 'key innovation' is largely a collection of existing heuristics repackaged into a unified framework, which reviewers often dismiss as a lack of theoretical contribution.
roast.analogy_name: The Michelin Star for Plastic Fruit
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Optimization Edition.' Everyone is obsessed with making hyper-realistic plastic fruit for display cases, but nobody knows if the fruit is any good. The authors didn't actually cook a meal; instead, they showed up with a 50-page manual on how to poke the plastic grapes with a toothpick to see if they 'feel' like real grapes. They're asking the dishwasher (the solver) to report back on how hard the plastic is to scrub, and then claiming this 'expert assessment' is a culinary breakthrough. It’s a very thorough rubric for a buffet where nothing is edible.

title: Comp-LTL: Temporal Logic Planning via Zero-Shot Policy Composition
