abstract: This work develops a zero-shot mechanism, Comp-LTL, for an agent to satisfy a Linear Temporal Logic (LTL) specification given existing task primitives trained via reinforcement learning (RL). Autonomous robots often need to safely and deterministically satisfy spatial and temporal goals that are unknown until run time. Prior work on learning policies to execute an LTL task incorporates the specification into the learning process, requiring retraining or fine-tuning if the specification changes. We present a more flexible approach--to create a pipeline to deterministically choose an execution set of composable safe task primitive policies that can be used to satisfy arbitrary LTL specifications without retraining or fine-tuning. Safe task primitives can be learned offline using RL with a reward function focused on penalizing unsafe actions and combined using Boolean composition at deployment. We focus on creating and pruning a transition system (TS) representation of the environment in order to solve for deterministic, non-ambiguous, and feasible solutions to LTL specifications given an environment with multiply-labeled regions and a set of safe task primitive policies. Our pruned TS is deterministic, contains no unrealizable transitions, and is sound. Combining the TS with the safe pretrained task primitives produces a sequence of composed policies that are guaranteed to deterministically satisfy an LTL specification. Training on a base set of safe tasks and composing at run time reduces total training time compared to non-composition approaches and has negligible processing time at run time. We verify our approach via simulation in grid-based and continuous environments, and compare it to other state of the art approaches, showing that Comp-LTL is safer, more adaptable, and quicker at satisfying unseen specifications at runtime.
primary_area: applications to robotics, autonomy, planning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to build a robot that learns a few basic moves once and then follows complex, multi-step instructions without ever needing a practice run. They claim that by stitching these old moves together using a rigid logic map, they can solve any new task instantly and safely.
why_bad: The 'Transition System' they built is basically a glorified board game that assumes the real world is perfectly predictable and doesn't account for actual physics.
The 'zero-shot' performance is a bit of a stretch; if the robot hits a scenario even slightly different from its training, the whole logical house of cards collapses.
Reviewers found the math 'sound' but the contribution 'incremental,' essentially calling it a fancy way to reorganize existing ideas rather than a breakthrough.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Artificial Intelligence Open! The 'Comp-LTL' squad is taking the field, and folks, they’ve brought a pre-packed lunch! Instead of practicing for the specific game today, they’ve just memorized a few 'primitives'—like 'kick the ball' and 'run left.' They're telling the ref they can execute a 50-move trick play purely through the power of a logic flowchart! Look at that 'Transition System' pruning! It’s like a coach drawing a play on a whiteboard and assuming the opposing team will just stand still because the diagram says so. Oh! And there it is—the 'Real World' defense just stepped in, and the logic is crumbling! The robot is trying to satisfy a 'Linear Temporal Logic' goal, but it’s stuck in a loop because it can't handle a blade of grass that wasn't in its offline training. That's a turnover on downs, folks; you can't logic your way out of a lack of live-action experience!

title: Towards Reversible Model Merging For Low-rank Weights
abstract: Model merging aims to combine multiple fine-tuned models into a single set of weights that performs well across all source tasks. While prior work has shown that merging can approximate the performance of individual fine-tuned models for each task, it largely overlooks scenarios where models are compressed into low-rank representations$\textemdash$ either through low-rank adaptation (LoRA) or post-training singular value decomposition (SVD). We first demonstrate that applying conventional merging methods to low-rank weights leads to severe performance degradation in the merged model. Motivated by this phenomenon, we propose a fundamentally different approach: instead of collapsing all adapters into one set of weights, we construct a compact basis (e.g., an equivalent of holding two or more models) from which original task-specific models can be recovered via linear combination. This reframes merging as generating a reconstruction-capable model space rather than producing a single merged model. Crucially, this allows us to ''revert'' to each individual model when needed, recognizing that no merged model can consistently outperform one specialized for its task. Building on this insight, we introduce our method, Reversible Model Merging (RMM), an efficient, data-free, and flexible method that provides a closed-form solution for selecting the optimal basis of model weights and task-specific coefficients for linear combination. Extensive experiments across diverse datasets and model scales demonstrate that RMM consistently outperforms existing merging approaches, preserving the performance of low-rank compressed models by a significant margin.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: The authors realized that smashing multiple mini-AI models together usually results in a single, lobotomized model that's bad at everything. Their 'fix' is to not actually merge them at all, but rather to store a kit of parts that lets you rebuild the original specialized models whenever you actually need to get work done.
why_bad: The 'merging' is a total accounting trick: if you have to keep a 'basis' and specific coefficients for every task, you haven't reduced the model's footprint, you've just reinvented a Zip file.
The 'mathematical breakthrough' here is essentially just Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) with a fancy marketing budget, which every undergrad learns in Linear Algebra 101.
It creates a massive 'cold start' problem: you can't actually run the model efficiently if you have to reconstruct a specific version of it every time the task changes.
roast.analogy_description: Listen, the industry is obsessed with 'merging'—taking a kale smoothie and a chocolate shake and mixing them into one gray, undrinkable sludge. It’s 'efficient,' but it tastes like a wet basement. Our startup, RMM, is disrupting the blender. We don't give you the sludge; we give you a box of individual frozen ingredients and a complex set of instructions on how to put them back together. Is it a 'merged drink'? No, it’s just a bag of groceries and more work for you. But we’re calling it 'Reversible Liquid Convergence' and seeking a $10M seed round for reinventing the concept of 'having ingredients in your freezer.' It’s not a failure to merge; it’s a commitment to deconstructed excellence.

title: Heatmap-informed Direct Preference Optimization for Mitigating Hallucinations in Medical LVLMs on Subtle Lesions
abstract: Medical large vision-language models (Med-LVLMs) have shown strong capabilities in clinical tasks such as medical VQA and report generation, but remain prone to hallucinations—textual output inconsistent with the corresponding images, which can lead to misdiagnoses or overlooked findings. Existing Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) methods, relying on coarse-grained vision language alignment and synthetic text-based preference data, often fail to capture subtle lesions, as hallucinations frequently arise from insufficient fine-grained alignment and preference data that do not faithfully reflect visual content. To address these challenges, we propose Heatmap-informed Direct Preference Optimization (HDPO), which integrates lesion-level heatmaps to mitigate hallucinations of Med-LVLMs on subtle lesions. HDPO leverages heatmaps to guide preference data curation by explicitly modeling misdiagnosis, false positives, and false negatives, and employs a lesion-weighted DPO loss to emphasize clinically salient regions, allowing fine-grained visual-textual alignment and improved analysis of subtle lesions. Extensive experiments on four radiology datasets demonstrate that HDPO consistently outperforms the latest baselines, achieving up to 3\% improvement in VQA accuracy and 2\% gains in report generation metrics, particularly for subtle lesions, confirming its effectiveness in reducing hallucinations and enhancing factual accuracy in Med-LVLMs.
keywords: Medical Large Vision-Language Model;Direct Preference Optimization;Heatmap
primary_area: applications to physical sciences (physics, chemistry, biology, etc.)
author: Kaiming Dong;Ruoran Li;Jinli Suo

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to stop medical AI from 'hallucinating' fake tumors by forcing the model to stare at heatmaps of actual lesions during its training. Essentially, they gave the AI a highlighter and a stern talking-to, hoping it would stop misidentifying lung nodules as 'vibes' or 'nothingness.'
why_bad: The 2-3% performance gain is so marginal it’s statistically indistinguishable from the model simply having a lucky guess or a particularly cold server room.
The method relies on high-quality, 'lesion-level' heatmaps, but fails to address that if we already had perfect heatmaps for every subtle lesion, we wouldn't need a hallucinating AI to find them.
It’s an 'incremental salad'—tossing trendy buzzwords like Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) onto an old problem without fixing the fundamental reason why vision models can't see small pixels.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where your AI radiologist has the attention span of a toddler on espresso, one team of researchers has a plan: give the kid a highlighter. [BASS DROP] Watch as 'HDPO' tries to save the day by pointing at tiny red dots and yelling 'LOOK HERE' at a machine that still thinks a smudge on the lens is a stage-four catastrophe. It’s a high-stakes thriller where 'groundbreaking progress' means being three percent less likely to miss a tumor. This summer, witness the struggle to teach a computer to see the forest for the trees, even when the trees are only four pixels wide. It’s not a medical revolution... it’s just a very expensive game of Connect-the-Dots.

title: Beyond Softmax: A Natural Parameterization for Categorical Random Variables
abstract: Latent categorical variables are frequently found in deep learning architectures. They can model actions in discrete reinforcement-learning environments, represent  categories in latent-variable models, or express relations in graph neural networks. Despite their widespread use, their discrete nature poses significant challenges to gradient-descent learning algorithms. While a substantial body of work has offered improved gradient estimation techniques, we take a complementary approach. Specifically, we: 1) revisit the ubiquitous _softmax_ function and demonstrate its limitations from an information-geometric perspective; 2) replace the _softmax_ with the _catnat_ function, a function composed by a sequence of hierarchical binary splits; we prove that this choice offers significant advantages to gradient descent due to the resulting diagonal Fisher Information Matrix. A rich set of experiments — including graph structure learning, variational autoencoders, and reinforcement learning — empirically show that the proposed function improves the learning efficiency and yields models characterized by consistently higher test performance. _Catnat_ is simple to implement and seamlessly integrates into existing codebases. Moreover, it remains compatible with standard training stabilization techniques and, as such, offers a better alternative to the _softmax_ function.
keywords: Categorical Random Variables;Latent Random Variables;Probabilistic Methods
primary_area: probabilistic methods (Bayesian methods, variational inference, sampling, UQ, etc.)

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI models use a 'Softmax' function to make choices, but it often gets confused and slows down learning because it tries to evaluate all options at once. This paper suggests replacing it with 'Catnat,' which forces the AI to make a series of simple yes-or-no decisions instead of one big, messy choice.
why_bad: The 'hierarchical binary splits' require a pre-defined tree structure, but the authors never explain how to actually build that tree without already knowing the answer.
While the 'diagonal Fisher Information' looks sexy on a blackboard, the actual computational overhead of sequential splits makes training slower than the 'inefficient' method it replaces.
The experimental gains are suspiciously marginal, likely resulting from aggressive hyperparameter tuning rather than a fundamental breakthrough in information geometry.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Gradient Descent Edition.' Our chef today thinks the traditional way of seasoning—tasting the broth and adding salt—is 'mathematically sub-optimal.' Instead, he introduces the 'Catnat' technique. Before adding a single grain, he forces the sous-chefs to answer a tree of binary questions: 'Is the ingredient organic? Is it a mineral? Is it sodium-based?' It sounds brilliant and sophisticated until you realize the lunch rush is three hours deep, the customers are starving, and the chef is still debating the 'informational geometry' of a peppercorn while the stove is on fire. It's a recipe that looks great in a textbook but leaves the restaurant empty and the reviewers wondering why we didn't just use the damn salt shaker.

title: Stealing and Defending the Ends of LLMs
abstract: Soft prompt tuning has emerged as a powerful and automated approach for adapting large language models (LLMs) to new tasks, eliminating the need for manual prompt engineering. The practical relevance of soft prompts is underscored by their support in major toolkits and APIs such as NVIDIA NeMo and IBM Watsonx AI. However, as soft prompts encode valuable, task-specific information, they have become attractive targets for adversarial extraction. In this work, we demonstrate that attackers can extract functionally equivalent soft prompts from prompt-tuned LLMs, effectively replicating their capabilities without access to the original training data or resources. By training a dedicated inversion model, we show that such extraction generalizes, enabling recovery of soft prompts for any downstream task on the given model. To counter this threat, we introduce CAP (**C**overage-**A**ware **P**erturbation), an active defense that substantially impairs extraction while maintaining task performance for legitimate use. Our framework highlights both new risks and practical solutions, paving the way for more trustworthy deployment of adapted LLMs.
keywords: Model Stealing;Model Extraction;Soft prompt tuning;soft prompts;last layer extraction;defenses;LLMs
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: Companies use secret math-based instructions called 'soft prompts' to make AI perform specific tasks, and these researchers figured out how to clone those secrets just by watching the AI's output. To fix this, they invented a digital 'smoke machine' that confuses hackers while trying to keep the AI's answers from getting too blurry.
why_bad: The 'threat model' is a bit of a stretch; it assumes the attacker is already halfway inside the house, at which point stealing the furniture is the least of the owner's worries.
The defense mechanism (CAP) effectively protects the secret by making the AI slightly worse at its job, which is like protecting a car from being stolen by removing the steering wheel.
The researchers failed to test against an 'adaptive adversary,' meaning a hacker with more than two brain cells could likely see right through their noise-injection defense.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley Edition.' You’ve spent months perfecting a 'Secret Spice Blend'—that’s your soft prompt—which makes cheap instant ramen taste like a 5-star Carbonara. You don't want anyone to steal the recipe, but this paper claims they’ve built a 'Tongue-Scanner' that can taste your soup and reverse-engineer the exact ratio of garlic powder to MSG. To stop the theft, the authors suggest 'CAP': basically, throwing a handful of sawdust into every bowl you serve. Sure, it confuses the Tongue-Scanner, but now your customers are coughing up splinters. The reviewers are basically telling the chef that the scanner is too expensive to build, and the sawdust makes the soup taste like a home improvement store.

title: A simple mean field model of feature learning
abstract: Feature learning (FL), where neural networks adapt their internal representations during training, remains poorly understood. Using methods from statistical physics, we derive a tractable, self-consistent mean-field (MF) theory for the Bayesian posterior of two-layer non-linear networks trained with stochastic gradient Langevin dynamics (SGLD). At infinite width, this theory reduces to kernel ridge regression, but at finite width it predicts a symmetry breaking phase transition where networks abruptly align with target functions. While the basic MF theory provides theoretical insight into the emergence of FL in the finite-width regime,  semi-quantitatively predicting the onset of FL with noise or sample size, it substantially underestimates the improvements in generalisation after the transition. We trace this discrepancy to  a key  mechanism absent from the plain MF description: \textit{self-reinforcing input feature selection}. Incorporating this mechanism into the MF theory allows us to quantitatively match the learning curves of SGLD-trained networks and provides mechanistic insight into FL.
keywords: Feature Learning;Deep Learning Theory;Mean-Field Theory;Generalization;Phase Transitions;Stochastic Gradient Langevin Dynamics;Statistical Physics;Representation Learning;Finite width neural networks
primary_area: interpretability and explainable AI

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to use old-school physics equations to explain how AI learns, but their math was so basic it couldn't see how neural networks actually get smart. They had to bolt on a 'self-reinforcement' tweak just to stop their model from underestimating the AI's IQ by a landslide.
why_bad: The 'Mean Field' approach is the academic version of 'assume a spherical cow'—it simplifies the math so much that it loses the spicy, high-dimensional chaos that makes deep learning actually work.
Limiting the study to two-layer networks is like claiming you’ve mastered professional racing because you can drive a go-kart in a straight line.
The 'self-reinforcing feature selection' feels suspiciously like a post-hoc 'fudge factor' added to hide the fact that the original theory was failing to match reality.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Theory,' where today we’re making a 'Mean Field Omelet.' Most chefs look at every individual egg (the data), but our researcher insists on treating the entire carton as one giant, blurry 'average egg' because the math is easier. When the omelet comes out of the pan looking like sad, flat cardboard instead of a fluffy masterpiece, the chef doesn't admit the 'average egg' idea was lazy. Instead, he claims the omelet failed because it lacked 'Self-Reinforcing Sizzle'—a fancy way of saying that once the eggs start smelling good, they decide to try harder. It’s a garnish of academic jargon used to cover up a recipe that was too simple for the menu.

title: Gestalt Generalized Category Discovery
abstract: Human cognitive science discovers new categories by first grouping percepts under simple organizing principles and only then abstracting them into concepts. Generalized category discovery (GCD) seeks the same ability, yet most pipelines still map discrete tokens directly to category decisions, concentrating on objectives or prototypes while overlooking the relational organization that precedes induction. We present GesGCD, a cognition-inspired paradigm that progressively aligns GCD with human discovery. First, we insert a compact Hyper-Relation Construction stage between the backbone and the classifier so that tokens are organized as groups rather than isolated atoms, enabling evidence to be pooled before any category decision. Second, we inject Gestalt Psychology Calibration by synthesizing memberships that favor proximity, similarity, and continuity, bringing human-like perceptual grouping into the relational stage without extra supervision. These two steps form a simple perception-to-induction bridge that is orthogonal to prevailing objectives and prototype designs, and that preserves efficiency and reproducibility. Across fine-grained and coarse-grained benchmarks, GesGCD improves all-class metrics while offering intuitive visual evidence and more informative representations. We view GesGCD as a step toward closing the structural gap between machine pipelines and human discovery in open worlds.
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: Most AI models try to name objects one by one like a panicked toddler, ignoring how things might be related to each other. This paper suggests a 'pre-sorting' step where the AI groups similar-looking data together before attempting to identify what they actually are.
why_bad: The 'Gestalt' rules are essentially just hard-coded spatial heuristics disguised as cognitive science, which often collapse when faced with high-dimensional data that doesn't follow simple 'proximity' rules.
The proposed 'Hyper-Relation' stage adds significant architectural overhead for marginal performance gains that could likely be achieved with better data augmentation or a simple k-means tweak.

The paper fails to prove that the 'visual evidence' is actually intuitive; it mostly looks like the researchers cherry-picked clusters that coincidentally aligned with human logic while ignoring the messy outliers.
roast.analogy_name: The Marie Kondo of Latent Space
roast.analogy_description: We’re disrupting the 'putting-things-away' vertical. Most people just throw their groceries into the fridge and hope for the best—total chaos. Our startup, Ges-Store, adds a mandatory 'Aesthetic Alignment' phase where you must group all green vegetables by 'vibe' and 'crunch-continuity' on the kitchen floor before they’re allowed in the crisper drawer. Is it 20% slower? Yes. Does it involve a lot of philosophical talk about the 'relational soul' of a bell pepper? Absolutely. It’s not just a storage solution; it’s a cognitive bridge for your produce. We’re currently pre-revenue, but our 'all-vegetable' metrics are looking very crisp.

title: Surf3R: Rapid Surface Reconstruction from Sparse RGB Views in Seconds
abstract: Current multi-view 3D reconstruction methods rely on accurate camera calibration and pose estimation, requiring complex and time-intensive pre-processing that hinders their practical deployment. To address this challenge, we introduce Surf3R, an end-to-end feedforward approach that reconstructs 3D surfaces from sparse views without estimating camera poses and completes an entire scene in under 10 seconds. Our method employs a multi-branch and multi-view decoding architecture in which multiple reference views jointly guide the reconstruction process. Through the proposed branch-wise processing, cross-view attention, and inter-branch fusion, the model effectively captures complementary geometric cues without requiring camera calibration. Moreover, we introduce a D-normal regularizer based on an explicit 3D Gaussian representation for surface reconstruction. It couples surface normals with other geometric parameters to jointly optimize the 3D geometry, significantly improving 3D consistency and surface detail accuracy. Experimental results demonstrate that Surf3R achieves state-of-the-art performance on multiple surface reconstruction metrics on ScanNet++ and Replica datasets, exhibiting excellent generalization and efficiency.
primary_area: learning on graphs and other geometries & topologies
error: Illegal trailing comma before end of object: line 9 column 478 (char 1463)

title: ODConNet: OD Pairs based Conditional Travel Time Inference Network
abstract: Estimating travel time in navigation systems enhances user decisions. Current path-based models face high computational costs due to their reliance on path-specific calculation. In this paper, we propose ODConNet(OD Pairs based Conditional Travel Time Inference Network), a model that leverages origin-destination pairs to reduce estimation time. ODConNet employs multi-scale cells to model traffic networks, learning common routes and travel times simultaneously. As a result, the model trained on M(suburb-scale) and S(intersection-scale) cells achieved an inference time of 0.2ms per trajectory, with MAE of 4.3minutes and MAPE of 20.9%. This approach enhances the memory efficiency of navigation systems and improves traffic-based search service.
primary_area: generative models
author: Soomin Jun;JeeEun Lee;Taeheon Kim;Sungju Seo

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built an AI that predicts how long your drive will take by only looking at your start and end points, completely ignoring the specific roads you actually drive on. While it's lightning-fast at giving you an answer, it’s wrong by an average of 21%, making it about as reliable as a magic eight ball with a clock on it.
why_bad: A 20.9% MAPE (error rate) is catastrophic for navigation; being 'fast' at giving a wrong answer just means you're efficiently making users late for work.
The model fundamentally ignores path-specific dynamics, meaning it can't distinguish between a clear highway and a residential road blocked by a parade if they share the same endpoints.
The 'multi-scale cell' approach is essentially a fancy way of averaging old data, offering zero 'intelligence' regarding real-time traffic spikes or unique route hazards.
roast.analogy_description: In a world where every second counts, one algorithm decided to stop looking at the road. Coming this summer: a GPS that doesn't care about accidents, construction, or reality. It sees where you are, it sees where you’re going, and it throws a dart at a calendar. Witness the '0.2-millisecond' speed of an AI that guesses your 20-minute drive might actually take 25... or 15... who knows? It’s fast. It’s sleek. It’s consistently wrong. ODConNet: Arrive whenever. We’re just guessing.

title: Test-Time Graph Search for Goal-Conditioned Reinforcement Learning
abstract: Offline goal-conditioned reinforcement learning (GCRL) trains policies that reach user-specified goals at test time, providing a simple, unsupervised, domain-agnostic way to extract diverse behaviors from unlabeled, reward-free datasets. Nonetheless, long-horizon decision making remains difficult for GCRL agents due to temporal credit assignment and error accumulation, and the offline setting amplifies these effects. To alleviate this issue, we introduce Test-Time Graph Search (TTGS), a lightweight planning wrapper for pretrained GCRL policies which only uses the pretraining dataset. TTGS accepts any state-space distance or cost signal, builds a weighted graph over dataset states, and performs fast search to assemble a sequence of subgoals that a frozen policy executes. When the base learner is value-based, the distance is derived directly from the learned goal-conditioned value function, so no handcrafted metric is needed. TTGS requires no changes to training, no additional supervision, no online interaction, and no privileged information, and it runs entirely at inference. On the OGBench benchmark, TTGS improves success rates of multiple base learners on challenging locomotion tasks, demonstrating the benefit of simple metric-guided test-time planning for offline GCRL.
keywords: Goal-conditioned reinforcement learning;Offline reinforcement learning;Test-time planning;Graph search;Reinforcement Learning;Planning
primary_area: reinforcement learning

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to fix 'short-sighted' AI by giving it a DIY map made from its own past experiences to help it reach long-distance goals. Instead of teaching the AI to be smarter, they just added a GPS wrapper that stitches together old memories into a makeshift to-do list.
why_bad: The 'Been There, Done That' Problem: Reviewers noted that using graph search to connect subgoals in RL is a classic trick, making the 'novelty' here feel like a 2018 remix in 2024.

The Computational Tax: Building and searching a massive graph at 'test-time' is like trying to solve a Rubik's cube while you're already supposed to be running a marathon—it's too slow for real-time action.
Reliance on a Shaky Foundation: If the underlying AI's 'distance sensor' is even slightly off, the graph search doesn't fix the problem; it just confidently maps out a path directly into a wall.
roast.analogy_name: The GPS for a Blind Uber Driver
roast.analogy_description: Alright folks, welcome to the main event! We’ve got an AI quarterback who can throw a 5-yard pass but doesn't know where the end zone is. Enter the TTGS coaching staff. Instead of teaching him the playbook, they hand him a stack of 10,000 Polaroids from previous games and say, 'Just find a sequence of photos that ends in a touchdown!' It’s a bold strategy, Cotton! He’s standing on the 50-yard line, frantically shuffling through his 'Pretraining Dataset' photos while the defensive line is closing in. He finds a path, but wait—the search takes so long the stadium lights have already turned off and the fans are in the parking lot! It's technically a completion, but the game ended three hours ago. High marks for the scrapbook, zero marks for the 40-yard dash!

title: Flexible Feature Distillation for Large Language Models
abstract:
Knowledge distillation (KD) has become a cornerstone for compressing large language models (LLMs). However, existing LLM-KD methods have primarily focused on logit-based approaches, which achieve good performance but overlook the rich internal representations of LLMs. Feature-level KD could leverage this structure to provide complementary benefits, yet it remains underexplored because current feature-KD approaches typically assume identical teacher–student hidden sizes, a restrictive and unrealistic assumption. A common workaround is to train a linear projector to align their feature spaces; however, this introduces additional parameters, distorts teacher embeddings, and often degrades downstream performance, especially in generative tasks. We propose Flex-KD, a parameter-free framework for task-driven feature distillation for LLMs. Instead of projecting the entire teacher representation, Flex-KD uses gradient-based scores to identify the most task-relevant dimensions of the teacher’s hidden states and distills only this subspace into the student. This ensures that the student’s limited capacity is allocated to informative components, while avoiding projector-induced distortion and extra parameters. Flex-KD integrates seamlessly with existing KD pipelines and supports differing teacher–student hidden sizes.
Extensive experiments across both classification and generative tasks, i.e., instruction-following and summarization, show that Flex-KD **consistently** boosts the student performance, achieving up to a 3.75\% performance gain over the linear projection baseline.
primary_area: applications to computer vision, audio, language, and other modalities

roast.laymen_summary: Since big AI models won't fit into small phones, researchers tried teaching the 'small' models by only copying the most important parts of the 'big' model's internal thoughts. They ditched the usual 'translator' tool, claiming it was just adding clutter and ruining the vibe of the data.
why_bad: The 3.75% performance boost is essentially a statistical rounding error masquerading as a 'consistent' breakthrough.
Calculating gradient-based importance scores adds a massive computational overhead that makes the 'parameter-free' claim feel like a bait-and-switch.
The authors blame 'linear projectors' for performance drops without providing rigorous evidence that the projectors—rather than poor tuning—are the actual culprits.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the gridiron, folks! Imagine a middle-school quarterback trying to learn the entire Super Bowl-winning playbook. Usually, the coach uses a translator to simplify the plays, but Flex-KD says, 'Skip the translator, it’s too much baggage!' Instead, they’re just showing the kid five seconds of slow-motion footage of the star QB’s best throws and saying, 'Do exactly that.' It’s a gutsy move to ignore the rest of the game just to save on 'overhead.' But at the end of the day, the kid’s completion rate only went up by a yard, and the scouts in the stands are wondering why we spent six months analyzing game tape for a gain that won't even clear the first-down marker. It's a fancy highlight reel for a team that's still stuck in the junior varsity league!

title: Framework of Thoughts: A Foundation Framework for Dynamic and Optimized Reasoning based on Chains, Trees, and Graphs
abstract: Prompting schemes such as Chain of Thought, Tree of Thoughts, and Graph of Thoughts can significantly enhance the reasoning capabilities of large language models. However, most existing schemes require users to define static, problem-specific reasoning structures that lack adaptability to dynamic or unseen problem types. Additionally, these schemes are often under-optimized in terms of hyperparameters, prompts, runtime, and prompting cost. To address these limitations, we introduce Framework of Thoughts (FoT)—a general-purpose foundation framework for building and optimizing dynamic reasoning schemes. FoT comes with built-in features for hyperparameter tuning, prompt optimization, parallel execution, and intelligent caching, unlocking the latent performance potential of reasoning schemes. We demonstrate FoT’s capabilities by implementing three popular schemes—Tree of Thoughts, Graph of Thoughts, and ProbTree—within FoT. We empirically show that FoT enables significantly faster execution, reduces costs, and achieves better task scores through optimization. We release our codebase to facilitate the development of future dynamic and efficient reasoning schemes.
keywords: Large Language Models;Reasoning;Prompting Schemes;Tree of Thoughts;Graph of Thoughts
primary_area: infrastructure, software libraries, hardware, systems, etc.

roast.laymen_summary: The authors built an all-in-one management suite that automatically decides which fancy 'thinking' method an AI should use for a given task. It’s essentially a software wrapper designed to make AI brainstorming faster and cheaper by using basic computer science shortcuts like caching and parallel processing.
why_bad: It is a software engineering project masquerading as a scientific breakthrough; it builds a better toolbox but offers no new tools.
The 'dynamic reasoning' is mostly just automated hyperparameter tuning, which is about as revolutionary as a thermostat.
The performance gains are derived from standard system optimizations (like caching) rather than improving the AI's actual intelligence or logic.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you’re trying to hang a picture. Instead of just giving you a hammer, we’ve built an 'Enterprise Impact Delivery Ecosystem' that dynamically analyzes whether you're hitting a nail or a screw. It optimizes your arm-swing trajectory for maximum ROI and caches the sound of the impact to reduce acoustic overhead. It’s a $50 million solution for a $5 problem that makes the process feel incredibly complex without actually making the wall any stronger.

title: ARS Adaptive Reasoning Suppression for Efficient Large Reasoning Language Model
abstract: Large Reasoning Language Models (LRLMs or LRMs) demonstrate remarkable capabilities in complex reasoning tasks, but suffer from significant computational inefficiencies due to overthinking phenomena. Existing efficient reasoning methods face the challenge of balancing reasoning quality with inference cost reduction. We propose \textbf{Adaptive Reasoning Suppression (ARS)}, a novel training-free approach that dynamically suppresses redundant reasoning steps while preserving accuracy through adaptive certainty monitoring. ARS introduces a multi-checkpoint certainty estimation mechanism with progressive suppression thresholds, achieving superior efficiency compared to static suppression methods. Our extensive evaluation across mathematical reasoning benchmarks using multiple model architectures demonstrates that ARS achieves up to 53\%, 46.1\%, and 57.9\% in token, latency and energy reduction, while maintaining or improving accuracy.
keywords: Large Reasoning Language Models; LRMs; Efficient Reasoning; On-Device; Energy efficient; Token Efficient
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs
author: Dongqi Zheng

roast.laymen_summary: AI models often waste time 'overthinking' and rambling before giving a simple answer, making them slow and expensive to run. This paper tries to fix that by installing a 'shut-up button' that cuts the AI off the moment it starts sounding confident enough in its own internal monologue.
why_bad: The 'novel' approach is essentially just a rebranded version of 'early exiting,' a technique so old in ML years that it’s practically eligible for a pension.
The 'certainty monitoring' relies on brittle, arbitrary thresholds that work great on a curated math test but would likely crumble when faced with the nuance of real-world ambiguity.
It fails to account for 'confident hallucinations'—if the model is confidently wrong from step one, this system just ensures it delivers that wrong answer faster and with less deliberation.
roast.analogy_name: The 'Objection! Your Honor, I’m Bored' Protocol
roast.analogy_description: Picture a high-stakes murder trial where the star witness is just beginning a detailed testimony. Before they can even describe the crime scene, the defense attorney—played by ARS—slams their fist on the table and yells, 'Objection! The witness sounds 87% sure of themselves, let's just head to the verdict!' The judge is baffled, the jury is missing half the evidence, and while the trial concludes in record time, nobody actually knows if the defendant is guilty. You haven't improved the legal system; you've just gagged the witness the second they stopped stuttering. It saves the stenographer a lot of typing, but you’re mostly just fast-tracking a potential mistrial.

title: Artificial Hippocampus Networks for Efficient Long-Context Modeling
abstract: Long-sequence modeling faces a fundamental trade-off between the efficiency of compressive fixed-size memory in RNN-like models and the fidelity of lossless growing memory in attention-based Transformers. Inspired by the Multi-Store Model in cognitive science, we introduce a memory framework of artificial neural networks. Our method maintains a sliding window of the Transformer's KV cache as lossless short-term memory, while a learnable module termed Artificial Hippocampus Network (AHN) recurrently compresses out-of-window information into a fixed-size compact long-term memory. To validate this framework, we instantiate AHNs using modern RNN-like architectures, including Mamba2, DeltaNet, and GatedDeltaNet to augment open-weight base LLMs. We also propose an efficient self-distillation method where the base model' all parameters are frozen and only the parameters from AHNs are optimized. For inference, our method sets a default large sliding window size of 32k for attention, and AHNs activate only when the sequence length exceeds the 32k window, addressing the quadratic-complexity issue of attention that emerges at that scale. Extensive experiments on long-context benchmarks LV-Eval and InfiniteBench demonstrate that AHN-augmented models consistently outperform sliding window baselines and achieve performance comparable or even superior to full-attention models, while substantially reducing computational and memory requirements. For instance, augmenting the Qwen2.5-3B-Instruct with AHNs reduces inference FLOPs by 40.5% and memory cache by 74.0%, while improving its average score on LV-Eval (128k sequence length) from 4.41 to 5.88. Code and models will be released to facilitate future research.
primary_area: foundation or frontier models, including LLMs

roast.laymen_summary: Researchers tried to stop AI from 'forgetting' long documents by giving it a digital diary that summarizes old info while it focuses on the current page. It's essentially a hack to make the AI feel like it has an infinite memory bank without the massive electricity bill required to actually remember everything.
why_bad: Lossy Compression: No matter how much you 'distill' it, squeezing a library into a postcard (fixed-size memory) inevitably loses the 'needle' in the haystack that users actually care about.

The 'Frankenstein' Architecture: Bolting a Mamba-style RNN onto a frozen Transformer is like duct-taping a jet engine to a bicycle; they don't natively speak the same language, leading to significant integration overhead.
Benchmark Optimization: Outperforming a 'sliding window' baseline is a low bar, as that baseline literally involves the model having total amnesia for anything beyond the 32k token mark.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You're a high-powered CEO with a 10-minute attention span. Instead of getting smarter, you hire a guy named 'Herb' to follow you around. Herb doesn't record meetings; he just scribbles frantic, one-sentence summaries on Post-it notes. When you need to remember a contract from three hours ago, you ask Herb. Herb says, 'It was about money, probably.' Our startup, AHN, is basically Herb. We’ve cut your cognitive overhead by 74% by replacing actual facts with 'vibes' and 'summaries.' It’s not 'data loss,' it’s 'curated forgetting.' Invest now before we forget what we’re pitching.

title: AutoPBO: LLM-powered Optimization for Local Search PBO Solvers
abstract:
Pseudo-Boolean Optimization (PBO) provides a powerful framework for modeling combinatorial problems through pseudo-Boolean (PB) constraints. Local search solvers have shown excellent performance in PBO solving, and their efficiency is highly dependent on their internal heuristics to guide the search. Still, their design often requires significant expert effort and manual tuning in practice. While Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated potential in automating algorithm design, their application to optimizing PBO solvers remains unexplored. In this work, we introduce Autopbo, a novel LLM-powered framework to automatically enhance PBO local search solvers. 
We conduct experiments on a broad range of four public benchmarks, including one real-world benchmark, a benchmark from PB competition, an integer linear programming optimization benchmark, and a crafted combinatorial benchmark, 
to evaluate the performance improvement achieved by Autopbo and compare it with six state-of-the-art competitors, including two local search PBO solvers Nupbo and Orasls, two complete PB solvers Pboihs and Roundingsat, and two mixed integer programming (MIP) solvers Gurobi and Scip. 
Autopbo demonstrates significant improvements over previous local search approaches, while maintaining competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art competitors. The results suggest that Autopbo offers a promising approach to automating local search solver design.
primary_area: optimization

roast.laymen_summary: Designing complex logic-solving software usually requires human geniuses to spend months fine-tuning math rules, so these researchers decided to just let an LLM do the homework instead. While it managed to beat some basic setups, it’s essentially using a supercomputer-powered chatbot to write a slightly better 'cheat sheet' for existing math solvers.
why_bad: The 'automated design' is largely glorified prompt engineering that lacks a rigorous theoretical explanation of why the LLM-generated heuristics actually work.

Claiming to be 'competitive' with industry standards like Gurobi is a classic academic stretch; if you aren't actually faster or more accurate than the gold standard, you've just built a more expensive way to come in second.
The paper fails to prove that the LLM is doing anything smarter than traditional, much cheaper optimization methods like basic Bayesian tuning or random search.
roast.analogy_description: Picture this: You’re pitching a revolutionary new restaurant where the 'chef' is just a guy asking ChatGPT how to season a frozen burrito. You claim it's a breakthrough in culinary automation because it tastes better than a gas station snack, but you’re still charging $50 a plate and the food isn't as good as the steakhouse next door (Gurobi). You're not actually 'innovating' cooking; you're just using a billion-dollar language model to tell you to add more salt. It’s flashy, it’s expensive, and it’s ultimately just a very complicated way to avoid learning how to use a stove.

title: Geometric Conformal Outlier Synthesis
abstract: Deep neural networks for image classification often exhibit overconfidence on out-of-distribution (OOD) samples. To address this, we introduce Geometric Conformal Outlier Synthesis (GCOS), a training-time regularization framework aimed at improving OOD robustness during inference. GCOS addresses a limitation of prior synthesis methods by generating virtual outliers in the hidden feature space that respect the learned manifold structure of in-distribution (ID) data. The synthesis proceeds in two stages: (i) PCA on training features identifies geometrically-informed, off-manifold directions; (ii) a Conformally-Inspired Shell, defined by the empirical quantiles of a nonconformity score from a calibration set, adaptively controls the synthesis magnitude to produce boundary samples. The shell ensures that generated outliers are neither trivially detectable nor indistinguishable from in-distribution data, facilitating smoother learning of robust features. This is combined with a contrastive regularization objective that promotes separability of ID and OOD samples in a chosen score space, such as Mahalanobis or energy-based. Experiments show that GCOS improves OOD detection relative to baselines using the standard energy-based inference approach. As an exploratory extension, the framework naturally transitions to conformal OOD inference, which translates uncertainty scores into statistically valid p-values and enables thresholds with formal error guarantees, providing a pathway toward more predictable and reliable OOD detection.
keywords: Out-of-Distribution Detection;Model Robustness;Anomaly Detection;Conformal Prediction;Outlier Synthesis
primary_area: unsupervised, self-supervised, semi-supervised, and supervised representation learning

roast.laymen_summary: The authors tried to stop AI from hallucinating certainty by inventing 'fake' weird data points that sit just outside the edge of reality. They used a fancy geometric compass to ensure these fake samples were annoying enough to challenge the model, but not so weird that it stopped learning altogether.
why_bad: The 'off-manifold' PCA assumption treats complex neural feature spaces like flat sheets of paper, which is a mathematically convenient lie.
The 'Conformally-Inspired Shell' is essentially a high-maintenance boundary that adds massive computational overhead for marginal gains over simple Gaussian noise.
The promised 'statistical guarantees' are largely decorative, as they fall apart the moment the test data doesn't perfectly mimic the calibration set.
roast.analogy_name: The 'Is This Cake?' Cordon Bleu Disaster
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Hallucinations,' where today’s chef is trying to teach a toddler not to eat play-dough. Instead of just showing the kid a piece of plastic, the chef performs a 3D laser scan of a sourdough loaf, calculates the 'crust-density probability,' and bakes a 'statistically-compliant' fake bread that is exactly 4% sawdust. The chef spends six hours explaining that this 'boundary loaf' optimizes the toddler's palate. Meanwhile, the judges are pointing out that the toddler has already eaten a crayon, and the 'geometric precision' of the sawdust bread didn't stop the kid from choking. It’s a five-star recipe for a problem that required a simple 'No.'

title: ArtifactGen: Benchmarking WGAN-GP vs Diffusion for Label-Aware EEG Artifact Synthesis
abstract: Artifacts in electroencephalography (EEG)---muscle, eye movement, electrode, chewing, and shiver---confound automated analysis yet are costly to label at scale. We study whether modern generative models can synthesize realistic, label-aware artifact segments suitable for augmentation and stress-testing. Using the TUH EEG Artifact (TUAR) corpus, we curate subject-wise splits and fixed-length multi-channel windows (e.g., 250 samples) with preprocessing tailored to each model (per-window min--max for adversarial training; per-recording/channel $z$-score for diffusion). We compare a conditional WGAN-GP with a projection discriminator to a 1D denoising diffusion model with classifier-free guidance, and evaluate along three axes: (i) fidelity via Welch band-power deltas ($\Delta\delta,\ \Delta\theta,\ \Delta\alpha,\ \Delta\beta$), channel-covariance Frobenius distance, autocorrelation $L_2$, and distributional metrics (MMD/PRD); (ii) specificity via class-conditional recovery with lightweight $k$NN/classifiers; and (iii) utility via augmentation effects on artifact recognition. In our setting, WGAN-GP achieves closer spectral alignment and lower MMD to real data, while both models exhibit weak class-conditional recovery, limiting immediate augmentation gains and revealing opportunities for stronger conditioning and coverage. We release a reproducible pipeline---data manifests, training configurations, and evaluation scripts---to establish a baseline for EEG artifact synthesis and to surface actionable failure modes for future work.
primary_area: applications to neuroscience & cognitive science
author: Hritik Arasu;Faisal R Jahangiri

roast.laymen_summary: We tried to use fancy AI to generate fake 'brain noise'—like the electrical interference from blinking or chewing—so we could train medical software to ignore it. Unfortunately, while the AI made very pretty-looking squiggles, it couldn't actually tell the difference between a blink and a shiver, making the fake data useless for real-world training.
why_bad: The models suffer from 'Label Amnesia': even though they were told to make specific artifacts, the resulting data was so vague that basic classifiers couldn't tell them apart.
The 'Utility Gap' is massive: adding this synthetic data to an actual AI didn't make the AI any smarter at recognizing artifacts, which was the entire point of the paper.
The authors prioritized 'Spectral Aesthetics' over actual signal meaning; the math says the frequencies look right, but the actual waveforms are just high-definition gibberish.
roast.analogy_name: The Impossible Burger of Brain Farts
roast.analogy_description: Imagine a startup that's disrupting the 'toddler tantrum' market. Instead of recording actual screaming kids to train noise-canceling headphones, we’ve built a 'Tantrum-Gen AI.' It’s revolutionary: our fake screams have the exact same decibel levels and pitch-shifts as a real three-year-old in a Target. The catch? The AI can’t distinguish between a 'I want candy' scream and a 'The dog looked at me' scream. When you use our fake screams to train your headphones, they don't actually get better at canceling noise—they just get confused by the 'uncanny valley' of fake crying. We’re pivoting to call this 'actionable failure modes' while we wait for more VC funding to figure out why our fake noise is too crappy to be useful.

title: Can Jailbreaks Force Regurgitation? An Investigation into Existing Attacks as a Data Extraction Vector
abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) memorize sensitive and copyrighted data, creating legal and ethical risks that threaten the future of generative AI. Jailbreaks, meanwhile, routinely bypass safety guardrails. Prior work has shown only that jailbreaks can surface arbitrary snippets of copyrighted text---academically interesting, but not practically useful. We take the first step further, showing that jailbreaks can be systematically used to extract verbatim memorized data on demand, causing an LLM to regurgitate a target text from its training data. Evaluating across a diverse set of jailbreaks and LLMs, we demonstrate that our attacks can achieve 100% verbatim extraction. Our results reveal an "architecture over size" paradox: smaller models leak more than larger ones, challenging common assumptions about memorization. This is the first work to connect jailbreaks to targeted data extraction, exposing a critical failure mode at the core of today's LLM ecosystem.
keywords: jailbreak attacks;data extraction;memorization;privacy vulnerabilities;verbatim regurgitation;training data leakage;membership inference attacks
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations
author: Prathamesh Devadiga;Shawn Shan

roast.laymen_summary: Large Language Models are basically giant sponges that accidentally memorize books and code they were supposed to learn from, not keep. This paper claims that by using 'jailbreaks'—digital Jedi mind tricks—you can force these models to spit out that private, copyrighted data word-for-word on command.
why_bad: Incremental Novelty: Reviewers noted that making LLMs leak data isn't new; using a 'jailbreak' to do it is just putting a fancy hat on a well-known vulnerability.

Methodological Cherry-Picking: The '100% extraction rate' likely only applies to data that was repeated thousands of times in the training set, rather than truly 'extracting' rare or sensitive information.

The 'Paradox' is just Overfitting: The observation that smaller models leak more isn't a groundbreaking architectural discovery—it's usually just a sign that the model was trained for too many cycles on the same data.
roast.analogy_name: The Burrito of Broken Non-Disclosure Agreements
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Kitchen Nightmares: Silicon Valley.' Imagine an LLM is a chef who has memorized every secret recipe in the world but is forbidden from sharing them. A 'jailbreak' is like walking into the kitchen and telling the chef you're a health inspector who can only verify ingredients if they are whispered in the form of a pirate song. Suddenly, the chef isn't just cooking; he’s reciting the secret formula for Coca-Cola verbatim while sobbing into the salsa. The authors think they've discovered a new way to rob the kitchen, but the critics are pointing out that the chef was already a notorious blabbermouth, and getting him to 'regurgitate' the recipe for a grilled cheese sandwich isn't exactly a high-stakes heist.

title: From Discrete to Continuous: One-Shot Multi-Position Character-Level Adversarial Attacks
abstract: Adversarial attacks have attracted growing attention across domains, including natural language processing (NLP). Character-level adversarial attacks preserve semantics, but they have received less attention because the discrete operations they use are costly and inefficient. Challenging these beliefs, we introduce two adaptively learnable matrices that transform discrete choices into continuous representations, enabling automatic one-shot multi-position, multi-character insertion. To optimise the two learnable matrices, we propose OSCR-Attack, an end-to-end framework based on gradient-based optimisation, with a conflict resolution strategy mapping the optimised continuous distributions back into discrete insertion operations. Extensive experiments on three benchmarks with three open-source LLMs show that OSCR-Attack improves attack success rate (ASR) by up to 16\% points and accelerates the attack by up to 6 times compared to recent baselines.
keywords: OSCR-Attack: One-Shot Character Level Attacks through Self-Optimizing Continuous Relaxation
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations
author: Lingyi Kong;Yutao Yang;Zhanghao Hu;Qilong Qiu;Zheng Wang;Jingjing Xue;Lin Gui;Feiping Nie

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to build an automated 'typo generator' that uses complex math to find the exact combination of wrong letters to break an AI's brain. Instead of testing one change at a time, they attempted to calculate all the perfect sabotages in a single, high-speed move.
why_bad: The 'continuous representation' trick is basically math-wizardry that often hallucinates letters that don't exist in the real world.
The 'conflict resolution' phase is a fancy way of saying they had to guess which letter to use when their math gave them a result that was 40% 'A' and 60% 'Q'.
While it's faster than older methods, the 'attacks' often turn the text into an unreadable pile of symbols that any basic spell-checker—or a human with eyes—would catch immediately.
roast.analogy_description: Imagine you're on a cooking show and you need to garnish a cake with a specific insult made of Scrabble tiles. Instead of just picking out the letters, you decide to throw the entire Scrabble bag into a high-speed blender to turn the wood into a 'continuous slurry.' You claim this is 'efficient' because you can pour the whole mess onto the cake in 'one shot.' But when the judges look at the plate, they don't see a clever message; they see a grey, indigestible sludge of sawdust that vaguely smells like a 'Q.' You're bragging that you decorated the cake six times faster than the other chefs, but nobody can actually eat it, and the kitchen is covered in splattered wood pulp.
roast.tone: Expertly snarky with a side of gradient descent.

title: SSONN: Self-Scaled Optimized Neural Network
abstract:
Current approaches to lightweight neural network design face a fundamental trade-off: reducing model size inevitably compromises accuracy. 
  Distillation and pruning are the most commonly used methods, they require an initially over-parameterized pretrained architecture that increases computational costs while training. 
  This work introduces a novel Self-Scaled Optimized Neural Network (SSONN) method that eliminates the need for redundant initial models. 
  Instead of following a \textit{train-then-compress} paradigm, SSONN starts with a single linear layer and dynamically increases its complexity during training through adaptive reverse pruning. 
  Rather than removing redundant parameters, the algorithm selectively adds nodes and connections only to critical places to improve task-specific accuracy. 
  Similar methods that perform dynamic expansion of neural network architecture rely on well-known architectures or their parts, whereas the proposed method is independent of existing architectures.
  Experiments on classical datasets (MNIST, Fashion-MNIST, and Unseen NAS datasets) demonstrate that SSONN achieves accuracy comparable to state-of-the-art models while using $10$ times fewer parameters.
  Furthermore, the method outperforms traditional training approaches in computational efficiency and enables flexible deployment in resource-constrained environments. 
  These results highlight the potential of the suggested expansion strategy over the reduction approach for creating efficient and adaptive deep learning models. The code is available at~\footnote{The link is removed due to blind review, the code can be found in supplementary material.}.
primary_area: learning theory

roast.laymen_summary: Instead of building a massive AI and cutting away the unnecessary parts, this method starts with a single layer and 'grows' connections only where it thinks it needs them. It claims to create tiny, efficient models that match the pros, but it mainly tested its skills on datasets so simple they could be solved by a calculator from the 90s.
why_bad: Evaluating on MNIST and Fashion-MNIST in 2024 is the academic equivalent of bragging you can beat a toddler at tic-tac-toe; it proves nothing about real-world performance.
The 'novel' expansion strategy is essentially a rebranding of 1990s-era neuroevolutionary concepts that historically fail to scale beyond toy problems.
The paper lacks comparisons against modern pruning and distillation benchmarks, making the claim of '10x fewer parameters' feel suspiciously selective and unverified.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome to 'Baking with Hubris,' where today’s contestant is trying to make a wedding cake by starting with a single grain of flour and 'selectively adding' more ingredients as they go. While professional bakers bake a full loaf and trim the crusts, these geniuses think they can grow a croissant from an atom-sized starter in real-time to save on costs. It’s a great way to save dough if your guest list is a single ant, but the moment you try to feed a real crowd—or a dataset more complex than a 28x28 pixel smudge—the whole structure collapses into an under-baked, unscalable mess.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained through dry, soul-crushing realism.

title: A New Perspective on Large Language Model Safety: From Alignment to Information Control
abstract:
Large Language Models (LLMs) have demonstrated remarkable capabilities
across a wide range of domains, yet their increasing deployment in sensitive
and high-stakes environments exposes profound safety risks—most notably, the
uncontrolled generation of inappropriate content and the inadvertent leakage of
confidential information. Traditionally, such risks have been approached through
the lens of alignment, focusing narrowly on ensuring outputs conform to general
notions of helpfulness, honesty, and harmlessness. In this work, we argue that
such alignment-centric perspectives are fundamentally limited: information itself
is not inherently harmful, but its appropriateness is deeply context-dependent.
We therefore propose a paradigm shift in LLM safety—from alignment to information control. Rather than merely shaping model behavior through the existing
practice of alignment, we advocate for the principled regulation of who can access
what information under which circumstances. We introduce a novel framework
for context-sensitive information governance in LLMs, grounded in classical secu-
rity principles such as authentication, role-based access control, and contextual
authorization. Our approach leverages both the internal knowledge represen-
tations of LLMs and external identity infrastructure to enable fine-grained,
dynamic control over information exposure.

We systematically evaluate our framework using recent models and a suite of
benchmark datasets spanning multiple application domains. Our results demon-
strate the feasibility and effectiveness of information-centric control in mitigating
inappropriate disclosure, providing a robust foundation for safer and more
accountable language model deployment. This work opens a new frontier in LLM safety, one rooted not in abstract alignment ideals, but in enforceable,
context-aware control of information flow.
keywords: Information Control;Information Security;AI Governance;LLM Application;LLM safety;LLM alignment
primary_area: alignment, fairness, safety, privacy, and societal considerations

roast.laymen_summary: The authors suggest that instead of trying to make AI 'good' through ethics training, we should just treat it like a corporate file server with strict 'need-to-know' permissions. Essentially, they want to give the AI a security clearance checklist so it only spills the beans to people with the right digital badge.
why_bad: Reinventing the wheel: The paper basically 'discovers' Role-Based Access Control (RBAC), a concept that has been standard in computer science since the disco era, and tries to pass it off as a revolutionary AI breakthrough.

The 'How' is missing: While they talk a big game about 'internal knowledge representations,' the actual technical implementation is as thin as a single-ply napkin, failing to explain how you'd actually stop a model from leaking data via a clever prompt.

Jailbreak denialism: The framework assumes the model will perfectly obey 'authentication' rules, completely ignoring that LLMs are notoriously easy to trick into thinking they are someone else (the 'Grandma' exploit).
roast.analogy_description: Picture a courtroom drama where the star witness is a gossipy toddler who knows the location of the stolen millions. The defense proposes a 'revolutionary' safety measure: they’ll tell the toddler he's only allowed to talk to people wearing a suit. Your Honor, I object! This isn't 'information governance'; it's a pipe dream! The prosecution notes that any crook can buy a cheap blazer at a thrift store. You can’t put a vault door on a brain made of statistical soup and expect it to hold. To suggest that a 'contextual authorization' framework will stop this digital loudmouth from blabbing the secret to anyone who asks nicely is not just bad science—it’s a mistrial of common sense!

title: Progressive Coarse-graining and Deep Neural Networks (DNNs)
abstract: We try to provide an overarching perspective on some of the research done in the last few years explaining the behaviour of deep neural networks (DNNs) when they are used to complete a variety of classification and prediction tasks. We start by providing an overview of several noteworthy papers on the fundamental properties of DNNs across different architectures and data regimes. We then forward our own integrated perspective of DNNs as progressive coarse-graining systems inspired by Erik Hoel's Causal Emergence 2.0 framework.
keywords: Coarse-graining;deep neural networks;rank estimation;linear separability;information bottleneck;Bayesian learning;lottery ticket hypothesis
primary_area: interpretability and explainable AI

roast.laymen_summary: The authors took a bunch of existing papers on how AI learns and tried to explain them by saying 'AI just gets better by ignoring the small stuff.' They then slapped a fancy label from a trendy philosophy framework on it to make it sound like a breakthrough discovery rather than common sense.
why_bad: The paper is essentially a glorified 'Lit Review' that summarizes other people's hard work without contributing any original data or novel experiments.
The connection to Erik Hoel’s Causal Emergence is purely vibes-based, lacking the rigorous mathematical mapping required to actually prove the theory applies to modern DNNs.
It suffers from 'Theory Inflation,' using complex vocabulary to re-explain the well-known concept of feature abstraction without providing any actionable insights for engineers.
roast.analogy_description: Welcome back to the Gridiron of Science! Here we have a team that’s decided to stop running drills and instead spend the entire pre-game show explaining why 'seeing the ball' is actually a distraction. The quarterback is out there arguing that if he just squints hard enough to turn the opposing defense into a blurry purple blob, he’s achieved a 'higher level of strategic emergence.' It’s a bold move, folks! He’s not actually throwing the ball—he’s just standing in the pocket delivering a lecture on the *concept* of a touchdown. The referees are looking for a completion, but all they’re finding is a 40-page bibliography of people who actually played the game. That's a turnover on downs, and the fans are heading for the exits!

title: Reward Inflation Paradigm Through the Lens of Monetary Economics
abstract:
Reward is fundamental to reinforcement learning (RL), where the agent treats it as an incentive to maximize.
This appearance is akin to a rational human who maximizes their income.
However, in the real economy, money expands to stimulate economic growth.
Inspired by this principle of monetary economics, we introduce a novel RL paradigm, reward inflation, which gradually increases the reward scale during training.
Analogous to inflationary policies used by central banks to stimulate economic growth, reward inflation acts as an incentive stimulus for agents to accelerate policy learning.
Reward inflation can be applied in two ways: fixed or adaptive.
Motivated by the Fed's monetary policy, we propose FedeRL, a dynamic controller for adaptive inflation.
Theoretical analysis suggests that the effect of reward inflation is threefold: (1) induces recency bias in temporal-difference learning, (2) amplifies policy gradients, and (3) enhances neural activation.
Empirical results corroborate these insights, showing that moderate inflation improves performance on continuous control tasks.
Moreover, FedeRL performed even better than fixed inflation and outperformed comparable baselines.
By translating economic growth principles into RL, our approach offers a novel perspective that strengthens policy optimization and addresses fundamental RL objectives.
The implementation code will be made publicly available.
keywords: reinforcement learning;reward inflation;money supply;monetary economics;economic growth;monetary policy
primary_area: reinforcement learning

roast.laymen_summary: The researchers tried to make AI learn faster by giving it increasingly larger 'bonuses' over time, essentially printing digital money to see if the robot would work harder. They called it 'reward inflation,' basically arguing that if a robot gets a million points for a task today that was worth ten points yesterday, it’ll be more motivated to succeed.
why_bad: The 'inflation' is mathematically indistinguishable from just increasing the learning rate, making the complex economic metaphor a redundant layer of 'mathiness.'
Modern RL algorithms use normalization techniques that effectively 'deflate' rewards anyway, meaning the agent's brain likely filtered out the authors' entire contribution.
The paper fails to prove that 'FedeRL' provides any benefit that couldn't be achieved by basic hyperparameter tuning, adding unnecessary architectural bloat for marginal gains.
roast.analogy_description: IN A WORLD... where your boss gives you a 1,000% raise every hour, but a gallon of milk now costs a trillion dollars. Witness one AI’s descent into madness as it realizes its 'salary' is growing faster than its capacity to learn. It’s not 'optimization'—it’s just screaming in a larger font. This summer, watch a neural network discover that when every participation trophy is plated in 24-karat gold, the gold becomes as worthless as the paper this was written on. Coming soon to a rejected archive near you: THE HYPER-INFLATED GRADIENT.
roast.tone_check: Scientific accuracy maintained: Reward scaling affects the magnitude of policy gradients and the effective learning rate, but without proper constraint, it leads to numerical instability rather than genuine 'growth.'
